title: Reliable Receiver Design for Frequency-Selective Channels under Bursty Impulsive Noise
authors: Hazem Barka, Md Sahabul Alam, Georges Kaddoum, Minh Au, Basile L. Agba
abstract: Wireless communication systems operating in industrial environments are often subject to bursty impulsive noise and frequency-selective fading that significantly degrade their performance. Under these conditions, conventional orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) receivers struggle to maintain reliable communication. In this paper, we address these challenges by introducing a reliable receiver design consisting of two key components. First, we develop an adaptive memory-aware noise covariance estimation method using neural networks to dynamically predict the noise level for each received symbol. Second, we propose a channel state information (CSI) enhancement method using two-dimensional average pooling and interpolation (2DAPI) to smooth out inaccuracies in CSI caused by bursty impulsive noise. We further derive a theoretical bit error rate (BER) performance approximation and incorporate a lower bound benchmark for rigorous evaluation. The results of our extensive simulations demonstrate the extreme power efficiency of the proposed receiver, which requires less than 10% of the transmit power as compared to conventional OFDM receivers that use linear minimum mean square error (LMMSE) equalizers and least squares (LS) CSI estimation to achieve the same BER. In addition, performance analyses across various modulation schemes and fading models confirm the reliability and adaptability of our design in diverse operational environments.

title: Split Learning-Enabled Framework for Secure and Lightweight Internet of Medical Things Systems
authors: Siva Sai, Manish Prasad, Animesh Bhargava, Vinay Chamola, Rajkumar Buyya
abstract: The rapid growth of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) devices has resulted in significant security risks, particularly the risk of malware attacks on resource-constrained devices. Conventional deep learning methods are impractical due to resource limitations, while Federated Learning (FL) suffers from high communication overhead and vulnerability to non-IID (heterogeneous) data. In this paper, we propose a split learning (SL) based framework for IoMT malware detection through image-based classification. By dividing the neural network training between the clients and an edge server, the framework reduces computational burden on resource-constrained clients while ensuring data privacy. We formulate a joint optimization problem that balances computation cost and communication efficiency by using a game-theoretic approach for attaining better training performance. Experimental evaluations show that the proposed framework outperforms popular FL methods in terms of accuracy, F1-score, high convergence speed, and less resource consumption. These results establish the potential of SL as a scalable and secure paradigm for next-generation IoMT security.

title: On-Orbit SWIR Imagery MTF Compensation for the Geology-1 Satellite Using Cross-Spectral Structural Priors
authors: Haixu He, Jining Yan, Zichen Jing, Lizhe Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Geology-1 hyperspectral satellite employs a lightweight and highly integrated payload architecture, establishing a novel paradigm for commercial small-satellite-based geological observation. However, the compact opto-mechanical configuration exacerbates the coupling among residual attitude disturbances, cryocooler-induced micro-vibrations, and the imaging chain, leading to systematic degradation of the system Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) in shortwave infrared (SWIR) imagery. To address this challenge, we propose a structure-guided, two-stage Geology-1 SWIR Imagery MTF Compensation (GSMC) framework. This framework leverages co-registered visible and near-infrared (VNIR) imagery, which offers superior spatial resolution and enhanced imaging stability, as a natural structural prior. Consequently, a zero-shot compensation model is constructed without requiring external training data. Experimental results demonstrate that GSMC delivers consistent and superior performance across diverse geological scenes. Quantitative evaluation shows that the MTF at the Nyquist frequency increases from 0.204 to 0.297 after compensation. The proposed method preserves high radiometric fidelity, with a mean relative error of only 0.57%, while also improving downstream task performance by increasing land-cover classification accuracy from 83.43% to 89.19%. In addition, spectral consistency analysis verifies that the cross-spectral guidance strategy does not distort SWIR-specific information, with spectral angle mapper (SAM) values remaining below 0.05. These results indicate that the proposed framework effectively compensates for time-varying jitter degradation in Geology-1 imagery and provides reliable technical support for quantitative on-orbit processing and high-precision geological mapping.

title: Semantic Noise Decomposition and Saliency-Weighted Mutual Information in Task-Oriented Communication
authors: Nargis Fayaz, Ranjan K. Mallik, Brejesh Lall
abstract: This work characterizes semantic noise from a task-oriented perspective in semantic communication systems. Semantic distortion is decomposed into source-side, channel-side, and receiver-side components, revealing that source and receiver semantic noise can dominate task performance even when channel-induced semantic errors are negligible. A dependency-aware, saliency-weighted semantic mutual information metric is introduced to quantify task-relevant meaning preservation and to establish a direct relationship between semantic fidelity and task distortion under a semantic rate–distortion framework. Triplet-based semantic examples and illustrative analysis validate how semantic noise and task saliency jointly influence task performance, providing an interpretable foundation for saliency-aware and robust task-oriented semantic communication design.

title: Periodically Poled Piezoelectric Lithium Niobate Resonator for Piezoelectric Power Conversion
authors: Ziqian Yao, Clarissa Daniel, Lezli Matto, Heather Chang, Vakhtang Chulukhadze, Michael Liao, Jack Kramer, Eric Stolt, Tzu-Hsuan Hsu, Mark S. Goorsky, Juan Rivas-Davila, Ruochen Lu
abstract: As demand for compact and efficient power conversion systems increases, piezoelectric power converters have attracted attention for their ability to replace bulky magnetic inductors with acoustic resonators, thereby enabling higher power density and improved efficiency. Achieving optimal converter performance requires resonators with high quality factor (Q), strong electromechanical coupling (k2), high power handling capability, and a spurious-free response. Lithium niobate (LN) has emerged as a promising material in this context due to its high figure of merit (FoM = Q · k2). While previous studies on single-layer LN resonators have demonstrated high FoM values, they typically operate at relatively low resonance frequencies (fs). Recently, periodically poled piezoelectric film (P3F) structures, formed by stacking piezoelectric layers with alternating crystal orientations, have shown the potential to both scale up the operating frequency and enhance the FoM compared to single-layer counterparts in piezoelectric power conversion. This work presents the first P3F thickness-extensional (TE) LN resonator for power conversion, operating at 19.23 MHz, with a large k2 of 29% and a high maximum Bode Q of 3187, achieving a state-of-the-art (fs · Q) product among piezoelectric power resonators. A high-power testing procedure is employed to systematically investigate the nonlinear behavior and power handling capabilities of P3F LN for power applications. With further optimization, P3F TE resonators have the potential to enable a new design space for high-power, high-frequency power conversion.

title: A Holistic Remote Fork Strategy Toward Fast Function Scaling in Serverless Edge Computing
authors: Zhexiong Li, Deze Zeng, Lin Gu, Wenjie Zhang, Quan Chen, Wenxin Li, Keqiu Li
abstract: Serverless edge computing, despite its flexibility and efficiency, is hindered by high startup latency during peak load. Remote fork, employing either Checkpoint/Restore (C/R) or Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA), offers a potential solution for function scaling acceleration. Although RDMA fork is faster, the opportunities are limited, whereas C/R fork is more common but slower. Moreover, the regeneration capability that a forked function can further fork new instances complicates the remote fork decisions for fast scaling. Therefore, in this paper, we are motivated to address the problem on how to holistically exploit C/R fork and RDMA fork with the consideration of the underlying infrastructure features (e.g., topology, resources, etc.) to realize fast function scaling. We first formulate it to an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) problem. We further introduce a Heat metric to assess the potential of an edge server as a fork destination according to the topology and resource availability, and propose a Heat-based fork strategy (HEAT) for both the fork destination site and the corresponding fork mode decisions. Experiment results demonstrate that HEAT improves the function scaling speed by 46% and RDMA resource utilization by 51%, compared to state-of-the-art scaling solutions.

title: Coupled Phase-Shift STAR-RIS Enabled Integrated Over-the-Air Computation and Communications
authors: Shuzhen Yuan, Chao Zhang, Junjie Fang, Yuanwei Liu, Suhua Tang, Qingqing Wu
abstract: To meet the emerging demands for rapid data aggregation and reliable information transmission in future wireless applications, a novel system architecture integrating Over-the-Air Computation (AirComp) and downlink multi-user communication via a simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS) is proposed in this paper. In the considered cellular scenario, an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) carries a STAR-RIS beneath its fuselage, creating a programmable aerial platform that concurrently serves Internet-of-Things (IoT) devices and conventional mobile users. The STAR-RIS operates in transmission mode to enable efficient wireless data aggregation of IoT devices, while its reflection mode establishes high-quality downlink channels from the base station (BS) to multiple users. Capturing the true electromagnetic behavior of the STAR-RIS, we explicitly model the practical coupling between the reflection and transmission phase shifts. Two optimization problems are then formulated: one minimizes AirComp distortion and the other maximizes the minimum user rate in the downlink. Both non-convex problems are tackled by efficient iterative algorithms derived from the penalty dual decomposition (PDD) framework. Extensive simulations confirm that the proposed design markedly outperforms baseline approaches and its performance can approach that of ideal phase-shift control by enhancing the key system parameters. Additionally, the trade-off between computation and communication performance is demonstrated.

title: Machine Learning Pipelines for Image-based Classification of Rice Grain Varieties: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Disathon Wattanadhirach, Worapan Kusakunniran
abstract: Rice is one of the staple foods whose value varies depending on the varieties. Inspection for its authenticity and purity is a standard process in the supply chain. Over the past decade, image-based machine learning has increasingly been studied as a non-destructive approach for rice variety classification using grain visual information. To methodically portray the existing work, this study conducts a systematic literature reviewby gathering, summarizing, and grouping related studies into categories based on their workflows. From 101 studies, the identified pipelines were categorized as classification-only, separation-and-classification, and reconstruction-and-classification structures. The review summarizes study characteristics, input transformations, model categories, reported evaluation metrics, normalized performance summaries, and cross-structure findings, with indexed end-to-end tables provided for further reference. Overall, this review supports researchers in selecting workflow designs aligned with their objectives or deployment conditions, while highlighting limitations, research directions, and reporting recommendations to improve reproducibility and cross-study comparability in future studies.

title: TransFM: Visible-to-Infrared Image Translation via Flow Matching
authors: Meiqi Gong, Hao Zhang, Bingwei Hui, Jiayi Ma
abstract: Dear Editor, Due to the scarcity of high-quality infrared data, translating visible images to infrared has become a practical solution to meet the growing demand for infrared images in low-light and adverse conditions. Due to the large modality gap and limited prior information, existing visible-to-infrared (VIS-to-IR) image translation methods often struggle with poor structural preservation, unclear cross-modal correspondence, and loss of thermal details. To address these challenges, this letter presents TransFM (Flow Matching based Translation), a conditional flow matching framework that, for the first time, models the VIS-to-IR translation process as a continuous and smooth trajectory. By avoiding the stochastic iterative denoising of diffusion models and explicitly learning a smooth transformation path, TransFM allows efficient and stable generation. To effectively steer flow matching optimization toward more accurate translation, TransFM incorporates three complementary priors including geometric priors, semantic priors and fine-detail priors, reinforcing structure consistency, modality-aware generation, and texture-level fidelity through-out the translation process. Experimental results show that TransFM significantly improves structural integrity, target saliency, and fine detail fidelity over existing methods.

title: An Agentic System for Schema Aware NL2SQL Generation
authors: David Onyango, Naseef Mansoor
abstract: The natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) task plays a pivotal role in democratizing data access by enabling non-expert users to interact with relational databases through natural language. While recent frameworks have enhanced translation accuracy via task specialization, their reliance on Large Language Models (LLMs) raises significant concerns regarding computational overhead, data privacy, and real-world deployability in resource-constrained environments. To address these challenges, we propose a schema based agentic system that strategically employs Small Language Models (SLMs) as primary agents, complemented by a selective LLM fallback mechanism. As the LLM is invoked only upon detection of errors in SLM-generated output, the proposed system significantly minimizes computational expenditure. Experimental results on the BIRD benchmark demonstrate that our system achieves an execution accuracy of 47.78% and a validation efficiency score of 51.05%, achieving over 90% cost reduction compared to LLM-centric baselines as approximately 67% of queries are resolved using local SLMs. The system achieves an average cost per query of $0.0085 compared to $0.094 for LLM-only systems, achieving near-zero operational costs for locally executed queries.

title: Comprehensive Multi-View Clustering via Low-Dimensional Subspace Representation
authors: Shaojun Shi, Xuanming Zhang, Canyu Zhang, Feiping Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Although previous multi-view subspace clustering approaches have achieved tremendous progress, there still exist some weaknesses in most of them. Firstly, previous algorithms usually adopt self-representation to construct the loss function, which often leads to high computational complexity. Secondly, they typically learn similarity matrices without block structures, which may adversely affect the clustering partition. Thirdly, the view weights are often assigned equally, neglecting that each view feature descriptor exerts different degrees of influence on clustering results. In light of this, we design a new clustering model with dynamic subspace representation and propose a novel Multi-view Subspace Clustering method based on Anchor Graph and Consensus Representation (MSCAGCR). Specifically, representative samples are selected and iteratively updated to reduce the time cost. Moreover, the introduction of a rank constraint on the Laplacian matrix corresponding to the similarity graph ensures that the clustering results contain connected components whose number is identical to the number of classes. Furthermore, the exponential weighting strategy enhances the usability of high-quality view features while reducing the impact of inferior ones. Extensive experiments conducted on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that MSCAGCR outperforms existing methods in terms of clustering effectiveness and efficiency.

title: TCSC: Time-Consistent Synchronization and Completion for Bridging the Temporal Sim-to-Real Gap in Embodied Intelligence
authors: Yizhe Chen, Zhibo Pang, Jia Wang, Yu Liu, Dapeng Lan, Chuanfen Feng
abstract: Temporal misalignment arising from heterogeneous hardware sampling rates and network jitter poses a critical challenge in embodied intelligence, especially for consumer-grade domestic robots, creating a fundamental mismatch between the policy’s perceived state and the physical world. Standard approaches typically rely on implicit alignment or rigid synchronization, which fail to balance temporal fidelity with information completeness. In this work, we introduce Time-Consistent Synchronization and Completion (TCSC), a unified protocol designed to mitigate this mismatch without altering policy architectures. We first formalize temporal inconsistency using a Wasserstein-based metric and analyze its impact on joint information structure. Our analysis reveals a non-monotonic trade-off: while tightening history windows improves temporal consistency, excessive strictness degrades performance due to modality dropout. TCSC resolves this by enforcing strict causal synchronization constraints during inference while leveraging offline interpolation-based completion during training to align data distributions end-to-end. Extensive evaluations across simulated and real-world domestic tasks (e.g., smart home object tidy-up on Cobot Magic) demonstrate that TCSC significantly improves policy robustness against asynchronous perturbations, outperforming baselines that neglect the temporal structure of multimodal data.

title: Decentralized Resilient Synchronization for Cyber-Physical Microgrids with FDI Attacks and Uncertainties
authors: Qian Zhang, Xiaoqing Lu, Chang Yu
abstract: The large amount of new energy with intermittency and volatility mainly connect to microgrids through electrical inverters with low inertia and weak damping, and the introduction of control signals make it highly vulnerable to cyber attacks, which exacerbates the frequency regulation difficulty for microgrids in open environments. This paper investigates a novel decentralized resilient synchronization strategy for cyber-physical microgrids with cyber FDI attacks and physical uncertainties, by establishing a class of second-order kuramoto-oscillator models. To endow the inverter-interfaced distributed generators (DGs) with virtual inertia support and comprehensively estimate the FDI attack and uncertainties, we propose a conditioned super-twisting sliding-mode controller together with a finite-time super-twisting sliding-mode observer, by integrating the attack detection and improved terminal sliding-mode technology with super-twisting control structure. Sufficient conditions are derived by constructing asymmetric barrier Lyapunov function, to guarantee the phase and frequency synchronization simultaneously. The effectiveness validation is carried out though both simulations in MATLAB/SimPowerSystems and experiments in dSPACE-based prototype physical platform.

title: Testing Method for Language Model Evaluation: A Case Study on a Localized Question Bank
authors: Tsu-Yi Yeh, Po-Yu Hsieh, Cheng-Chuan Huang, Pang-Chieh Wang, Tzy-Shiah Wang, Yu-Tai Li
abstract: This study proposes a reproducible and verifiable methodology to standardize test bank comparisons for language model assessments. The proposed method integrates statistical analysis and psychometric theory, employing Classical Test Theory (CTT) and Item Response Theory (IRT) to examine the validity and reliability of the question bank, while utilizing structured prompts to enable automated testing. Furthermore, a Model Group Benchmarking approach is introduced to establish three statistical reference tiers—High, Average, and Low benchmarks—as quantitative indicators for comparing model performance. To validate the feasibility of the proposed framework, a Taiwanese localized language question bank was used as an application example to evaluate model performance across five dimensions: accuracy, fairness, reliability, information security, and privacy. The results demonstrate that this method effectively establishes a standardized process for language model testing, ensuring consistency in testing conditions and procedures. In addition, the proposed testing method provides reasonable, reliable, and discriminative evaluation results, serving as empirical support to inform future ISO/IEC experimental testing standards for language model evaluation and to support ISO/IEC 17025 accreditation as a reference.

title: High-fidelity 3D reconstruction for planetary exploration
authors: A. Martínez-Petersen, L. Gerdes, D. Rodríguez-Martínez, C. J. Pérez-Del-Pulgar
abstract: Planetary exploration increasingly relies on autonomous robotic systems capable of perceiving, interpreting, and reconstructing their surroundings in the absence of global positioning or real-time communication with Earth. Rovers operating on planetary surfaces must navigate under severe environmental constraints, limited visual redundancy, and communication delays, making onboard spatial awareness and visual localization key components for mission success. Traditional techniques based on Structure-from-Motion (SfM) or Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) provide geometric consistency but struggle to capture radiometric detail or to scale efficiently in unstructured, low-texture terrains typical of extraterrestrial environments. This work explores the integration of radiance field-based methods—specifically Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and Gaussian Splatting— into a unified, automated environment reconstruction pipeline for planetary robotics. Our system combines the Nerfstudio and COLMAP frameworks with a Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2)-compatible workflow capable of processing raw rover data directly from rosbag recordings. This approach enables the generation of dense, photorealistic, and metrically consistent 3D representations from minimal visual input, supporting improved perception and planning for autonomous systems operating in planetary-like conditions. The resulting pipeline establishes a foundation for future research in radiance field–based mapping, bridging the gap between geometric and neural representations in planetary exploration.

title: The Potential of Thermal Sand Based Storage for Renewables in Saudi Arabia
authors: A. A. Alsager, K. A. Alhamdan, M. A. Alanazi, M. Qurayn
abstract: The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia possesses abundant solar energy resources and experiences a hot and arid climate, rendering it an ideal location for thermal sand-based energy storage. This technology enables the heating of sand to high temperatures for extended periods. Thermal sand-based storage operates by using solar energy to heat sand to high temperature. The heated sand can be effectively stored in insulated tanks for days, or even weeks. When electricity demand rises, the stored heat can be employed to rise the temperature of a heat transfer agent which, consecutively, generates steam for power generation. Compared to other storage technologies, Sand-based thermal storage technology has several advantages. These include scalability, extended life span, and comparatively cost-effective operation and construction. The paper’s main objective is to investigate the possible technological and economic advantages of thermal sand-based storage technology, emphasizing its contribution in the development of renewable energy, particularly in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. Additionally, it investigates how this technology may enhance the affordability and reliability of renewable-based energy, ultimately reducing the nation's dependence on non-renewable energy sources.

title: Blockchain and Ai for Smart Cities Energy Systems
authors: Volodymyr Pavlenko, Ihor Ponomarenko, Oksana Morhulets, Dmytro Ponomarenko, Olena Hrygorevska, Maryna Melnyk
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The importance of the evolution of energy systems in smart cities by the principles of sustainable development has been proven. The significance of digitalization processes' influence on the generation of large volumes of heterogeneous information by smart cities, which can be used for the development of effective management solutions, has been established. The prequisites for the development of various technologies for the accumulation and processing of big data, primarily blockchain, and artificial intelligence algorithms, are revealed. The necessity of conducting comprehensive research on the integration of blockchain technology and artificial intelligence into the energy systems of smart cities has been proven, which will allow for qualitative infrastructure transformations and achieve optimal results. The expediency of identifying new directions of transformation of energy systems by the economic optimization of smart cities' operations and the need to achieve zero emissions of carbon and other harmful substances is substantiated. The relationship between the level of innovations introduced in the energy sphere of smart cities and consumer satisfaction with the comfort of life in the corresponding agglomeration has been established. The structure of energy systems in smart cities is presented, which allows to achievement of a high level of efficiency and is characterized by the innovativeness of all elements and accompanying infrastructure. The expediency of conducting energy management as an important component of smart cities' energy management has been proven, which allows identifying potential risks and quickly adapting the system to the specifics of the manifestation of internal and external environmental factors. The scheme of blockchain integration into the energy systems of smart cities and the main areas of the specified technology use are presented. The advantages of using artificial intelligence are revealed due to the presence of a large number of algorithms, which are characterized by the ability to constantly learn based on input information and find optimal solutions. The scheme of using artificial intelligence for the formation of effective solutions based on the data of the energy system of a smart city is given. The feasibility of using artificial intelligence algorithms in the energy systems of smart cities on an ongoing basis has been established, which involves the continuous collection of relevant information and the use of the obtained data for modeling with the subsequent introduction of the obtained solutions into the energy infrastructure and the achievement of optimal economic and energy-saving results.

title: Graph Neural Networks for Class Imbalance Learning and Detecting Fraud Transactions
authors: Wei Liu, Ehsan Hallaji, Roozbeh Razavi-Far
abstract: Detection of credit card fraud remains a serious challenge due to the extremely low rate of fraud transactions and the limitation of current tabular datasets with the assumption of transactions as independent samples. This paper explores the potential of graph neural networks (GNNs) for fraud detection through the transformation of tabular transaction data into graph representations that capture relational patterns between samples. We develop and analyze a graph-based learning pipeline, emphasizing its practical trade-offs in deployment and evaluation. Apart from training the model, we emphasize the importance of graph construction choice, imbalance control, and interpretability through GNN explainers. We study several GNNs in the same graph settings and use GNNExplainer to understand model decisions. This helps us identify important features and structural relationships that influence fraud predictions. The experimental results show that the employed GNNs effectively use relational structure and achieve desirable fraud detection performance. These findings demonstrate that a table-to-graph learning pipeline for fraud detection is possible. They also highlight the importance of graph construction, handling imbalances, and ensuring interpretability in real-world applications.

title: Profitability and Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Wireless and Conductive Charging Infrastructure for Autonomous Fleets: Insights From Real-World Data
authors: M. Tiemann, M. Boehm, N. Haussmann, M. Clemens, B. Schmuelling
publish: IEEE Journal on Wireless Power Technologies
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive technoeconomic assessment of conductive, static wireless (SWPT), and dynamic wireless power transfer (DWPT) for autonomous electric shuttle fleets. Using real-world cost and operational data from the MILAS test deployment, the study evaluates lifecycle cost, net profit, and amortization under realistic utilization patterns. A Monte-Carlo-based sensitivity analysis quantifies the impact of electricity price volatility, labor cost, utilization and component degradation on total cost of ownership. A RAM analysis incorporating mean time between failures (MTBF), mean time to repair (MTTR) and scheduled charging downtime highlights the availability limitations of conductive charging and the operational advantages of wireless alternatives. Results show that SWPT offers the highest economic robustness, while DWPT becomes most advantageous in high-utilization, continuous-operation scenarios. Conductive charging remains viable only for low-utilization, manually operated fleets. Overall, wireless charging enables higher profitability, resilience, and automation readiness for future autonomous transport systems.

title: Emergency Dispatch Strategy for Electric Vehicles Based on Spatio-Temporal Distribution Characteristics and User Response Willingness
authors: Haiyan Wang, Jidong Chen, Xiaotao Zhou
abstract: Traditional emergency power supply strategies in distribution networks often suffer from delayed response, limited deployment flexibility, and high operating and emission costs during unexpected outages. Most existing methods rely on static or heuristic scheduling while neglecting the heterogeneous spatio-temporal distribution of electric vehicles (EVs) and their varying willingness to provide grid support. This paper proposes an EV-based emergency dispatch strategy that jointly incorporates spatio-temporal availability and user response willingness. A coupled traffic–power framework is established to integrate travel-chain-based charging-load forecasting with dynamic price negotiation between the distribution network operator and EV users. By jointly considering stochastic mobility, initial state of charge (SOC), and users’ temporal anxiety, a fuzzy willingness model is developed to characterize participation propensity and derive willingness scores for online screening. A multi-round negotiation mechanism is then introduced to determine individualized compensation prices and link the negotiation outcomes with the subsequent dispatch stage. The dispatch model aims to prioritize critical-load restoration, reduce outage losses, and improve user-side economic benefits. Case studies show that the proposed framework can effectively mobilize EV resources for emergency support, improve load-restoration performance, reduce total economic loss, and enhance distribution-system resilience compared with conventional backup-generator-based strategies.

title: Structure-Aware Matrix Pencil for Estimating the Number of Damped Complex Exponentials_supp1-3698108.pdf
authors: Yehonatan Itay Segman
abstract: We address the problem of detecting the number of damped complex exponentials and estimating their parameters from a noisy signal using the Matrix Pencil (MP) method. We introduce the MP modes and present their informative spectral structure. We show theoretically that these modes can be divided into signal and noise modes, where the signal modes exhibit a perturbed Vandermonde structure. Leveraging this structure, we propose a new MP algorithm, termed the SAMP algorithm, which has two novel components: (i) a robust, theoretically grounded model order detection method, and (ii) an efficient estimation of the signal amplitudes. We show empirically that SAMP significantly outperforms the standard MP method in challenging conditions, with closely-spaced frequencies and low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) values, approaching the Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRB) for a broad SNR range. Additionally, compared with prevalent information-based criteria, we show that SAMP achieves comparable or better results with lower computational time, and lower sensitivity to noise model mismatch.

title: Transferability Reinforcement of Adversarial Attacks for Remote Sensing Image Classification via Hierarchical Transformation Composition
authors: Yimin Fu, Yuefeng Bai, Jialin Lyu, Baicheng Pan, Zhunga Liu, Michael K. Ng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Adversarial attacks constitute an effective means of evaluating model robustness and revealing intrinsic weaknesses. Since practical model deployment typically adheres to black-box settings, existing attack methods often employ multiple input transformations to enhance the transferability of adversarial examples. However, remote sensing images often exhibit ambiguous foreground–background distinctions and various geospatial discrepancies, resulting in high model specificity in discriminative cues for classification. Consequently, the input patterns simulated through fixed transformation schemes are insufficient to prevent overfitting to the source model, thereby limiting the transferability of the generated adversarial examples. To solve this issue, we propose a hierarchical transformation composition (HTC) framework that reinforces adversarial transferability through a coordinated action execution strategy. Specifically, the determination of input transformations is formulated as an adaptive action selection procedure, which is progressively executed by multi-level policy networks. Then, the policy networks are iteratively updated via proximal policy optimization based on the advantage estimates from a shared value network. Moreover, a hybrid reward mechanism is introduced to dynamically integrate loss information from both feature and output layers. Rather than directly imposing directional constraints on gradient calculation, the supervision is shifted to the optimization of policy networks, which prevents the sacrifice of intrinsic attack capacity while enhancing transferability. Extensive experiments on the UCM and SIRI-WHU datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance across various model architectures. The code will be released at https://github.com/fuyimin96/HTC upon acceptance.

title: Leveraging Cross-Modal Information to Reduce Gender Bias in Facial Analysis Systems
authors: Iris Dominguez-Catena, Daniel Paternain, Aranzazu Jurio, Mikel Galar
abstract: Facial analysis systems exhibit well-documented biases, particularly at the intersection of demographic attributes and facial expressions. Common mitigation approaches typically require costly model retraining or impose explicit fairness constraints that may compromise overall performance. We propose a novel cross-modal correction approach that leverages the complementary biases present in specialized models to achieve fairness through awareness. By combining gender predictions from FairFace with emotion predictions from EmoNet through a lightweight correction network, we demonstrate that models can naturally discover and correct bias patterns without explicit fairness objectives. Our method requires no retraining of base models and adds minimal computational overhead. Experiments on FACES and ADFES datasets show substantial improvements in both accuracy and fairness: emotion prediction accuracy increases from 80.7% to 92.9% on unseen test data, while average gender gaps decrease by from 13% to 7.5%. These gains transfer to gender prediction as well, with accuracy improvements of 2% and gap reductions of 6% respectively. This work presents a practical, model-agnostic solution for bias mitigation that can be readily applied to existing deployed systems.

title: Study of Factors Influencing the Energy Characteristics of Electromechanical Systems
authors: Oleg Zakladnyi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article analyzes the factors influencing the energy efficiency of electromechanical systems: the quality of the supply voltage, load mode, operating conditions; the quality of manufacturing, maintenance and repair; and the quality of active and structural materials. The quality of the supply voltage and the load mode have a critical impact in real operating conditions. The mathematical modeling shows that if the deviations of the PQI exceed the values normalized by the standards, the normal operation of electromechanical systems is either impossible or can be ensured only in the case of a significant load reduction. A significant reduction in the energy efficiency of electromechanical systems occurs even when the PQI vary within the limits allowed by the standards.

title: Oscillation-Based Locomotion of a Spherical Robot Driven by Flywheels With Low Control Torque*
authors: Yituo Song, Yanfang Liu, Jianhang Sun, Muhang Liu, Debo Kong, Xu Wang, Lei Chen
abstract: This paper presents a spherical robot driven by flywheels with low control torque, which combines terrain adaptability with flexible steering capability. The robot's center of mass is located below the sphere center, enabling sustained oscillations on the ground. Meanwhile, by periodically swinging its heading from side to side, the robot walks forward, eventually achieving locomotion along a zigzag trajectory. The robot can accumulate energy via oscillation under flywheel excitation, reducing the torque requirement and enabling a small actuator to drive a relatively heavy system. Experiments validate the robot's locomotion performance across various scenarios. The proposed actuation scheme demonstrates balanced performance while achieving lower torque demand under comparable normalized conditions.

title: ORION: Option-Regularized Deep Reinforcement Learning for Cooperative Multi-Agent Online Navigation
authors: Shizhe Zhang, Jingsong Liang, Zhitao Zhou, Shuhan Ye, Yizhuo Wang, Derek Ming Siang Tan, Jimmy Chiun, Yuhong Cao, Guillaume Sartoretti
abstract: Existing methods for multi-agent navigation typically assume fully known environments, offering limited support for partially known scenarios with outdated or imperfect prior maps, such as warehouses or factory floors. There, agents need to balance path optimality with collecting and sharing environmental information to help teammates reach their own targets. To these ends, we propose ORION, a novel deep reinforcement learning framework for cooperative multi-agent online navigation in partially known environments. Starting from an imperfect prior map, ORION trains agents to make decentralized decisions, coordinate toward individual targets, and actively reduce task-relevant map uncertainty through online observation sharing in a closed perception—action loop. We first design a shared graph encoder that fuses prior map with online perception into a unified representation, providing robust state embeddings under environmental discrepancies. At the core of ORION is an option-critic framework that learns high-level cooperative modes translated into sequences of low-level actions, enabling adaptive switching between individual navigation and team-level exploration. We further introduce a dual-stage cooperation strategy that allows agents to assist teammates under map uncertainty, thereby reducing the overall makespan. Across extensive maze-like maps and large-scale warehouse environments, ORION achieves high-quality real-time decentralized cooperation while scaling to up to 10 robots, outperforming state-of-the-art classical and learning-based baselines. Finally, we validate ORION on physical robot teams, demonstrating its robustness and practicality for real-world cooperative navigation.

title: An Overwriting Vulnerability in IoT Authentication Protocols: Attacks and Countermeasures
authors: Feng Li, Danfeng Lin, Yaobin Shen, Lu Tang, Rongxin Wu, Qiao Xiang
abstract: Authentication protocols are the foundation for se cure communication between Internet of Things (IoT) entities, enabling different entities to mutually authenticate each other and negotiate a session key. We discover an “overwriting vulnerability” that exists in two authentication protocols: [IEEE TMC, 2025, 24(5): 4529-4542] and [JSA, 2024, 152, 103176]. By exploiting this vulnerability, an adversary can easily overwrite the authentication information of honest users on the server side, thus permanently preventing them from completing the authentication. We provide concrete attack methods for the protocols and implement the two protocols with code and successfully conduct attacks on them within a local area network. Then, we propose two distinct countermeasures designed to address the deployment status of the protocol: one is intended for protocols that have already been deployed, and the other for those that are yet to be deployed. Finally, we take the TMC protocol as an example for repair, subsequently discussing the security of the repaired protocol, and conducting comparative experiments and analysis with existing protocols. The experiments demonstrate that the repaired protocol still holds an advantage in terms of security and efficiency.

title: MigMate: A VS Code Extension for LLM-based Library Migration of Python Projects
authors: Matthias Kebede, May Mahmoud, Mohayeminul Islam, Sarah Nadi
publish: Proceedings of the 3rd ACM/IEEE International Workshop on Integrated Development Environments
abstract: None

title: Design of Quarter Mode SIW Self Diplexing Slotted Antenna
authors: Kinny Garg, Ashish Kumar, Geetanjali Singla, Preeti Sharma, Ashwini Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: A two-port slotted self-diplexing antenna is designed based on Quarter Mode SIW (QMSIW) resonators is presented in this article. The anticipated antenna structure resonates at 14.8 GHz and 15.7 GHz with the input from port 1 and 2 respectively. The QMSIW has been formed by bisection of the full mode SIW (FMSIW) and half mode SIW (HMSIW), then final QMSIW has been joined for the proposed configuration. The proposed design occupies the compact area of 0.588 $\lambda _0^2$ (Where λ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf> is calculated at 7.2 GHz). The proposed design resonates in Ku-band frequency with peak gain of about 5.91 dB and also exhibits the mutual coupling less than -20 dB between the two ports. The anticipated antenna is suitable for the automotive satellite communications.

title: Energy-Efficient Federated Learning for Space Observation via Neuromorphic Computing: a Use Case
authors: Domenico Lofù, Paolo Sorino, Tommaso Di Noia, Eugenio Di Sciascio
abstract: Fully reconfigurable and software-defined payloads characterize recent space missions requiring onboard image recognition. This allows image recognition algorithms to adapt dynamically in response to various perturbations. As image recognition solutions based on Artificial Intelligence are computationally intensive and lacking flexibility; neuromorphic-based methods have emerged as promising alternatives. In this regard, SNN-based models are designed for optimal energy efficiency and enhanced performance in image recognition tasks. To address these challenges, we propose an energy-efficient approach, instrumental in space missions, involving the implementation of image recognition systems based on neuromorphic computing principles. This approach also includes their distribution through Federated Learning (FL) and the use of a Salomon map to protect images from being captured or attacked. We adopt an SNN to identify the images in space with 85% of Precision. In addition, the image recognition task exhibits a voltage-based activity proxy totalling 210 mV. Finally, by adopting our optimized approach, we achieve savings of 60%, 64%, 150%, in Transfer Time, Energy Consumption and Average Transmission Speed, respectively. Our analysis shows that the proposed approach is ideal for identifying objects in space and transmitting them to earth, guaranteeing privacy and data security.

title: Deepfake Detection in Solar Panel Imagery Using Hybrid Vision Transformer Architecture
authors: Ananya Verma, Pratyush Chandra, Lohitha Chandana Vemury, Pallabi Saikia
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Deepfake is an application that is derived from deep learning, and has emerged over time. As deepfake technology advances and spreads, new categories of fabricated images are constantly emerging. Simultaneously, detection methods are also evolving. This paper involves using a hybrid deep learning architecture combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) to detect synthetic/ manipulated solar images. The proposed model achieves 95.76% accuracy on a diverse dataset of 7,547 solar panel images.

title: Design of a Cable-Driven, Self-Aligning Knee Exoskeleton to Eliminate Joints' Misalignment Across Users With Varying Leg Dimensions
authors: Xinhua Yang, Sheng Guo, Lianzheng Niu, Duxin Liu, Majun Song, Peiyi Wang, Xiangyang Wang
abstract: One of the primary difficulties in exoskeleton use is the misalignment between the device's mechanical joints and the user's natural biological joints. Knee exoskeletons, widely studied for assisting walking and stand-to-sit transitions, typically require precise adjustment of the rigid frame to match the user's knee joint axis. If not properly aligned, parasitic forces arise at the physical human–robot interface (pHRI), causing significant discomfort. To address this problem, we propose a cable-driven, self-aligning knee exoskeleton that accommodates different wearing positions without the need for accurate pre-use alignment. Platform experiments showed that the exoskeleton adapted well to variations in wearing position in the deactivated mode and transmitted assistive torque with limited changes in parasitic forces when activated. At an assistive torque of 18 Nm, the relative error between the measured and target values was 7.27%. Human-subject experiments further demonstrated substantial reductions in relative sliding distance (RSD) and parasitic forces during activities such as walking and stand-to-sit transitions: the maximum RSD was reduced by 84.9% compared with the Orthosis condition and by 85.1% compared with the Locked condition; the maximum parasitic force was reduced by 89.1% and 89.0%, respectively, and the maximum parasitic torque was reduced by 95.6% and 96.1%, respectively.

title: Unlocking High-Frequency Temporal Visual Cues for Personality Traits Recognition
authors: Yusong Wang, Keyu Mao, Minghao Shao, Zheyuan Gu, Qingsong Zhao, Kotaro Funakoshi
abstract: Personality traits recognition (PTR) plays a pivotal role in developing intuitive human-computer interaction systems. Visual-based PTR methods have achieved notable success by leveraging dynamic cues such as facial expressions and body movements. However, they typically rely on sparse frame sampling to maintain computational efficiency. This strategy inherently discards high-frequency temporal visual cues such as micro-expressions and instantaneous gaze shifts, which are important for accurate personality traits recognition. To address this limitation, we propose FrePTR, a framework that captures and integrates high-frequency temporal visual cues for visual-based PTR. Unlike existing motion- or event-based representations, which mainly quantify geometric displacement or generic intensity variation, FrePTR introduces a retina-inspired dual-pathway extractor to model high-frequency cues for PTR. Motivated by the observation that personality impressions from visual behavior are formed through the eye, whose retina captures rapid temporal transients, the extractor decomposes high-frequency PTR cues into two complementary types: cone-inspired chromatic physiological transients and rod-inspired achromatic motion dynamics. Since the high-frequency and low-frequency streams originate from different signal sources, they exhibit heterogeneous feature distributions and reliability patterns, which makes feature-level fusion difficult. We therefore develop a heteroscedastic uncertainty-guided late fusion mechanism for reliability-aware integration at the decision level. Extensive experiments on the ChaLearn First Impressions and ELEA datasets show that FrePTR outperforms advanced visual-only methods and remains competitive with multimodal methods, indicating the benefit of incorporating high-frequency temporal visual cues into PTR.

title: From Co-Pilots to Co-Workers: A Formal Typology of Human–Agent Collaboration in Organizations
authors: Francisco-Javier Rodrigo-Ginés, Jorge Chamorro-Padial, Jorge Pretel-Villanueva, Javier Aguilera-Aguilera, Valentino Lugli
abstract: Organizations are increasingly deploying agentic AI to function as semi-autonomous collaborators within end-to-end business processes. Yet, existing human–autonomy teaming research seldom addresses organizational roles, handoffs, and accountability in enterprise contexts. This paper introduces a design-oriented framework for human–agent collaboration that defines a typology of roles characterized by four measurable dimensions (autonomy, reversibility, criticality, and accountability) and a governance-oriented taxonomy of human control levels. The framework includes an indicative mapping to major AI governance standards (IEEE 7001 transparency levels, NIST AI RMF, and ISO/IEC 23894) and proposes process-level metrics such as task success, end-to-end latency, override rate, incident or near-miss frequency, and explainability coverage. Three enterprise scenarios (IT Operations, Customer Service, and Legal Operations) illustrate how to calibrate autonomy and oversight without eroding accountability, offering a practical guide for designing trustworthy hybrid organizations.

title: A Minimal Brain–Computer Interface for Efficient Mission-Level Control in Industrial Human–Robot Interaction
authors: Mojtaba Porghoveh, Raffaele Carli, Mariagrazia Dotoli
abstract: As robots increasingly operate in industrial environments, ergonomic and hands-free human–robot interaction becomes essential. This paper proposes a brain–computer interface (BCI) for mission-level icon selection (e.g., scan, mill, pick, return), emphasizing operator comfort and low computational cost. Electroencephalography data are preprocessed by low-pass filtering, and wavelet coefficients are extracted as descriptive features. To reduce hardware complexity, two electrode selection methods are tested: (i) Forward Stepwise Feature Selection and (ii) Independent Component Analysis based ranking. For classification, a compact neural network with Fuzzy C-means clustering is employed. Using a public P300 speller dataset, mission icons are mapped to the stimulus matrix to emulate drone mission selection in warehouse scenarios. The reduced-channel approach achieves accuracy comparable to full-channel models while requiring only six electrodes, a training set of 360 samples, and fewer than 10–16 training epochs. The results show that compute-efficient BCIs with minimal electrodes can effectively support practical mission-level robot control in industrial settings.

title: Adaptive Knowledge Retrieval in Multimodal GraphRAG via Cross-Modal Relationship Learning
authors: Dipanjan Sengupta, Samriddha Chatterjee, Prayati Dutta, Avinandan Bandyopadhyay
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enriching large language models (LLM) with domain-specific knowledge. While GraphRAG extends this approach through Knowledge Graphs (KG), existing methods remain largely confined to text-based entities. This paper proposes a multimodal framework that integrates textual and illustration content into a unified KG, further aggregated into community-level knowledge for deeper contextualization, while preserving modality specific indices for efficient data storage. The framework supports client-driven customization at two stages: (1) during graph construction, where relationship-building strategies can be tailored to control how associations are derived, and (2) at query time, where users can select retrieval scope-text-only, illustration-only, or multimodal. Retrieval follows a two-stage process: direct matches are first identified within modality-specific indices, then expanded through the KG to capture semantically related entities from the other modality. For multimodal queries, a reranking step is applied after LLM inference to optimize relevance and coherence across modalities. This architecture enables faster, adaptive and context-rich augmentation of LLM, overcoming the limitations of existing GraphRAG approaches. By supporting multimodal reasoning, customizable retrieval pathways and inference-aware reranking, the framework provides a scalable foundation for enterprises to enhance decision-making, compliance and knowledge management across diverse domains.

title: Target Tracking in Cell-Free Multi-Static ISAC Systems with Network-Assisted Full-Duplex
authors: Maowen Luo, Ruoyun Liu, Xiaoyu Sun, Jiamin Li, Pengcheng Zhu, Dongming Wang
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has become a key technology for enabling joint data transmission and environmental sensing in future wireless networks. This letter investigates target tracking in a multi-static ISAC system with network-assisted full-duplex, where all access points are equipped with half-duplex transceivers and dynamically assigned downlink or uplink duplex modes by a central processing unit. To enable target tracking, a low-complexity delay–Doppler extraction method based on two-dimensional fast Fourier transform with spectral peak refinement using Quinn’s method is developed, and the multi-static measurements results are fused via an extended Kalman filter. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves reliable tracking performance.

title: Photonics Breakthroughs 2025: Three-Dimensional Holographic Incoherent Imaging Through Scattering Media
authors: YoonSeok Baek, Hilton B. de Aguiar, Sylvain Gigan
abstract: High-resolution three-dimensional (3D) imaging through optically complex media remains a highly desirable yet persistent challenge for numerous applications. This Highlight focuses on our recent method for noninvasive 3D reconstruction from scattered incoherent light, while placing it within the broader context of recent progress in imaging through highly scattering environments, particularly for spatially incoherent modalities such as fluorescence microscopy. In contrast to earlier approaches that were largely limited to two-dimensional imaging, our framework enables volumetric reconstruction by computationally retrieving the underlying optical field together with the associated scattering information. Using emission signals alone, the method achieves diffraction-limited 3D reconstruction and provides a route toward robust imaging through complex media in a fully noninvasive manner. Finally, we outline remaining technical limitations and discuss computational strategies for improving robustness and extending this approach, as well as related methods, to demanding microscopy applications.

title: VBT-MPC: Vision-Based Tactile MPC for Contour Following_supp1-3698362.mp4
authors: Edison Patricio Velasco Sanchez

title: Self-supervised Representation Learning for Dynamic Functional Connectivity with Subject-wise and Temporal Contrasts_supp1-3698236.pdf
authors: Chunzhi Yi

title: Access Point Deployment for Robust Line-of-Sight Coverage Under Stochastic Obstacles
authors: Mohsen Abedi, Alexis A. Dowhuszko, Ahmed Badawy, Mehdi Sookhak, Risto Wichman
abstract: Advancements in high-frequency communication technologies, including millimeter-wave (mmWave), terahertz (THz), and optical wireless bands, play a crucial role in extending wireless connectivity beyond 5 G. These bands provide ultra-wide bandwidths that enable very high data rates, support dense device deployments, and precise positioning. However, their performance critically depends on maintaining clear Line-of-Sight (LoS) conditions, since Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) components suffer from strong attenuation and reflection losses. While mmWave links may provide limited connectivity through NLoS reflections, LoS propagation remains the main factor governing the link budget and reliability of the target applications. In contrast, THz and optical wireless links are almost completely blocked by opaque materials, making LoS assurance essential. This paper tackles the issue of LoS coverage by determining the minimum number and optimal placement of Access Points (APs) required to ensure LoS connectivity in stochastic environments with random obstacles. The environment is modeled as a visibility graph whose nodes represent sub-polygons and edges denote visibility overlaps. Using maximal-clique clustering and maximum-clique packing algorithms, the proposed deterministic framework ensures LoS coverage under all realizations within the modeled stochastic ensemble, achieving up to a 50% reduction in the number of required APs while maintaining over 90% LoS coverage for every realization of obstacle locations.

title: A Caterpillar-Type Miniature Robot for Adaptive Locomotion and Exploration of Tiny Rigid/Soft Pipes_supp4-3697140.mp4
authors: Jinyang Gao
abstract: Caterpillar-type robots are widely used for medium- and large-sized pipe inspections. However, existing prototypes smaller than 80 mm lack both an active variable diameter capability and a contact force sensing function, which are crucial for safe and automatic exploration of unknown rigid/soft pipes (e.g., the colon). This study develops a variable diameter caterpillar-type miniature robot (VCMR) featuring a small size of Φ34.6 mm × 41 mm, a large variable-diameter range of 34.6-89.6 mm, and an integrated contact force sensing function. The VCMR actively adapts to pipe diameter changes using contact force feedback, demonstrates high load capacity in both vertical and horizontal rigid/soft pipes, and traverses a 150-cm colon phantom with sharp bends at an average velocity of 3.07 ± 0.48 cm/s. It holds promise for exploring tiny variable-diameter rigid/soft pipes and delivering cargoes through such pipes.

title: Co-seismic ionospheric disturbances detection with multi-GNSS Case study: Turkey double earthquakes on February 6, 2023
authors: Qiaoli Kong, Yingluo Liu, Yunqing Huang, Lingxin Sun, Qi Bai, Qian Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: On February 6, 2023, at 01:17:34 UTC, a Mw 7.8 earthquake occurred in Turkey, with the epicenter located at 37.15°N, 36.95°E. Approximately 9 hours later, another Mw 7.5 earthquake struck, with the epicenter located at 38.00°N, 37.15°E. In order to thoroughly investigate the ionospheric disturbances caused by the two earthquakes, the Slant Total Electron Content (STEC) was gained utilizing dual-frequency data from more than 300 Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) stations in Turkey and its surrounding areas, and then filtered to more significantly present the disturbances. The results revealed that the significant ionospheric disturbance phenomena caused by both earthquakes were detected by the filtered TEC time series, with the disturbances occurring within 1000 km off the epicenters. The amplitudes of the ionospheric STEC disturbance caused by the second earthquake was approximately five times greater than those of the first earthquake. Ionospheric disturbances induced by acoustic waves were observed in both earthquakes. The acoustic wave velocities for the first and second earthquakes were 0.83 km/s and 1.13 km/s, respectively. Additionally, during the second earthquake, Rayleigh waves with a velocity of 2.33 km/s were detected, which also caused significant ionospheric disturbances. The maximum amplitude of the ionospheric disturbance for the first earthquake was 0.037 TECU, located southwest of the epicenter, while for the second earthquake, it was 0.21 TECU, located northwest of the epicenter. The ionospheric anomalies caused by both earthquakes decreased with increasing distance from the epicenters.

title: A GAN-Based Method for Mountain Shadow Restoration in Optical Remote Sensing Image
authors: Zirui Song, Jun Lu, Yuemin Yue, Xiaowei Tong, Kelin Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Topographic shadows in high-resolution optical re mote sensing cause coupled degradation of texture, contrast, and spectral fidelity, conventional illumination normalization or histogram transfer methods offer limited effectiveness on deep shadow regions, as the missing information cannot be recovered through brightness correction alone. This study proposes a boundary-guided generative adversarial network (GAN) for restoring vegetation shadows in high-resolution multispectral imagery over karst terrain: boundary cues explicitly control shadow transitions, and a selective fusion rule preserves non shadow pixels by construction, training employs a realistic shadow synthesis pipeline that jointly simulates illumination attenuation, chromatic shift, contrast suppression, and texture degradation, combined with complementary losses enforcing texture fidelity, edge consistency, and brightness recovery. Experiments on Gaofen-2 (GF-2) multispectral data show that the proposed method achieves a peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of 35.78 dB and an structural similarity index (SSIM) of 0.96 at 200 epochs, outperforming the U-Net baseline by 13.8% and 4.3% and the ResNet baseline by 28.5% and 12.9%, respectively, notably, it reaches 20.34 dB PSNR at epoch 1 (versus 8.42 dB for U-Net and 9.81 dB for ResNet), demonstrating significantly faster convergence, while both baselines plateau or degrade after epoch 100. These results indicate that generative shadow restoration can effectively extend the usable coverage of high-resolution optical imagery in topographically complex regions.

title: Suite of Ingestible Devices Opens Window to the Gut Nervous System, Microbiome
authors: Leslie Mertz
abstract: Ingestible capsules reveal gut neural signaling and microbiome dynamics through sensing and optogenetics.

title: A Tiny Neuromorphic Processor Enabling On-Chip Spike-Based Learning for Edge Intelligent System
authors: Tengxiao Wang, Haibing Wang, Zhengqing Zhong, Ying Jiang, Yi Chen, Liyuan Liu, Cong Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Neuromorphic processors deploying spiking neural networks (SNNs) hold great potential for edge intelligence applications. However, achieving high accuracy, high throughput, and highly energy-efficient on-chip learning under limited energy and resource budgets remains a critical challenge. To address this, we propose a tiny neuromorphic architecture to support a fully spike-based backpropagation learning algorithm tailored for a lightweight two-layer SNN aiming at edge scenarios. To enhance processing efficiency, this architecture tightly couples control and data flows, and features fully spike-based inference and learning. Furthermore, we propose a structured weight mapping scheme that satisfies different parallel memory access patterns during forward and backward passes, and introduce a group-based event-driven paradigm to exploit spike sparsity. A prototype was implemented on a very-low-cost Xilinx Zynq-7010 FPGA device. It achieved high on-chip learning accuracies comparable to state-of-the-art works across multiple benchmark datasets, while delivering a throughput of 2269/9082 frames/s and an energy efficiency of 56/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$14~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>J/image for learning/inferring MNIST images, which outperforms existing approaches and shows its potential for wide deployments in extreme edge intelligence scenarios.

title: Convivial Robotics and Silicopathy: Toward a Cross-Cultural Ethics of Human-Robot Coexistence
authors: Minoru Asada, Michio Okada
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Recent discussions on the Ethical, Legal, and Social Issues (ELSI) of social robots highlight the importance of understanding the ethical nature of human-robot relationships beyond issues of safety and regulation. Drawing on the philosophy of technology-from Ivan Illich's concept of convivial tools to contemporary relational approaches-this paper proposes a conceptual framework for human-robot coexistence based on two complementary paradigms: convivial robotics and silicopathy. Convivial robotics, inspired by Illich's notion of conviviality and exemplified by the “weak robot” approach, emphasizes robots that enhance human capabilities through imperfect yet mutually supportive interaction. Silicopathy, by contrast, refers to the emergence of artificial empathy grounded in representations of pain and affective states, through which ethical behavior is generated from internally constructed value dynamics, enabling morally meaningful relationships between humans and robots. Together these paradigms form a dual ethical framework that integrates human capability enhancement with empathic moral relations. The framework is further discussed from a cross-cultural perspective, contrasting Western autonomy-centered ethics with Eastern relational philosophies emphasizing harmony and compassion, thereby offering a conceptual basis for future human-robot coexistence, including its governance across diverse cultural and societal contexts.

title: Impact of Renewable Integration on Day-Ahead and Real-Time Market Prices under One- and Two-Price Imbalance Settlements
authors: Gergo Varhegyi, Mutasim Nour
abstract: This paper investigates the influence of renewable energy integration on the pricing dynamics of day-ahead and real-time markets under one- and two-price imbalance settlement methods. As the penetration of renewable energy sources increases, the accurate allocation of revenue and payments becomes critical to ensure market efficiency and participant equity. One-price settlements ensure that all imbalances are settled at a single price, typically the system marginal price, leading to revenue and payment neutrality. In contrast, two-price settlements differentiate prices based on whether the imbalance decreases or increases the overall system balance, often resulting in different financial outcomes for participants, as well as profit for the market operator. This study, powered by PLEXOS, provides a comprehensive methodology to accurately model one- and two-price imbalance settlements. The methodology combines in-software adjustments with Python-based automation to enhance data handling and reporting accuracy, enabling precise reflection of the financial impacts of renewable energy on market prices and settlements. Through a detailed test network model, the study analyses the discrepancies in market prices and allocations caused by the variability of renewable energy. The findings highlight the significant impact of renewable energy on both the day-ahead and real-time market prices, providing actionable insights for market operators and participants to optimise financial outcomes and maintain market stability. This work contributes to the broader goal of integrating renewable energy sources into electricity markets, offering practical solutions to manage price volatility and ensuring transparent and equitable market operations.

title: Perception and Acceptance of Quadruped Robots Based on Indirect Exposure
authors: Veronika Egger, Layali Mashkaf, Günter Josef Hofer, Dea Hasanaj, Mathias Brandstötter
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Creating social robots is a multidisciplinary challenge that requires understanding human perception, behavior interpretation, and social–ethical expectations in human–robot interaction. Animal-like robots are a key category in this domain, yet quantitative evidence on user acceptance and perceived safety, especially for quadruped robots, remains limited. We therefore conducted a multilingual survey on the perception and acceptance of quadruped robots with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{4 5 4}$</tex> participants. Importantly, participants formed their judgments from indirect exposure to standardized visual stimuli (images and short videos) rather than from direct physical interaction. The results suggest that reported comfort under indirect exposure is associated with robot appearance and the coherence of design cues across animal-like and machine-like constructions, with mismatched zoomorphic cues tending to be viewed less favorably. Concerns about job loss and accidents are negatively associated with perceived safety, while concerns about unfair decisions and privacy show no significant influence. Prior experience with robotics is associated with higher acceptance and reduced anxiety. Appearance shows up to support initial sense-making, but did not correspond to uniformly high reported trust; responsibility attribution in the event of malfunction also remained contested, with responses clustering into technology-centered, human-centered, and shared-responsibility positions. Overall, the findings provide exploratory quantitative evidence on mediated first impressions of quadruped robots and motivate follow-up studies using direct human–robot interaction to test, validate, and extend these observations.

title: Privacy-Preserving and Coercion-Resistant E-Voting via Unlinkable Biometric Credentials on a Hybrid Permissioned Blockchain
authors: Kumar Shubhranshu, Harsh Kumar Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The research presents a secure voting system which enables verification of all electoral processes through its complete verification mechanisms at the ballot box using fingerprint authentication and permissioned blockchain technology to issue anonymous credentials. The proposed system enables local eligibility authentication through its biometric data protection which does not require biometric template storage in its on-chain system. Voters use the blind signature system to obtain credentials which allow them to send encrypted votes with zero-knowledge Aproofs that verify their ballot’s authenticity while keeping their voting choice hidden. The system records votes onto a Byzantine fault-tolerant ledger which uses threshold decryption to calculate final results. The system establishes auditability through its Merkle root system which periodically registers ballot Merkle roots on the public chain. The system maintains voter privacy through its revoting feature which enables voters to cast new votes while the system handles large election events. The experimental tests performed on a Hyperledger Besu system with ten nodes showed that the system maintained a throughput rate above 1600 TPS while it achieved confirmation times below one second during the controlled testing input conditions.

title: Lightweight LLM Adaptation for Intrusion Detection via Token-Efficient Flow Representation
authors: Akram Laamari, Hajar Moudoud, Zakaria Abou El Houda
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are emerging as a promising approach for intrusion detection using structured network flow data. However, their practical deployment is constrained by context window limitations and the excessive token overhead introduced by conventional tabular serialization formats such as JSON. Verbose data representations inflate sequence lengths, often exceeding model input limits and causing feature truncation. Additionally, it remains unclear which LLM architecture is more suitable for structured intrusion detection tasks under limited training resources. To tackle this issue, we propose a novel representation-aware intrusion detection framework based on Token-Oriented Object Notation (TOON), a compact serialization format that maximizes token efficiency while preserving schema structure. Also, we integrate a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) scheme to enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Finally, we evaluate the proposed framework as an encoder–decoder model (T5-Small) with a decoder-only model (Qwen2.5) on three benchmark datasets, including NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, and CIC-IDS2018 for binary and multi-class classification scenarios. The results show that our tokenizer-aligned, representation-aware preprocessing combined with lightweight encoder–decoder adaptation provides a practical and resource-efficient foundation for LLM-based intrusion detection.

title: Interpretable Topic Modeling with Online Dirichlet Compound Negative Multinomial Mixture Model and Feature Saliency
authors: Ornela Bregu, Nizar Bouguila
abstract: The Dirichlet Compound Negative Multinomial (DCNM) distribution is a flexible probabilistic model used to model overdispersion and burstiness in multivariate count data. In this paper, we adopt a rising-factorial parametrization of the DCNM and develop a Minorization–Maximization (MM) algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation in a DCNM mixture model with feature saliency (DCNM-FS). The novel MM scheme yields closed-form updates for both mixture parameters and feature-saliency weights, improves numerical stability, reduces dependence on initialization, and avoids repeated evaluation of expensive derivatives and Hessians. To simultaneously select the number of components and the salient features, we use a convex combination of the Minimum Message Length (MML) and the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). The weighted criterion yields a more reliable model-selection signal for DCNM-FS mixtures by exploiting MML’s information-theoretic foundation while grounding it with BIC’s stronger sparsity-inducing tendency, resulting in more stable estimates of both the number of components and the salient feature set. Moreover, we introduce an online extension of DCNM-FS in which parameters and feature-saliency weights are updated incrementally from mini-batches, enabling streaming topic discovery without retraining from scratch. We embed the offline and online DCNM-FS mixtures in a modular topic-modeling pipeline, where the learned component parameters, in particular the feature saliencies, directly define interpretable topic keywords, reducing reliance on large language models for document representation. The proposed approach is validated on standard topic-detection benchmarks, where it demonstrates improved robustness, interpretability, and efficiency compared to existing clustering and topic-modeling baselines.

title: AI-based Tools Test Cases Generation and Software Correctness: A Critical Review
authors: Sara Abu Dabaa’t, MohammadNoor Injadat, Ahmad Samhan
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is now increasingly transforming software quality assurance, and test case generation is among its most notable use cases. Here, this study provides a review of AI-based techniques and tools facilitating automated test case generation, evidencing their effectiveness in verifying software correctness. From over fifty of the latest academic and industrial contributions published from 2020 to 2025, machine learning models, natural language processing approaches, symbolic execution techniques, and generative AI models have been reviewed. The review identifies key accomplishments of AI approaches, including improvements in test coverage of up to 60%, correction of up to 30% of post-deployment defects, and significant testing cycle reduction. However, challenges persist in aspects of data quality, model interpretability, deployment in continuous development pipelines, and the balancing of automation and manual intervention. Due to these gaps, the study introduces a comparative overview of testing strategies with a focus on AI and reveal the demand for empirical validation, standardized tests, and hybrid solutions that combine AI automation and expert knowledge. The findings demonstrate the revolutionary effect of AI on current software testing and reveal important milestones to be attained for its fruitful use in research and practice.

title: HeuRiskUNet: UAV Navigation in Risk Maps through Improved Path Planning Heuristics
authors: E. Aldao, F. Veiga-López, C. P. C. Chanel, Y. Watanabe, H. González-Jorge
abstract: In recent years, UAVs have been increasingly deployed across various applications, including environmental monitoring, infrastructure inspection, and logistics. While many of these operations are still conducted by human remote pilots, the current trend is towards the development of fully autonomous solutions, which require efficient and robust control and path-planning systems. Traditionally, the most widely used path-planning algorithm is A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> due to its deterministic behaviour and computational efficiency. This approach discretises the navigation environment into a grid with adjacent-node connectivity, thus constructing a graph, and minimises the path length through a graph search guided by heuristic functions. Nevertheless, the applicability of this approach is often limited to shortest-path problems where admissible heuristic functions are available. In other optimisation scenarios, such as when accounting for factors like operational risk, identifying heuristics that are both admissible and capable of efficiently guiding the search can be highly challenging.
