Another drawback of A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> approaches lies in their discretisation, which restricts UAV movement to adjacent nodes. This limitation reduces vehicle manoeuvrability and compromises both path smoothness and optimality. To address these challenges, this paper presents a system based on machine-learning based heuristics for navigation within complex risk maps. The system is integrated into a customised A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> framework that employs a k-Non-Aligned Nearest Neighbours (k-NANN) approach to compute smooth and optimal paths while maintaining low computational cost, deterministic behaviour, and interpretability.

title: Relay Protection Functioning Under Integration of Distributed Generation: Challenges for Ukraine
authors: Mykola P. Bolotnyi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The integration of small-scale distributed generation into power system requires an impact assessment on stability and reliability of adjacent electric network. The characteristics research of electric power systems modes with small-scale distributed generation was carried out. The features of electrical modes that have the greatest influence on relay protection functioning was revealed in paper. Approaches to management of small-scale distributed generation under emergency are proposed. The relay protection requirements for electric power systems with smallscale distributed generation have been formed for management decentralization in power system of Ukraine.

title: Energy Efficient Control of Electric Heaters in Biogas Reactors
authors: Mykhailo Spodoba, Oleksandr Spodoba
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article examines the influence of the positional and linear laws of change of the power applied at the beginning of operation to the electric heating device on the consumption of electrical energy for heating the substrate in a biogas reactor. The paper presents the results of numerical studies using differential equations, which were carried out in the Wolfram Mathematica software package. The paper shows graphical dependences of changes in the temperature of the substrate in the biogas reactor using two laws of control of the heating device placed directly in the volume of the substrate. The change in temperature of the substrate in the first 100 seconds of operation of the electric heating device is shown graphically. The result of the numerical study is the determination of the influence of the control laws on the consumption of energy for heating and the establishment that, using the positional and linear laws of power change, the time of heating the substrate in a metal biogas reactor with a volume of 40 liters is the same, while the amount of energy spent on heating is different. Using the linear law of power change, the energy consumption for one substrate heating cycle in the biogas reactor is 4.3 kJ less. The significance of the conducted research is that the use of the linear law of change of the applied power at the beginning of work to the heating device (cable) leads to a positive effect, which consists in reducing the energy costs for one cycle of heating the substance in the biogas reactor, while increasing its profitability.

title: From Episodic Screening to Continuous Insight: AI Architectures for Colorectal Care
authors: Tejas Padliya
abstract: Integrating wearables and clinical data to reshape colorectal cancer (CRC) prevention.Colorectal cancer (CRC) pathways are still dominated by episodic screening through colonoscopy and stool-based tests, despite growing access to consumer sensors and mobile computing. This article presents an end-to-end AI architecture for multimodal colorectal risk stratification that combines traditional screening data with passively collected activity patterns, heart rate variability, stool frequency logs, and nutrition context. We describe a layered design consisting of data ingestion, feature engineering, temporal modeling, risk scoring, and clinician-facing decision support. Implementation patterns are illustrated using cloud-native and edge components suitable for deployment in both high-resource and resource-constrained health systems. The article discusses issues such as bias, data sparsity, longitudinal drift, and integration with existing screening guidelines. As a result, it provides a reference model that future clinical and engineering teams can adapt when building continuous, AI-assisted colorectal screening and monitoring tools.

title: Intelligent Safe Optimal Control Towards Koopman Operator-Driven Nonlinear Systems with Asymmetric State and Input Constraints
authors: Yalu Su, Ding Wang, Mingming Zhao, Dan Xiong, Yiyong Huang, Wei Han
abstract: For unknown nonlinear systems subject to asymmetric state and input constraints simultaneously, this article establishes a safe value iteration paradigm to learn an optimal control policy in a data-based manner. Initially, the Koopman operator, instead of the black-box neural network, is applied to extract the inherent dynamics of the controlled systems from the measured data, thereby allowing for explicit analysis of the prediction error. To tackle the issue posed by state and input constraints, a crafted control barrier function is seamlessly incorporated into the canonical utility function, which retains the property of positive definiteness for the asymmetric case. Moreover, the value iteration algorithm with regard to the augmented utility function is adopted to attain a safe optimal controller, where the actor and critic networks are leveraged to approximate the control input and associated value function, respectively. The mono-tonicity, safety, and stability of the raised algorithm are further verified rigorously. Via performing three experiments on the linear system, the nonlinear system, and the manipulator plant, comparative results are obtained to substantiate the superiority and efficacy of the developed approach in achieving optimal performance and safe guarantee.

title: Modern Trends in the Use of Hydrogen as a Motor Fuel: Analysis, Recent Advancementsand Prospects
authors: Sergii Boichenko, Iryna Shkilniuk
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The global shift towards sustainable and environmentally friendly energy sources has necessitated the exploration of alternative fuels and energy carriers. Hydrogen, with its inexhaustible reserves and minimal environmental impact, stands out as a promising contender in the transportation sector. This article provides an informational and analytical review of the current state of hydrogen technologies use as motor fuel.

title: HMSA-Net: Hierarchical Modality-Speaker Adaptive Network for Audio-Visual Speaker Diarization
authors: Hang Chen, Mao-Kui He, Jun Du, Sabato Marco Siniscalchi, Li-Juan Liu, Gen-Shun Wan, Chin-Hui Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Traditional audio-visual speaker diarization (AVSD) approaches exhibit limited robustness to cross-modal heterogeneity and complex inter-speaker interactions, particularly under dynamic, unconstrained real-world conditions. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel neural architecture, termed the hierarchical modality–speaker adaptive network (HMSA-Net), which integrates two sequentially structured modules and an ad-hoc multi-stage training strategy. The first consistency-gated inter-modal attention (CGIMA) module dynamically estimates cross-modal synchrony between audio and visual embeddings and adaptively regulates their mutual influence during feature fusion, thereby mitigating modality mismatch. The second dense inter-speaker attention (DISA) module explicitly captures complex inter-speaker relationships by applying multi-head attention from a target speaker representation to a densely aggregated bank of non-target speaker embeddings, enabling fine-grained discrimination in overlapping speech conditions without enforcing a fixed upper bound on the number of speakers. To further enhance optimization stability, a multi-stage optimization (MSO) scheme is introduced, which consistently achieves lower convergence loss than end-to-end training. Extensive evaluations on standard AVSD benchmarks demonstrate that CGIMA effectively suppresses modality-specific noise while amplifying complementary cross-modal cues, resulting in more robust fused representations. Meanwhile, DISA improves frame-level speaker discrimination by modeling dense cross-speaker dependencies. As a result, HMSA-Net trained with MSO achieves state-of-the-art performance on the AMI, MISP2022, and AVA-AVD.

title: Numerical Modeling of Tidal Current Patterns for Anchorage Channel Planning at Amborawang Laut Port
authors: Arif Rendi Prasetya, Luh Putri Adnyani, Nurmawati, Ahmad Azwar Mas'ud M
publish: 2025 IEEE Ocean Engineering Technology and Innovation Conference: Management and Conservation for Sustainable and Resilient Marine and Coastal Resources (OETIC)
abstract: Amborawang Laut Port, located in the Makassar Strait, is under development and currently lacks a designated anchorage channel. This study investigates tidal current patterns and bathymetric conditions to inform safe and efficient port infrastructure planning. Numerical modeling using MIKE 21 simulated tidal currents over a one-year period, complemented by bathymetric analysis from BATNAS data processed in Surfer. Field measurements validated tidal data, yielding an RMSE of 0.08293 m (8%), indicating high model accuracy. The tidal regime is mixed, predominantly semi-diurnal. Bathymetric analysis reveals a gently sloping seabed (slope <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 0.2 \%$</tex>) with depths ranging from 0 to −50 m, providing adequate under-keel clearance for tugboats and barges. Simulated tidal currents are low, from near zero during neap tides to 0.096 m/s during spring tides, well below the PIANC safety threshold of 0.5 m/s. Based on these findings, an anchorage channel with a minimum depth of 5.03 m and width of 49.37 m was designed. The port basin layout includes a landing basin (110.73 × 41.15 m), maneuvering area (248.03 × 413.38 m), and turning basin (diameter 310.04 m), covering <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 10$</tex> hectares. These results provide a quantitative basis for hydrodynamically safe, efficient, and sustainable port development at Amborawang Laut.

title: Towards Automated Discovery of Asymmetric Mempool DoS in Blockchains
authors: Yibo Wang, Yuzhe Tang, Kai Li, Wanning Ding
abstract: In blockchains, mempool controls transaction flow before consensus, denial of whose service hurts the health and security of blockchain networks. This paper presents MPFUZZ, the first mempool fuzzer to find asymmetric DoS bugs by exploring the space of symbolized mempool states and optimistically estimating the promisingness of an intermediate state in reaching bug oracles. Compared to the baseline blockchain fuzzers, MPFUZZ achieves a > 100× speedup in finding known DETER exploits. Running MPFUZZ on major Ethereum clients leads to discovering new mempool vulnerabilities, which exhibit a wide variety of sophisticated patterns, including stealthy mempool eviction and mempool locking. Rule-based mitigation schemes are proposed against all newly discovered vulnerabilities.

title: Improving the cyber security of IEC 61850 based transmission line protection (128)
authors: A. P. Apostolov
abstract: The paper first looks at some options available for communications between the protection IEDs at the ends of the protected line. It then analyzes the GOOSE communication messages being exchanged between the protection devices and the possible cyber-attacks on the protection scheme. This analysis is based on the understanding of the peer-to-peer communications mechanism and the structure of the messages. The next part of the paper looks at the effect of the attacker’s skills on the impact of the attack on the operation of the protection scheme, followed by the description of the functionality of specific communications -based protection schemes from the point of view of their vulnerability to cyber-attacks and identifies methods and tools to detect cyber security intrusions. The last sections of the paper focus on cyber security issues related to the testing of transmission line protection schemes. Testing of solutions based on "functional security" methodology are discussed.

title: Disaster Response Gross Motor, Fine Motor and Tactile Workload Prediction Using Multi-layer Perceptrons
authors: Mark-Robin Giolando, Julie A. Adams
abstract: Human-robot teams executing disaster response missions require human workload predictions to enable future robots to preemptively adapt to adverse workload conditions. Workload estimation algorithms have demonstrated the ability to estimate the current human workload based on physiological response metrics; however, existing prediction methods for future workload values have only made mid-mission workload predictions up to 30 seconds, and only for cognitive workload. Three physical workload component (i.e., gross motor, fine motor and tactile) prediction models are presented in this work, making predictions up to 420 seconds. Pretrained workload estimation models converted physiological response signals into historical and current workload inputs to act as inputs for the forecasting models. Multi-layer perceptron models were trained to forecast gross motor, fine motor and tactile workload, with accurate predictions made for three minutes for the gross motor, and seven minutes for the fine motor and tactile. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of multi-minute workload predictions for human-robot teams executing physical tasks.

title: Intelligent Spectrum Sensing and Access Using Deep Learning for Cognitive Communication Systems
authors: Mridul Gupta, Nookala Venu, Karthick Cherladine, Tailor Nikunjkumar Champakbhai, Deepak Upadhyay, Aakash Singh Ranswal
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Wireless communications are expanding rapidly; as a result, at times, the radio frequency spectrum will be overcrowded (some bands used heavily) while other bands remain underutilized. An investigation into an intelligent spectrum sensing and access framework utilizing deep learning for cognitive communication systems has been conducted. The proposed system utilizes a convolutional neural network to identify whether a given spectrum band is occupied or vacant under various levels of interference. The CNN was trained and tested on synthetic wireless signal data generated within a simulated environment. Results from this research demonstrated the use of the proposed CNN resulted in higher detection probabilities and lower false alarm rates than those produced by energy detection methods. When utilized in conjunction with intelligent decision making processes related to access, the CNN also improved spectrum usage efficiency. Although the signals used to generate the training data were simulated, results show that deep learning can enhance cognitive communication systems efficiency.

title: Fuzzy Logic Optimization of Electrode Geometry in a Microfluidic Device for Malaria Cell Separation
authors: Jenil Patel, Rushvi Patel, Mahesh Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Current work demonstrates numerical investigation of a microfluidic cell sorting device for separation of malaria infected cells from healthy red blood cells using dielectrophoresis (DEP) phenomenon. In present study, a microfluidic device microchannel was designed using finite element method-based simulations to examine the impact of electrode geometry concerning its height and width on cell separation trajectories. The cell separation performance was evaluated based on four performance parameters which include red blood cell purity, malaria cell purity, red blood cell yield, and malaria cell yield and used to model a fuzzy inference system model implemented in MATLAB to determine suitable geometrical dimension of electrode geometry for desired performance parameters. The results have shown that there is a direct impact of electrode dimension variation on electric field distribution and further on dielectrophoretic force acting on the cells. The optimized geometry can provide high purity and yield of malaria –infected cells. Results have shown that ~100% blood cell purity and ~100% malaria cell yield has been achieved at 3V and 100 kHz with effectively focused cells and aligned cell trajectories before entering in DEP cell separation effects.

title: Automated Detection of Hip Replacements and Fiducial Markers in Pelvic CT Scans: A Comparative Study of Rule-Based vs Deep Learning Approaches
authors: Sambit Bhattacharya, Aniruddh Karunaker, Brian Anderson, Daxell Wells, Yuan Gao, Lawrence Marks, Shiva Das, Arcot Rajasekar, Lukasz Mazur
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive comparative study of automated detection of hip replacements and fiducial markers in pelvic computed tomography (CT) scans, evaluating rule-based anatomically-constrained methods against modern deep learning techniques. We develop and independently evaluate two distinct approaches: (1) a novel rule-based system utilizing metallic implant detection, fiducial marker identification, and anatomical constraint analysis specifically designed for hip replacement detection, and (2) a convolutional neural network (CNN) enhanced with Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for hip prosthesis and fiducial detection. Our evaluation on a comprehensive dataset of 444 pelvic CT cases demonstrates that the rule-based approach achieves superior performance (sensitivity: 90.4%, specificity: 88.2%, accuracy: 89.0%) while providing interpretability and clinical reasoning. The CNN achieves 70.8% ± 5.4% accuracy with GradCAM visualizations enabling spatial understanding of predictions for hip prosthesis and fiducial detection. This comparative analysis reveals that specialized rule-based methods can significantly outperform deep learning approaches while offering the interpretability essential for clinical adoption in medical imaging applications focused on data curation and quality control.

title: Mathematical Modeling of Negative Environmental Consequences Due to Thermal Radiation
authors: Yurii Skob, Oksana Pichugina, Mykola Kalinichenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The relevance of this work stems from the imperative to address the contemporary scientific a nd practical challenge of minimizing the risks associated with potential disruptions to the safe operation of man-made objects. This entails a comprehensive analysis of gas mixture movement in the emission zone of gas impurities, predicting air conditions in man-made environments under the influence of temperature disturbances, and the exploration of effective designs for protective equipment to mitigate or eliminate the negative consequences of such impacts. This research aims to systematically enhance and improve mathematical models, methodologies, and software for the computer implementation of a decision support system model. The model is intended to analyze the movement of a multicomponent gas mixture within the vicinity of manmade objects under the influence of t hermal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r$</tex> adiation. The theoretical foundation of the work is rooted in mathematical modeling methods, computational techniques in mathematical physics, probit analysis, risk assessment theories, and decisionmaking principles. The software tool developed for the computer implementation of the system model adheres to the principles of the theory of computer modeling systems. Based on the modeling results, distributions of the conditional probability of a fatal injury to a person due to exposure to thermal radiation from a high-temperature gas jet at specific control points a re derived for two design scenarios: one without protection for the personnel workplace and another with a continuous protective barrier impervious to radiation. The observed distribution patterns emphasize the crucial consideration of barriers impervious to heat flows when assessing safety at man-made facilities releasing high-temperature gases into the atmosphere.

title: Design and Simulation of a Battery Electric Vehicle Model for Underground Mining Applications with Synthetic Data Generation for Machine Learning Model
authors: Tamsa, Arpit Garg, Wen L. Soong, S. Ali Pourmousavi
abstract: The shift to electrifying underground mining operations poses considerable challenges in accurately predicting energy consumption due to the intricate nature of operations and the scarcity of real-world performance data for mining electric trucks. This paper introduces a comprehensive dataset synthesis framework as well as the application of four machine learning techniques aimed at forecasting energy usage in electric mining trucks used underground. This addresses the essential need for data-driven optimisation tools in sustainable mining practices. Four machine learning models, including decision tree, random forest, extreme gradient boosting, and deep neural network, were thoroughly assessed using the synthetic dataset comprising 50,000 operational scenarios created through physics-based modelling involving 11 operational parameters. Extensive hyperparameter tuning was conducted using 5-fold cross-validation with a focus on minimising mean absolute error. The Deep Neural Network exhibited outstanding predictive performance (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9998, RMSE = 1.02 kWh, MAE = 0.60 kWh), showcasing strong generalisation across various operational scenarios. Model validation against physics-based calculations for a typical haul profile confirmed prediction accuracy. This framework provides mining operators with reliable tools to manage energy-efficient fleets, optimise routes, and plan operations.

title: LLM-Augmented Feature Engineering for Machine Learning Pipelines
authors: A. Rancea, I. Anghel, T. Cioara
abstract: Feature engineering represents a fundamental challenge in machine learning (ML) pipelines for extracting meaningful representations that capture underlying patterns, starting from raw data. Usually the process requires manual, time consuming, deep domain expertise, and is difficult to be scaled across multiple domains. Automatic methods attempt to address these challenges by generating predefined statistical features or selecting optimal subsets from a feature set, rather than creating new ones. In this paper, we propose a Large Language Model (LLM)-based automated feature engineering pipeline using Mistral-7B-Instruct with Retrieval-Augmented Generation and a multi-criteria validation process. It starts with prompt text and additionally integrates domain and data knowledge, to create and validate new features. We evaluate the solution in the context of an ML-based heart rate prediction process and show that the generated features achieve 9-48% MSE improvements across three different time series architectures (LSTM, Transformer, and Informer). The results demonstrate that LLM-based feature generation can significantly optimize and improve ML prediction tasks.

title: The Impact of Quality Control, Curated Gene Sets, and Statistical Frameworks on Alzheimer’s Disease Gene Expression Analysis in Single-Cell Data
authors: Natasha Petrovska, Maria Larrondo-Petrie, Mirjana Pavlovic
abstract: Single-cell transcriptomics enables detailed investigation of Alzheimer’s disease (AD)–associated gene expression, yet findings are highly sensitive to preprocessing, gene selection, and statistical methods. Motivated by the need for reproducible detection of sex- and age-specific transcriptional patterns, we systematically evaluated the impact of quality control (QC), curated gene sets, and non-parametric analyses on human and mouse datasets. Our objective was to determine how these methodological choices influence gene-level variance, differential expression, and cross-species reproducibility. QC improved data integrity by removing low-quality cells and outliers, enhancing variance and interpretability. Comparison of three curated gene sets—GeneCards, Seligman, and Driessens—revealed that the GeneCards subset captured a balanced spectrum of AD-related variability. Sex-stratified Wilcoxon analyses identified female-biased upregulation of UCHL1, while LITAF and OPCML displayed opposite sex-biased modulation in mice versus humans. Age-dependent Kruskal–Wallis tests revealed multiple conserved genes (SQSTM1, APOE, ITM2B, MAPT, UCHL1, MT-ND1, PRNP, FUS, VCP, and BLMH) with significant changes across age groups. This study demonstrates robust sex-and age-specific signatures and highlights how QC, gene-set selection, and statistical frameworks jointly influence reproducible single-cell AD analyses.

title:
Dynamic Event-Triggered MPC for Nonlinear Systems: Application to Vehicle Active Suspensions
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Wenting He, Daiwen Yao, Michael Shi, Xin Yu, Ning Zhao, Huiyan Zhang, Levente Kovács
abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) faces the challenge of heavy online computational burden in vehicle active suspension systems. To alleviate this issue, this paper proposes a Dynamic Event-Triggered MPC (DET-MPC) method considering nonlinear actuator constraints. By introducing an internal dynamic variable to adaptively adjust the triggering threshold, this method updates the control law only when significant changes occur in the system states, thereby substantially reducing the number of optimization computations. Theoretical analysis demonstrates the uniform asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system and excludes Zeno behavior. Simulation results show that, compared with time-triggered and static event-triggered strategies, the proposed method reduces the controller update frequency by up to 82.9% and significantly lowers the CPU execution time, while maintaining comparable performance in body acceleration and suspension travel control, significantly alleviating the computational and communication burden.

title: Resilient Distributed Optimal Power Flow Algorithm Against False Data Injection Attacks
authors: Mohannad Alkhraijah
abstract: Distributed algorithms allow multiple agents to collaboratively solve large-scale optimization problems by solving a sequence of smaller subproblems. Solving optimization problems using distributed algorithms requires repeated data communication. The communication dependency increases the vulnerability of distributed algorithms to communication noise and cyberattacks. This paper presents a machine learning-based method to enhance the resiliency of distributed optimization algorithms solving optimal power flow (OPF) problems to false data injection attacks. We propose a variation to the Auxiliary Problem Principle (APP) algorithm that is secure against false data injection attacks. The proposed variation uses a classification algorithm to validate the received data from the neighboring agents. We train the model on data generated from running multiple simulations of the APP algorithm. We benchmark the results with a naive model that eliminates large deviations from previous iterations. The results show that the proposed algorithm in this paper enhances the resiliency of the APP algorithm to error injection cyberattacks and outperforms the naive model in terms of convergence speed and accuracy.

title: Landfill Site Allocation Multi-Objective Optimization with NSGA-II: A Case Study on Fogo Island, Cape Verde
authors: Julio Leite Azancort Neto, Evelin Helena Cardoso Gomes, Glenda Camila De Melo Freitas, Romário Da Costa Silva, Jasmine Priscyla Leite De Araújo, Carlos Renato Lisboa Francês
abstract: Effective landfill site selection is essential for minimizing environmental impact and construction costs while mitigating public health risks, particularly in Small Island Developing States (SIDS) where geographical and financial constraints are severe. Traditional spatial analysis methods aggregate conflicting objectives into single suitability scores, masking crucial trade-offs and neglecting operational resilience. This paper introduces a rigorously validated multi-phase framework integrating Geographic Information Systems (GIS) with the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to address the Facility Location Problem on Fogo Island, Cape Verde. The model balances three conflicting objectives, namely implementation cost, environmental impact, and Social Safety, across 473 candidate sites. A minimum pairwise distance constraint (10 km) enforces geographic dispersion, reducing the feasible solution space from 17.5 million to 2.90 million valid configurations while supporting geographic resilience against localized natural hazard scenarios. Validated against a complete deterministic Exhaustive Search, NSGA-II captured 99.50% of the true Pareto front hypervolume with an optimality gap of 0.50% in the best run (mean gap: 2.45% across 30 independent runs) and a 4.0-fold reduction in computational time. The Best Compromise Solution forms a triangular network with an average inter-facility spread of 13.41 km, covering two of the island’s three municipalities and achieving a 50.3% improvement in geographic distribution at a trade-off ratio of 8.52:1 between spread gain and hypervolume reduction. This work transitions waste infrastructure planning from static suitability mapping to dynamic, validated, and resilient systems engineering.

title: Synthesize, Adapt, Steal: A Few-Shot Domain Adaptive Model Stealing Attack for Tabular Data
authors: Maxime Schwarzer, Gustavo Sánchez, Thies Möhlenhof, Laurin Holz, Johannes F. Loevenich, Roberto Rigolin F. Lopes, Veit Hagenmeyer
abstract: The growing popularity of Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) is creating new attack vectors. Model theft poses a particular threat because it enables Intellectual Property (IP) to be stolen and can serve as a starting point for further attacks. While attacks on computer vision (CV) are well-studied, tabular data crucial for industrial applications remains less explored. We present a few-shot, domain-adaptive, model-stealing attack optimized specifically for tabular data. Our approach combines three components: (1) generating a synthetic yet plausible data pool using a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CTGAN); (2) a hybrid query strategy combining active learning with adversarial augmentation to efficiently exploit the query budget; and (3) a novel Relational Attention Corrector (RAC) that implicitly learns and enforces semantic validity. Experiments on an energy critical infrastructure intrusion detection dataset (specifically, the ERENO IEC 61850 dataset) and standard benchmarks (Adult Income and Diabetes) demonstrate that our attack significantly outperforms previous methods. It improves the macro F1-score by over 24 percentage points with only 7,000 queries, closely approaching the performance of an attacker with full data knowledge.

title: SAFE-GUARD: Audio Emotion Recognition System for Real-Time Public Place Abuse Detection
authors: Kanan, Rashmi Chawla, Giancarlo Fortino
abstract: Ensuring safety in privacy-sensitive environments requires unobtrusive monitoring methods that respect user dignity while remaining effective. Audio-based approaches provide a promising alternative to video surveillance but face challenges of robustness, generalization, and interpretability. To address these, we propose a multimodal Voice Distress Detection System for real-time safety monitoring. The system combines acoustic descriptors extracted with TorchAudio, speech transcripts generated by Whisper, and schema-constrained inference through a fine-tuned DialoGPT-medium model. Outputs are structured to include distress level, confidence, reasoning, and recommended action, ensuring interpretability in safety-critical contexts. Evaluation on four benchmark datasets (RAVDESS, CREMA-D, TESS, IEMOCAP) demonstrates strong performance, achieving 90.65% accuracy and 98.72% recall on distress cases. Compared to a logistic regression baseline trained on acoustic features alone, our multimodal fusion approach significantly improves sensitivity. These results highlight the potential of our framework as a privacy-preserving solution for real-time abuse detection in sensitive spaces.

title: Ensemble learning methods as a tool for more robust prediction of mobile payment usage
authors: Mariia Godz, Francisco Liébana-Cabanillas, Juan Lara-Rubio, David A. Pelta
abstract: The rapid digitalization of financial services has transformed mobile peer-to-peer (P2P) payments, exemplified by Bizum in Spain, into a critical area for understanding and shaping consumer behavior. This paper examines the application of machine learning (ML) techniques for predicting individual adoption of P2P mobile payments, with a particular focus on the practical utility of predictions for FinTech companies and financial institutions, rather than solely on forecasting performance. It evaluates a range of individual classifiers alongside two ensemble learning methods - Hard Voting and a Weighted Average method - those aggregates predictions according to the strengths of each individual model. The results show that the ensemble learning methods not only improve forecast reliability but also bring practical operational value to FinTech stakeholders.

title: Generative AI-driven Group Emotion Recognition: A Comparative Analysis of Holistic and Compositional Strategies
authors: Pasquale Molinaro, Mattia Presta, Giancarlo Fortino
abstract: Group Emotion Recognition (GER) represents a significant challenge in computer vision, as traditional Convolutional Neural Networks often fail to capture the semantic context required to interpret collective affective states. This paper investigates the potential of Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) applied to GER in a zero-shot setting, eliminating the need for task-specific training. We propose a comparative study between two distinct methodological pipelines: a Compositional approach, which aggregates individual facial expressions detected in the scene, and a Holistic approach, which leverages the VLM’s capability to interpret global scene context and interactions. Six state-of-the-art open-source VLMs are evaluated (LLaVA-1.6, GLM-4.1V, MiniCPM-V-4.5, InternVL3, Moondream2 and PaliGemma2) on a curated dataset derived from MELD. To overcome the limitations of standard binary classification, performance is assessed using a proposed semantic metric based on Russell’s Circumplex Model, measuring the coherence of predictions, assessing a more nuanced, sophisticated and precise evaluation. Experimental results demonstrate the distinct superiority of the Holistic approach (Subset Accuracy 86% vs. 62%). Moreover the study leverages a new methodology for GER in real-time, with a latency time near to the zero (Paligemma model inference time in ≈ 0.16 seconds)

title: A Containerized Multimodal Architecture for On-Device Driver-State Feedback with ROS 2 and Edge AI Services
authors: Raul Fernandez-Matellan, David Martin Gomez, Arturo de la Escalera Hueso
abstract: Modern automated-driving platforms increasingly rely on multimodal driver monitoring to support supervision and adaptive interaction. However, real-vehicle deployment is often hindered by dependency coupling across robotics middle-ware, sensor drivers, and heterogeneous artificial-intelligence runtimes. We validated an on-device, containerized driver-state feedback architecture that combines Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) acquisition with ROS-free inference containers via a shared-memory (SHM) Bridge, distilling multi-camera video and wearable physiology into compact semantic messages. Vision pipelines were evaluated across heterogeneous runtimes (OpenVINO for head pose, YOLOv8 via ONNX Runtime for person/proximity, and Ultralytics YOLOv8 for driver–object interaction), together with a physiological awareness module. The loop was closed with a local large language model (LLM), served via Ollama (Llama 3.2), to generate structured on-screen and audio feedback. In a one-hour sustained benchmark, the SHM-Bridge overhead averaged 2.45 ms (median 0.21 ms), remaining small relative to inference latency (5.23 ms physiology, 39.26 ms OpenVINO, 189.40 ms ONNX, 313.74 ms Ultralytics, and 2093.05 ms LLM). These results support SHM-Bridge decoupling as a practical strategy for modular multimodal driver monitoring in real vehicles by isolating software dependencies and mitigating runtime conflicts.

title: SKYWALKER: Dual-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Crawling in a Planar Microgravity Analog : Sim-to-Real Evaluation at ESA’s Orbital Robotics Lab
authors: Dadi H. Davidsson, Bruno M. De Salas, Glenn G. Svendsen, Rasmus D. Kristiansen, Gustav Hansen, Artem Melnyk, Alaa E. Kawara, Thor Nielsen, Jon Argjaboda, Simon Bøgh, Anton B. Mortensen
abstract: SKYWALKER is a dual-agent reinforcement learning framework that enables a free-floating robotic platform to "crawl" hand-over-hand between fixed anchor points in a planar microgravity analog. The task is divided between two cooperating RL agents, one for arm-based anchoring and one for base repositioning. Both agents are trained in a frictionless simulation environment.In hardware trials conducted at the European Space Agency’s Orbital Robotics Lab (ORL) as part of the ESA Academy Experiments Programme, the learned arm policy (Agent A) successfully performed autonomous docking, while the base-motion policy (Agent B) encountered sim-to-real transfer issues and was temporarily replaced with a scripted fallback. Subsequently, Agent B was refined and validated on a physical ball-bearing testbed at Aalborg University, enabling fully learned anchor-to-anchor crawling.These results demonstrate the feasibility of learning-based control for on-orbit assembly tasks and highlight the critical role of high-fidelity simulation and robust safety measures in deploying reinforcement learning for space robotics.

title: Bounds on Maximum Hermitian Hull Dimension of MDS Codes and MDS Codes with Explicit Hermitian Hulls
authors: Huimin Lao, Hao Chen, Yeow Meng Chee, San Ling, Yang Li
abstract: MDS codes with determined Hermitian hull dimensions have attracted significant attention for their application in quantum error correction. From an MDS code over Fq2 with fixed Hermitian hull dimension ℓ, where q is a prime power larger than 2, one can obtain an MDS code with any smaller ℓ′-dimensional Hermitian hull for 0 ≤ ℓ′ ≤ ℓ. Then it is natural to consider the problem of determining the maximum Hermitian hull dimension, denoted by Lq(n, k), among all MDS codes with the same length n and dimension k over Fq2. Some constructions of Hermitian self-orthogonal generalized Reed-Solomon (GRS) codes had been proposed, which addressed this problem for certain parameter regimes. However, it is still unknown for many cases, in particular for k ≥ q + 1. In this paper, we study the Hermitian hulls of a class of codes which generalizes GRS codes, called twisted generalized Reed-Solomon (TGRS) codes. TGRS codes contain MDS subclasses that are not linearly equivalent to GRS codes (called non-GRS codes). We give a bound on the Hermitian hull dimensions of certain TGRS codes of general twists. In addition, we derive a lower bound on Lq(n, k) for n | q2 − 1 and 1 ≤ k ≤ n, which generalizes and improves some previous results. For some parameter regimes where n ≥ q + 1 and k ≥ q + 1, we prove that Lq(n, k) ≥ k/2 and explicitly construct [n, k]q2 MDS codes whose Hermitian hulls have dimension at least k/2. This result solves partially an open problem pointed out in the literature. The constructed MDS codes arise from either GRS or non-GRS TGRS codes. Furthermore, some sufficient conditions for TGRS codes with general twists to be Hermitian self-orthogonal are given, and Hermitian self-orthogonal non-GRS MDS codes are constructed. Based on our constructions, we provide several families of MDS entanglement-assisted quantum error-correcting codes.

title: Consistency-based Semi-supervised Evidential Active Learning Framework for Robust Classification of Radiology Images_supp1-3696868.pdf
authors: Pavitra Krishnaswamy
abstract: Deep learning offers high performance for radiology image classification, but relies on large, expert annotated datasets. Semi-supervised learning and active learning approaches can leverage unlabelled samples and mitigate the annotation burden. Combining these techniques via semi-supervised active learning (SSAL) can compound their benefits, yet the effectiveness of this approach may be hindered by unreliable uncertainty estimation and consistency enforcement. To address these challenges, we propose Consistency-based Semi-supervised Evidential Active Learning (CSEAL). CSEAL is a principled SSAL framework leveraging evidential learning for reliable estimation of predictive uncertainty for consistency enforcement and prioritised sampling. First, we develop evidential counterparts of leading semi-supervised methods with different consistency enforcement mechanisms: Pseudo-labelling, Virtual Adversarial Training, Mean Teacher, and NoTeacher, and demonstrate customisability of CSEAL. Second, we introduce Noise Robust-evidential NoTeacher, an enhancement over evidential NoTeacher, that uses consensus principles and a small-loss inclusion mechanism to learn with noisyannotations. In extensive experiments on many X-ray, CT, and MRI datasets, we demon strate that CSEAL offers substantial performance gains over competitive SSAL baselines and enhances robustness in noisy annotation scenarios. Third, we translate CSEAL into an annotation platform and demonstrate its value as an aid for real-world radiology image annotation. Specifically, our CSEAL-assisted platform performs close to fully supervised learning with very small labelled datasets, en ables accurate auto-labelling, and maintains performance even when only noisy labels from junior annotators are available for training. Our work offers new opportunities to enhance the efficiency of clinical image annotation and model development workflows.

title: Efficient Disruption of Criminal Networks through Multi-Objective Genetic Algorithms
authors: Yehezkiel Darmadi, Thanh Thi Nguyen, Campbell Wilson
abstract: Criminal networks, such as the Sicilian Mafia, pose substantial threats to public safety, national security, and economic stability. Outdated disruption methods with a focus on removing influential individuals or key players have proven ineffective due to the covertness of the network. Thus, researchers have been trying to apply Social Network Analysis (SNA) techniques, such as centrality-based measures, to identify key players. However, removing individuals with high centrality often proves to be inefficient, as it does not mimic the real-world scenarios that Law Enforcement Agencies (LEAs) face. For instance, the operational costs limit the LEAs from exploiting the results of the centrality-based methods. This study proposes a multi-objective optimisation framework like the Weighted Sum Genetic Algorithm (WS-GA) and the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to identify disruption strategies that balance two conflicting goals, maximising fragmentation and minimising operational cost which is captured by the spatial distance between nodes and the nearest LEA headquarters. The study utilises the "Montagna Operation" dataset for the experiments. The results demonstrate that although centrality-based approaches can fragment network effectively, they tend to incur higher operational costs. In contrast, the proposed algorithms achieve comparable disruption outcomes with significantly lower operational costs. The contribution of this work lies in incorporating operational costs in a form of spatial distance constraints into disruption strategy, which has been largely overlooked in prior studies. This research offers a scalable multi-objective capability that improves practical application of SNA in guiding LEAs in disrupting criminal networks more efficiently and strategically.

title: Generalized Bayesian Linearized AVO Inversion using Box–Cox Transform
authors: Dario Grana, Leandro Passos De Figueiredo, Fernando Luis Bordignon, Rodrigo Exterkoetter, Mauro Roisenberg, Bruno Barbosa Rodrigues
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: A generalized Bayesian linearized seismic inversion framework is presented to extend the classical Bayesian linearized inversion by assuming non-Gaussian prior distributions for elastic properties through a parametric normalizing transformation applied directly to the elastic variables. The approach applies a monotonic invertible power function, namely the Box–Cox transformation, to map non-Gaussian elastic variables into a Gaussian space, where computationally efficient Bayesian linearized inversion is performed using linearized amplitude-versus-offset (AVO) models. A low-frequency background trend is incorporated to mitigate the band-limited nature of seismic data. Posterior mean and covariance in the Gaussian space are obtained analytically and subsequently mapped back to the physical domain, yielding posterior distributions that capture realistic uncertainty and prior statistical behavior. The Box–Cox transformation implicitly defines a flexible parametric family for the elastic properties, referred to as the Box–Cox normal distribution, whose shape is controlled by a single parameter λ estimated from measured data by minimizing the skewness of the transformed variable. This family encompasses the lognormal distribution and both right-and left-skewed distributions. The methodology is demonstrated on synthetic and field seismic datasets to estimate the posterior distributions of P-wave velocity, S-wave velocity, and density conditioned on the seismic observations, and to infer the most likely elastic model of the reservoir.

title: Optimal Techno-Economic Energy Storage System Sizing for Islanded Microgrids
authors: Abdullah Alharbi, Abdullah Alamri, Azeddine Draou
abstract: This work employed HOMER Pro software to simulate an islanded microgrid (MG) and determine energy storage systems' (ESSs) technical and economic impact. Data related to weather, like global horizontal irradiance (GHI) and temperature, were collected. The energy-generating units used were photovoltaics (PVs) panels and diesel generators (DGs). The other MG components were the converter and ESSs. The work involved designing a base case, that has only DGs, and two case studies to analyze and determine the optimal solution. The first case study comprised DGs, PVs, and ESSs (lead-acid batteries). The second case study comprised generators, DGs, PVs, and ESSs (lithium-ion batteries). The analysis showed that the optimal solution would be a case that consists of lithium-ion batteries with PVs and DGs. Such a case would be technically and economically feasible, and its levelized energy cost would be $0.154.

title: The analysis of customized tokenizer for the development of isiNdebele language model
authors: Promise Tshepiso Malatji, Thipe Isaiah Modipa
abstract: The quality of tokenization depends on the dataset quality used to develop the tokenizer. SentencePiece model requires enormous data to train a tokenizer. IsiNdebele is a low-resourced language with little dataset to train a tokenizer or text generation model. The use of customized tokenizers (monolingual) has proved to be more effective for the development of low-resourced language models. In this study, we develop two custom tokenizers, the isiNdebele monolingual, and the Nguni language family, using existing datasets from National Centre for Human Language Technology. To evaluate the tokenizers, we then developed isiNdebele language models using the multilingual text-to-text transfer transformer. After evaluating the two tokenizers we then optimized the tokenizer that showed potential to properly handle the isiNdebele language. Model type and vocabulary size experiments were conducted to improve the ROUGE F1 and the BERTScore of the tokenizer. The optimized tokenizer was then used in the development of our final isiNdebele language model. The BLEU and perplexity scores of 0.8837 and 1.0258 respectively, achieved by the developed language model indicate that the custom tokenizer has a promising ability to deal with a morphologically rich low-resourced language.

title: Evaluating Histogram Matching for Robust Deep Learning–Based Grapevine Disease Detection
authors: Ruben Pascual, Inés Hernández, Salvador Gutiérrez, Javier Tardaguila, Pedro Melo-Pinto, Daniel Paternain, Mikel Galar
abstract: Variability in illumination is a primary factor limiting deep learning robustness for field-based plant disease detection. This study evaluates Histogram Matching (HM), a technique that transforms the pixel intensity distribution of an image to match a reference profile, to mitigate this in grapevine classification, distinguishing among healthy leaves, downy mildew, and spider mite damage. We propose a dual-stage integration of HM: (i) as a preprocessing step for normalization, and (ii) as a data augmentation technique to introduce controlled training variability. Experiments using 1,469 RGB images (comprising homogeneous leaf-focused and heterogeneous canopy samples) to train ResNet-18 models demonstrate that this combination significantly enhances robustness on real-world canopy images. While leaf-focused samples showed marginal gains, the canopy subset improved markedly, indicating that balancing normalization with histogram-based diversification effectively bridges the domain gap caused by uncontrolled lighting.

title: Comprehensive comparison of acceleration voltage of selected dielectric liquids as a critical non-standard parameter describing dielectric properties of insulating liquids
authors: Maciej Lalik, Filip Stuchala, Pawel Rozga, Bartlomiej Pasternak
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This paper presents the results of comparative studies on acceleration voltage determination of various dielectric liquids of different origin. The results obtained for classical non-uniform field represented by point-sphere gap of 25 mm are discussed in terms of both voltage polarities of standard lightning impulse voltage. The data on experimental approach are discussed as well to show that comparison is only possible when same testing conditions are applied to each dielectric liquid considered. The results obtained show that acceleration voltage parameter brings important information on behavior of the liquid under lightning impulse stress, which is critical issue when considering the alternative liquid for transformer applications. The differences noted indicate that acceleration voltage may be a new indicator, which can be considered as standard parameter used in assessing the dielectric properties of liquids. When comes to the numbers, both in case of negative and positive lightning impulse voltage hydrocarbon based liquids (mineral oil, GTL, bio-based hydrocarbon) are characterized by much higher acceleration voltage than alternative liquids such as esters.

title: Effective-rotor model in frequency-domain stray losses estimation of the stator end-zone in a 240 MW turbogenerator
authors: Janusz Kołodziej, Mariusz Jagieła, Piotr Mynarek, Paweł Berger, Tomasz Dusza, Rafał Maniara
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: The analysis of stray losses in the turbogenerator's stator end-zone often requires a computationally intensive 3D time-stepping analysis. In this work, a much less demanding frequency-domain model is considered. In such a case, one must address the altered rotor geometry, excitation, and saturation mechanisms. All these differ from those in a real system. This study introduces an improvement to the conventional approach, based on linear rotor material, by incorporating the rotor saturation factor, thereby broadening its applicability across a wider range of operating conditions. The study is conducted for a 240 MW turbogenerator operating in an overexcitation area, where a noticeable change in the predicted ohmic losses is observed in response to the reduction in rotor magnetomotive force due to magnetic saturation.

title: Analysis of Routing Problems in Payment Channels and Optimization of the Payment System in Trading Platforms of Microgrid Networks
authors: Volodymyr Khilenko, Bence Ladoczki, Halyna Bielokha, Oleksii Stepanov, Serhii Denysiuk, Vasyl Khylenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article is devoted to the development of smart management systems of trading platforms for energy systems and consideration of the problems of building a system of optimal calculations in Microgrid networks. It is believed that the trading platform of the participants of the energy network makes payments using the blockchain, which makes it urgent to solve the problems of routing and dynamic selection of the amount of the commission for transactions. Optimizing the operation of the network imposes requirements on the management system regarding the selection of the shortest route between the participants of the trade operation. The paper addresses the problem of unbalanced channel states in payment channel networks (PCN) and investigates the robustness of fee adjustment strategies by conducting simulations on different PCN graphs to show that dynamic methods can lead to better performance.

title: Intermittent Event-Triggered Fixed-Time Containment Control for Disturbed Second-Order Multi-Agent Systems Under DoS Attacks
authors: Xinyi Lin, Zhuoning Zhang, Lu Ren, Mengwei Sun, Jian Liu, Changyin Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: This paper proposes an intermittent dynamic event triggered control (ETC) algorithm for disturbed second-order multi-agent systems (MASs). The proposed control algorithm aims to guarantee practical fixed-time containment under Denial of-Service (DoS) attacks. First, a novel detection algorithm is designed to identify whether the MAS is under connectivity-broken DoS attacks. Subsequently, based on the detection algorithm, an intermittent dynamic event-triggered control algorithm is devised to attain the fixed-time containment for disturbed second-order MASs by considering the connectivity-broken DoS attack periods as rest intervals. By introducing suitable parameters, the control algorithm obtains an upper bound for the convergence time, which is independent of the initial conditions. Then, based on the dynamic ETC algorithm, a self-triggered control (STC) algorithm is proposed to avoid continuous monitoring of neighboring followers and leaders. Furthermore, Zeno phenomenon is avoided in both the dynamic ETC and STC algorithms. Ultimately, the effectiveness of the proposed control algorithms is systematically evaluated through numerical simulations.

title: Mechanistic Insights into Voltage Maintaining Performance of Dielectric Films: Material Dependence and Field Effects_supp1-3698069.docx
authors: Zhi-Min Dang
abstract: Metallized film capacitors in pulsed power technology are capable of releasing large amounts of energy within a short time. After charging is completed and the power supply is disconnected, the capacitor enters a break stage during which inevitable voltage decay occurs. Understanding the voltage maintaining performance (VMP) of capacitors and the dielectric films is essential for improving the efficiency of pulsed systems and enabling spatiotemporal control of stored energy. In this work, the field effects and material dependence of VMP were investigated using representative dielectrics—BOPP, PEI, and PVDF. The field-accelerated decay of BOPP and PEI follows the Poole-Frenkel-like hopping model, whereas PVDF exhibits an exponential relationship consistent with the Landau–Khalatnikov (L–K) ferroelectric dynamics. Voltage decay mainly comes from leakage and depolarization, with depolarization being the dominant factor. The electric field enhances both contributions synergistically. During depolarization, ferroelectric PVDF exhibits a higher proportion of fast polarization channels, while BOPP and PEI show a more balanced contribution of fast and slow components. This study aims to provide new insights into VMP and offers guidelines for material selection and optimization in different application scenarios.

title: Parallel Secure Pattern Matching with Differential Privacy and Consistency Checking
authors: Zhicheng Li, Jian Xu, Teng Lu, Jianting Ning, Qiang Wang, Fucai Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Secure Pattern Matching (SPM) aims to identify all occurrences of a target pattern within a text while preserving data confidentiality and has vital applications in bioinformatics, digital forensics, and cloud-based healthcare. However, existing SPM schemes often suffer from limited scalability on large scale datasets and provide insufficient correctness assurances under outsourced cloud settings. To address these limitations, we propose PSPM, a parallel SPM framework built upon secure multi-party computation (MPC), supporting both single- and multi-pattern queries with comprehensive wildcard functionality. The proposed scheme integrates differentially private Read and Write primitives to obfuscate memory access patterns and enable secure, oblivious data operations. To enhance efficiency, the input text is divided into overlapping sliding windows, each processed in parallel under SIMD-style execution. Each window performs bidirectional scanning to fully leverage parallelism and maximize throughput. For single-pattern queries, local matching is achieved through a border-array–based algorithm, while multi-pattern matching employs an MPC-adapted Aho–Corasick automaton. We design a lightweight cross-consistency checking mechanism that validates outputs via wildcard-augmented variants, thereby enabling detection of inconsistency-inducing single-path computation faults under the standard non-colluding semi-honest setting. Formal security proofs and extensive experimental evaluations on large genomic datasets demonstrate that our framework outperforms prior SPM protocols by up to 1.73× in single-pattern tasks and 10.56× in multi-pattern tasks.

title: Essential Proteins Prediction Using Features Synergy Model and GO Pure Centrality
authors: Xinlong Luo, Gaoshi Li, Zhipeng Hu, Jingli Wu, Wei Peng, Jiafei Liu, Xiaoshu Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Essential proteins are a crucial component of living organisms, and their absence will lead to cell death or reproductive arrest. Discovering these proteins can propel advancements in synthetic biology and facilitate the development of novel antibiotics and therapies for various diseases. However, current computational methods suffer from two major drawbacks that hinder their discovery rate: one is the significant noise in protein-protein interaction (PPI) data, and the other is the inadequate consideration of feature relationships. To enhance identification capabilities, this study proposes a novel essential protein prediction method, Feature Synergy Method (FSM), which leverages a features synergy model and GO pure centrality. The FSM is described as folows:Firstly, based on the principle of co-expression, gene expression data are integrated with the original PPI network to construct a pure PPI network (PPIN). Subsequently, GO annotation data are employed to calculate GO_sim weights for the interactions within the original PPI network, forming a GS_PIN. The PPIN and GS_PIN are then fused to establish the GS_PPIN, which helps mitigate the impact of noise in PPI data. Secondly, a new centrality measure, GO pure centrality (GPC), is designed based on this GO similarity-weighted pure PPI network. Thirdly, an evolutionary conservation score (ECS) is extracted from subcellular localization and orthologous proteins data. Fourthly, after analyzing the relationship between GPC and ECS, a novel fusion model, the features synergy model, is developed to integrate GPC and ECS, ultimately leading to the proposal of the new essential protein prediction method, FSM. To validate the performance of FSM, six computational methods (PeC, WDC, ION, NCCO, E_POC, and JDC) and six centrality measures (NC, IC, EC, SC, CC, and DC) were evaluated on three distinct yeast datasets. The results demonstrate that FSM achieves a higher essential protein identification rate. Similarly, GPC identifies more essential proteins compared to the six centrality-based approaches (NC, IC, EC, SC, CC, and DC).

title: Probabilistic Causal Modelling of Factors Contributing to Stress, Anxiety, and Depression for Nurses and Social Workers
authors: Olha Shaposhnyk, Daria Zahorska, Amy Beck, Katherine Bright, Linda Duffett-Leger, Svetlana Yanushkevich
abstract: This study aims to explore the causal relationships between psychological and demographic factors using data from a survey of nurses and social workers, applying data-driven causal modelling techniques. The primary goal was to identify potential causal relationships between the factors such as stress, anxiety, depression, age, education, and professional experience.We employed a multi-stage analysis integrating reliability and correlation analysis, synthetic data generation, and causal structure learning. Statistical techniques, such as Copulas, were used to reproduce the joint distribution of observed variables, allowing for the reliable estimation of dependencies under limited data conditions. Several causal discovery algorithms, both data-driven and score-based, were evaluated. The resulting causal graphs revealed strong associations between stress, anxiety, and depression scores, and education level and years of experience in practice. Time-related features of stress events (i.e. minimum and modal frequency) were linked to higher stress and anxiety scores, suggesting their potential as behavioural indicators of daily stress exposure. A combination of data-driven and structure-based approaches provided the most stable and interpretable network structure.

title: Model Predictive Control for Constrained Linear Positive Systems on Graphs
authors: Roland Schurig, David Ohlin, Anders Rantzer, Emma Tegling, Rolf Findeisen
abstract: Positive systems describing networks with inherently non-negative states and inputs arise naturally in routing, logistics, and compartmental modelling. We consider problems modelled as positive linear systems in incidence form with linear cost. The addition of capacity constraints on states (storage) and inputs (flows between nodes) significantly increases the problem complexity. Leveraging the analytic structure of the unconstrained problem, an explicit suboptimal admissible controller is constructed. This yields graph-computable performance bounds and a minimum stabilising horizon length for a model predictive controller without terminal conditions. A convex program enables efficient computation of the optimal bound and horizon. These results highlight how system structure enables explicit MPC guarantees that are typically not available.

title: Reconstruction of Graph Signals on Complex Manifolds with Kernel Methods
authors: Yu Zhang, Linyu Peng, Bing-Zhao Li
abstract: Graph signals are widely used to describe vertex attributes or features in graph-structured data, with applications spanning the internet, social media, transportation, sensor networks, and biomedicine. Graph signal processing (GSP) has emerged to facilitate the analysis, processing, and sampling of such signals. While kernel methods have been extensively studied for estimating graph signals from samples provided on a subset of vertices, their application to complex-valued graph signals remains largely unexplored. This paper introduces a novel framework for reconstructing graph signals using kernel methods on complex manifolds. By embedding graph vertices into a higher-dimensional complex ambient space that approximates a lower-dimensional manifold, the framework extends the reproducing kernel Hilbert space to complex manifolds. It leverages Hermitian metrics and geometric measures to characterize kernels and graph signals. Additionally, several metric-induced and graph fractional spectral kernels are proposed for reconstructing complex graph signals. Finally, experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of this framework in accurately reconstructing complex graph signals, outperforming conventional kernel-based approaches. This work lays a foundational basis for integrating complex geometry and kernel methods in GSP.

title: LEABS : A Latency- and Resource-Aware Bi-Directional Service Scheduling Framework for Relay Chains in the Internet of Vehicles
authors: Yawen Zhao, Nan Ding, Chengyi Shuai, Li Xu
abstract: Multiple stakeholders such as automakers, service providers, and regulators tend to maintain their own blockchain systems in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). This fragmentation leads to data silos and hinders service interoperability across domains. Relay Chains are emerging as a cross-chain service-governance layer, enabling coordination among heterogeneous blockchains. However, scheduling multi-input multi-output (MIMO) transactions introduces new challenges, including complex transaction dependencies, heterogeneous node capabilities, and strict latency constraints. To address these issues, this paper proposes LEABS, a bi-directional service-aware scheduling framework that simul taneously considers user-side service quality and relay-chain-side resource governance. LEABS formulates cross-chain transaction scheduling as a service-delivery optimization problem, modeled by a Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP) formulation and stabilized through Lyapunov-based queue governance. A Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) method is further in troduced to learn adaptive policies under dynamic workloads and heterogeneous network conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LEABS achieves a superior balance among confirmation latency and load fairness, highlighting its effec tiveness as a Quality-of-Service(QoS)-driven cross-chain service governance mechanism for future IoV ecosystems.

title: AdRo-FL: Secure and Informed Client Selection for Federated Learning under Adversarial Aggregator_supp1-3696080.pdf
authors: Walid Aljoby

title: Perception of Gendered Voice and Expressed Apology in Warehouse Robots
authors: Ava Megyeri, Maria Kyrarini, Natasha Kholgade Banerjee, Sean Banerjee
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: When industrial collaborative robotic assistants make mistakes, trust between the human and robot is likely to be enhanced if the robot issues an apology when it makes a mistake in its assigned task. To evaluate user perceptions toward an industrial robot, we design a virtual reality (VR) digital twin of a warehouse where a worker and the twin of a Universal Robots UR10 manipulator arm collaborate to lift boxes from a conveyor belt and sort them based on the adhesive tape color. Using VR enables us to collect data at scale due to the challenges of replicating the warehouse in a typical lab setting. We evaluate four versions of the robot responding to the event that the human misses a box—an idle robot that never lifts a box, a robot that attempts to lift human-missed boxes but does not apologize for a missed box, and versions of the robot embodied with female and male voices to issue apologies for a missed box. We conduct a human subject study with 32 participants, including 7 identifying as female, 1 Nonbinary, 1 Man, and 23 male to understand how perceptions change based on the gendered voice and expressed apology from the robot. We show significant differences in likeability, animacy, and perceived intelligence between idle robots and robots that issue apologies. We show that self-identified gender and prior experience in warehousing play roles in how participants perceive gendered voice and expressed apology in robots.

title: Granular Convolutional Neural Networks: Interpretable Classification with Uncertainty Quantification
authors: Inas Shadoul, Majdi Mansouri, Mostefa Mesbah, Rami Al-Hmouz
abstract: This paper presents an adapted framework of Granular Convolutional Neural Networks (GCNNs) designed for classification tasks. Built upon conventional convolutional architectures, the framework introduces interval-valued (granular) convolutional connections, where each interval forms an information granule around the pixel value. This representation provides a quantifiable measure of uncertainty and interpretability in convolutional feature extraction and decision making. By optimally distributing information granularity across the input space, the proposed GCNN model seeks to maximize classification confidence while preserving specificity. To achieve this objective, a single objective particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm is employed, guided by classification coverage and specificity criteria. Using this approach, the regions that contribute the most to the classes can be identified by the formation of the intervals.

title: The Equivalent Parameters of Losses in Inactive Parts of Transformers Under Nonsinusoidal Loads
authors: Volodumyr Zinovkin, Iurii Krysan, Vira Savchenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The conditions and criteria for applying the relevant equivalence parameters of surface losses in non-active components of power transformer structures are provided, taking into account the nonlinear dependence on the magnetic field intensity scattered on their surface.

title: A Low Noise Amplifier Design with High Image Rejection and Selectivity Using Integrated BPF
authors: Sadhana Kumari, Arun Kumar G, Rakesh Kumar, Naman Jain, Mohammad Masir, Naveen Kumar Maurya
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: A low-noise amplifier (LNA) with integrated image-suppression and bandpass selectivity is designed in this paper. A cheap FR4 substrate is used to create the circuit. The simulated performance demonstrates that the amplifier forms transmission zeros at 1.99 GHz and 2.98 GHz, while maintaining a gain of 14.49 dB and a noise figure (NF) of 1.27 dB at the operating frequency of 2.4 GHz. For intermediate frequencies between 400 MHz and 600 MHzThe simulation outcomes demonstrate image suppression levels of 35 dB and 48 dB at IFs of 400 MHz and 600 MHz, respectively.

title: An Agentic Framework for Efficient and Scalable Web Content Classification
authors: Dan-Gabriel Tudor, Costin Bădică
abstract: This paper introduces an automated framework designed for classifying web page content into the IAB Content Taxonomy in a resource-efficient and cost-effective way, addressing the needs of companies and researchers in the space of Digital Advertising and Search Engine Optimization. Motivated by the high cost of Large Language Models (LLMs) for high-volume tasks, we investigate the efficacy for this task of BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), a small transformer model, and further compare it with smaller Machine Learning models. The agent's architecture encompasses a robust pipeline for web-content processing, including content extraction, multilingual translation, classification, and results logging. Due to the hierarchical nature of the taxonomy and the large number of categories (345 categories), a great amount of work was dedicated to the construction of a broad knowledge base, created by aggregating real web data alongside synthetically generated data. We present a comparative evaluation of five models—BERT, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Naïve Bayes, and Random Forest—as the agent's classification engine. Experimental results demonstrate that a fine-tuned BERT model achieves the highest accuracy (0.8923 Tier 1 Accuracy), while a Calibrated LinearSVC offers a superior balance of performance (0.731 Tier 1 Accuracy) and computational efficiency. The research is concluded by building a functional, user-ready prototype, validating the agentic framework as a useful solution to real-world SEO and Ad-tech applications.

title: Design, Modeling, and Validation of a 6-DoF Wearable Puncture Robot
authors: Jianfeng Yao, Canhui Wu, Bang Liu, Zhuang Fu, Zi Fang, Fei Jing, Xufan Jiang
abstract: Puncture robots are increasingly pivotal for minimally invasive interventions. However, achieving a balance among compactness, dexterity and sufficient load capacity remains a challenge. This article presents the design, modeling, and validation of a novel 6-DoF wearable puncture robot intended for precise percutaneous procedures. The system features a dual layer parallel mechanism for orientation and positioning, integrated with an oblique-roller feeding unit to realize spin and axial insertion. Kinematic models were derived to support precise pose regulation and needle steering. A fully functional prototype was constructed and comprehensive benchtop experiments demonstrated that the robot generates a maximum axial feed force of 2.54 N, sufficient to penetrate viscoelastic biological tissues. Furthermore, phantom puncture tests validated a mean targeting accuracy of 1.33 mm under varying insertion angles. These results confirm that the proposed system effectively combines high-load capability with precise kinematic control, offering a promising solution for robot assisted microsurgery.

title: Bridging the Infrastructure Gap: An AI-Driven Framework for Automated Grant Matching in Environmentally Threatened Rural Communities
authors: Alpana Desai
abstract: Identifying relevant federal funding opportunities for rural infrastructure development remains a significant challenge, particularly for underserved communities such as those in Alaska. Existing platforms such as Grants.gov rely primarily on keyword-based search, limiting their ability to capture the contextual needs expressed in community planning documents. This paper presents a hybrid retrieval framework that integrates keyword-based grant discovery with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to semantically interpret local planning documents. The proposed system ingests publicly available community plans and hazard mitigation reports, embeds them using transformer-based models, and aligns extracted infrastructure needs with funding opportunities retrieved via the Grants.gov API. By combining structured federal data with unstructured local knowledge, the framework enhances the relevance and contextual accuracy of grant recommendations. This work contributes a practical architecture for bridging semantic gaps in public-sector funding discovery and provides a foundation for future evaluation and deployment.

title: DSFF: Dynamic–Static Feature Fusion Framework for Robust Cross-Dataset Deepfake Detection
authors: Hui-Ying Huang, Ming-Yi Huang, Hao Teng, Chai-Yu Lin
abstract: Deepfake, a technology driven by generative AI, has become an urgent threat due to its capability to generate fake images and synthesize realistic videos. Ensuring the authenticity of digital content requires robust deepfake detection; however, existing detectors struggle with cross-manipulation and cross-dataset generalization as new forgery techniques emerge and dataset distributions vary. To address these challenges, we propose a Dynamic and Static Feature Fusion (DSFF) Framework. DSFF jointly leverages static appearance cues from RGB images and dynamic motion features derived from dense optical flow. It further incorporates cross-attention for modality alignment and channel attention for feature recalibration, enabling the model to capture subtle spatial–temporal inconsistencies introduced by diverse manipulation methods. Extensive experiments on FF++, SBI, and cross-quality settings demonstrate that DSFF consistently outperforms blending-based baselines. Notably, it surpasses the baseline method by approximately 3% AUROC under unseen manipulation types and cross-dataset evaluation. Moreover, on the low-resolution cross-manipulation data, DSFF improves performance by an average of 2% improvement over the baseline method, demonstrating greater stability and stronger robustness to resolution degradation across all manipulation types. Overall, the proposed DSFF framework offers a robust and generalizable approach to deepfake detection, particularly in unseen manipulation scenarios and cross-dataset conditions.

title: PCR-GP: Prior-Coupled Residual Gaussian Processes for Few-Shot Lipid Nanoparticle Formulation
authors: Adrian Kucia, Jacob Sanderson, Wai Lok Woo, Gary Montague, Yvonne Perrie, Thomas McLean, David Vaughan Palmer, Bruce Richard Williams
abstract: We study few-shot surface transfer in formulation science: given a dense response surface at pH 4.5 over inputs (DDAB, TFR) to Z-average (DLS), infer the full pH 6 surface while measuring as few pH 6 points as possible. We propose a pH-anchored Prior–Coupled Residual Gaussian Process (PCR–GP) that treats the target surface as a global affine transform of a source GP plus a learnable discrepancy: f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf>(x) = ρg(x) + β + r(x). The source GP g is trained once on the dense pH 4.5 data and provides a strong spatial prior. The coupling (ρ, β) is estimated from the few pH 6 labels using a stability-aware freeze→ridge→clip procedure, after which a residual GP r is fit on the target labels to capture cross-pH differences. Active selection evaluates target uncertainty on a feasible candidate set and snaps the next experiment to the highest-uncertainty unused location. The framework reports fixed-scale uncertainty maps and log-scaled error curves for comparability across rounds. On real formulation data, PCR–GP reconstructs the pH 6 surface with very few additional assays while maintaining high accuracy, outperforming naive direct learning and prior-informed baselines. The approach is simple, sample-efficient, and broadly applicable whenever one condition is cheap/dense and another is expensive/sparse.

title: LLM-Seeded Genetic Improvement: Hierarchical AST Evolution with Multi-Level Donor-Code Transplants
authors: Michael Schladt, John C. Gallagher
abstract: Automated software transformation remains a key challenge in search-based software engineering, particularly when targeting high-level behavioral changes.
