title:
LSBMAR: Latent Schrödinger Bridge for Metal Artifact Reduction in CT Scans
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Luca Zaccagna, Andrea Salfinger, Tiziano Vallisa, Lauro Snidaro
abstract: Computed Tomography (CT) scans are often degraded by metal artifacts arising from implants or prosthetic devices, which manifest as bright and dark streaks that compromise diagnostic interpretability. Current Metal Artifact Reduction (MAR) methods typically treat this task as sonogram-domain inpainting, using the image domain as a guide for enforcing consistency. Recently, Diffusion Models (DMs) have been employed and show improved performance. However, because these models are applied directly in the high-dimensional spaces, they remain computationally demanding, while the well-established baselines struggle to achieve the same results as diffusion models. Thus, to overcome these limitations, in this work, we propose a novel MAR framework based on Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) that reinterprets the task as an image-to-image translation problem in latent space. By leveraging the Schrödinger Bridge formulation, our method learns to map corrupted latent representations to their artifact-free counterparts, conditioned directly on the distribution of the degraded images. This design maintains the expressiveness and yields to a more efficient generation process while preserving anatomical fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive performance compared to traditional MAR baselines, effectively reducing artifacts and enhancing image quality.

title: A SINS/OFSS Integrated Positioning Method for Enhancing Shearer Positioning Accuracy in Intelligent Coal Mining Face
authors: Haotian Feng, Xinqiu Fang, Gang Wu, Ningning Chen, Yang Song, Dexing He, Junyue Fan, Minfu Liang, Lijun Sun
abstract: In the context of intelligent coal mining faces, the strapdown inertial navigation system (SINS) serves as a pivotal technology for realizing the autonomous positioning of shearers. However, due to the complex underground environment, the positioning precision and stability of SINS have yet to meet the requirements for the normalized operation of intelligent mining faces. This limitation severely impedes the widespread application of SINS in coal mines. To address these challenges and enhance the performance of shearer SINS, this paper proposes a positioning accuracy improvement method based on the SINS and optical fiber sensing system (OFSS). By leveraging the spatial consistency between the shearer trajectory and the shape curve of the scraper conveyor, a SINS/OFSS integrated positioning model tailored to the shearer’s motion characteristics is established. Furthermore, a position mapping mechanism is introduced to achieve spatiotemporal consistency between the real-time position of the shearer and the discrete points on the scraper conveyor’s shape curve. Subsequently, a shearer trajectory time-series prediction model is constructed using historical states to predict future trajectories. This model serves to evaluate and perform a secondary correction on the positioning results, thereby further strengthening the error correction capability. Finally, simulation experiments and platform tests are conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The results indicate that under both normal cutting and oblique cutting conditions, the positioning precision and stability of the proposed method significantly outperform comparative methods. The combination of the SINS/OFSS integrated positioning model and the TCN-BiLSTM-Attention (TBA) prediction model effectively mitigates SINS positioning errors, realizing a substantial improvement in shearer positioning accuracy.

title: Subject-Adaptive EEG-EMG Fusion Network for Motor Intention Decoding Across a Broad Impairment Spectrum
authors: Yihan Wang, Jiaxing Wang, Weiqun Wang, Jianqiang Su, Bin Hu, Yuze Feng, Shuai Han, Hao Li, Jianghong He, Zeng-Guang Hou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The fusion of electroencephalography (EEG) and electromyography (EMG) holds significant potential for clinical motor intention decoding. However, existing methods are constrained by the dual challenges of data scarcity and high physiological heterogeneity across patient populations. To address these limitations, this paper proposes the Brain-Muscle based Motor Planning to Execution Network (BM-MP2E), a framework leveraging brain-muscle complementarity to ensure reliable decoding across the full impairment spectrum. We introduce a physiologically-informed architecture that explicitly mirrors the neuromuscular transmission pathway. Specifically, movement-related cortical potential (MRCP) for motor planning, αβ bands for activation, and EMG for execution are extracted to constrain the model’s solution space. Furthermore, a subject-adaptive fusion module is designed to dynamically modulate modality contributions based on individual functional status. Experimental validation on 21 patients (ranging from Disorders of Consciousness to stroke) in a unimanual five-class task demonstrates that BM-MP2E achieves an average accuracy of 60.81%, yielding a 6.94% improvement over single-modality EMG. The average learned weights were 0.265 (MRCP), 0.323 (αβ), and 0.412 (EMG). Quantitative analysis reveals a strong positive correlation between adaptive EMG weights and motor function scores (r =0.884, p < 0.0001). This confirms a compensatory measurement logic: the system automatically prioritizes stable cortical features in severe cases while progressively leveraging high-fidelity peripheral signals as function recovers. These findings validate BM-MP2E as an adaptive clinical solution capable of facilitating motor assistance across the entire recovery spectrum.

title: SUVI: Scalable Unified Vector Intelligence for Efficient Edge Deployment
authors: Nakul Vyas
abstract: Many multimodal language models are implemented as dual-tower systems that combine a language model with a separate visual encoder. This design is effective, but it also adds parameters, runtime complexity, and memory overhead. SUVI (Scalable Unified Vector Intelligence) is an alternative architecture in which images are converted into discrete token IDs and processed in the same autoregressive stream as text.In the current implementation, a frozen Stable Diffusion VAE produces latent features, local latent patches are discretized into a fixed visual vocabulary, and the resulting image tokens are concatenated with text tokens for causal modeling by a decoder-only language model. We present SUVI as an implemented single-stream multimodal design and a practical training recipe for studying discrete early fusion with standard open-source components. In earlier development variants, we observed a sharp early optimization transient when newly introduced visual tokens were insufficiently aligned with the language-model embedding space; the current initialization and patchwise discretization recipe appears to reduce this multimodality-shock behavior. An approximately 7,500-step run on Flickr30k with 4-bit LoRA training on an NVIDIA L4 remained stable and used approximately 9.22 GB of allocated GPU memory at peak during training. Using the saved adapter for single-image generation on the same L4 required 6.15 GB of allocated memory after model load and peaked at 6.45 GB during inference. Together, these results position SUVI as a workable architecture for low-overhead multimodal systems research, while more extensive quality evaluation and ablation remain future work.

title: Distributed Semantic Communication via Nonlinear Transform Coding for Correlated Images
authors: Yufei Bo, Meixia Tao, Kai Niu
abstract: This paper investigates distributed source-channel coding for correlated image semantic communication, where different but correlated image sources from distributed transmitters are separately encoded and transmitted through dedicated wireless channels to a common receiver. We propose a novel distributed nonlinear transform coding (NTC)-based approach to explicitly model source correlation from both probabilistic and geometric perspectives. The probabilistic perspective is achieved by a joint entropy model that approximates the joint distribution of latent representations and guides adaptive rate allocation, while the geometric perspective is achieved by a feature alignment module that aligns latent features for maximal correlation learning at the decoder.We implement this distributed NTC framework for both source coding only, referred to as D-NTSC, and joint source-channel coding (JSCC), referred to as D-NTSCC. Variational inference is employed to derive principled loss functions that jointly optimize encoding, decoding, and joint entropy modeling. Extensive experiments on real-world multi-view datasets demonstrate that D-NTSC and D-NTSCC outperform existing distributed source coding and distributed JSCC baselines, respectively, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both pixel-level and perceptual quality metrics.

title: Reliable Covert Communication for Integrated Cognitive Satellite Aerial Terrestrial Networks with NOMA and Poisson Distributed Jammers
authors: Kefeng Guo, Peilin Qi, Shahid Mumtaz, Yuzhen Huang, Ali Nauman, Lei Zhang, Qihui Wu
abstract: With the increasing threats of eavesdropping in wireless networks, covert transmission has become a growing area of concerns. To address this challenge, this paper investigates the reliability of covert communication (CC) in integrated satellite aerial terrestrial networks with homogeneous Poisson point process (HPPP) distributed jammers. In addition, non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and cognitive radio (CR) techniques are employed to enhance spectrum efficiency. To assess the covert performance of the system under consideration, closed-form expressions for the detection error probability (DEP), covert outage probability (COP), and covert transmission rate (CTR) are derived. Moreover, an optimization method is also provided to achieve the optimal COP by adjusting the power allocation factors under power constraints. Furthermore, Monte Carlo (MC) simulation results are conducted to verify the accuracy of the theoretical results. It is revealed that, compared to constant or uniformly distributed interference power, the ability of the considered system to maintain covert operations is significantly enhanced by the presence of Poisson distributed jammers, which indicates the importance of considering realistic, random interference models in CC systems and offers valuable insights for improving system reliability and security.

title: Characterization and Initial Optimization of Reconstruction Performance for Detector Crosstalk of Ultra-high Performance Brain PET Imager
authors: Jiazhen Zhang, Kathryn Fontaine, Tiantian Li, Paul Gravel, Takuya Toyonaga, Yifan Hu, Jean-Dominique Gallezot, Tianyi Zeng, Tommaso Volpi, Yihuan Lu, Thibault Marin, Jinyi Qi, Richard E. Carson
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: The NeuroEXPLORER (NX) is a next-generation, ultra-high-performance brain PET/CT system designed for both high spatial resolution and sensitivity. To meet high-sensitivity requirements, the system uses 2-cm-long scintillation crystals, while the novel U-shaped depth-of-interaction (DOI) encoding and small crystal elements enable high resolution. However, using long, small crystals increases signal crosstalk between crystals arising from Compton scattering, photoelectron escape, and incomplete optical isolation, which can degrade spatial resolution. Although the NX electronics detect crosstalk between micro-blocks and support real-time recovery using measured energy, accurate identification of the first interaction point is not always possible. Incorporating crosstalk events increases coincidence sensitivity by approximately 50% but compromises spatial resolution. Given such a large fraction of detector crosstalk, evaluating its impact on reconstruction performance in the NX is imperative. In this work, we evaluated image resolution in three reconstruction modes: high-sensitivity (HS; all events), higher-resolution (HR; non-crosstalk events), and lower-resolution (LR; crosstalk events), using real phantoms and human studies with multiple tracers. Guided by the resolution characterization of the HYDRA-OLG brain phantom, we introduced a two-Gaussian mixture point spread function (2G-PSF) for HS reconstruction to improve contrast while suppressing noise. At matched iterations, HR yielded the highest image contrast, HS showed slightly reduced contrast, and LR exhibited the largest partial volume effect, as expected. Modeling crosstalk with 2G-PSF for HS increased contrast toward HR levels without sacrificing sensitivity. Overall, these results provide a practical strategy for including crosstalk events in high-resolution PET systems while mitigating their impact on spatial resolution.

title: MPDA: Multimodal Prompt Decoupling Attack on the Safety Filters in Text-to-Image Models
authors: Xingkai Peng, Jun Jiang, Meng Tong, Shuai Li, Weiming Zhang, Nenghai Yu, Kejiang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) models have been widely applied in generating high-fidelity images across various domains. However, these models may also be abused to produce Not-Safe-for-Work (NSFW) content via jailbreak attacks. Existing jailbreak methods primarily manipulate the textual prompt, leaving potential vulnerabilities in image-based inputs largely unexplored. Moreover, text-based methods face challenges in bypassing the model's safety filters. In response to these limitations, we propose the Multimodal Prompt Decoupling Attack (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {MPDA}$</tex-math></inline-formula>), which utilizes image modality to separate the harmful semantic components of the original unsafe prompt. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {MPDA}$</tex-math></inline-formula> follows three core steps: firstly, a large language model (LLM) decouples unsafe prompts into pseudo-safe prompts and harmful prompts. The former are seemingly harmless sub-prompts that can bypass filters, while the latter are sub-prompts with unsafe semantics that trigger filters. Subsequently, the LLM rewrites the harmful prompts into natural adversarial prompts to bypass safety filters, which guide the T2I model to modify the base image into an NSFW output. Finally, to ensure semantic consistency between the generated NSFW images and the original unsafe prompts, the visual language model generates image captions, providing a new pathway to guide the LLM in iterative rewriting and refining the generated content. Extensive experiments empirically demonstrate that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {MPDA}$</tex-math></inline-formula> outperforms the baseline methods in two attack scenarios across Stable Diffusion 3.5 and three commercial models: CogView, Tongyiwanxiang, and Midjourney. Specifically, in the pornographic content attack scenario with Midjourney as the victim model, our method has a 29% higher bypass rate than the previous method, thereby providing robust empirical evidence supporting the effectiveness and generalizability of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathsf {MPDA}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Disclaimer: This paper contains content that readers may find offensive, distressing, or upsetting. Reader discretion is advised.

title: Towards a Sustainable Energy Future: Analyzing the Impact of Hourly Electricity Trade in the GCC Countries, plus Egypt, Jordan, and Iraq
authors: M. Petitet, B. Ricaud, F. A. Felder, A. M. Elshurafa
abstract: Cross-border electricity trading in the Middle East and North Africa region can provide cost and environmental benefits. However, for historical reasons, electricity trading has not reached a mature stage in this region. Based on an economic dispatch model run for 2030, this paper investigates the cost, emission and renewable implications of the electricity trade in the Gulf Cooperation Council countries plus Egypt, Jordan, and Iraq. The model adopts an hourly resolution and considers current and future network interconnections. The results indicate that implementing an hourly market coupling can decrease the region’s annual generation cost by 1.6%, while avoiding 34% of renewable curtailment and reducing carbon dioxide emissions by 8 million metric tons in 2030. The results also suggest that Saudi Arabia is the largest electricity exporter within the considered nine countries, with an annual export volume of 28 TWh. To do so, interconnection lines through which Saudi Arabia exports electricity to Jordan, Iraq and the GCC, respectively, are highly utilized in that single direction. However, Saudi Arabia and Egypt show complementarities, with Saudi Arabia exporting to Egypt during the day when solar photovoltaic generation is high and importing from Egypt during summer nights.

title: Non-Contact Detection of Aeolian Vibrations in Overhead Power Lines via Magnetic Field Spectral Analysis
authors: Jozef Bendík, Martin Ďuriš, Matej Cenký
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Mechanical oscillations, particularly aeolian vibrations, represent a significant threat to the structural integrity and service life of overhead transmission lines. Traditional monitoring methods, such as accelerometers or optical fiber sensors, often require direct physical contact or complex installation procedures. This paper proposes a non-contact method for detecting aeolian vibrations by analyzing the modulation of the magnetic flux density generated by the line current. Using a simulation of a 110 kV double-circuit transmission line, we investigate the interaction between the 50 Hz power frequency and mechanical oscillation frequencies. The results demonstrate that conductor movement induces distinct spectral sidebands in the magnetic field (e.g., at 30 Hz and 70 Hz for a 20 Hz vibration). The amplitude of these sidebands exhibits a linear correlation with the vibration amplitude. This study provides a theoretical proof-of-concept for a magnetic-field-based monitoring system that offers a cost-effective, non-intrusive alternative to conventional vibration assessment techniques.

title: Features of Measurement Parameters in Monitoring at Electric Power Enterprises
authors: Vitalii Kalinchyk, Larysa Tretiakova
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The reliability and safety of electric power systems and their individual installations are of paramount importance due to the potential risks they pose to people and the environment if normal operating modes are disrupted. Operational reliability, defined as the ability to function flawlessly over extended periods under established regimes, is a critical requirement for modern electrical installations. To achieve this, constant monitoring, preventive testing, and maintenance involving electrical workers are essential. Specialized monitoring systems play a crucial role in addressing complex tasks related to ensuring operational reliability and safe conditions in electrical installations. The concept of ‘monitoring’ has evolved to encompass both observation and assessment of the state, particularly in the context of electric power engineering. Monitoring in this field involves not only tracking energy indicators but also assessing working conditions and evaluating the hazardous impacts on employees. This paper highlights the need for a comprehensive approach to monitoring that considers the peculiarities of production processes in electric power engineering and addresses the complex interplay of hazardous and harmful occupational factors affecting workers.

title: Fourier-Enhanced Transformer Encoder Network for Efficient Haptic Texture Modeling/Rendering
authors: Mudassir Ibrahim Awan, Sungjoo Kang, Dongbeom Ko, Waseem Hassan, Seong Tae Kim, Seokhee Jeon
abstract: Haptic texture modeling is essential for immersive environments, yet accurate texture vibration synthesis remains challenging due to high-frequency nature and strong dependence on interaction parameters, such as speed and force. Traditional piecewise approaches handle this variability through algorithmic segmentation of signals into stationary components, followed by labeling across discrete contact conditions. However, the resulting segmentation overhead and postmodeling validation create a procedural bottleneck, limiting their ability to represent continuous user interactions. Recent deep learning methods eliminate explicit segmentation by learning continuous mappings, but their high computational cost hinders real-time deployment. To address these limitations, a lightweight Fourier-enhanced Transformer encoder network is proposed that eliminates segmentation and enables real-time texture rendering while maintaining high reconstruction fidelity. The model integrates a transformer encoder with the Fourier transform to process uniform sliding window inputs, separating spectral magnitude and phase in a Fourier block to capture texture-dependent frequency structure, while temporal features are extracted in a compact encoder for one-step prediction at approximately 1 kHz. The proposed approach was evaluated on a diverse set of textures, achieving strong reconstruction accuracy (time domain: mean absolute error 0.149; spectral domain: goodness-of-fit criterion 92.15%). A psychophysical experiment further confirms its perceptual effectiveness against existing methods when comparing real and synthesized textures.

title: Coupling-Aware Edge-Cloud Computing for Multi-UAV Cooperative Sensing via Evolutionary Game-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yishan Chen, Tong Zhou, Qing Xu, Guanjie Cheng
abstract: For multi-UAV cooperative perception applications in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), edge-cloud collaborative computing is severely constrained by co-channel interference and limited edge computing resources, which induce strong network-level coupling among UAV offloading decisions. In distributed settings where global information is unavailable, this coupling exacerbates environmental non-stationarity during multi-agent training, leading to slow convergence and policy oscillations. To address these issues, we propose a distributed decision-making framework based on evolutionary game theory (EGT). Specifically, we first formulate the multi-UAV task offloading problem as an evolutionary game, capturing the payoff interdependence caused by wireless interference and edge resource contention. Subsequently, we apply replicator dynamics to analyze the evolution of offloading strategies, derive the interior evolutionary equilibrium, and establish its asymptotic stability. Finally, we develop an EGT-enhanced multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (EGT-MADQN) algorithm, which incorporates relative fitness derived from coupling-affected payoffs into strategy fusion through adaptive step-size modulation to to facilitate stable policy adaptation during training. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms baseline approaches in minimizing total cost and task latency while achieving superior convergence stability. It also exhibits strong robustness and scalability across varying UAV fleet sizes and resource configurations.

title: Visual Deep Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Traffic Signal Control: A Neuro-Symbolic XAI Approach
authors: Diego Caballero, María-Paz Sesmero, José María Armingol, Araceli Sanchis
abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) offers promising solutions for Adaptive Traffic Signal Control. However, visual DRL models that map raw pixels directly to control actions operate as "black boxes," hindering their deployment in safety-critical infrastructure due to a lack of interpretability. This paper presents a visual Deep Q-Network agent trained in a high-fidelity CARLA-SUMO co-simulation environment, capable of reducing vehicle waiting times by approximately 23% compared to fixed-time controllers. Beyond performance, we introduce a novel Neuro-Symbolic Explainable AI framework to validate the agent’s decision-making. We employ SHAP for visual introspection and train symbolic surrogate models (Decision Trees and Explainable Boosting Machines) to extract human-readable logic rules from the neural policy. Results demonstrate that the agent implicitly learns to estimate traffic density from photorealistic images, with symbolic models achieving high fidelity in approximating the visual policy. This approach effectively bridges the gap between the performance of deep learning and the transparency required for real-world traffic management.

title: Fill in the Gaps - Applying Polynomial-based Imputation techniques for Heart Rate Data
authors: Vaibhav Gupta, Florian Grensing, Beyza Cinar, Louisa Van Den Boom, Maria Maleshkova
abstract: Advances in clinical time-series modeling increasingly rely on AI-driven anomaly detection to identify the occurrence of adverse physiological events. The integration of multi-modal wearable sensor data with modern machine learning (ML) architectures has substantially improved the detection of extreme events in healthcare, surpassing the capabilities of conventional analytical methods. For instance, in individuals with type 1 diabetes (T1D), recent AI-based predictive models have demonstrated notable gains in forecasting critical states such as hypoglycemia (blood glucose below 70 mg/dL). Research on hypoglycemic prediction typically uses a combination of blood glucose (BG) readings and heart rate (HR) data to predict hypoglycemic events. Given that these features are collected through wearable sensors, they can have missing values, necessitating efficient imputation methods for improved prediction performance. This work makes significant contributions to the current state-of-the-art by introducing two novel polynomial-based imputation techniques for imputing HR values over short-term horizons: Controlled Weighted Rational Bezier Curves (CRBC) and Controlled PCHIP with Mapped Peak and Valleys of Control Points (CMPV). To evaluate pattern fidelity and error with respect to original values for short-term gaps, we employ RMSE and introduce two complementary pattern capture metrics and propose a combined metric (CM) that integrates them. Across all time gaps, CMPV achieves the best average CM score (0.33), followed by CRBC (0.48), outperforming alternative approaches. We then impute the missing HR values of the D1NAMO dataset to predict short-term hypoglycemia (before 30 min) as a binary classification task, using Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) classification models. Results show that our imputation methods both accurately reconstruct missing heart rate values and improve downstream prediction performance, underscoring their value for real-time hypoglycemia risk assessment.

title: Transformer-UNet Architecture for Document Dewarping and Layout Analysis
authors: Olesia Omelchuk, Saad B. Ahmed
abstract: Document image dewarping remains challenging due to complex distortions and reliance on the synthetic training data. We propose a Transformer-based Encoder with a U-Net–style Decoder that predicts flow maps for document dewarping. To reduce dependence on paired supervision, we also explore an unsupervised cycle-consistency training paradigm and analyze its limitations. Beyond conventional OCR-based and perceptual similarity metrics, we introduce a complementary evaluation perspective by analyzing how dewarping quality affects the downstream document layout analysis task. Supervised experiments on Doc3D training set and evaluations on DIR300 and DocUNet benchmarks show that the proposed architecture achieves competitive performance, reaching a CER of 0.45 and MS-SSIM of 0.54 on DIR300. Our results further reveal a strong dependency between document flatness and layout analysis accuracy, highlighting the importance of robust dewarping for document understanding pipelines.

title: AHFF-Net: Adaptive Hierarchical Feature Fusion via Fractal Recursive Bottlenecks for Fine-Grained Cancer Cell Classification
authors: Muhammad Attique Khan, Muhammad John Abbas, Juan M. Gorriz
abstract: Cancer is one of the leading causes of death worldwide, and its effective treatment requires the accurate histopathological classification, which is time-consuming if done manually. Existing deep learning architectures allocate uniform computational resources regardless of image complexity and fail to adequately integrate multi-scale features, limiting the effectiveness of fine-grained cancer classification models. We propose a novel Advanced Hierarchical Feature Fusion Network (AHFF-Net) that addresses these problems through its fractal recursive blocks, dynamic depth gate, and two-level cross-scale fusion mechanism enhanced with channel attention. A detailed evaluation of AHFF-Net across two cancer datasets demonstrated that it provides significantly improved classification performance compared to previously reported state-of-the-art solutions, while also offering computational performance advantages. Systematic ablation studies validate each component's contribution, supporting the model's potential as a reliable and practical computer-aided diagnostic tool.

title: OGD4All: A Framework for Accessible Interaction with Geospatial Open Government Data Based on Large Language Models
authors: Michael Siebenmann, Javier Argota Sánchez-Vaquerizo, Stefan Arisona, Krystian Samp, Luis Gisler, Dirk Helbing
abstract: We present OGD4All, a transparent, auditable, and reproducible framework based on Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance citizens’ interaction with geospatial Open Government Data (OGD). The system combines semantic data retrieval, agentic reasoning for iterative code generation, and secure sandboxed execution that produces verifiable multimodal outputs. Evaluated on a 199-question benchmark covering both factual and unanswerable questions, across 430 City-of-Zurich datasets and 11 LLMs, OGD4All reaches 98% analytical correctness and 94% recall while reliably rejecting questions unsupported by available data, which minimizes hallucination risks. Statistical robustness tests, as well as expert feedback, show reliability and social relevance. The proposed approach shows how LLMs can provide explainable, multimodal access to public data, advancing trustworthy AI for open governance.

title: Design and Evaluation of a Steerable Polymer-Based Soft Continuum Neuroendoscope
authors: Nidhi Malhotra, Revanth Konda, Jaydev P. Desai
abstract: Continuum robotic tools present a promising alternative to rigid surgical tools for performing minimally invasive surgeries (MIS). Robotic tools developed for MIS are characterized by small outer diameters (ODs). Due to this constraint, most studies utilize metal tubes to realize the body of the robot. While these robotic tools have exhibited acceptable performance, these devices are susceptible to failure due to kinking and breakage if not designed or handled appropriately. Robotic tools fabricated using soft materials, due to their inherent compliance, can exhibit relatively higher deformation without failure. However, fabricating surgical robots with small outer diameters using soft materials can be challenging using existing techniques such as 3D-printing and molding. In this study, we explore the usage of thermoplastic polyurethane tubing with an OD of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<4 ~\text{mm}$</tex> in combination with laser micromachining to fabricate a steerable robotic cannula. The repeatability in joint motion of a tubing sample after laser micromachining with a notch pattern is experimentally evaluated. The tubing is then machined with two bending joints capable of generating bending in opposite directions. Models to estimate the behavior of the robot are derived and experimentally validated. The models exhibited RMSE of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.72^{\circ}, 3.16^{\circ}$</tex>, and 7.11° for the proximal joint, distal joint, and roll joint deflection, respectively. These values corresponded to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.15 \%, 4.77 \%$</tex>, and 4.9 % of the maximum output angles exhibited by the proximal, distal, and roll joints, respectively. Finally, the proposed robotic cannula is demonstrated as a steerable neuroendoscope inside a pediatric brain phantom model.

title: A Fuzzy Based-rule Adaptive Federated Learning for constructing a Trustworthy GPAIS
authors: María Asunción Padilla-Rascón, Ángel Miguel García-Vico, Cristóbal J. Carmona
abstract: The development of General-Purpose Artificial Intelligent Systems (GPAIS) demands adaptive models capable of operating autonomously across diverse environments while maintaining high levels of transparency and reliability. While Evolutionary Fuzzy Systems (EFS) in Federated Learning (FL) provide interpretability, local evolution of linguistic labels often leads to semantic inconsistency, hindering generalization. We propose a semantic synchronization mechanism that establishes a unified linguistic framework via securely aggregated fuzzy set definitions. This approach ensures global coherence and resilience to concept drift without compromising privacy. Experimental results show significant improvements in model interpretability through reduced rule complexity and reliability. This contribution represents a step forward in designing GPAIS architectures that are not only robust and adaptive but also semantically consistent and fully explainable.

title: A phase correlation-based time series InSAR algorithm
authors: Jiahui Wang, Jinwoo Kim, Zhong Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Coherence loss due to changes in surface scattering properties presents a significant challenge for deformation mapping using Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR). A key objective in time-series InSAR processing is identifying ground targets that remain relatively stable over time. Many existing time-series InSAR algorithms rely on the statistical analysis of individual scatterers. In this study, we introduce a novel phase correlation-based InSAR algorithm that exploits the spatial continuity of time-series phase measurements. We develop a statistical model for the wrapped phase spatial difference and identify Spatially Correlated Pixels (SCPs)—pixels that exhibit strong spatial correlation with neighboring pixels, quantified using temporal coherence of their relative wrapped phase histories. Bayesian inference is employed to select these SCPs, which are then processed using an adaptive filter weighted by the estimated temporal coherence with neighboring pixels. We apply the proposed algorithm to C-band Sentinel-1 data over the Greater Houston Area (GHA) in Texas, United States. The results demonstrate the algorithm’s ability to effectively identify coherent scatterers and retrieve deformation signals even in challenging conditions, offering improved spatial coverage and phase quality.

title: Foundation Model-Driven Auto-Annotation: A Multi-Modal Toolbox for General-Purpose Data Labeling*
authors: Nikolaos Ntampakis, George Tziolas, Vlasios Dimitriadis, Christos Vasilakis, Georgios Tsiamitas, Albertos Markakis, Charalampos Kolokouris, Vasileios Argyriou, Panagiotis Sarigiannidis
abstract: The extensive spread of unlabeled data across diverse modalities presents a fundamental bottleneck in developing General-Purpose Artificial Intelligence Systems (GPAIS). We introduce two complementary tools leveraging state-of-the-art foundation models for unsupervised data annotation across visual and temporal domains. Our approach integrates vision transformers (DINOv2, CLIP, SigLIP2) and temporal foundation models (Chronos family) with adaptive clustering algorithms to enable zero-shot semantic organization of unlabeled datasets. The tools exhibit properties relevant to GPAIS preprocessing pipelines: cross-domain adaptability without task-specific training, competence in previously unseen data distributions, and effective learning from limited labeled examples through intelligent representative sampling. We present AutoAnnotate-Vision and AutoAnnotate-TimeSeries, production-ready tools that reduce manual annotation effort by up to 98.8% while maintaining semantic coherence. The modular architecture allows seamless integration of emerging foundation models, positioning these tools as core preprocessing components for GPAIS pipelines. Evaluation across benchmark datasets demonstrates superior clustering quality and significant reduction in cold-start annotation requirements for supervised learning tasks. PyPI links: pypi.org/project/autoannotatevision/ and /pypi.org/project/autoannotate-timeseries/

title: AI-driven Scaling of Design of Experiments to High-Throughput Automation: A Genetic Algorithm for Large Orthogonal Designs
authors: Jade Lejeune Herman, Peter Goos
abstract: High-throughput experimental testing in product development creates new opportunities and challenges for intelligent design and optimization methods. While Design of Experiments (DOE) traditionally maximizes insight from limited tests, today’s robotic platforms enable massive parallel testing. Existing methods struggle to generate experimental designs that preserve essential statistical properties like orthogonality and minimal aliasing without exhaustive enumeration. This paper introduces an AI-driven Genetic Algorithm (GA) framework specifically tailored for constructing large-scale, uniform-precision Orthogonal Minimally Aliased Response Surface (OMARS) designs. These three-level designs ensure that main effects are mutually orthogonal and also orthogonal to both two-factor interactions and quadratic terms, making them ideal for modeling nonlinear relationships. By integrating AI techniques through constraint-aware operators and fitness-guided evolution, the GA efficiently explores vast search spaces to identify high-quality orthogonal designs or near-orthogonal designs. The results demonstrate successful generation of uniform-precision OMARS designs with up to 96 tests and 12 factors. These designs significantly exceed prior constructions in scale, enabling DOE in high-throughput, automated environments and supporting efficient large-scale experimentation in both research and industrial settings.

title: UWarp: A Whole Slide Image Registration Pipeline to Characterize Scanner-Induced Local Domain Shift_supp1-3698161.docx
authors: Alain Lalande

title: Learning Collaborative Reasoning Strategies Through Trust-Weighted Multi-Agent Consensus
authors: Projan Shakya, Kashish Bataju, Kristina Ghimire, Ashwini Mandal, Sadikshya Gyawali, Manish Awale, Manish Dahal, Shital Adhikari, Sanjay Rijal, You Young, Vaghawan Ojha
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong reasoning capabilities yet often exhibit inconsistency and variability in multi-step or high-stakes tasks, limiting their dependability in autonomous problem solving. To mitigate these challenges, we introduce a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework that fosters collaborative reasoning among multiple LLM agents through adaptive strategy selection and reward-guided policy optimization. Within this framework, a Graph Attention Network (GAT) facilitates strategy selection, while a dynamic trust model prioritizes contributions from reliable agents to achieve consensus, promoting both coordination and reasoning diversity. Experimental evaluations on standard mathematical and scientific reasoning benchmarks reveal substantial performance gains, particularly for smaller models, while larger models exhibit consistent yet more moderate boosts. The findings highlight the effectiveness of trust-aware, reinforcement-driven collaboration in enhancing the accuracy, stability, and robustness of LLM-based reasoning systems, making their outputs more deterministic.

title: Mitigating Distributional Shifts in Predicting Conformational Properties of Intrinsically Disordered Proteins
authors: Diego Linares Gonzalez, Shahana Ibrahim, Aniket Bhattacharya, George Atia
abstract: Recent advances in machine learning (ML) are accelerating scientific discovery across various disciplines of life sciences. In proteomics, ML models show great promise for studying intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs)–highly dynamic proteins that lack stable three-dimensional structures, yet play key roles in many biological functions. These models are useful for fast predictions of IDP properties and substantially reducing simulation costs. Nonetheless, ML models often generalize poorly when the training and test distributions differ. In this work, we address this by designing domain-adaptation (DA)–based ML models via adversarial learning for robust IDP conformational property prediction under realistic distribution shifts, e.g., differences in sequence length and net charge. Our approach enables training on IDP sequences that are inexpensive to simulate while transferring reliably to harder regimes, thereby cutting simulation costs. For example, models trained on short-sequence simulations accurately predict properties of long sequences. Experiment results across multiple IDP datasets show substantial gains in generalization to unseen domains, reducing prediction errors by 40–80% in several scenarios.

title: SOCC-ICP: Semantics-Assisted Odometry based on Occupancy Grids and ICP
authors: Johannes Scherer, Sebastian Hirt, Henri Meeß
abstract: Reliable pose estimation in previously unseen environments is a fundamental capability of autonomous systems. Existing LiDAR odometry methods typically employ point-, surfel-, or NDT-based map representations, which are distinct from the semantic occupancy grids commonly used for downstream tasks such as motion planning. We introduce SOCC-ICP, a semanticsassisted odometry framework that jointly performs Semantic OCCupancy grid mapping and LiDAR scan alignment. Each map voxel encodes geometric and semantic statistics, enabling adaptive point-to-point or point-to-plane ICP based on local planarity. Further, the occupancy grid naturally filters dynamic objects through raycasting-based free-space updates. Across diverse evaluation scenarios, SOCC-ICP achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art LiDAR odometry and remains robust in geometrically degenerate environments, even in the absence of semantic cues. When semantic labels are available, integrating them into map construction, downsampling, and correspondence weighting yields further accuracy gains. By unifying odometry and semantic occupancy grid mapping within a single representation, SOCC-ICP eliminates redundant map structures and directly provides a map suitable for downstream robotic applications.

title: Flexible β - Phase PVDF-Cu-MAA Self-Powered Nanofiber Nanogenerator for Energy Harvesting Applications
authors: Sachin, Sumit Choudhary, Satinder K. Sharma
abstract: This work reports the efficient development of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofibers doped with copper methacrylate (Cu-MAA) to form metal-organic clusters (MOCs), demonstrating notable energy generation and harvesting capabilities, and having strong potential for flexible electronics applications. A flexible piezoelectric nanogenerator (PENG) device was fabricated using electrospun PVDF-Cu-MAA nanofibers. These nanofibers form a matrix embedded between two flexible electrodes to fabricate device structures capable of converting mechanical force into electrical energy, and thereby enabling self-powered sensing. The pristine PVDF-based PENG devices and PVDF-Cu-MAA hybrid PENG devices produced a peak-to-peak output voltage of approximately 2.14V and 6.14V under mechanical tapping, respectively. The incorporation of 5 w/v% Cu-MAA significantly improved the piezoelectric response without compromising the flexibility of the polymer mat. These enhancements are primarily attributed to the increased formation of the polar β-phase in the hybrid nanofibers. Furthermore, the PENG devices were integrated with a DHT11 sensor and EPS-32 microcontroller, enabling real-time temperature and humidity sensing. These findings demonstrate the high potential of Cu-MAA-doped PVDF hybrid nanofibers PENG device for use in self-powered energy harvesting systems and sustainable sensing applications.

title: ASTRA-Net: Adaptive Spatiotemporal Track-Before-Detect Recurrent Attention Net for UAV
authors: Yang Gao, Yuguan Hou, Xingpeng Mao
abstract: Stable tracking of maneuvering targets, such as Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), in low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) environments remains a significant challenge. Weak target signals, often obscured by noise, frequently lead to failures in multi-frame energy accumulation, substantial state estimation errors, and degraded tracking performance. To address these limitations, this paper proposes the Adaptive Spatio-temporal Tracking Recurrent Attention Network (ASTRA-Net), an adaptive dual-task learning extension of the Track-Before-Detect (TBD) framework. ASTRA-Net integrates spatiotemporal feature fusion embedding with joint pre-detection and tracking. It captures temporal and spatial dependencies within noisy radar measurements and employs a multi-head self-attention mechanism to dynamically recalibrate feature weights based on varying motion patterns. This framework shares underlying features without stringent prior dynamic assumptions, enhancing overall robustness. It simultaneously learns trajectory prediction and state recognition, discriminates between model states using an information entropy threshold, and adaptively adjusts TBD model parameters during abrupt maneuvers. Experimental results demonstrate that ASTRA-Net excels in low-SNR, noise dominated environments, better than conventional methods in tracking accuracy. This approach offers a novel technical solution for the perception and tracking of low observable targets, particularly suitable for security surveillance and airspace management systems operating in noisy conditions.

title: Sparse-Prior-Assisted Dual-Encoder Multi-Scale Fusion Network for Acoustic Tomography Temperature Reconstruction
authors: Yurun Yang, Lifeng Zhang
abstract: Acoustic tomography (AT) is a non-contact, radiation-free method well suited for measurement of temperature fields in complex industrial environments. To address the ill-posed inverse problem encountered by AT, a temperature reconstruction method that integrates compressed sensing and deep learning is presented to improve reconstruction accuracy and stability. First, the Smoothed lp-over-lq (SPOQ) regularization term is incorporated into the sparse reconstruction model for AT temperature measurement, leading to a compressed sensing–based initial reconstruction approach. On this basis, a Dual-Encoder Multi-Scale Fusion Network (DEMF-Net) is developed. The CNN encoder is used to extract multi-scale features from the TOF measurements. The Transformer encoder captures global dependencies in the initial temperature distribution. The extracted features are then fused for temperature reconstruction. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves an average relative error of 0.1% and a root mean square error of 0.15% for single-peak, double-peak, and four-peak temperature distributions. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves improved reconstruction accuracy and anti-noise performance. The static experiments further verify the practical potential of the proposed method.

title: Active Learning and Explainable AI for Multiobjective Optimization of Spin Coated Polymers
authors: Brendan Young, Brendan Alvey, Andreas Werbrouck, Will Murphy, James M. Keller, Matthias J. Young, Matthew Maschmann
abstract: Spin coating polymer thin films to achieve specific mechanical properties is inherently a complex multi-objective optimization problem. We present a framework that integrates an active Pareto front learning algorithm (ϵ-PAL) with Explainable AI (XAI) techniques to optimize processing parameters for Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) films. The framework uses Gaussian process models to predict conflicting objective values—specifically hardness and elasticity—guiding the adaptive selection of samples toward promising regions of the design space. To interpret the high-dimensional decision boundaries, we utilize Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) for visualization and Fuzzy Linguistic Summaries (FLS) to translate learned relationships into natural language. Experimental application demonstrates that while data sparsity challenges traditional modeling, our XAI components successfully contextualize active learning suggestions. Specifically, the linguistic summaries identify non-intuitive constraints regarding high spin speeds and film thickness early in the optimization loop, facilitating expert validation of the AI-driven experimental design.

title: Development of a Python-Based Load Flow Tool for Power Systems Incorporating STATCOM
authors: Dung Vo Tien, Tomas Gono, Bretislav Stacho
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This article presents the design and development of a load flow analysis software for power systems incorporating a Static Synchronous Compensator (STATCOM), based on the Newton-Raphson algorithm. Implemented in Python, the software provides an open-source, platform independent, and user-friendly alternative to commercial tools. A novel extension of the Jacobian matrix within the Newton-Raphson algorithm is proposed to enable accurate load flow analysis in the presence of STATCOM devices. To evaluate the accuracy of the algorithm and the effectiveness of the software, the 9-bus system and a regional power system are analyzed and the results are compared with those obtained from DIgSILENT PowerFactory simulations. The comparison reveals a maximum deviation of less than 1%, confirming the reliability and practical applicability of the proposed tool in both educational and research settings for power system analysis.

title: A Method for RUL Prediction of Rotating Machinery Integrating Prior Information and Physical Knowledge
authors: Liyao Yu, Guofa Li, Jialong He, Jia Li, Wei Zhang
abstract: Rotating machinery is the backbone of modern industry, where unexpected failures can cause catastrophic downtime and enormous economic losses. Accurate and interpretable remaining useful life (RUL) prediction is therefore crucial, yet the black-box nature of existing models makes this task highly challenging. To address this, we propose an interpretable cloud-edge collaborative prediction framework that integrates physical knowledge. At the edge, vibration data are preprocessed, and an improved particle filter guided by the Damage Discrepancy Accumulation Hypothesis (DDAH) extracts robust degradation features. In the cloud, prior weights are assigned based on perturbation deviation, and a prior-weighted self-attention (PWSA) mechanism is employed to enhance feature representation capability. A fast-slow coupling physics-informed neural network (PINN) is then developed to directly embed physical degradation information into the learning process, endowing the model with high accuracy and inherent interpretability. Experiments on a public dataset and a self-built test rig demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy, while also exhibiting excellent operational efficiency and noise robustness, thereby delivering more accurate and trustworthy RUL prediction. This work offers a principled solution for harmonizing data-driven learning with domain expertise, substantially advancing the transparency and real-world impact of predictive maintenance.

title: Guest Editorial: Advancements in MPC and Learning Algorithms for Legged Robots
authors: Luca Rossini, Enrico Mingo Hoffman, Francesco Ruscelli, Guillaume Bellegarda, Carlos Mastalli, Luis Sentis

title: Human-Guided Online Reward Adaptation for Real-Robot Arm Manipulation
authors: Tianxing Zhou, Haojia Ao, Haoyang Lu, Guangyan Chen, Zichen Zhou, Te Cui, Chao Yu, Yufeng Yue
abstract: Real-world reinforcement learning enables robots to adapt directly to complex physical dynamics, avoiding the reality gap inherent in simulation and rendering previously infeasible manipulation skills a reality. However, the high cost of real-world exploration strictly limits sample availability. Such tight interaction budgets make sparse rewards insufficient for sample-efficient learning in real-robot arm manipulation, creating the need for dense reward supervision. Although pretrained reward models provide useful semantic priors, their generic predictions are often misaligned with embodiment-specific and online dynamics during physical deployment. To address this issue, we propose adaptive reward via human interaction (ARHI), a parameter-efficient framework for online adaptation of VLM-based rewards in real-robot reinforcement learning. Rather than treating the pretrained reward as a fixed module, ARHI continuously recalibrates it using lightweight online updates, while preserving the semantic priors of the frozen backbone. To support this online reward adaptation under sparse human feedback, we further integrate the method into an asynchronous dual-loop system that converts sparse interventions into dense supervisory signals for reward and policy learning. Extensive real-world experiments across six manipulation tasks demonstrate that ARHI outperforms strong baselines, reducing human effort by 28.0%, while achieving a 47.6% reduction in performance drop under challenging deployment shifts.

title: Agentic Narrative Audio Guides: A Hybrid Multi-Agent System for Cultural Audio Guides
authors: Gabriel-Cătălin Nedianu, Mircea-Aurelian Bădoi, Daniel Ciochiu, Ionuţ Murareţu, Costin Bădică
abstract: In this paper, we introduce Agentic Narrative Audio Guides (ANAG), a hybrid multi-agent system used to personalize narratives inside cultural Points of Interest: rooms, paintings, sculptures, monuments and everything that could be an attraction. ANAG grounds LLM generation with expert-curated descriptions and adapts tone and focus using a user profile based on its language, interest tags, and a self-description of the user. We present a coordinated multi-agent system that produces a structured narrative composed of a title, intro, story, and takeaways for immediate client playback. We present a proof-of-concept mobile application with real-world locations and discuss design choices. We discuss ethics, accessibility, and future possible extensions. Our contribution is a deployable, hybrid architecture that illustrates how LLM-enabled MAS can support attraction-level cultural engagement.

title: Neuro-Adaptive Agentic AI Platform (NAAP): A Cognitive-Technological Framework for Cybersecurity and Human Performance Optimization
authors: Andrea Pitrone, Ouns Bouachir, Intissar Haddiya
abstract: This paper presents a Neuro-Adaptive Agentic AI Platform (NAAP) that integrates a Large Language Models (LLMs), agentic AI architecture, neuro-fuzzy inference systems, and principles from habit psychology and cognitive-behavioral science. Designed to address the human factor— responsible for the majority of cybersecurity breaches—NAAP redefines human behavior as an optimizable asset rather than a vulnerability. It continuously analyzes multimodal data—behavioral, linguistic, and physiological—to learn user-specific patterns and stress responses, providing adaptive interventions such as nudges, multimedia coaching, and arousal regulation. Key innovations include the Neuro-Fuzzy Decision Engine (NFDE), a Conflict Resolution agentic orchestration layer, and a habit-adaptive reinforcement module. The paper outlines the NAAP’s architecture, key components, and ethical implications, establishing a foundation for a new class of Cognitive-Cybersecurity Performance Science that bridges AI, behavioral science, and human-centered design.

title: Application of Three-Capacitance Model for Simulation of Partial Discharges Generated at Ripple Voltage Waveform
authors: Yevgeniy Trotsenko, Julia Peretyatko, Olexandr Protsenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: This paper explores the applicability of the threecapacitance model (ABC model) for simulating partial discharges under pulsating DC voltage or DC ripple conditions. Partial discharges (PD) are localized breakdowns within solid insulation material, posing risks of insulation deterioration and system failure. While widely studied in alternating current (AC) systems, the behavior of PD under non-sinusoidal waveforms, particularly pulsating DC, is less understood. Real-world highvoltage systems often involve pulsating DC, such as high-voltage direct current (HVDC) transmission systems and DC-DC converters. This study focuses on the ABC model, initially designed for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{A C}$</tex> conditions, to assess its effectiveness in simulating PD under ripple voltage. The ABC model modifications for non-AC scenarios are discussed. Ripple voltage, common in various applications, exhibits distinct patterns of PD inception and extinction. Time-resolved patterns, including initiation during the rising slope and extinction during the falling slope, align with the voltage waveform. This paper presents real oscillograms of PD under ripple conditions, validating the simulation results. The extended threecapacitance model incorporates leakage resistances and diodes to represent ripple voltage conditions. Circuit simulation, utilizing Micro-Cap software, demonstrates the model's ability to replicate PD behavior under varied ripple voltages. Results suggest the model's correctness in capturing PD initiation and cessation phases, crucial for assessing insulation integrity. This research provides valuable insights into PD behavior under ripple voltage, essential for designing reliable electrical systems. The study serves as a foundation for further investigations into PD modeling under diverse voltage waveforms, contributing to enhanced understanding and improved design practices in highvoltage applications.

title: A Comparative Study of Text-Driven Diffusion Models for Generative Human-Like Motion
authors: Ali Ihsan Ozcetin, Burak Tantay, Kadir Yavuz Kurt, Hakan Temeltas
abstract: Text-conditioned human motion generation enables synthesizing realistic humanoid movements directly from natural language descriptions for applications in animation, virtual agents, and robot motion planning. Although diffusion-based models have recently achieved strong performance in this area, objective comparison across methods is still challenging due to differences in preprocessing, normalization conventions, and evaluation protocols. This paper presents a unified and controlled comparative study for three representative text-to-motion diffusion models: MDM, MotionDiffuse, and ReMoDiffuse, evaluated on the HumanML3D dataset under identical conditions. Alongside standard generative metrics such as FID, Diversity, Multi-Modality, and Multi-Modal Distance, we incorporate two physically motivated indicators, Average Jerk and Foot Sliding, to assess kinematic smoothness and contact stability. In addition to global evaluation, a six-category per-action analysis covering locomotion and basic object interactions is performed, which reveals task-specific behaviors that remain hidden in aggregate scores. The results provide a consistent comparison of current text-to-motion diffusion models and offer insights into their realism, diversity, semantic alignment, and physical plausibility. This benchmarking framework establishes a reliable reference point for future research in text-driven human motion generation and humanoid control.

title: Stability Analysis of Interconnected Periodic Systems
authors: Valentin Spiezia, Jamal Daafouz, Pierre Riedinger
abstract: This paper studies the stability of interconnected linear systems with periodic dynamics. While comparison-based Lyapunov methods provide tractable conditions for time-invariant systems, extending them to periodic settings is challenging due to harmonic interactions induced by time-varying coefficients. We develop a harmonic-domain comparison framework that represents periodic subsystems in an equivalent infinite-dimensional form while yielding finite-dimensional stability conditions based on comparison matrices. Classical norm-based interconnection bounds are shown to be conservative since they ignore spectral separation between subsystem energies and coupling dynamics. To address this issue, we introduce a frequency-weighted comparison framework that yields refined interconnection gains and less conservative stability conditions. The resulting criteria are expressed as nonsingular M-matrix conditions and computed via an LMI-based synthesis formulation. A numerical example illustrates the effectiveness of the approach.

title: Communication-Pipelined Split Federated Learning for Foundation Model Fine-Tuning in UAV Networks
authors: Zizhen Zhou, Ying-Chang Liang, Yanyu Cheng, Wei Yang Bryan Lim
abstract: Deploying foundation models (FMs) on uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) promises broad “low-altitude economy” applications. Split federated learning (SFL)-based fine-tuning leverages distributed data while keeping raw data local and reduces client-side burden by partitioning the model between client and server. However, the per-round training latency is dominated by stragglers. Training paradigms featuring parallel gradient transmission (GT) allocate dedicated portions of downlink communication resources to each client. They may leave resources idle and suffer from prolonged GT latency, especially in UAV networks, where the communication latency typically far exceeds the computation latency. To address this, we propose a sequential GT paradigm, where the server dedicates all downlink resources for the current GT. We further propose communication-pipelined SFL (CPSFL), characterized by downlink GT priority scheduling and intra-round asynchronous training. We investigate CPSFL-based LoRA fine-tuning of FMs in UAV networks and formulate an optimization problem to minimize a weighted sum of per-round training latency and worst-case client energy consumption by optimizing the split point selection (SPS) and the computing and communication resource allocation (CCRA) (the uplink bandwidth allocation and the server computing frequency allocation). To solve this problem, we develop an attention-based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework, where the base station agent decides the split point and the CCRA in each round by leveraging previous round information, including UAV trajectories. Simulation results show that the proposed DRL-based CPSFL scheme outperforms the parallel GT baselines, the ablation variants, and the heuristic CCRA scheme while approaching the best fixed-SPS scheme.

title: Urine-Based Spectroscopy/AI Platform for Early Detection of Multiple Cancers
authors: Leslie Mertz
abstract: A new test uses artificial intelligence to identify cancer-indicative patterns of volatile organic compounds in urine. The test received U.S. FDA breakthrough device designation for early bladder cancer detection. The test is also designed for the early detection of other cancers including colorectal, stomach, pancreatic, prostate, kidney, bladder, breast, ovarian, cervical, and lung cancers.

title: The Economic Efficiency of Algorithms for Forced Limitation of Electricity Consumption in Crisis Situations
authors: Nataliia Volosnikova, Vitaliy Serzhanov, Fedir Abramov, Berislav Andrlić, Vladimir Ristanović, Alexei Pavlichev
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article compares the economic efficiency of various algorithms for forced limitation of electricity consumption in crisis situations and the prerequisites for their application. The main algorithms for forced limitation of electricity consumption in crisis situations, which can be implemented thanks to the wide use of advanced metering infrastructure, are highlighted. It has been proven that the deployment of advanced metering infrastructure is a necessary, but not sufficient, condition for achieving economic efficiency in the distribution of electricity in crisis situations. The final level of efficiency in the distribution of electricity is determined by the choice of the algorithm for the forced limitation of electricity consumption in crisis situations. It is shown that the highest level of efficiency in the distribution of electricity during a crisis situation can be achieved by using the crisis price algorithm and the extended crisis price algorithm.

title: Performance Evaluation of a Dual-Electrode Electro-Aero-Dynamic Propulsion System versus Conventional Propellers Under Flight-Representative Atmospheric Conditions
authors: Yuhaendan Bhaskaran, Abhay Polavajram
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: Commercial aviation accounts for approximately 2.5% of global carbon emissions, with current propulsion systems relying heavily on unsustainable fuels. This study examines the performance of electro-aerodynamic (EAD) thrusters, a potentially zero-emission propulsion system, under varying atmospheric pressures in comparison to conventional propeller systems, aiming to identify the optimal operating conditions of EAD technology within Earth's atmosphere. We used a propeller system as the representative “conventional propulsor” due to its usage in Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). An EAD thruster requires a sharp crown-shaped cathode and a smooth ring-shaped anode, which produces “ionic wind” when passing 25 kV of electricity through the electrodes. The thruster is then placed into a vacuum chamber to replicate low-pressure conditions similar to those experienced by aircraft at high altitudes. The air pressure was manipulated using a vacuum pump during our tests. Data analysis revealed that ionic propulsion is generally more efficient than the conventional propeller system, peaking at 1700 mN of thrust at 75 kPa of pressure. The conventional propellers performed their best at 100 kPa, producing 1308 mN of thrust. However, a limitation with EAD thrusters was their tendency to create arcs of electricity when the pressure dropped too low (around 30 kPa), potentially making them unreliable in high-altitude applications. This experiment demonstrates that in environments where pressure exceeds 30 kPa, EAD thrusters offer a greater magnitude of thrust than conventional propeller systems, making them a more efficient and sustainable alternative to current commercial propulsion systems such as those used in UAVs. This work will contribute to the implementation of EAD thrusters on atmospheric aircraft by advancing the understanding of their performance across various atmospheric conditions.

title: Narrow Linewidth Butterfly Laser with Dual-Zone Thermally Symmetric TEC for FMCW LiDAR
authors: Liang Zhang, Xiaochuan Xia, Haoran Ma, Yang Liu, Hongwei Liang
abstract: This paper designs a dual-zone thermally symmetric thermoelectric control (TEC) architecture to improve the thermal and power stability of narrow-linewidth laser modules for frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) light detection and ranging (LiDAR). Two independently regulated TEC zones with mirrored heat paths are employed to balance heat flow between the laser-diode (LD) and fiber-coupling regions. Finite-element simulations and experiments indicate that the internal temperature difference decreases from approximately 3.5°C to below 2°C. As a result, an output power of up to 67.6 mW at the current 250 mA can be achieved, while the linewidth is 81.5 kHz, the relative intensity noise is less than -140 dB/Hz over 0 Hz–1000 MHz, and the power variation is reduced to ±0.2 dB from −40°C to +85°C. The results demonstrate the effective suppression of stress-induced optical noise and frequency drift via thermal symmetry. The constructed architecture provides a compact, manufacturable, and temperature-resilient solution for high-coherence laser modules in advanced FMCW LiDAR and relevant photonic applications.

title: Responsible AI Rollout: Ethical AI Implementation in Engineering Teams Based on NSPE and IEEE Frameworks
authors: Armando Herrera
abstract: AI is impacting engineering practice in a variety of ways, including accelerating documentation, analysis, and routine decision support. Recent National Society of Professional Engineers (NSPE) rulings and IEEE transparency standards emphasize that licensed engineers retain non-delegable duties of review, disclosure, and accountability whenever AI contributes to engineering deliverables. The presented work translates NSPE's recent guidance and IEEE standards on transparency into a practical, team-level operating rhythm consisting of three core components: a three-pillar framework (Ethics in Practice, Fit-for-Purpose Risk Assessment, Values Alignment), a clear boundary between AI-capable tasks—also known as complicated tasks (AI-supportable)—and those requiring human intervention—also known as complex tasks (human-critical), and a 90-day leader-led pilot plan with lightweight controls (logs, rubrics, disclosures). The proposed approach emphasizes augmentation over automation, aiming for improved efficiency and consistency while preserving human judgment and public trust. By structuring AI adoption around these professional obligations and transparency requirements, engineering teams can move beyond ad hoc tool use toward a repeatable, accountable system that aligns with licensure duties and risk management best practices.

title: An Analytical Computational Model for Bearingless Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors Based on Distributed Magnetic Circuit Method
authors: Jiawang Pan, Hao Jiang, Zhenzhong Su, Dong Wang
abstract: Bearingless permanent magnet synchronous motors (BPMSMs) demonstrate considerable potential for application in high-speed and high-power-density scenarios. However, their electromagnetic characteristics and design methods are comparatively more complex than those of traditional motors. The intricate nonlinear saturation within the motor and the multi-source excitation nature of the magnetic field present substantial challenges to the accurate modeling of BPMSMs. To enable efficient and precise computation of BPMSMs, this paper proposes an analytical model for the electromagnetic performance of BPMSMs that combines the distributed magnetic circuit method (DMCM) with the airgap relative permeance. This approach not only accounts for magnetic field coupling and core material saturation effects via iterative correlation of multiple magnetic circuits but also characterizes the spatiotemporal variation features of multi-source excitation, cogging effects, and asymmetric magnetic field distributions induced by rotor eccentricity through time-step calculations and relative permeability. The effectiveness of the method is finally verified using the finite element method (FEM) and experiments. In comparison with the FEM, the error in calculating the no-load air gap magnetic flux density using this method is within 0.01T. When evaluated against experimental results, the error in calculating the no-load back Electromotive force is within 2.5%, and the error in calculating the radial suspension force is within 5%.

title: An Enhanced Strategy for Wind Turbine Participation in Primary Frequency Regulation
authors: Abdul Haseeb, Eugenio Gubia, Jesus Lopez, Javier Samanes
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This work presents an enhanced deloading control strategy that enables wind turbines to create an active power reserve for primary frequency support. The strategy is based on deloading via combined rotor speed control and pitch control. The rotor speed control-based deloading is dominant when the generator operates below its maximum speed, and the pitch control-based deloading is dominant at the maximum generator speed. In the proposed strategy, power reserves are created by operating the turbine away from the maximum power point using Cp (λ, β) aerodynamic characteristics. The strategy ensures reliable availability of reserves under varying wind conditions and operational constraints. The proposed enhanced strategy is validated on a 5-MW wind turbine model using co-simulation in Simulink and OpenFAST. For validation, realistic Cp (λ, β) characteristics that incorporate the structural flexibility are employed. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach, unlike conventional deloading strategies constrained by generator speed limits, provides a consistent active power reserve capability. This ensures the wind turbine's reliable participation in primary frequency control.
