title: CATS: Cross-Modal Autoencoding for Time Series Summarization
authors: Nika Strem, Nikhila Joshy, Devendra Singh Dhami, Kristian Kersting
abstract: Time series summarizarion is highly relevant in the industrial monitoring tasks: description of characteristic patterns and trends in time series can facilitate data analytics and enable flexible user experience. Yet, due to the scarcity of labeled data, existing data-driven methods have not seen definitive successes so far, while approaches relying on LLMs are impractical in real-world settings due to privacy, cyber-security, and computational constraints, not to mention their big carbon footprint. In this work we ask whether a small model trained on a small dataset can produce accurate, relevant, and readable time series summaries. We propose a lightweight encoder-decoder architecture trained with a novel cross-modal autoencoding method and demonstrate that, despite its size, the model achieves performance comparable to the state-of-the-art LLMs and outperforms existing open-source baselines. Our results suggest that effective time series captioning is feasible under real industrial requirements.

title: Decentralized AI-Powered Multilingual and Multimodal Cross-Meeting Summarizer
authors: Mainak Das, Amlan Mrigam Kashyap, Deepshikha Gupta, Malaya Dutta Borah
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: In this era of virtualization, meetings and collabo- rations are increasingly conducted through virtual collaboration platforms, leading to a surge in the number of online meetings. Despite improved accessibility, documenting meeting summaries still requires manual effort. Existing meeting summarizers are predominantly based on a centralized, unimodal architecture, lacking multimodal cross-meeting summarization, capable of transcribing individual attendee speech, raising concerns about privacy and limitations in context gathering. In this work, we propose a meet summarizer with a decentralized architecture that provides AI-powered multilingual summaries with multi-modal cross-meeting summarization functionality. The system transcribes attendees’ speech on their respective systems with timestamp synchronization, eliminating the need to transmit raw audio to a centralized server and ensuring privacy measures. Merging time-aligned transcripts with our synchronized merging algorithm ensures consensus. Furthermore, a context tree is created that preserves session-level decisions, enabling cross-meeting discourse tracking. Experimental evaluations are conducted on real-world meetings, demonstrating the competitive performance of ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and BERTScore metrics. Furthermore, the system supports summary translation in multiple Indian languages using the NLLB model (transformer-based multilingual model). The proposed architecture ensures context- preserving multilingual meeting summarization for academic or industrial use.

title: Leveraging Artificial Intelligence for the Multi-objective Optimization of Energy Communities
authors: Maria Ruxandra Cojocaru, Daniel Vallejo España, David Criado-Ramón, Luis Gonzaga Baca Ruiz, María Del Carmen Pegalajar Jiménez
abstract: The ongoing research on sustainable energy has given rise to the concept of energy communities—groups of prosumers that produce and consume their own green energy. To strategically design an energy community, resources such as solar panels, batteries, and wind turbines can be distributed among participants to optimize objectives such as the levelized cost of energy, self-sufficiency, and self-consumption, using artificial intelligence. Metaheuristics such as the Multi-Objective Political Optimizer with two phases (MOPO2), Guided Population Archive Whale Optimization Algorithm (GPAWOA), Strength Pareto Evolutionary Algorithm (SPEA), Multi-objective Salp Swarm Algorithm (MSSA), Multi-objective Particle Swarm Optimization (MOPSO) and Multi-objective Equilibrium Optimizer (MOEO) are applied and compared to assess the adequacy of each metaheuristic algorithm for the design of an energy community.

title: BadIQA: Backdoor Attack against No-Reference Image Quality Assessment Models
authors: Yinghao Wu, Liyan Zhang
abstract: No-Reference Image Quality Assessment (NR-IQA) methods aim to predict image quality without any reference information, which is broadly employed for medical image diagnosis, social media content moderation, e-commerce product image assessment and so on. In the past decade, the rapid advancement of deep learning makes Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based NR-IQA methods show superior performance compared to traditional NR-IQA methods based on Natural Scene Statistic (NSS), and become a foundational component for high-level complex applications. However, recent researches have proven that Deep Neural Networks (DNN) are susceptible to backdoor attacks, which can cause model misclassification by providing attacker-chosen specific trigger. Therefore, to safely deploy NR-IQA models in security-critical applications, it is imperative to thoroughly investigate potential vulnerabilities to facilitate further model refinement. In this paper, we make the attempt to explore the backdoor attack against NR-IQA models. However, naïvely transplanting mislabel-based backdoor attacks from classification task to regression task will cause catastrophic degradation in normal regression performance. To break the hurdle, we treat the backdoor injection process as a rank reordering problem and correspondingly propose two special loss functions (KL divergence loss and Rank loss) to jointly optimize the whole regression model, while maintaining its original functionality. Extensive experiments further demonstrate the effectiveness of BadIQA under different datasets, backbones, and trigger settings, as well as its resilience against representative backdoor defense methods.

title: Evaluating the Resilience of Urban Road Networks Against Recurrent Disruptions: A Hysteretic Approach
authors: Leonardo Flores, Jorge Vargas, Gonzalo Panizo, Edwin Salas, Jeremy Ramos
abstract: Performance-based resilience measures arise from the need to measure the dynamic response of transportation systems to extreme disruptive events, where recovery is central. Extending the scope of these measures for recurrent events demands a proper assessment of the disruptive stage, which exhibits a progressive behavior determined by cascading failure. To account for both disruptive and recovery stages, we propose a hysteretic model based on commonplace data from microsimulation models, whose arriving flow can be described by an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic process that addresses the stochastic nature of the performance variable. By relating the deterministic component of the calibrated stochastic process with the solution of a differential equation analogous to a mass-spring system, the resulting hysteresis curve can describe system resilience as a unified process, covering both the disruptive and recovery stages, while its area can describe performance loss. Finally, we propose a new quantitative resilience measure, the normalized hysteretic resilience (NHR), associated to the hysteresis loop. The methodology is demonstrated for the case study of the influence area of a bus rapid transit (BRT) terminal when a critical segment is blocked by a traffic accident. Findings show that the NHR measure is suitable to quantify the nonlinear behavior of traffic flows and complements well with existing resilience measures.

title: MixED: A Mixed Entanglement Distribution Design for Efficient Quantum Teleportation in Quantum Communication Networks
authors: Zhonghui Li, Jian Li, Kaiping Xue, Hyundong Shin
abstract: Entanglement is the crucial resource for teleporting quantum bits (qubits) in quantum communication networks. However, the imperfection of entanglement swapping significantly degrades the rate and fidelity of long-distance entanglement distribution. To alleviate the negative effects of imperfect swap operations, this paper proposes a mixed entanglement distribution design, named MixED, aiming at improving the performance of qubit teleportation. Specifically, MixED mainly consists of two important schemes: the Entanglement Distribution Decision Scheme (EDDS) and the Entanglement Resource Allocation Scheme (ERAS). Based on entanglement distribution capability and the success probability of entanglement swapping, EDDS decides whether the memory-source-memory or memory-memory architecture is adopted to establish short-haul entanglement among three consecutive nodes under the guidance of a mixed strategy. This scheme guarantees the number of short-haul entanglements while effectively reducing the number of swap operations required on the selected path, thereby alleviating the degradation of rate and fidelity during long-distance entanglement distribution. To reduce the waste of entanglement resources caused by imperfect swap operations, ERAS adopts a priority-based strategy to determine the order in which each request is processed and then allocates high-fidelity entanglement resources in a greedy manner. ERAS improves resource utilization and thus enhances network throughput. Extensive simulations demonstrate that MixED outperforms four representative schemes regarding throughput, average fidelity, and the utilization of allocated entanglement resources, thus facilitating efficient qubit teleportation.

title: Unregistered Spectral Image Fusion: Unmixing, Adversarial Learning, and Recoverability
authors: Jiahui Song, Sagar Shrestha, Xiao Fu
abstract: This paper addresses the fusion of a pair of spatially unregistered hyperspectral image (HSI) and multispectral image (MSI) covering roughly overlapping regions. HSIs offer high spectral but low spatial resolution, while MSIs provide the opposite. The goal is to integrate their complementary information to enhance both HSI spatial resolution and MSI spectral resolution. While hyperspectral-multispectral fusion (HMF) has been widely studied, the unregistered setting remains challenging. Many existing methods focus solely on MSI super-resolution, leaving HSI unchanged. Supervised deep learning approaches were proposed for HSI super-resolution, but rely on accurate training data, which is often unavailable. Moreover, theoretical analyses largely address the co-registered case, leaving unregistered HMF poorly understood. In this work, an unsupervised framework is proposed to simultaneously super-resolve both MSI and HSI. The method integrates coupled spectral unmixing for MSI super-resolution with latent-space adversarial learning for HSI superresolution. Theoretical guarantees on the recoverability of the super-resolution MSI and HSI are established under reasonable generative models—providing, to our best knowledge, the first such insights for unregistered HMF. The approach is validated on semi-real and real HSI-MSI pairs across diverse conditions.

title: Ultra-compact 2.5D Integration of RF Interfaces and Opto-Electronic Devices via Silicon Interposer
authors: Yueqi Zhao, Jinsheng Xu, Jia Li, Qian Zhang, Chen Hu, Qingwei Mo, Gang Luo, Chenhui Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: This study presents a compact 2.5D opto-electronic system-in-package solution based on a silicon interposer. This single package integrates opto-electronic chips, electrical chips, and directly soldered radio frequency (RF) interfaces (GPPO connector). This novel approach eliminates RF bond wires and enables direct connection between RF cables and optical chips, leading to a smaller form factor and improved signal integrity. Both single-ended and differential signal transmission lines are co-designed with RF connector and patterned through wafer-level fabrication. The chips and connectors are connected via flip-chip bonding and solder reflow. Optical fiber is aligned through optical vias on the silicon interposer. In the demonstration, clear eye diagrams are achieved for both VCSEL-based optical transmitter and optical receiver. The directly modulated VCSEL exhibits a 106.25 Gb/s PAM4 eye diagram under a PRBS13Q pattern, while the receiver achieves a 25.78125 Gb/s NRZ.

title: Vertical GaN Schottky Barrier Diodes with Polarized P-InGaN/P-GaN Heterojunction Termination Extension Structure
authors: Bin Zhao, Liyang Chai, Weizhe Wang, Ningyang Liu, Lei Li
abstract: In this work, a novel vertical GaN Schottky barrier diode (SBD) with a p-InGaN/p-GaN polarized heterojunction (PolHJ) as junction termination extension (JTE) structure has been proposed and demonstrated. The design concept is based on the PolHJ that polarization charges generated at the p-InGaN/p-GaN heterointerface could enable the extension of the depletion region and therefore optimize the edge electric field distribution at the p-n junction under reverse bias, which would be able to greatly improve the electric field crowding, limited breakdown voltage (BV), and high reverse leakage current commonly observed in GaN SBDs. Experimental results show that superior performance of PolHJ-JTE vertical GaN SBDs, outperforming the referenced GaN SBDs, have been obtained. More specifically, BV of the PolHJ-JTE vertical GaN SBDs has been enhanced by 203 V (133%) and 147 V (70%), respectively, compared with the GaN SBDs without JTE and with a single-layer p-GaN JTE structure, which is consistent with the simulation results. The significantly improved breakdown characteristic of the GaN SBDs is attributed to the outstanding edge electric field suppression by the polarized p-InGaN/p-GaN heterojunction, as evidenced by simulation results. This work provides a new approach for the development of high-performance vertical GaN SBDs, and is also helpful for design of future devices, which can be beneficially exploited from polarized III-nitride heterojunctions.

title: Interpretable Layered Health Assessment for Gas-Insulated Switchgear via Differentiated Thresholding and Dempster–Shafer Fusion
authors: Chunjia Gao, Hao Ge, Peng Zhang, Chengyang Xiong, Huidong Tian, Bo Qi, Chengrong Li
abstract: Gas-insulated switchgear (GIS) condition assessment must cope with heterogeneous components and monitoring variables while remaining interpretable for maintenance decisions. This article proposes an interpretable layered health-assessment framework that couples reliability-aware differentiated thresholding with Dempster–Shafer (D-S) evidence fusion. Historical monitoring records are fitted with a Weibull model to derive reliability-related priors, and differentiated thresholds are estimated by integrating guideline limits, voltage-category subgroup statistics, and sample-size-weighted fusion. A lightweight neural calibrator is used only during offline threshold learning to refine voltage-specific boundaries, while online inference stays rule-based. Four-layer architecture (parameter-performance-component-equipment) maps observations to fuzzy memberships and basic belief assignments (BBA), applies degradation-aware analytic hierarchy process weighting, and performs hierarchical D-S fusion to output four ordered health grades (normal/attention/abnormal/severe) with cross-layer explanations. Experiments on a 220 kV GIS insulator-defect platform (184 tests) achieve 95.65% accuracy. Fleet-scale validation on 621 GIS units achieves 95.27% accuracy, improving accuracy by 8.23% over guideline-based assessment, reducing false positive and false negative rates by 6.30% and 12.00%, and increasing recall by 12.00%. The framework models uncertainty under missing indicators via BBA and D-S fusion and decouples offline learning from online assessment, achieving 2.02 ms per-sample inference on a single-thread central processing unit.

title: Unsupervised Frequency-Guided Network for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
authors: Yan Li, Jun Li, Quan Zhang, Jian Long, Weilong Zhu, Dongzi Wang, Yuanxi Peng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (HMIF) aims to reconstruct high-resolution hyperspectral images by integrating the spectral richness of low-resolution HSIs with the spatial structures of high-resolution MSIs. While supervised methods achieve high fusion accuracy, they are inherently limited by the practical difficulty of acquiring ground-truth labels. Existing unsupervised approaches, though more suitable for real-world scenarios, often yield suboptimal performance due to the neglect of explicit high-frequency texture reconstruction. To address this issue, we propose the Unsupervised Frequency-Guided Network (UFGNet), which eliminates label dependency while effectively restoring fine texture details. Specifically, a Quick Initial Estimation Module (QIEM) is first utilized to perform truncated singular value decomposition for extracting low-rank spectral bases, supplemented by learnable components to enhance feature representation. Subsequently, the Frequency-Guided Module (FGM) leverages Fourier transforms to map features into the frequency domain for multi-dimensional feature enhancement and noise suppression. Furthermore, a Cross-Complementary Refinement Module (CCRM) is introduced to perform cascaded spatial and spectral residual optimization for high-fidelity image fusion. A Frequency Consistency Loss is designed to guide the learning of frequency-domain priors and enforce strict constraints on the spectral-spatial reconstruction process. Experiments on the Pavia University, CAVE, and Botswana datasets at 8× super-resolution demonstrate that UFGNet improves PSNR by 2.122 dB, 3.424 dB, and 1.053 dB over twelve SOTA methods, respectively, validating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Beyond the Clinic Visit: Continuous Bladder Pressure Monitoring After Cystectomy
authors: Nathan Allen-Zimmerer
abstract: Continuous wireless bladder pressure monitoring detects silent postcystectomy risks between clinic visits.

title: Phased Methodology for Optimal PMU Placement in Distribution Networks with Consideration of Diverse Monitoring Requirements
authors: WanMeng Chao, Hao Liu, TianShu Bi, Kenneth Martin
abstract: This paper introduces a phased PMU (Phasor Measurement Unit) placement method that adheres to the post-fault sequence of ‘perception-diagnosis-restoration’. The method enables real-time monitoring of transient and steady-state grid conditions through a phased optimization framework that addresses the objectives of voltage sag monitoring, fault location, and network reconfiguration. For voltage sag monitoring, uncertainties in photovoltaic output and fault information are accounted for, and a probabilistic voltage sag observable area is proposed to ensure observability across different scenarios. In the context of fault location monitoring, a PMU placement model is developed that requires fewer PMUs than traditional methods while still meeting fault location requirements. For steady-state monitoring, a monitoring reliability constraint ensures observability in the reconfigured distribution network. Moreover, this work reveals that the combined use of power flow measurements and zero-injection buses may fail to ensure system observability. To address this limitation, new constraints are established. The proposed method was finally validated using the IEEE 33-bus and 69-bus test systems, demonstrating its effectiveness under both transient and steady-state conditions.

title: Benchmarking Deep Learning Models for Aerial LiDAR Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation under Real Acquisition Conditions: A Case Study in Navarre
authors: Alex Salvatierra, José Antonio Sanz, Christian Gutiérrez, Mikel Galar
abstract: Recent advances in deep learning have significantly improved 3D semantic segmentation, but most models focus on indoor or terrestrial datasets. Their behavior under real aerial acquisition conditions remains insufficiently explored, and although a few studies have addressed similar scenarios, they differ in dataset design, acquisition conditions, and model selection. To address this gap, we conduct an experimental benchmark evaluating several state-of-the-art architectures on a large-scale aerial LiDAR dataset acquired under operational flight conditions in Navarre, Spain, covering heterogeneous urban, rural, and industrial landscapes. This study compares four representative deep learning models, namely KPConv, RandLA-Net, Superpoint Transformer, and Point Transformer V3, across five semantic classes commonly found in airborne surveys, such as ground, vegetation, buildings, and vehicles, highlighting the inherent challenges of class imbalance and geometric variability in aerial data. Results show that all tested models achieve high overall accuracy exceeding 93%, with KPConv attaining the highest mean IoU (78.51%) through consistent performance across classes, particularly on challenging and underrepresented categories. Point Transformer V3 demonstrates superior performance on the underrepresented vehicle class (75.11% IoU), Superpoint Transformer achieves competitive segmentation quality with the fewest parameters, while RandLA-Net offers computational efficiency at the cost of lower overall performance.

title: Evaluating Generalizability of Population-Based and Age-Segmented Models for Hypoglycemia Classification
authors: Beyza Cinar, Maria Maleshkova
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) augments medical care by providing personalized predictions not restricted to fixed rules and parameters. This data-driven improvement is gaining importance for chronic diseases like type 1 diabetes (T1D), where disease progression varies with demographic and genetic factors. Notably, patients with T1D depend on insulin therapy, which can lead to hypoglycemia, a dangerous condition of low blood glucose levels (≤70 mg/dL) associated with increased mortality risk. Hypoglycemia risk can be reduced through AI-based early alarms leveraging data from continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) devices. However, while glucose variability and hypoglycemia occurrence differ across demographics, hypoglycemia classification most often relies on population-based models restricted to specific age ranges. Thus, we classify hypoglycemia 0, 5-15, 20-45, and 50-120 minutes before onset using DiaData, a large CGM dataset of patients with T1D, including children, teenagers, adults, and seniors. In particular, we investigate: 1) the performance of a Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) trained on the whole population and tested separately on subjects across four age groups, 2) the impact of age-segmented models, where the same model architecture is trained separately for the age groups, and 3) the effect of individualization through transfer learning, where the model is fine-tuned with the test subjects’ data. The results show that the FCN model trained on the whole population captures temporal patterns leading to hypoglycemia with similar or superior performance compared to models trained on age-specific subsets, indicating strong cross-demographic generalization. Likewise, age-segmented models generalize well across age groups. However, models specialized on children’s data achieve the highest performance on test sets of children but the lowest on other age groups. Thus, a use case restricted to children should train only on children’s data.

title: Neural effects of meditation following a randomized controlled trial of the Emotion Awareness and Skills Enhancement (EASE)
authors: Busra T. Susam, Nathan T. Riek, Richard T. Gall, Caitlin M. Conner, Susan W. White, Carla A. Mazefsky, Murat Akcakaya
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Mindfulness has promise for enhancing emotion regulation in autistic individuals. Electroencephalography (EEG) emerges as an ideal, objective measure of neural responses before and after mindfulness practice. In a study with 39 autistic individuals, EEG analysis assessed the impact of mindfulness in the participants in a randomized controlled clinical trial of the Emotion Awareness and Skills Enhancement (EASE) program compared to a control group. Participants completed an affective Posner Task while wearing an EEG headset during two visits. In between visits, each participant either received EASE or active control therapy. Using random forest classifiers over the Post-Visit EEG features baseline corrected by Pre-Visit, the study achieved 88.48% to 91.86% accuracy in distinguishing EASE and Control groups under distress, non-distress, and neutral conditions. Linear mixed-effects models applied across the full EEG feature set revealed significant Visit × Feedback × Group interactions in central theta and midline and occipital beta band power, with post-hoc analyses indicating these effects were primarily driven by differential neural responses to rewarding versus unfavorable feedback. The EASE group demonstrated distinct pre-to-post changes in these features relative to the Control group, suggesting intervention-related modulation of neural systems supporting adaptive responses to emotionally meaningful feedback. These findings underscore mindfulness' positive influence on emotion regulation potentially showing its effects as neural oscillations matching the results from existing literature. Subgroup analysis based on Clinical Global Impressions threshold scores identified responders and non-responders with LME analyses confirming greater intervention-related EEG changes in EASE responders compared to Control responders.Our findings provide valuable insights into potential benefits of mindfulness-based interventions for autistic people, highlighting the neurophysiological effects of such programs.

title: Explainable Machine Learning for Photovoltaic Fault Diagnosis: A Comparative Study
authors: Giuseppe D’Aniello, Mario Della Corte, Matteo Gaeta, Giovanni Spagnuolo
abstract: In recent years, photovoltaic (PV) systems have become a key component of the global energy transition. However, PV systems are exposed to different types of faults, such as physical, environmental, and electrical, which reduce energy production and pose safety risks if not properly diagnosed. Machine Learning (ML) models are able to classify faults with high accuracy but often act as black boxes, hindering the ability of the users to interpret the models’ predictions. Explainable AI (XAI) aims to address this issue by providing tools that generate explanations by analyzing the relations between input and output of the trained model. This study optimizes and compares a set of ML models to find the most performing one on a dataset collected from a real PV plant. Among them, LightGBM demonstrated the best overall performance. Then, a quantitative evaluation framework was used to compare two XAI methods: Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) and Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP), which proved to be the most effective in the PV domain.

title: Forecasting the Consumption of Electric Energy When Solving the Problem of Determining the Limit Values of Consumption
authors: Dmytro Yatsenko, Vladimir Popov, Vadym Kondratyuk, Anatolii Zamulko, Yuliia Chernetska
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: In this study, various types and approaches to the formation of restrictions on the supply of electrical energy to consumers are considered. Factors influencing the limitation of electricity supply are considered. The problem of forecasting the consumption of electrical energy in conditions of uncertainty of the initial information, considering the influencing factors, has been solved. It is proposed to use the forecasting results for a more effective solution to the problem of power distribution between different groups of electricity consumers daily.

title: Prosumer as the Smallest Energy Hub and the Prerequisites for its Operation at a Decentralized Power Grid
authors: Andrii Ivakhnov, Oleksandr Kulapin, Stanislav Fedorchuk, Kostiantyn Makhotilo, Dmytro Danylchenko, Olexii Bulhakov
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Modern global energy trends are characterized by a paradigm shift from traditional centralized management toward decentralized architectures. This transition involves incorporating distributed generation and structuring local networks into functional energy hubs, which serves to mitigate the operational challenges imposed by the volatile nature of intermittent renewable energy sources. This study explores the structural layout of energy hubs, focusing specifically on prosumers as the fundamental, granular units within decentralized power systems.

title: Spatial location of inertia
authors: Péter Kádár
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: The inertia breaks the RoCoF. The resiliency of the high renewable penetrated networks is low against disturbances. It happened in Australia in 2016 and maybe the 2025 major blackout in Spain could have different outcome. It is certain that the available inertia of the system could not save the system from collapsing in about 8 seconds. All this drew attention to the importance of inertia and the importance of its quantification. In this article, we will consider the factors that need to be taken into account in addition to the traditional rotary generator inertia.The relevance of all this is given by the fact that e.g. In Hungary, the operating solar panel capacity exceeds the country's consumption, with only one third of the production mix being rotary.In a MATLAB model we have shown that in a distributed system not only the amount, but the location of the inertia is a real issue. The RoCoF damping capacity of the installed inertia depends on its electrical distance from the disturbance site. The best if all the injection points have respectable inertia. So the effect of the different location of the new artificial inertias must be investigated in the future.

title: Retaining Explicit and Implicit Knowledge with RAG-enhanced Generative AI
authors: Sergej Miliaev, Knut Hinkelmann, Barbara Eisenbart
abstract: Organizational knowledge, both explicit in documents and implicit in employees’ minds, is a key source of competitive advantage but is often lost through turnover and inadequate knowledge management. This study demonstrates how Generative AI (GenAI) combined with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) can help retain and reuse such knowledge. Using the Design Science Research methodology, a GenAI system was developed and applied in a case study of the purchasing department of a major European engineering and technology company. The solution uses transcripts from expert debriefing interviews to elicit and categorize implicit knowledge at both surface and deep levels. The AI system interprets and contextualizes expert insights, transforming them into accessible organizational knowledge. The resulting artefact enables efficient retrieval and reuse of codified expertise and is transferable across business contexts. Workshop evaluations confirmed its effectiveness in capturing and applying implicit knowledge, demonstrating that GenAI with RAG offers a practical approach to mitigating knowledge loss and leveraging organizational expertise more effectively.

title: A Three-Port Bidirectional DC–DC Converter with Wide Voltage Conversion and Flexible Power Flow Control for DC Microgrid Applications
authors: Sunil Mandal, Prajof Prabhakaran
abstract: Non-isolated bidirectional DC-DC converters (BDCs) are essential for efficient power flow management in energy storage systems, DC microgrids (DC-MGs), electric vehicles (EVs), and industrial electronics applications. However, most existing topologies are limited by narrow duty cycle ranges, high component counts, increased cost, bulky structures, and elevated voltage stress while achieving the required voltage gain. To address these issues, this paper presents a three-port non-isolated high-gain BDC designed for renewable energy, energy storage, and DC-MG integration. The proposed converter supports multiple power flow paths, including battery-to-DC-MG, photovoltaic (PV)-to-both battery and DC-MG, PV-to-DC-MG, and DC-MG-to-battery. It operates in boost mode during power transfer from the PV source to the battery and DC-MG, and from the battery to the DC-MG, while functioning in buck mode when power flows from the DC-MG to the battery. The topology achieves a wide voltage conversion ratio with low voltage stress across most switches, improving both efficiency and reliability. A 200 W experimental prototype employing SiC MOSFETs was developed and tested. The converter demonstrated stable operation under both open-loop and closed-loop modes, achieving a peak experimental efficiency of 95.8%. Furthermore, it can operate in continuous (CCM) and discontinuous conduction modes (DCM), confirming its flexibility and robustness under varying conditions.

title: Uncertainty-risk-adaptive-based Offline Context-aware Reinforcement Learning Framework for Vehicle-Cloud End-to-End Autonomous Driving
authors: Zhixun Lan, Jiankun Peng, Shen Li, Di Wu, Sichen Yu, Chunye Ma
abstract: Offline reinforcement learning plays a prominent role in reducing training costs in autonomous driving. However, owing to uncontrollable training-data distributions and high sensitivity policy-parameters, its generalization performance across scenarios exhibits large gaps. Considering risk–uncertainty adaptation, an offline context-aware reinforcement-learning vehicle-cloud end-to-end model for autonomous driving is proposed. For the original training set collected from the vehicle-end, a Diffusion-based Generative Experience Replay (DGER) mechanism is constructed in the cloud-end to expand the data. A real-time driving-risk potential field assessment module is built for uncertainty mapping offline reinforcement-learning parameters. Concurrently, a context-aware policy embedded with a Semantic Attention Encoder (SAE) is established, enabling the on-board control unit to switch and adapt across scenarios. Experiments are conducted on the CARLA simulation platform. Relative to baseline algorithms, the proposed method delivers improved stability, safety, efficiency, and fuel-efficiently. The capacity for precise control-action regulation under extreme conditions and the scene-parsing ability across different scenarios are also verified.

title: A Party Blower-Inspired Soft Robotic Device for Post-Stroke Finger Rehabilitation
authors: Eric Olson, Devin Raleigh, Kai Boydon, Yen-Lin Han
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Stroke survivors often develop hand and finger spasticity, limiting independence and quality of life. This paper presents a soft robotic hand-rehabilitation device inspired by party blowers, fabricated from Thermoplastic Polyurethane (TPU) using Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3D printing, and featuring an inflatable chamber that replicates the blower's extensibility. We describe the device's design, optimization via computational simulation, fabrication, and experimental validation through force–resistance testing. When pressed against a calibrated force gauge at multiple angular positions along its curved profile, the device generates up to 19.7 N at 40 psi (275 kPa), corresponding to 94% of the maximum voluntary extension force benchmark (20.9 N) reported for stroke populations. Under graded resistance at clinically relevant angles corresponding to finger joints, it produces 2.7–4.5 N at 100-150 kPa (14.5–22 psi) at 45°, aligned with the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint. These engineering results establish a quantitative foundation for future human-subject evaluation, and its intuitive design, validated force–pressure behavior, and compact form factor offer potential advantages over existing glove-based rehabilitation systems.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)

title: Experimental Studies on Enhancing the Thermal Insulation Performance of Window Systems
authors: Borys Basok, Svitlana Goncharuck, Vitalii Opryshko, Yegor Fareniuk, Denys Derevianko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: This paper presents the findings of laboratory and industrial studies investigating heat losses through window systems in buildings. The primary objective is to provide scientific and technical justification, along with developing methodologies, mechanisms, and measures to mitigate dissipative heat losses through window structures, thereby enhancing their overall energy efficiency, performance, and service life. Experimental evaluations were conducted using heat flux transducers (HFT) and platinum resistance thermometers (PRT) to characterize the thermal performance of various window configurations, including double-glazed, triple-glazed, vacuum insulating glazing, and systems incorporating nanoporous aerogels. Detailed temperature and heat flux measurements were acquired, enabling the determination of heat transfer resistances under steady-state and quasi-steady-state conditions. The study explores the efficacy of different window designs, glazing formulations, and insulating materials in improving thermal insulation capabilities. The results demonstrate that double-glazed and triple-glazed units offer significantly higher heat transfer resistances compared to conventional single-glazed windows, exceeding current building standards. The incorporation of lowemissivity coatings, vacuum insulation, and nanostructured aerogels further enhances thermal performance, albeit with varying degrees of effectiveness. The findings provide valuable insights into the development of energy-efficient window systems, contributing to reduced heat losses and improved thermal comfort in buildings, while aligning with global trends in sustainable energy practices within the construction sector.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing

title: CFCT: A Framework Using ConvNeXt With FPN, CBAM and A Topology Enhanced Loss Function for Skin Lesion Segmentation
authors: Md Shakilur Rahman, Hong Cheng
abstract: In this paper, we present a novel hybrid deep learning framework CFCT for skin lesion segmentation. Our method utilizes a ConvNeXt-based backbone for feature extraction. The framework incorporates a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) decoder, which is further enhanced by Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) and an auxiliary edge detection branch. We integrate multi-scale deep supervision and leverage a composite loss function that combines Dice-BCE, edge-aware, and topological (Betti number-difference) penalties to improve boundary precision and topological correctness of segmentation outputs. We conducted experiments on widely used datasets: HAM 10000 and ISIC 2018. Our results outperform the state-of-the-art methods which prove the reliability of our work. (Abstract)

title: Quantized Medical LLMs for Edge Deployment: A Privacy-Preserving RAG System
authors: Filippo Bianchini, Edoardo Bianchini, Cyril Wyon-Boyault, Eric Folco, Mathilde Proponnet-Guerault, Nicolas Vuillerme, Massimo Mecella
abstract: Cloud-based medical AI systems raise concerns regarding data privacy and regulatory compliance under GDPR and EU AI Act frameworks. We present a privacy-preserving, edge-deployable clinical decision support system achieving 81.20% accuracy on Italian National Medical Specialization Exams (2020-2024) while operating entirely on local hardware. The system combines a T5-base retriever with a 6-bit quantized Meditron3-8B model (8B parameters), requiring no external APIs and processing queries in 0.45s on consumer GPUs. While proprietary models (GPT-4.1, Claude-4.5) achieve 96% accuracy with 1T+ parameters and cloud dependencies, our system demonstrates that optimized open-source models can deliver clinically relevant performance at two orders of magnitude fewer parameters while preserving complete data sovereignty required for GDPR and EU AI Act compliance

title: Sampling-Horizon Neural Operator Predictors for Nonlinear Control under Delayed Inputs
authors: Luke Bhan, Peter Quawas, Miroslav Krstic, Yuanyuan Shi
abstract: Control systems often operate with input delays and sampled measurements. However, practical implementations require solving an implicit ODE online, resulting in intractable computational cost. Moreover, predictor formulations typically assume continuously available state measurements, whereas in practice measurements may be sampled, irregular, or temporarily missing due to hardware faults. In this work, we develop two neural-operator predictor-feedback designs for nonlinear systems with delayed inputs and sampled measurements. In the first design, we introduce a sampling-horizon prediction operator that maps the current measurement and input history to the predicted state trajectory over the next sampling interval. In the second design, the neural operator approximates only the delay-compensating predictor, which is then composed with the closed-loop flow between measurements. The first approach requires uniform sampling but yields residual bounds that scale directly with the operator approximation error. In contrast, the second accommodates non-uniform, but bounded sampling schedules at the cost of amplified approximation error, revealing a practical tradeoff between sampling flexibility and approximation sensitivity for the control engineer. For both schemes, we establish semi-global practical stability with explicit neural operator error-dependent bounds. Numerical experiments on a 6-link nonlinear robotic manipulator demonstrate accurate tracking and substantial computational speedup of 25× over a baseline approach.

title: Federated Behavioral Identification via Grassmann Manifold Aggregation
authors: Shiang Cao, YangQuan Chen
abstract: This paper studies the identification of linear systems using behaviors generated by multiple similar systems. Instead of estimating a parametric model, we adopt a framework rooted in behavioral systems theory, where restricted behaviors correspond to fixed-dimensional linear subspaces represented as points on the Grassmann manifold. Motivated by federated learning, we develop a manifold-based aggregation mechanism that combines information from multiple systems while preserving data privacy. The method aggregates locally identified behavior subspaces via geometric operations on the Grassmann manifold, enabling collaborative system identification without sharing raw data. We show that, under mild assumptions, the aggregated identification is stable in the sense that its error is bounded by a constant multiple of the local identification error. Under stronger conditions, we further establish that the identification accuracy improves as the number of participating systems increases. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed approach accurately reconstructs the system dynamics using data from multiple similar systems.

title: Hybrid Mode Rectifier With Reduced Impact on the Tuning Condition for Efficient Wireless Power Transfer Systems
authors: Xiaoqiang Wang, Jia Mao, Shipeng Qin, Fei Xie, Ping Yang, Jianping Xu
abstract: In wireless power transfer (WPT) systems, active rectifiers (ARs) are widely used for output-power regulation without dual-side communication. Their main drawback is the additional reactance introduced at the receiver side, which perturbs the resonant condition, reduces power-transfer capability, and degrades system efficiency. This article proposes a hybrid mode rectifier (HMR) that combines full-bridge rectifier with voltage-doubler rectifier to regulate the output with substantially less impact on the tuning condition. The regulation characteristics of the HMR are derived for both current-source-mode compensation topologies (CMCTs) and voltage-source-mode compensation topologies (VMCTs). Compared with conventional ARs, the proposed HMR maintains a lower input-impedance phase deviation and lower reactive-power circulation, thereby enabling high power factor and high efficiency. Zero-voltage switching is also achieved, which reduces switching loss. Comparative analysis with conventional ARs is provided, and a concept-of-proof 600 W laboratory prototype is built to validate the proposed method.

title: Vibration-Based Detection of Cable Bending in Cable GIS Terminations via Experimental Measurements and Interface Stress Simulation
authors: Zeyan Zhang, Binlong Cao, Zhidong Jia
abstract: Cable GIS terminations are prone to insulation defects induced by abnormal internal stress and strain, due to their complex internal structure, latent defects, and sensitivity to installation quality. One typical manifestation of such abnormal mechanical states is internal cable bending, whose influence on the vibration response of cable GIS terminations remains insufficiently understood. To address this issue, this study proposes a vibration based method for detecting abnormal stress-strain conditions at insulation interfaces, enabling early identification of hidden insulation defects. First, vibration theory and simulation models are combined to examine how internal cable bending influences interfacial insulation pressure and the corresponding vibration response. Subsequently, a vibration testing platform for 110-kV cable GIS terminations is established to measure and analyze the vibration characteristics under normal and simulated abnormal stress-strain conditions, with the latter induced by cable bending. Finally, the vibration characteristics are evaluated using time domain and frequency-domain indicators. The results demonstrate that internal cable bending leads to non-uniform stress distributions at the insulation interfaces between the bushing, stress cone, and cable. The cable-end vibration, which is typically the most pronounced under normal conditions, is significantly suppressed, accompanied by a noticeable shift of the vibration signal toward the lower-frequency domain and an increase in damping. These findings establish a vibration response characterization framework for cable bending defects and provide a basis for non-intrusive condition assessment of cable GIS terminations.

title: COMETS: Coordinated Multi-Destination Video Transmission with In-Network Rate Adaptation
authors: Yulong Zhang, Ying Cui, Zili Meng, Abhishek Kumar, Dirk Kutscher
abstract: Large-scale video streaming events attract millions of simultaneous viewers, stressing existing delivery infrastructures. Client-driven adaptation reacts slowly to shared congestion, while server-based coordination introduces scalability bottlenecks and single points of failure. We present COMETS, a coordinated multi-destination video transmission framework that leverages information-centric networking principles such as request aggregation and in-network state awareness to enable scalable, fair, and adaptive rate control. COMETS introduces a novel range-interest protocol and distributed in-network decision process that aligns video quality across receiver groups while minimizing redundant transmissions. To achieve this, we develop a lightweight distributed optimization framework that guides per-hop quality adaptation without centralized control. Extensive emulation shows that COMETS consistently improves bandwidth utilization, fairness, and user-perceived quality of experience over DASH, MoQ, and ICN baselines, particularly under high concurrency. The results highlight COMETS as a practical, deployable approach for next-generation scalable video delivery.

title: AG-STDF: Anatomy-Guided ECG Spatiotemporal Dynamical Fusion for Screening Coronary Microvascular Dysfunction
authors: Jiali Li, Qinghua Sun, Lei Wang, Hao Zhang, Jianmin Yang, Chengqiu Zhang, Jiaojiao Pang, Yuguo Chen, Cong Wang
abstract: Objective: Coronary slow flow (CSF), present in 1-7% of coronary angiograms, occurs in patients with angiographically normal epicardial arteries and is associated with acute coronary syndrome, representing an unmet need in coronary microvascular dysfunction (CMD) management. This study proposes an anatomy-guided spatiotemporal dynamical fusion (AG-STDF) framework for CSF screening. Methods: AG-STDF integrates anatomical electrophysiological territory mapping with 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) dynamics. ECG signals are decomposed into coronary territories-left anterior descending (LAD), left circumflex (LCX), and right coronary artery (RCA)—and static morphology is fused with intrinsic dynamics (derived from dynamic modeling) using joint recurrence quantification and multi-scale decomposition. Results: AG-STDF achieves an AUROC of 0.9467 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.0272 for CSF detection. At the vessel level, where therapeutic decisions are made per 2024 European Society of Cardiology guidelines, AG-STDF predicts corrected thrombolysis in myocardial infarction frame counts (CTFC) with an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.5951 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.0666 (AUROC: 0.8428). Notably, AG-STDF performs steadily in right-dominant coronary patients, with favorable results in LAD (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.6753) and mild fluctuations in LCX (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.5673) and RCA (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.5296) due to anatomical variations and competitive perfusion. Anatomical constraints and spatiotemporal fusion improve AUROC by 3.5% (p <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< $</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.01) and 9.47% (p <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< $</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.05). When equipped with an Intel Core i5-10500 and NVIDIA GTX 1660S, AG-STDF processes a 10-second ECG segment in 19.37 s. Conclusion: AG-STDF allows accurate, non-invasive CSF detection and vessel-level assessment in right-dominant populations. Significance: It serves as a cost-effective CMD screening tool that may reduce invasive testing and support early diagnosis.

title: Accelerating Surrogate-Assisted Decomposition-Based Multiobjective Optimization via Effective Assignment of Pre-Collected Solutions
authors: Ryotaro Nakahashi, Ryudai Kato, Yuma Horaguchi, Masaya Nakata
abstract: Many practical applications, such as molecule design, involve computationally and/or financially expensive multiobjective optimization problems, where the number of function evaluations is highly limited. Surrogate-assisted multiobjective evolutionary algorithms (SAMOEAs), particularly modern decomposition-based methods, have demonstrated strong performance in addressing such problems. However, most of these decomposition-based approaches assume the absence of pre-collected samples (i.e., pre-evaluated solutions), even though such samples are often available in practice from previous optimization and/or development results. Consequently, there has been little consideration of how to effectively initialize the population using available pre-collected samples. To fill this gap, this paper proposes a population initialization method for decomposition-based SAMOEAs that effectively incorporates pre-collected samples. The proposed approach solves an assignment problem to select solutions that minimize the total scalarization value across all subproblems, thereby assigning a good solution to each subproblem in a balanced manner. Experimental results show that the proposed method significantly improves the performance of decomposition-based SAMOEAs compared with greedy selection and commonly used environmental selection methods.

title: Reject-Option Ensemble Learning for Neural Network-Based Channel Equalization
authors: Wellington D. Almeida, Ajalmar R. Rocha Neto
abstract: This paper introduces a selective ensemble of Extreme Learning Machines (ELMs) with reject option for nonlinear channel equalization. Traditional neural and metaheuristicbased equalizers often suffer from high computational costs and reduced reliability when the received signal is heavily distorted. Although ELMs offer fast analytical training, conventional ensembles treat all base models equally, allowing uncertain predictions to propagate through the decision process. The proposed method combines the speed and structural diversity of ELMs with a confidence-based rejection mechanism that suppresses low-reliability symbol estimates. Only high-confidence models contribute to the final decision, enabling the ensemble to adapt dynamically to challenging channel regions. Experimental results across four nonlinear channel configurations show that the proposed approach achieves lower bit-error rates than conventional ELMs, nature-inspired metaheuristic equalizers, and heterogeneous neural-network ensembles with reject option. The method also provides substantial computational savings, requiring only a linear training cost relative to the number of ELMs and less effort than gradient-based neural ensembles.

title: Human Recognition–Driven Interaction with Hello Robot Stretch for Supporting Older Adults
authors: Aakriti Upadhyay, Allen Marshall, Rose Gamble
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Supporting older adults in aging safely and independently at home is a key application area for service and assistive robotics. While commercial mobile manipulators provide a promising foundation, their ability to autonomously perceive and locate humans in unconstrained indoor environments, particularly for floor-level detection, remains an underexplored engineering challenge. In this work, we present a unified framework for autonomous monitoring and person-finding using the Hello Robot Stretch 3. We evaluate the robot's autonomous search and perception performance in distinguishing a person lying on the floor from a person who is seated or standing in its frontal view, enabling appropriate initiation of interaction or assistance. Experiments are conducted in a realistic, cluttered indoor environment show <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 0 - 1 0 0 \%}$</tex> success rate with random robot starting positions. The results demonstrate the feasibility of using the Stretch 3 for assistive service tasks such as fall detection and in-home user monitoring, while also revealing perception challenges in cluttered scenes that must be addressed for robust real-world deployment. Our findings provide practical insights into leveraging commercial service robots for assistive aging-in-place applications.

title: DRIFTS: Distributed Robustness for Time Series is Feasible, Distributed, and Fun
authors: Daniel Amadori, Emanuele Chini, Pietro Sala
abstract: As complex machine learning models are increasingly integrated into critical systems, ensuring their robustness, transparency, and trustworthiness becomes essential. Explainability methods that offer formal and verifiable guarantees about model behavior are crucial not only for interpretability but also for robust decision assurance. In this work, we address the problem of generating formally grounded and robustness-aware explanations for classifiers operating in the time series domain. Our main objective is to introduce the concept of a continuous anti reason. We formalize this concept as an Interval Constraint Function (ICF) which, given a classifier and a sample, assigns an interval for each feature value of the sample. An ICF that constitutes a continuous anti reason for a given sample ensures that all data points whose feature values lie within the corresponding intervals are classified differently from the sample class. Each continuous anti reason is associated with a cost, quantifying the extent of perturbation required to induce a class change. The higher the cost of a continuous anti reason, less robust the classifier is on that sample. Therefore such a property is paramount for precisely characterizing the robustness boundaries of the classifier on a sample-wise basis. Given that computing a continuous anti reason is CONP-complete, we develop the framework called Distributed Robustness Interval for Time Series (DRIFTS), a distributed algorithm that leverages an in-memory key-value store to coordinate multiple workers. The effectiveness and scalability of our approach are validated empirically on Random Forest models using univariate time series datasets from the UCR Archive.

title: Experimental Assessment of Multiple Underwater Image Enhancement Techniques
authors: Dharmendra Kumar Mahato, Md. Imtiyaz Anwar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: In this paper, various underwater image enhancement and restoration methods are compared on the basis of results obtained using U-45 image dataset with the help of evaluation metrics. The methods evaluated with comparison of various image restoration techniques, including the Dark Channel Prior (DCP), the blue-green channel dehazing and red channel correction-based approach known as Image Blurriness and Light Absorption (IBLA), a novel optical model (NewOpticalModel, NOM), the Underwater Dark Channel Prior (UDCP), which is a modification of DCP for underwater noisy images, and a method based on the Underwater Light Attenuation Prior (ULAP). In contrast, the image enhancement techniques assessed include histogram equalization (HE), an improved method known as contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), a technique incorporating gamma correction (GC), the unsupervised color correction method (UCM), and methods based on Rayleigh-stretching and dual-intensity image composition, specifically the Rayleigh Distribution (RD). While all of the aforementioned techniques are effective with nearly all kinds of underwater image, UCM and RD produced the best results which are validated by evaluation criteria. To improve the results in the future, we could attempt a combination based on the fusion of two or more methods.

title: Short-Circuit Contributions of Current Controlled Voltage Source Converters
authors: Thomas Hunger, Goetz Lipphardt
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Electrical power grids are more and more penetrated by power electronic converters used in renewable energy sources, storage systems or DC links. The voltage source converter type is widely used in these applications. A fundamental task in planning and operation of electrical power grids is the short-circuit current calculation, which is based on the standard IEC 60909-0:2016 [1]. The short-circuit current calculation has to consider the influence of power electronic converters and the standard provides information accordingly. This paper thoroughly analyzes the short-circuit current contribution of voltage source converters. It gives a detailed description of the behaviour of the converter during grid faults and identifies the parameters which influence the size and time response of the short-circuit current. The results are compared with the short-circuit current calculation according to the standard.

title: Grid-Connected Hybrid Renewable Energy System for Livestock Farm Using Electric Vehicles
authors: Olexandr Shavolkin, Yurii Haman
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The purpose of the work is to improve the control and structure of a grid-connected hybrid renewable energy system for a livestock farm using electric vehicles. A hybrid structure is considered, which includes a photovoltaic system with a battery, a wind turbine, a biogas electric generator, and two electric vehicles. The selection of system parameters and operating scenarios for indoor livestock keeping is justified for specific applications. In off-grid mode, the energy of electric vehicle batteries is used during peak demand. When the grid is connected, batteries are used together with dump loads to regulate the consumption of excess energy. The added power reference is provided by the controller of the control unit. Simulation results confirm the possibility of full energy consumption with average monthly RES generation in March with a decrease of grid power consumption by 4.43 times (20 % improvement). For the average monthly generation of renewable energy sources in December there is a 2.5-fold decrease. The farm can operate in off-grid mode for at least 24 hours.

title: Collection: Datasets Applied to the Development of Automatic Tuberculosis Diagnostic Methods in Brightfield Sputum Smear Microscopy (Tb_UFAM)
authors: Marly G. F. Costa, Mikaela K. M. S. De Andrade, Luciana B. Fujimoto, Mauricio M. Ogusku, Francisco P. Romero, Liset V. Romaguera, Yadini P. López, Fabio C. Binatti, Cícero F. F. Costa Filho
abstract: This paper presents a set of sputum smear microscopy image datasets. These datasets make it possible to carry out various studies and developments aimed at: obtaining autofocus metrics and extended focus images; detecting and segmenting bacilli; developing and evaluating algorithms for automatic brightfield smear microscopy for diagnosing pulmonary tuberculosis. The datasets are comprised of the following image types: digital field images of 1040x1388 pixels, patches of 40x40 pixels, and mosaic images that are composed of 100 patches in a 10x10 array. Two types of ground truth annotation are provided: cartesian coordinates of a pixel belonging to the object (the bacillus), and a binary image where the bacilli pixels are in white color and the background pixels are in black. Four use cases are presented for the validation of the set of images datasets presented: multi-focus fusion Image; bacilli detection using patches, bacilli segmentation and classification using mosaic images, and the automatic brightfield smear microscopy for diagnosing Tb. The volume of data available makes it possible to use the most modern machine learning techniques.
