title: On-Antenna Power Combining Dual-band and Dual-polarized Phased Array Antenna Module for Extreme-Large-Scale Arrays with High EIRP and Minimal Footprint
authors: Bumhyun Kim, Sirous Bahrami, Junho Park, Donggeun An, Pei-yuan Qin, Wonbin Hong
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a dual-band and dual-polarized phased array antenna module that implements on-antenna power combining in an active hardware configuration. The proposed design integrates 14 and 28 GHz operation within a compact element using slot aperture coupling and a dual-feed structure, enabling efficient impedance tuning and stable radiation patterns. A 1×5 phased array prototype with 20 RF ports is implemented using three commercial Tx beamforming ICs. Experimental results demonstrate approximately 3 dB EIRP improvement over conventional single-feed architectures, with 3 dB beam scanning ranges exceeding 74° at Ku band and 70° at Ka band for both polarizations. The module is implemented using an LTCC process with flip-chip integration, and its modular architecture enables scalable implementation toward extreme large-scale array systems.

title: Thermal Runaway Warning for Lithium-Ion Battery in Energy Storage Station Based on Quasi-Energy Consumption
authors: Kaidi Zeng, Chao Li, Bin Li, Peiyu Chen, Jun Wang
abstract: Battery thermal runaway (TR) poses a significant threat to the safety of battery energy storage systems (BESS). To enable timely and reliable TR warning under practical BESS, this article proposes a TR warning method based on quasi-energy consumption. The method calculates quasi-energy consumption from measured voltage and current signals during charging when an abnormal voltage drop occurs. A triggering condition is designed to activate the warning procedure, and an adaptive threshold is established based on the intrinsic electrical characteristics of the battery to ensure correct TR warning and to prevent misdiagnosis under fluctuating BESS operating conditions and signal noise. Overcharge-induced TR experiments on batteries of different capacities and real BESS operating data are utilized to validate the proposed method. Results show that the proposed method can effectively achieve TR warning 458 s in advance without any misdiagnosis, while requiring only 0.2 ms computation per cell, confirming its high reliability, robustness, and suitability for integration into practical BESS applications.

title: Energy-Adaptive WDF Notch Filter for Fast Transient Suppression and Narrowband Interference Rejection
authors: Abhay Sharma, Md. Irfanul Hasan, Mridul Gupta, Satyam, Vijay Mohan, Bharti Panjwani
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Diagnostic inaccuracies in biomedical systems are often caused by power-line interference, which significantly decreases the accuracy of electrocardiogram (ECG) data. While traditional IIR notch filters work well for steady-state noise reduction, they distort important ECG components like QRS complexes due to their large transient responses. Further, the traditional IIR filter structures are sensitive to coefficient quantization. In this work, a low coefficient sensitive wave digital structure based notch filter is presented with rapid transient suppression. The undesired transient duration can be reduced by taking pole radius of notch filter close to unity but this will increase the notch bandwidth resulting in suppression of other frequency components. This work proposes an adaptive Wave Digital Filter-based Notch Filter using an energy-gated pole radius scheduling technique to address this constraint. The approach changes the pole radius in real time based on the filter’s output energy at that moment. To quickly suppress transients, a smaller pole radius is employed at first. As the signal energy diminishes, the pole radius is slowly raised to provide a narrow steady-state notch. When compared to fixed-radius notch filters, the proposed filter greatly improves denoising performance in terms of mean square error and signal-to-noise ratio. This work directly supports United Nations Sustainable Development Goal 3 promoting Good Health and Well-Being by enhancing signal reliability of biomedical devices.

title:
Fabrication of Piezoresistive Sensor using PVA/Sugarcane/Fe
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    blend
authors: Priya Khanduri, Varij Panwar, Aarush Goyal
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The development of sustainable and high-performance flexible strain sensors remains a critical challenge for next-generation wearable and structural health monitoring applications. In this study, a biodegradable piezo-resistive membrane was engineered by integrating magnetite (Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>) nanoparticles into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/sugarcane juice bio polymeric matrix. The impact of conductive filler concentration on dielectric, electrical, and electromechanical properties was examined in detail using three compositions with different Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> loadings. Impedance spectroscopy revealed enhanced dielectric permittivity and reduced impedance with increasing Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> content, indicating improved charge transport pathways. Stable and reproducible piezo-resistive performance was validated by time-dependent resistance measurements conducted under mechanical bending. However, due to the optimal percolation network of the polymer matrix, the membrane containing 5 g Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> exhibited the best sensitivity and the largest decrease in relative resistance. Even though greater filler loading improved conductivity, partial agglomeration effects reduced strain sensitivity. The computed gauge factor showed that tunneling resistance modulation and conductive channel reconfiguration under strain control electrical responsiveness. A highly sensitive, adaptable, and biocompatible sensing platform was developed as a result of the combined action of magnetic Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> nanoparticles, hydroxyl-rich PVA, and naturally occurring ionic species in sugarcane juice. The potential of bio-hybrid composites as environmentally friendly substitutes for flexible strain sensing and smart materials is highlighted by this work.

title: Towards Robot-Assisted MRI-Guided Lumbar Injections
authors: Tyler Lehrfeld, Qinhan Wang, Aabhas Jain, Vivek Chari, Iulian Iordachita
abstract: Lumbar epidural injection requires precise needle placement to ensure efficient drug delivery into the epidural space. MRI-compatible robotic systems offer unique advantages for this procedure by combining the advantages of intraoperative MRI guidance with the precision and dexterity of robotic assistance. This paper presents a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4}$</tex>-degree-of-freedom (DOF) MRI-compatible robotic system designed to assist surgeons in performing lumbar injections with improved accuracy and consistency. The proposed system features a modular architecture comprising an actuation unit, a two-layer linkage mechanism, and a needle placer. The kinematics of the system were derived, and a control framework incorporating backlash compensation was implemented. A workspace analysis was conducted, with the effective workspace found to be <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 72.9 ~\text{mm}$</tex> (medial-lateral) and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 32.5 ~\text{mm}$</tex> (superior-inferior), and a tilting range exceeding 25°. As a proof of concept, the prototype was experimentally evaluated to validate its mechanical performance. The results demonstrated sub-millimeter precision with an average deviation from a mean location of 0.33 mm, confirming the feasibility of accurate and repeatable needle guidance, and marking a step toward clinical translation of robot-assisted MRI-guided lumbar injection procedures.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing

title: Adaptive Control for Vehicle Platoons With Uncertain Input Delays: An Observer-Based Estimation Approach
authors: Juan Liu, Hao Zhang, Zhuping Wang, Huaicheng Yan
abstract: This article investigates a connected vehicle platoon with uncertain input delays in the absence of communication between vehicles. The primary control objective is to stabilize the platoon while estimating these uncertain input delays, ensuring that all vehicles maintain the same speed and a safe following distance. An observer is introduced to estimate the velocity and acceleration of the leading vehicle based on position information obtained from onboard sensors. In addition, an adaptive switching logic algorithm is designed to estimate the uncertain input delays for all vehicles. An improved decentralized controller is also proposed to enhance the control of the connected vehicle platoon. The stability analysis demonstrates the potential for achieving string stability within the system. Simulation results further validate the effectiveness of the proposed observer and controller.

title: Global Transient Stability Control for Grid-Following Inverter Based on Elimination of Pendulum-like Periodic Trajectory
authors: Yumei Ma, Shiying Zhou, Donghai Zhu, Xudong Zou, Yong Kang
abstract: From the perspective of pendulum-like system, this letter analyzes the transient instability mechanism of grid following (GFL) inverter. Based on it, a global transient stability control is proposed, by eliminating the second category periodic trajectory of pendulum-like system. Compared with previous methods, the proposed control can achieve transient stability during extreme and severe weak grid faults.

title: Impact of Radar-Derived Occupant Location on Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring Accuracy Using Deep Neural Networks
authors: Lukas Demel, Josef Fiala, Roman Hrbac, Vaclav Kolar
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Traditional non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM) systems are intentionally limited to only active power, which is obtainable from electricity meters. With the rise of smart homes, additional and more detailed data about the household are available, which can be used to improve the accuracy of NILM. This paper quantifies the benefit of radar-derived occupant location as contextual information for deep neural NILM. Using a self-recorded dataset with synchronized electrical measurements and radar-based occupancy, we trained deep neural classifiers under four input configurations: (i) active power only, (ii) active power plus radar-based occupancy, (iii) rich electrical features from a power analyzer, and (iv) analyzer features plus occupancy. For each configuration, 19 525 independently trained networks were evaluated to estimate the radar performance impact. When electrical input was limited to active power, adding radar consistently improved median macro-F1 across all appliance sets, from 0.727 to 0.832 for the 4-load set, from 0.771 to 0.882 for the 5-load set, and from 0.697 to 0.843 for the 12-load set. When analyzer features were available, the benefit of radar was smaller: median macro-F1 improved from 0.936 to 0.957 for 4 loads and from 0.843 to 0.884 for 12 loads, while a slight decrease was observed for 5 loads (0.968 to 0.960). The best overall performance was achieved by the combined analyzer and radar configuration. The results show that radar-based occupancy context can substantially improve NILM, particularly when only simple power measurements are available, and that active power plus radar offers an attractive cost-performance trade-off for practical deployments.

title: Impact of Data Filtering on Correlation Analysis Between PV Energy Production and Meteorological Variables
authors: Daria Wotzka, Paweł Kurtasz, Jarosław Mękal, Oskar Zmarzły
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This paper investigates the influence of data preparation and filtering procedures on correlation analysis between photovoltaic (PV) energy production and selected meteorological variables. The study is based on measurement data from two real-world PV installations and focuses on the preliminary analysis stage preceding the development of forecasting models. Daily Spearman rank correlation coefficients between energy active production (EAP) and multiple irradiance-related variables are computed under three data preparation variants: unfiltered data, data restricted to daylight hours and installation availability, and data additionally filtered by positive energy production. The temporal variability and stability of correlation values are analyzed using daily and weekly representations. To assess the comparability of relationships observed in both installations, daily correlation coefficients are paired by date and statistically evaluated using nonparametric tests with false discovery rate correction. The results demonstrate that correlation values strongly depend on the adopted data filtering strategy. Inadequate preprocessing leads to increased variability, sign changes, and spurious statistical significance, while successive filtering steps yield more stable and physically interpretable relationships. The findings highlight the critical role of data preparation in correlation-based analyses of PV generation and provide a methodological foundation for subsequent predictive modeling.

title: PDF: Prompt-Guided Decoupled Feature Learning for Cloth-Changing Person Re-Identification
authors: Hao Chen, Gang Shen, Xuefei Lv, Ziwei Liu, Limin Su, Yunhai Tong, Shiliang Zhang
abstract: Cloth-changing person re-identification (CC-ReID) aims to extract cloth-irrelevant visual features to match individuals across different outfits. While images contain rich visual cues, textual descriptions often provide explicit and structured attribute information, offering a potential pathway to decouple and suppress cloth-relevant features in visual representations. In this paper, we propose a novel prompt-guided decoupled feature learning framework that leverages text prompts to guide the learning of cloth-irrelevant features for CC-ReID. Given that human-annotated descriptions are typically unavailable in CC-ReID datasets, we explore the use of prompts generated by multimodal large language models (MLLMs) as a substitute. To extract cloth-relevant features, we design a prompt-guided visual feature extractor that learns attribute-aware features aligned with the prompts. A decoupled feature learning module is then employed to suppress the cloth-relevant components from the raw visual features. Through this prompt-guided learning strategy, our framework is able to construct cloth-irrelevant representations. Extensive experiments on multiple CC-ReID benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches, validating its effectiveness and robustness.

title: Modeling Safe Variability Areas of Power for NPP Power Unit Models
authors: Sergii Saukh, Oleg Godun
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The inadequacy of presenting nuclear power plant (NPP) with predetermined load schedules is demonstrated, particularly in tasks related to planning the development of electric power systems with significant shares of electricity production from renewable energy sources. The conditions for the safe operation of NPP under load fluctuations are analyzed, focusing on preventing the exceedance of permissible design limits. A mathematical description of the region of safe power maneuvering for NPP is introduced, ensuring their adequate representation in electric power system development planning tasks. The derived mathematical model for the safe maneuvering area of the nuclear reactor's thermal power, along with the proposed approximation, is designed for integration into in unit commitment and economic dispatch models.

title: Some Features of Linear Autoregressive Processes Kernel Application to Diagnostic Problems of Power Equipment
authors: Valerij Zvaritch, Yuriy Gyzhko, Olena Glazkova, Bogdan Samkov, Maryna Gutorova
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The paper considers some methods of diagnosing the technical condition of rotating units of power equipment. It is proposed to use linear of autoregressive processes (AR) as mathematical models of vibrations of power equipment units. Such processes belong to linear random processes with discrete time, which have infinitely divisible distribution laws. The peculiarities of such processes are that the autoregressive coefficients are directly related to the kernel of linear random processes with discrete time. This makes it possible to construct recurrent algorithms for estimating the kernels of linear random processes with discrete time. As an example of the use of the proposed approach, the vibration signal of the rotating unit of the rolling bearing of Power equipment is considered. For the study of vibration signals, a prototype of the power equipment diagnostic system developed at the Institute of electrodynamics of the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine was used. Vibration signals were registered and estimates of the kernels of linear random processes were obtained. Some parameters of the kernels of linear AR processes are shown, which can be used as diagnostic parameters of the technical condition of the units of power equipment.

title: Measurements of the Corona Discharge Field of an Extra-High Voltage Transmission Line
authors: Vladislav Kuchanskyy, Ievgen Zaitsev
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The paper presented study dealing with corona discharge field of an ultra-high voltage transmission line. Methods determination of corona power losses are highlighted in the research. Proposed method for defining of corona power losses on transmission power line wires has been presented, which, unlike the existing ones, allows measuring corona power losses directly.

title: Towards Secure Smart Grid Infrastructure in Saudi Arabia: A Review and Perspective of AI-Powered False Data Injection Attack Detection Methods.
authors: Abdullah Alsaedi, Ghanim Alofi, Juhar Abdulla, Zahir Tari
abstract: The rapid adoption of smart grid technologies in Saudi Arabia promises significant improvements in efficiency and reliability of the power grid, and renewable integration. However, the interconnected nature of smart grids makes them vulnerable to cyberattacks, particularly False Data Injection (FDI) attacks that can manipulate critical grid data. This paper presents a focused review and perspective on Artificial Intelligence (AI)-powered FDI attack detection methods tailored to the unique challenges of Saudi Arabia’s smart grid infrastructure. We examine state-of-the-art supervised machine learning methods, demonstrating their effectiveness in detecting and mitigating FDI attacks. Furthermore, we provide insights into the development of adaptive AI-driven cybersecurity solutions that can learn and evolve in response to emerging threats, ensuring the long-term security and resilience of the region’s smart grids. We also highlight potential areas for future research, emphasizing the importance of collaboration to ensure the security and resilience of Saudi Arabia’s smart grid.

title: EVmem: Air-Gap Covert Channel Using Electromagnetic Virtual Memory Allocation and Deallocation
authors: Hyeonjun An, Dong-Guk Han
abstract: A covert channel enables a sender and receiver to secretly exchange data using a pre-shared and concealed communication method, but such channels can also be abused to exfiltrate sensitive information from secured systems. Existing electromagnetic (EM) covert channels primarily rely on direct Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) access using non-temporal memory instructions, which generate distinctive and easily detectable memory-access patterns. To address this limitation, we propose a novel air-gapped covert channel EVmem that induces EM emissions through indirect DRAM activation triggered by virtual memory allocation and deallocation. Because this mechanism operates by updating page table entries rather than performing explicit memory writes, it is less exposed to instruction-level monitors targeting non-temporal access patterns, although OS-level behavioral footprints such as elevated page-fault activity may still remain observable. To match the temporal characteristics of virtual memory operations, we introduce a time-gap modulation technique. We also analyze the EM signatures resulting from these operations and design a preprocessing pipeline that substantially improves signal clarity. Experimental evaluation on a Windows-based system confirms that the proposed technique can successfully establish a practical air-gapped EM covert channel. Notably, our preprocessing increases the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) from 1.24 to 7.34, enabling stable demodulation under low-SNR conditions. Finally, we demonstrate that the channel can be reliably demodulated with a consistently low bit error rate (BER) even in a full operating-system environment, confirming that the proposed method forms a viable and covert communication channel in realistic system conditions.

title: A High-Performance Modular Linear Actuator With Enhanced Compensation of Cogging Torque and Friction_supp2-3687954.pdf
authors: Fenglei Ni
abstract: This article presents a compact linear actuator featuring a high force-to-weight ratio enabled by integrating all the drive, transmission, and sensing control modules. A four-guide-rod design enhances bending and torsional stiffness with low mass. An iterative method collects and compensates cogging torque of the linear actuator, ensuring reduced speed fluctuations, improved motion stability, and rapid convergence. Position-dependent friction is addressed by a hybrid Stribeck-neural network strategy, which captures residual effects to enhance low-speed smoothness and tracking accuracy. The integrated framework combines model-based and data-driven strengths to deliver precise, robust, and high-performance linear actuation and effectively mitigate dynamic disturbances.

title: GenIA: Generative Index Advisor for Dynamic Workloads and Data
authors: Xian Lyu, Chen Lin, Yihang Zheng, Zhifeng Bao, Yiming Zhang, Guoliang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: An ideal index advisor needs to effectively manage changes in workload and data, but current approaches fall short in both effectiveness and efficiency because of intrinsic limitations in their frameworks. Heuristic-based methods struggle with efficiency due to their rigid algorithms and lack of adaptive learning capabilities. Reinforcement learning techniques often fail to consistently reach an optimal policy. Classification methods require vast amounts of labeled workloads that include optimal indexes. Additionally, none of the learning-based strategies are equipped to handle shifts in data. To overcome these limitations, this paper presents a new index advisor for dynamic workloads and data, GenIA, which learns to generate a sequence of the recommended index configuration based on historical experience. The generative framework of GenIA avoids erroneous trials to explore bad actions and reliance on high-quality positive and negative examples. Specifically, its novelty exhibits in three aspects. (1) GenIA is empowered with novel attention mechanisms to capture implicit relationships between indexable columns. (2) GenIA combines comprehensive features extracted from workloads, data manipulation statements, and underlying data to effectively capture workload shifts and subtle data shifts. (3) GenIA adopts a novel perturbation-based training strategy to enhance the diversity of training samples and to improve the model parameters' robustness. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks under varying levels of workload and data shifts demonstrate that GenIA outperforms SOTA heuristic-based IA Extend on average by about 7.5%, while utilizing less than 1% of the inference time, and surpasses SOTA learning-based IA SWIRL by 25% − 30% in scenarios with significant workload and data shifts.

title: Modelling Children’s Language: Vikidia-based Resources to Support Italian Text Simplification
authors: Andrea Tirico, Marco Braga, Emiliana Murgia, Gabriella Pasi
abstract: With the rapid advancement of Large Language Models, their role in educational contexts has gained increasing attention due to the text generation capabilities and their emerging value as tools for supporting students and teachers. One relevant application is the task of Text Simplification (TS), which aims to reduce text complexity to enhance readability and accessibility for users with limited linguistic abilities, such as children. Many approaches to Text Simplification focus on the English language with children as target user group, while they are limited for the Italian language. In this paper, to fill this gap, we introduce new resources designed for children aged 8 to 11, which will support the definition of the TS approach. We build our dataset by collecting texts from both the Italian Wikipedia and Vikidia, which is an online encyclopedia written for a young audience. Subsequently, we fine-tune a pre-trained language model on our proposed resource to generate simplified versions of Wikipedia pages that align with Vikidia’s linguistic style. The simplification process is further guided by a vocabulary built from Vikidia. This post-processing step enables the replacement of potentially complex terms in the output text with synonyms in the vocabulary that are accessible to children. The effectiveness of the system was evaluated through a qualitative human evaluation conducted by elementary school teachers, showing general positive assessments, alongside some divergent views and suggestions for refinement before the introduction of the educational context. We release code, prompts and the proposed dataset at https://github.com/marostiri/italian-children-text-simplification.

title: A Language-Driven Framework for Improving Personalized Recommendations: Merging LLMs with Traditional Algorithms
authors: Aaron Goldstein, Ayan Dutta
abstract: Traditional recommendation algorithms are not designed to provide personalized recommendations based on user preferences provided through text, e.g., "I enjoy lighthearted comedies with a lot of humor". Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as one of the most promising tools for natural language processing in recent years. This research proposes a novel framework that mimics how a close friend would recommend items based on their knowledge of an individual’s tastes. We leverage LLMs to enhance movie recommendation systems by refining traditional algorithm outputs and integrating them with language-based user preference inputs. We employ Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) or SVD<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">++</inf> algorithms to generate initial movie recommendations, implemented using the Surprise Python library and trained on the MovieLens-Latest-Small dataset. We compare the performance of the base algorithms with our LLM-enhanced versions using leave-one-out validation hit rates and cumulative hit rates. Additionally, to compare the performance of our framework against the current state-of-the-art recommendation systems, we use rating and ranking metrics with item-based stratified 0.75 train, 0.25 test split. Our framework can generate preference profiles automatically based on users’ favorite movies or allow manual preference specification for more personalized results. Using an automated approach, our framework overwhelmingly surpassed SVD and SVD<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">++</inf> on every evaluation metric used (e.g., improvements of up to ∼ 6x in cumulative hit rate, ∼ 3.7x in NDCG, etc.), albeit at the cost of a slight increase in computational overhead.

title: Comparative Characteristics of Aviation Fuels Properties of Domestic and Foreign Production According to ASTM and NATO Standards
authors: Sergii Boichenko, Iryna Shkilniuk, Oleksandr Bavykin
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The operational efficiency, reliability, technical service life, and durability of aviation equipment are directly and proportionally determined by the rational selection and utilisation of aviation fuels and associated operating materials. Aviation fuel quality must be precisely matched to the operational conditions and technical requirements of the equipment in service. The use of substandard fuels results in degradation of reliability and reduction of service life, while the use of fuels exceeding the quality level prescribed for a given aircraft system leads to unjustified economic expenditure without a corresponding operational benefit. The reliability, technical resource, and durability of both civil and military aviation equipment can be assured through a high level of chemical reliability — defined as the capacity of aviation equipment to sustain required operational parameters through the consistent application of aviation fuels of a technically and economically justified quality grade. The present study conducts a comparative analysis of the physicochemical properties of aviation fuels of domestic and foreign production in accordance with the requirements of current ASTM and NATO standardisation documents, with the aim of identifying compliance gaps and informing rational fuel selection for aviation equipment operated under Ukrainian conditions.

title: Graph-Based Early Warning from Therapy Narratives: A System-of-Systems Copilot for Behavioral Health
authors: Rawan AlMakinah, M. Abdullah Canbaz
abstract: Behavioral health clinicians face persistent challenges in tracking patients’ trajectories across sessions while managing the cognitive demands of documentation and electronic health records (EHRs). Subtle early warning signs of deterioration are easy to miss, yet timely detection can prevent serious outcomes such as hospitalization and suicide. In this paper, we present a graph-based AI copilot for therapy narratives that aims to augment—not replace—clinical judgment. The copilot combines (i) an emotion annotator that tags each utterance in a session and (ii) a graph neural network (GNN) that models each visit as a temporally ordered sentence graph and predicts a coarse-grained session status (improving, stable, deteriorating). Its outputs drive clinician-facing dashboards that visualize polarity→topic flows and longitudinal "personal-stories" graphs, supporting rapid audit of model signals.Because accessing real-world therapy transcripts is difficult and ethically sensitive, we train and stress-test the framework on a synthetic corpus of multi-session patient–practitioner narratives augmented with PHQ-9 and GAD-7 style self-report scores. Empirically, the GNN achieves robust generalization for session-level status under patient-wise cross-validation, while sentence-level emotion remains a bottleneck under small, imbalanced synthetic data. We surface these limitations explicitly and argue that session-scale relational structure carries a more reliable signal than individual utterances in this setting. Overall, the work contributes: (1) a system-of-systems formulation of a behavioral-health copilot centered on sentence graphs and practitioner-in-the-loop controls; (2) a multi-task GNN that links sentence emotions to session-level status; and (3) dashboard analytics that make model behavior legible and auditable, providing a foundation for future work with de-identified clinical data.

title: Real-Time Holography Guided 3D Printing for Photocurable Hydrogel Microstructures With Tailored Morphology and Stiffness
authors: Xinyi Dong, Yanfeng Zhao, Kaijun Lin, Haotian Yang, Yaozhen Hou, Qing Shi, Qiang Huang, Toshio Fukuda, Huaping Wang
abstract: Digital light processing (DLP) enables rapid fabrication of photocurable hydrogel microstructures, which serve as critical functional components in micro-optical systems and microrobotics, and whose performance depends on micron-scale morphological features and local stiffness. Traditional visual feedback methods provide horizontal data but struggle to quantify axial topography and local stiffness in real-time. Although digital holographic microscopy (DHM) offers advantages for sample characterization, its real-time capabilities are limited by the challenge of dynamically correcting for optical distortions. To address this, we present a real-time feedback control algorithm featuring a novel partial matrix Zernike fitting (PMZF) method. PMZF is analytically derived to estimate background phase distortion from partially occluded fields of view, enabling accurate phase reconstruction at 5 fps. With PMZF-based feedback, the system achieves 4.88 μm axial precision and 4.29 kPa stiffness precision, improving accuracy by 71.9% over open-loop DLP. Moreover, the spatially resolved control of stiffness within single microgels leads to region-specific fluorescent release, demonstrating a functional behavior not attainable with conventional printing. This work provides an effective closed-loop strategy for controlling both geometry and stiffness, paving the way for advanced functional devices in tissue engineering, MEMS, and beyond.

title: Design of Cadmium-Tellurium Oxide Heterostructures for Portable Solar Cells
authors: Yana Suchikova, Sergii Kovachov, Zhakyp Karipbaev, Yaroslav Zhydachevskyy, Ihor Bohdanov, Anatoli I. Popov
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: This study introduces the development of a novel <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Cd}_{\mathrm{x}} \text{Te}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{O}_{z} / \text{CdS} /$</tex> por- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{ZnO} / \text{ZnO}$</tex> heterostructure synthesized through an economically efficient and straightforward method for use in portable solar cells. Employing the Successive Ionic Layer Adsorption and Reaction (SILAR) method in conjunction with atmospheric air annealing, we have successfully fabricated a multilayer structure without requiring expensive equipment or vacuum processes. The simplicity of the manufacturing process, combined with the heterostructure's promising optical and electronic properties, indicates its viability in the application of portable solar cells. This research not only advances the field of photovoltaic materials but also paves the way for further optimization and exploration of cost-effective solar energy solutions.

title: The Power of Two in Large Service-Marketplaces
authors: Dheeraj Narasimha, Srinivas Nomula, Srinivas Shakkottai, Parimal Parag
abstract: We consider a large-scale service marketplace with numerous servers that scale with the job arrival rate. Jobs arrive with private valuations representing their willingness to pay. In a centralized system, jobs are matched to available servers, and prices are set in a centralized manner to maximize revenue. We investigate whether similar scalability can be achieved in a distributed marketplace where jobs are randomly matched to servers, which set their own prices based on job valuations and system occupancy. Our results show that matching a job to a single randomly selected server leads to higher blocking, resulting in lower throughput and reduced revenue. We then examine matching jobs to two servers, which compete to provide service if unoccupied. We demonstrate the existence of a mean field equilibrium (MFE) in this setup, where servers strategically respond to competitors’ prices. We characterize the MFE and show that this two-server choice ensures lower blocking probabilities and higher system revenue. Our findings are validated through simulations illustrating a variety of operating scenarios.

title: Joint Background–Anomaly–Noise Decomposition for Robust Hyperspectral Anomaly Detection via Constrained Convex Optimization
authors: Koyo Sato, Shunsuke Ono
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: We propose a novel hyperspectral (HS) anomaly detection method that is robust to various types of noise. Most existing HS anomaly detection methods are designed without explicit consideration of noise or are based on the assumption of Gaussian noise. However, in real-world situations, observed HS images are often degraded by various types of noise, such as sparse noise and stripe noise, due to sensor failure or calibration errors, significantly affecting the detection performance. To address this problem, this article establishes a robust HS anomaly detection method with a mechanism that can properly remove mixed noise while separating background and anomaly parts. Specifically, we newly formulate a constrained convex optimization problem to decompose background and anomaly parts, and three types of noise from a given HS image. Then, we develop an efficient algorithm based on a preconditioned variant of a primal dual splitting method to solve this problem. Experimental results using seven real HS datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves detection accuracy comparable to state-of-the art methods on original images and exhibits significantly higher robustness in scenarios where various types of mixed noise are added.

title: The Pragmatic Openness of Open RAN: Navigating the Path from Vendor Lock-in to Interoperability Islands
authors: Nima Afraz, Mohammad Shojafar, Johann M. Marquez-Barja, Hamed Ahmadi
abstract: Open Radio Access Network (Open RAN) promises to dismantle traditional vendor lock-in through disaggregation and standardization. This article analyzes its current trajectory, finding that the ecosystem is not evolving toward universal interoperability but is instead consolidating into islands of interoperability or pre-validated multi-vendor clusters that risk becoming new forms of lock-in. Through a review of global deployments and industry collaboration patterns, we examine the technical and market forces that may contribute to this fragmentation. We argue that while these islands represent a pragmatic compromise that increases operator choice, they also pose a significant long-term risk to the open ecosystem by stifling innovation and competition. Ultimately, we conclude that avoiding permanent fragmentation is not a technical challenge alone but a strategic imperative. It requires a concerted shift from writing standards to enforcing rigorous, transparent certification and fostering a market that rewards true interoperability over closed partnerships. The future of Open RAN depends on building bridges between these islands to preserve its foundational promise.

title: Smart grids on MV/LV Distribution: From Transformer Substation to Intelligent Energy Management Node
authors: Iker Martin
abstract: Smart grids have long been in the scope of MV Electrical distribution and different approaches have shown around the World involving many actors.Now, lead by the 4 Ds (Decarbonization, Digitalization, Decentralization, Deregulation) traditional transformer substations become Intelligent Energy Management Nodes (IENM).The paper presents the most common use cases on Smart MV Distribution as background to the IEMN: ADA (Advanced Distribution Automation), FLISR (Fault Location Isolation and Service Restoration), Volt/Var control, communications network, cybersecurity or webservices. All are everyday challenges on MV distribution, proving that the IEMN is a need and a reality.Throughout this paper the needs and trends on the features and technologies on the IEMN are detailed.The paper wraps up the expertise acquired by Ormazabal for fifteen years on the deployment of MV automation on the Distribution Network for some of the most prominent DNOs in the World.

title: Automated Pothole Detection for Smart Road Infrastructure Using YOLOv8s and Cross-Dataset Learning
authors: Shikhar Agrawal, Bina Kotiyal
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Detection of road defects should be done accurately and in a timely manner to ensure the safety of traffic and pedestrians, to reduce the damage to vehicles, and to ensure timely maintenance of the road network. However, automated pothole detection remains challenging due to large variations in pothole shape, shadows, depth, and weather conditions, along with dataset biases. Present approaches suffer from the limitation of limited cross domain generalization and high computational cost, making scalability an issue. This study proposes an efficient and lightweight approach of using YOLOv8s based architecture powered by 2 stage training on two totally distinct datasets named MWPD and BharatPotHole to enhance the cross domain generalization and mitigate the risk of dataset bias. The model first learns the generalized features from MWPD and then region specific features. The backbone consisted of C2F blocks and pooling layers to enhance feature extraction, along with FPN and PAN in the neck to help in effective learning of low and high level features. Overall, the work provides a scalable and practical pothole detection framework addressing the challenges like dataset bias, lighting and weather variations, and real time inference and provides an intelligent and cost effective monitoring system.

title: A High-Speed Omnidirectional Ceiling Mobile Robot Using a Synchronized Crawler-Hanging Mechanism
authors: Hiroto Hasegawa, Ryota Yokomura, Rui Fukui
abstract: We present a ceiling mobile robot capable of high-speed transport with versatile routing. Based on the crawler-hanging mechanism proposed in previous work, the robot introduces several key improvements. First, we enhanced hanging stability during high-speed travel by synchronizing the ceiling-hanging motion with the crawler's rotation via guide mechanisms. This approach achieved reliable operation at 1.35 m/s, a 35% improvement over earlier designs. Additionally, a position correction method utilizing claw insertion into ceiling holes effectively corrected <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula>2 mm positional errors per hanging action, ensuring accurate alignment during travel. Second, we developed a direction-changing mechanism for static ceiling panels by relocating rolling mechanisms from turntables to the robot itself. This design enabled turning at 45 deg/s with a 60 kg payload and sideway movement at 0.2 m/s, enhancing routing versatility. These advancements improve ceiling robot mobility and expand the applicability of ceiling transport systems for industrial and logistics environments.

title: DST-Planner: Diffusion-Augmented Sequential Topological Planning for Robotic Exploration in Complex Environments
authors: Yiqing Yuan, Zhi Li, Jiahui Liang, Peiming Duan, Xiaoxun Zhang, Jihe Guo, Hui Cheng
abstract: Robotic exploration in large-scale unstructured 3D environments presents significant challenges, requiring efficient long-horizon reasoning and robust decision-making. Traditional heuristic-based planners often struggle with local minima and lack scalability, while conventional online Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches face prohibitive sample inefficiency and training instability. To overcome these challenges, we present DST-Planner, a novel framework that integrates 3D topological reasoning with offline diffusion-based reinforcement learning. Specifically, we leverage an incremental 3D skeleton graph to abstract complex environments into lightweight connectivity representations. We then introduce a novel diffusion critic architecture to accurately capture the multi-modal value distribution of exploration actions for robust value estimation. Simultaneously, we design a sequence-based actor to generate long-horizon topological plans that promote temporal consistency and topological continuity. By optimizing the policy via an advantage-weighted offline RL objective, our method ensures stable learning from fixed datasets and preserves exploration diversity. Extensive experiments in diverse simulation environments demonstrate that DST-Planner significantly outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines in coverage efficiency. Notably, we demonstrate the zero-shot transferability of our simulation-trained policy to both aerial and ground robots in the real world, confirming its robustness to drastically different robotic kinematics and motion constraints without fine-tuning.

title: Defrag: Reducing Resource Fragmentation in Large-scale Heterogeneous GPU Clusters
authors: Chenhao Wang, Yuedong Xu, Jun Wu, Yinghao Yu, Liang Ye, Guodong Yang
abstract: Technology companies have built large-scale heterogeneous GPU clusters to support various workloads. However, their cluster machines are found underutilized with severe resource fragmentation. The main causes are myopic online scheduling of incoming tasks and complex placement constraints specified by users or systems. In this paper, we propose to use task migration as a measure to alleviate resource fragmentation. Our trace-driven analysis on a production cluster with 12k machines reveals that almost at any random snapshot, most of the concurrent tasks have long run-times and small migration times, thus justifying the feasibility of task migration. By making the complex constraints mathematically tractable, we formulate an integer linear programming problem. An efficient heuristic algorithm called Iterative Partitioned Defragmentation (IPD) is presented to perform task migration in multiple iterations of computation. We design and implement Defrag, an operational resource defragmentation system on Kubernetes, and deploy it on the production clusters. Trace-driven experiments show that Defrag can reduce up to 80% idle CPUs and 29% idle GPUs on average. Furthermore, Defrag can refine the performance of online scheduling strategies by reducing up to 57% idle CPUs and 35% idle GPUs at the time of execution. Our real-world experiment also demonstrates the effectiveness of Defrag.

title: Feasibility Study of Programming Compliant Bimanual Tasks with Reasoning LLMs
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Programming bimanual robot manipulation in contact-aware environments is a complex process, requiring the simultaneous tuning of trajectories, coordination strategies, and impedance parameters. This feasibility study investigates the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) with reasoning capabilities to automate this workflow, moving beyond high-level planning to direct low-level controller parameterization. This approach is validated on two setups: Softbank's Pepper robot (hardware) and a dual-arm Franka FR3 system (simulation). It is demonstrated that reasoning models can generate a unified control policy, encompassing Cartesian trajectories, impedance gains, and coupling stiffness, from natural language instructions. This work provides a framework for reasoning-based code generation in compliant manipulation, feasibility results on physical hardware, and the first implementation of such compliant tasks on the Pepper robot.

title: DTMOS-Based Third-Order Quadrature Ring Oscillator
authors: Pradnya Moon, Shweta Gautam, Bhawna Aggarwal, Satvik Ojha
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: A third-order sinusoidal oscillator with quadrature-phase outputs based on a CMOS inverter is proposed in this paper. The oscillator uses three CMOS inverters (6 MOSFETs), 2 grounded resistors, and 3 grounded capacitors, leading to a small and highly integrable circuit. The proposed oscillator design is more power efficient, less complex, and more layout-friendly than traditional oscillator designs based on complex active elements. The oscillator has the advantage of independent electronic control of the condition of oscillation (COO) and frequency of oscillation (FOO), which allows for design flexibility and tunability. The design is capable of operating with low voltage power supply; CMOS inverters operate at ±0.9 V and DTMOS inverters operate at 0.3 V. Simulation results using LTspice with 180 nm CMOS technology are in close agreement to the theory and verify the proposed design.

title: Verification of the Tightening of an Aluminium Flat Bar Using a Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Krzysztof Dziarski, Arkadiusz Hulewicz, Grzegorz Dombek, Zbigniew Nadolny
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: A method for assessing the correctness of tightening of a bolted joint in an aluminum flat bar is proposed. Thermograms and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) were employed. The experimental investigations were carried out using a measurement setup in which an electric current of varying intensity flowed through the bolted joint, resulting in an increase in its temperature. The recorded thermograms were converted into temperature matrices, which were subsequently used as input data in the neural network training process. The dataset was randomly divided into training and validation subsets in an 80/20 ratio. The thermograms were classified into two categories representing correctly and incorrectly tightened bolted joints. The effectiveness of the proposed method was evaluated using a confusion matrix. For the validation dataset, a classification accuracy of 90% was achieved. The obtained results demonstrate the feasibility and practical potential of thermography combined with CNN-based analysis for non-contact diagnostics of bolted joints.

title: An Ultra-Compact 64–98GHz Power-Combining Frequency Doubler with Magnetic-Enhanced Harmonic Reflectors
authors: Zesen Chen, Tingting Yuan, Zhiwei Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This article presents a compact 64–98GHz power-combining frequency doubler with magnetic enhanced harmonic reflectors. The 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nd</sup> harmonic outputs of two identical push-push frequency doublers are combined to improve the maximum output power. Two ways share an L-C harmonic reflector, and each way employs a C-L harmonic reflector. Two C-L harmonic reflectors are magnetically coupled to enhance each other and save area. Fabricated in a 65-nm bulk CMOS technology, the doubler achieves a 3dB bandwidth of 64–98GHz, a peak saturation output power of 11.4dBm, a maximum drain efficiency of 21.1%, a maximum conversion gain of 3.3dB, with a core area of only 0.053mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: A Caterpillar-Type Miniature Robot for Adaptive Locomotion and Exploration of Tiny Rigid/Soft Pipes_supp5-3697140.mp4
authors: Jinyang Gao
abstract: Caterpillar-type robots are widely used for medium- and large-sized pipe inspections. However, existing prototypes smaller than 80 mm lack both an active variable diameter capability and a contact force sensing function, which are crucial for safe and automatic exploration of unknown rigid/soft pipes (e.g., the colon). This study develops a variable diameter caterpillar-type miniature robot (VCMR) featuring a small size of Φ34.6 mm × 41 mm, a large variable-diameter range of 34.6-89.6 mm, and an integrated contact force sensing function. The VCMR actively adapts to pipe diameter changes using contact force feedback, demonstrates high load capacity in both vertical and horizontal rigid/soft pipes, and traverses a 150-cm colon phantom with sharp bends at an average velocity of 3.07 ± 0.48 cm/s. It holds promise for exploring tiny variable-diameter rigid/soft pipes and delivering cargoes through such pipes.

title: AgriPred: An Image-Based Rice Disease Classification System with Inference-Time Text Assistance and Advisory Support
authors: Ayush Arya, Arsh Anand, Ayush Biswas, Adrija Bhattacharya
abstract: AgriPred is an automated rice disease classification and decision-support system that integrates image-based deep learning with lightweight textual assistance and advisory support. The primary classifier is based on a ResNet18 convolutional neural network trained on labeled rice leaf images spanning ten health categories, including nine disease classes and one healthy class. Unlike joint multimodal training approaches, the proposed system employs supervised learning exclusively on visual features, while incorporating a TF-IDF-based symptom similarity module and a curated advisory engine at inference time to enhance usability and interpretability. This design enables flexible interaction, allowing users to obtain predictions from either images or textual symptom descriptions when image data is unavailable. The system is deployed as a Flask-based REST API with MongoDB for data persistence, supporting real-time interaction in practical agricultural settings. Experimental results demonstrate strong classification performance on heterogeneous field-acquired images. The deployed system achieves an average end-to-end inference latency of approximately 1.8 seconds per image under CPU-only cloud conditions. Overall, AgriPred provides a practical, scalable, and deployment-ready decision-support tool for rice disease management in real-world agricultural environments.

title: Sequential Modulated Predictive Voltage Control of an LC -Filtered Neutral-Point Clamped Inverter With Reduced Computational Burden
authors: Liming Zheng, Venkata Yaramasu
abstract: Modulated model predictive voltage control (M2PVC) offers excellent dynamic performance for LC-filtered three-level neutral-point-clamped (3L-NPC) inverters. However, its practical implementation is significantly hindered by the high computational burden and the reliance on multiple weighting factors, which complicate real-time deployment. Existing reduced-complexity approaches, primarily developed for non-LC-filtered systems, cannot be directly applied due to the additional filter dynamics and altered system behavior. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a computationally efficient sequential modulated model predictive voltage control (CE-SM2PVC) scheme for an LC-filtered 3L-NPC inverter. An optimal triangle method based on the discrete-time models is proposed to directly identify the operating region and restrict candidate voltage vectors to adjacent triangular regions, thereby eliminating redundant evaluations. As a result, the computational burden is reduced by approximately 95% compared with classical M2PVC. In addition, a sequential optimization framework is employed to eliminate the weighting factors associated with inverter current and DC-link capacitor voltage control. The dwell times of voltage vectors are dynamically adjusted to regulate the DC-link capacitor voltages. Experimental validation on a 3.6 kW prototype using a dSPACE MicroLabBox demonstrates that the proposed scheme achieves fixed switching frequency operation, reduced total harmonic distortion and steady-state error, lower switching losses, higher efficiency, and effective DC-link voltage balancing under both linear and nonlinear load conditions.

title: Reducing Pilots in Channel Estimation with Predictive Foundation Models
authors: Xingyu Zhou, Le Liang, Hao Ye, Jing Zhang, Chao-Kai Wen, Shi Jin
abstract: Accurate channel state information (CSI) acquisition is essential for modern wireless systems, which becomes increasingly difficult under large antenna arrays, strict pilot overhead constraints, and diverse deployment environments. Existing artificial intelligence-based solutions often lack robustness and fail to generalize across scenarios. To address this limitation, this paper introduces a predictive-foundation-model-based channel estimation framework that enables accurate, low-overhead, and generalizable CSI acquisition. The proposed framework employs a predictive foundation model trained on large-scale cross-domain data to extract universal channel representations and provide predictive priors with strong cross-scenario transferability. A pilot processing network based on a vision transformer architecture is further designed to capture spatial, temporal, and frequency correlations from pilot observations. An efficient fusion mechanism integrates predictive priors with real-time measurements, enabling reliable CSI reconstruction even under sparse or noisy conditions. Extensive evaluations across diverse configurations demonstrate that the proposed estimator significantly outperforms both classical and data-driven baselines in accuracy, robustness, and generalization capability.

title: LAPS: Improving Incremental LiDAR Mapping using Active Pooling and Sampling for Neural Distance Fields
authors: Dongjae Lee, Wooseong Yang, Yifu Tao, Maurice Fallon, Ayoung Kim
abstract: Neural distance fields offer a compact and continu ous representation of 3D geometry, making them attractive for incremental LiDAR mapping. However, their online optimization is vulnerable to catastrophic forgetting, where new observations can degrade previously reconstructed geometry. Replay-based training is commonly used to address this issue, but existing methods typically rely on passive replay buffers and uniform sampling, which can waste memory on redundant observations and under-train poorly constrained regions. We propose LAPS, a replay management framework for incremental neural mapping that improves both replay retention and replay allocation during online updates. LAPS combines reliability-based active pooling to retain reliable historical samples under limited memory with uncertainty-guided active sampling to focus optimization on under-constrained regions. Experiments on synthetic and real world benchmarks show that LAPS consistently improves recon struction completeness while maintaining competitive geometric accuracy. On Oxford Spires, it improves recall by 4.66 pp and F1-score by 3.79 pp over PIN-SLAM on the Blenheim Palace 05 sequence. We release our open source implementation at: https://github.com/dongjae0107/LAPS.

title: Spatial Clustering and Controlling Factors of Active Landslides in the Upper Yellow River: Insights from SBAS-InSAR and XGBoost–SHAP Modeling
authors: Yuxin Zhou, Xiaoyu Liu, Wu Zhu, Yue Wang, Zhanxi Wei, Jing Wu, Haixing Shang, Tong Yang, Chao Du
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Focusing on the key issues of unclear development patterns and formation mechanisms of landslide hazards in the section from Longyang Gorge to Liujia Gorge in the upper Yellow River, this study proposes a method of machine learning-based landslide identification and interpretability analysis. The surface deformation rate field was obtained with Small Baseline Subset Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (SBAS-InSAR) technology. After that, building upon an improved Faster RCNN framework, a multi-scale overlapping cropping strategy was introduced to divide the large-scale deformation field into continuous subregions, constructing a multi-scale sample set and achieving accurate identification of active landslides. A total of 963 active landslides were identified. Through spatial analysis and statistical testing, it was revealed that landslides exhibit significant spatial clustering characteristics: geomorphologically, they are mainly concentrated at elevations of 2200-3400 m, slope angles of 0-16° and 24-36°, and are predominantly in medium-relief middle mountain areas. The distribution density of landslides shows a negative correlation with the distance to rivers, roads, and faults. On this basis, an XGBoost-SHAP coupled model was constructed to quantitatively analyze the degree and influence of multiple factors such as topography, geomorphology, geology, and hydrology on landslide development, significantly enhancing the model's interpretability. The results indicate that geomorphological type is the primary factor controlling landslide development, followed by annual cumulative rainfall. These results deepen the theoretical understanding of landslide development mechanisms under the synergistic control of “topography-geology-human activities”.
