title: Field-Validated Accelerometer-Based Tension Estimation for Overhead Distribution Lines
authors: J. H. Kim
abstract: This paper presents a proof-of-concept framework validated using field data that estimates the mechanical tension and corresponding sag of overhead distribution conductors using on-conductor accelerometers and a Random Forest (RF) model. One minute-resolution datasets were collected over a one-month period from three 30-m spans of a 22.9-kV-class distribution line test facility at the KEPCO Gochang Test Center under non-energized conditions. The direct sensor output channels (AccX, AccY, AccZ, Roll, and Yaw), together with temperature and wind variables, are used as inputs to the RF regressor. Across the three spans under the tested conditions, the RF model achieves the lowest normalized root mean square error and the highest coefficient of determination, with NRMSE in the range of 0.007–0.012 and R² values approaching unity for tension estimation. Sag is then computed from the estimated tension using a standard parabolic approximation consistent with IEEE Std 524. The resulting sag traces closely match those obtained from measured tension, with R² between 0.996 and 0.999 and mean sag errors in the few-millimeter range, well within the installation tolerances prescribed by IEEE Std 524. A rolling 5-fold cross-validation analysis shows small fold-to-fold dispersion within the available dataset, supporting the feasibility of the proposed framework as a proof-of-concept under the tested conditions. The proposed framework therefore demonstrates the feasibility of using accelerometer signals for tension and sag estimation and suggests potential applicability to clearance assessment and condition monitoring under similar operating conditions.

title: Maintaining Stability in Renewable-Integrated Grids: The Vital Role of Frequency Support
authors: Ahmed M. Alshehri, Abdulmalik A. Alghamdi, Mojahed M. Albetairi
abstract: Expected higher penetration of renewable energy sources (RES, mostly solar and wind) over the next few years in the Saudi electricity grid, based on the publicly available data, impacts several aspects of system operations and control such as system frequency swings, reactive adequacy deficiencies leading to voltage fluctuations, in addition to transient and dynamic stability challenges among other issues. The intermittency nature of solar and wind generation leaves system operators with less conventional resources to control system frequency as a higher amount of these generation technologies are constructed and interconnected to the Saudi grid. This paper focused on the frequency control aspects in system operations and presented an assessment of types and amounts of conventional generation reserves required for tight system frequency control in the Saudi grid. It also outlined the various types of reserves or ancillary services, assessed the amount of MW reserves available for frequency support, and evaluated the flexible reserves that could be provided by online conventional generation technology during low system inertia and higher ramping of renewable production during winter seasons. This paper intended to highlight to system operators and operation planners the challenges and needs for flexible generation resources to ride-through frequency disturbance events throughout the timelines from seconds to a few minutes post-disturbance and the generation flexible ramping capability that could reliably meet the daily cyclical nature of more than 22 GW of solar expected generation by 2027. The results of the analysis presented in this paper revealed potential deficiencies in available Grid Inertia and Flexible Ramping Reserve starting in 2025 where system operators need to consider additions of potential solutions such as Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), Synchronous Condensers etc.

title: Transmitter Identification via Volterra Series Based Radio Frequency Fingerprint
authors: Rundong Jiang, Jun Hu, Zhiyuan Xie, Yunqi Song, Shiyou Xu
abstract: Radio Frequency Fingerprint (RFF), a physical-layer authentication technique that requires no cryptographic pairing and exhibits inherent resistance to cloning or spoofing, has emerged as a promising solution for device authentication in the Internet of Things (IoT) and unmanned systems. However, practical RFF authentication depends on extracting device-specific hardware signatures from received waveforms in a physically explainable manner. Existing deep-learning-based methods often provide limited interpretability, whereas feature-specific methods usually capture only a small subset of handcrafted impairments. This motivates a more general, hardware-oriented RFF representation. In this paper, we construct such a representation by using the received signal together with its corresponding ideal reference waveform to estimate the transmitter’s linear and nonlinear behavioral features. The estimated behavior features are then used as the radio frequency fingerprint for device classification and identification. Under a fixed receiver and a standard preprocessing pipeline (timing alignment, CFO compensation, and amplitude normalization), we represent this transmitter-dependent behavior with a Volterra series: the zeroth-order term accounts for the constant offset, the first-order kernel describes the equivalent linear response with memory, and the higher-order kernels describe nonlinear interactions among delayed signal samples. To obtain a compact representation, we approximate the kernels using wavelet bases and estimate the corresponding coefficients via regularized least-squares fitting. The magnitudes of the fitted coefficients are then classified using a lightweight convolutional neural network. Experiments on a public LoRa dataset achieve 99.49% mean accuracy in the static-channel setting and above 90% mean accuracy under combined multipath and Doppler impairments. Compared with recent RFF methods, the proposed approach provides competitive accuracy and substantially lower complexity than most deep-learning baselines, while offering a more physically interpretable representation of transmitter behavior. We further validate dataset-level generalization on a self-constructed 16-device dataset. The implementation is publicly available at: https://github.com/thomas-smith123/RFFI.

title: Current-State Opacity Verification in Petri Nets Using Fusion-Based Opacity Analysis and Graph Isomorphism Networks
authors: Sichen Ding, Zhiwu Li
abstract: Current-state opacity is a critical security property for discrete event systems, but its verification in large-scale Petri nets is hampered by the state-space explosion problem. To address this challenge, we propose the fusion-based opacity analysis and graph isomorphism network (FOA-GIN), a novel deep learning framework. The method transforms current-state opacity verification into a graph classification task by applying a tailored graph isomorphism network to basis reachability graphs—a compact representation of the system's dynamics. This approach integrates the theoretical strengths of basis reachability graphs with the scalability of graph neural networks to capture essential structural and behavioral features for opacity analysis. Unlike classical algorithms that require exponential state-space traversal, the proposed model's online verification complexity is linear in the size of the input basis reachability graph. Experiments demonstrate high accuracy and robustness, establishing FOA-GIN as a powerful and practical solution for verifying current-state opacity in complex, large-scale systems.

title: Accuracy of Quantifying Hypotension During Surgery Using Physiological Sensor Data_supp1-3698197.pdf
authors: Martin Jacobsson

title: AutoStress Benchmark: Evaluating Factors that influence Cross Dataset Generalizabilty in Stress Recognition
authors: Paul Schreiber, Maria Maleshkova
abstract: Developing machine learning models to monitor and react to cognitive states, particularly stressful events, may help mitigate the severe health risks of long-term stress exposure. This work provides a benchmark for automated stress recognition using well established physiological features and standard machine learning methods, applied to an integrated version of publicly available datasets. We evaluate a binary classification task (baseline vs. stress) and achieve accuracies of up to 84%. In addition, we examine cross dataset generalizability as well as the dataset characteristics that influence model performance. In particular, we compare differences in sample size, sensor hardware, or stress protocol, which could have an impact on the generalizability. Our findings suggest that differences in the classification performance of unseen subjects is largely driven by cross-dataset heterogeneity, particularly mismatches in stressor tasks.

title: Matrix Product States as a framework for efficient simulation of quantum kernel methods for classification
authors: Antonio J. Rivera-Pérez, Noelia Sánchez-Gómez, Jara J. Bermejo-Vega, Carlos Cano
abstract: Tensor networks and Matrix Product States (MPS) are commonly used to efficiently perform classical simulations of specific quantum circuits with low entanglement. In this work, we propose an approach to use MPS as an efficient framework for quantum kernel simulation for binary classification via SVMs. To this end, classical data are first embedded into higher-dimensional quantum states using shallow ZZ feature maps. We employ ZZ feature maps with low connectivity that limits entanglement, enabling us to assess whether effective classical data encoding can be achieved while retaining efficient classical simulation. This way, quantum kernels are efficiently computed through MPS overlap. We study the computational cost and accuracy as a function of the depth of the feature maps. Experiments on different classical datasets show that low entanglement quantum feature maps can achieve competitive classification performance at the same time as being efficiently simulable on classical computers.

title: WheelArm-Sim: A Manipulation and Navigation Combined Multimodal Synthetic Data Generation Simulator for Unified Control in Assistive Robotics
authors: Guangping Liu, Tipu Sultan, Vittorio Di Giorgio, Nick Hawkins, Flavio Esposito, Madi Babaias
abstract: Wheelchairs and robotic arms enhance independent living by assisting individuals with upper-body and mobility limitations in their activities of daily living (ADLs). Although recent advancements in assistive robotics have focused on Wheelchair-Mounted Robotic Arms (WMRAs) and wheelchairs separately, integrated and unified control of the combination using machine learning models remains largely underexplored. To fill this gap, we introduce the concept of WheelArm, an integrated cyber-physical system (CPS) that combines wheelchair and robotic arm controls. Data collection is the first step toward developing WheelArm models. In this paper, we present WheelArm-Sim, a simulation framework developed in Isaac Sim for synthetic data collection. We evaluate its capability by collecting a manipulation and navigation combined multimodal dataset, comprising 13 tasks, 232 trajectories, and 67,783 samples. To demonstrate the potential of the WheelArm dataset, we implement a baseline model for action prediction in the mustard-picking task. The results illustrate that data collected from WheelArm-Sim is feasible for a data-driven machine learning model for integrated control. The data, codes, and video of the paper can be accessed at https://github.com/madibabaiasl/WheelArmSimISMR2026.

title: Machine Learning-Assisted Simulation and Parameter Prediction System for Optoelectronic Oscillators
authors: Ugra Mohan Roy, Divya Kiran, Malathi S, Aditya Kulkarni, Tejas Hemanth Kumar Naik, Leela MS
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: This work presents a machine learning based prediction framework for designing Integrated Optoelectronic Oscillators (IOEOs) operating between 109–111GHz and 0.9-1.1GHz. The machine learning (ML) models learned complex relationships between parameters based on the required performance, and provide a rapid and accurate parameters value prediction. Different machine learning algorithms have been trained and the predicted parameters values have been validated through subsequent simulations. For architecture1, Random Forest Regressor model has shown better performance by achieving R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 0.9343 with a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.3675. In case of architecture2, XGBoost has outperformed other linear and ensemble models by achieving R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 0.976 and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.155. The proposed ML-integrated framework eliminates manual error during parameters tuning, reduces designing complexity, and accelerates the development time cycle of high-performance IOEOs for advanced photonic and microwave applications. Qualitative assessments confirm the generation of microwave signals with low phase noise and high spectral purity.

title: Physically Guided Diffusion Framework With Neural Information Bottleneck Regulation for Robust Small Object Detection
authors: Yongcheng Zhou, Shilei Tan, Wei Li, Yan Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Robust small object detection in adverse environments remains challenging due to physical degradations and unstable feature representations. Existing detectors often struggle to maintain semantic consistency in haze, rain, and low-light conditions, leading to degraded accuracy and poor generalization. To address these issues, we propose a unified neural information bottleneck with physics-guided diffusion (NIB-PGD) framework. The proposed framework couples physical priors with information-theoretic regularization in a dual-diffusion paradigm, enabling robust feature encoding and noise-resilient object localization. A multiscale enhance–fuse–context attention (EFC-A) encoder captures hierarchical semantics and contextual dependencies, while a physics-guided feature-level diffusion injects realistic degradation kernels into the noise schedule to enhance robustness. In parallel, a box-level diffusion reformulates detection as iterative denoising, progressively refining bounding boxes without anchors or queries. A second-order information bottleneck (2O-IB) constraint dynamically regulates mutual information to suppress redundancy and preserve task-relevant semantics. On our composite benchmark (constructed from BDD100K, SODA-D, and an in-house small object set), NIB-PGD achieves 52.3% AP and 35.7% AP<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{s}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, outperforming the strongest AP baseline by + 1.2 AP and the strongest AP<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{s}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> baseline by +1.1 AP<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{s}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Comprehensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that NIB-PGD achieves state-of-the-art performance in detection accuracy, robustness, and generalization.

title: Design, Simulation, and Validation of a Self-Compensating ALTP Sensor for High-Speed Heat Flux Measurement
authors: Tian Xie, Bowan Tao, Ruipeng Zhao, Wanqing Luo, Yuanjian Yang, Xi Chen, Zhenzhe Li, Mingyuan Zhao, Ziwen Jin, Yihui Zheng, Xinze Liu
abstract: The atomic-layer thermopile (ALTP) heat flux sensor, based on the transverse Seebeck effect (TSE), is a thin-film sensor with ultrafast response. However, when exposed to nonuniform heat flux fields, an in-plane temperature gradient is generated across the sensitive film of the ALTP sensor, inducing the ordinary Seebeck effect (SE). This effect gives rise to an additional bias voltage, which severely degrades the accuracy of heat flux measurement results. This work presents a breakthrough self-compensating ALTP sensor based on a differential structure. The error source and compensation mechanism were verified through multiphysics finite element simulations and experimental tests. The ALTP heat flux sensor with this differential configuration enables real-time self-compensation without the need for additional data processing. Validation tests were conducted in an arc-heated wind tunnel, and the results were compared with those obtained from a slug calorimeter heat flux sensor. The measured heat flux trends of the two sensors showed excellent consistency. This self-compensating ALTP sensor achieves fast response and high accuracy, providing a promising solution for heat flux measurement in complex thermal environments.

title: Performance–Efficiency Tradeoff of Deep CNN Models for Brain Tumor MRI Classification on Edge Devices
authors: Amit Kumar Behera, Ankit Kumar, Asmita, Suresh Kumar Gaddi
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: In this article, deep convolutional neural network architectures for multi-class brain tumor classification are systematically evaluated. In T1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging with a view to deployment feasibility on edge hardware. We consider six pre-trained architectures – MobileNetV2, ResNet18, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB4, and VGG16, and compare them to a custom baseline CNN model trained from scratch. The experiments were conducted on a balanced dataset of 7,200 MRI images of four classes. The classification results are based on images distributed over Glioma, Meningioma, Pituitary Tumor, and No Tumor are the four classifications. First, training and testing are done on a workstation with an RTX 4070 Super GPU, then TensorFlow Lite (TFLite) is deployed on a Raspberry Pi 5 (8GB RAM) for latency and throughput measurements. Workstation performance obtains the highest accuracy of 95.64% for EfficientNetB0, whereas the baseline CNN network gains 93.44% with 0.5M parameters. On the edge device, accuracy is lowered by 1–3% while latency is between 40 ms (MobileNetV2) and 320 ms (VGG16). EfficientNetB0 and MobileNetv2 give the most balanced tradeoff for accuracy (less than 91%) with a latency below 60 ms. These findings further provide empirical evidence to the belief that lightweight CNN architectures can be tailored to brain tumor classification tasks at performance levels comparable to their more complex counterparts, with much fewer computational resources.

title: Exploratory Machine Learning Modeling of Cyanobacteria and Cyanotoxin Transport in the Raritan Basin of New Jersey
authors: Suvid Bordia, Vandana Rao Kondapalli, Harshini Rikkala, Kyle Clonan, Meiyin Wu, Hao Liu, Weitian Wang, Michelle Zhu
abstract: Harmful algal blooms (HABs), often dominated by toxin-producing cyanobacteria, pose increasing ecological, economic, and public health risks in freshwater systems. Microcystins, among the most common cyanotoxins, contaminate drinking water sources and threaten aquatic life, making accurate prediction of bloom dynamics essential for early warning and mitigation. This study examines three key biological targets in the Raritan Basin Water Supply Complex (RBWSC) of New Jersey: microcystin concentration (μg/L), total cyanobacteria abundance (cells/mL), and total phytoplankton biomass (cells/mL). Field measurements collected by the U.S. Geological Survey from August 2020 to August 2021 included biological, chemical, and physical water-quality parameters at nine RBWSC monitoring sites. We evaluate statistical, deep learning, and ensemble approaches for concentration regression and find that all methods perform inadequately under temporal validation due to sparse sampling and skewed target ranges. We therefore reframe the task as binary regulatory threshold exceedance aligned with the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) water-quality guidelines to support intervention decisions. Binary classification achieves F1 = 0.900 and Matthews Correlation Coefficient MCC = 0.883 for phytoplankton and F1 = 0.889 (MCC = 0.873) for cyanobacteria, with constrained-depth tree ensembles achieving F1 = 0.800 (MCC = 0.808) for microcystin detection, all exceeding the 70–85% classification accuracy seen in HAB prediction literature. Extending to two-week forecasting, gradient-boosted trees incorporating prior blooms achieve F1 = 0.923 for cyanobacteria, surpassing the same-time classification performance and confirming the temporal persistence of bloom conditions. Leave-one-site-out cross-validation confirms spatial generalizability across unseen monitoring stations with F1 : 0.65–0.75. Framing water safety as a binary classification task based on regulatory thresholds allows for the development of a reliable HAB early warning system that can be used to inform water treatment and public health response.

title: High School and Undergraduate Students’ Integrative Thinking in Educational Robotics
authors: Huberth Perez, Igor M. Verner, Rea Lavi
abstract:
$Contribution$ : This study extends the concept of integrative thinking (InT) from the areas of systems engineering and management to educational robotics. The study defines and classifies InT competencies that high school and undergraduate students employ while learning to construct, program, and operate robots in technology-rich settings.

$Background$ : Modern engineers need to apply InT to analyze, design, and operate complex systems across knowledge domains and relationships. Educational robotics provides opportunities for developing InT competencies but approaches to fostering and assessing these competencies have not been sufficiently developed. Research.

$Questions$ : What integrative thinking competencies are observed in students involved in experiential learning with robots? What characteristics of learning settings support students’ use of integrative thinking?

$Methodology$ : A multiple case study approach was employed with four groups of high school and undergraduate students (N = 198). Data were collected through observations, student reports, logbooks, and interviews, and analyzed using grounded theory methods. While the limited sample constrains generalizability, the study provides exploratory evidence.

$Findings$ : The analysis revealed four InT competencies in robotics activities: interconnectedness, functionality, externalities, and alternatives. These competencies were observed across both educational levels and aligned with established frameworks in management and systems engineering. Learning settings involving mechatronic projects and system integration tasks were found to effectively elicit InT. The findings suggest that educational robotics can scaffold integrative thinking development from secondary to higher education, preparing students for the interdisciplinary challenges of modern engineering.

title: Remission of Torque Pulsations in Power System Using UPFC Including PI and Fuzzy Controllers
authors: J. Sreeranganayakulu, A. Suresh Kumar, M. Padma Lalitha, Nimay Chandra Giri, Olena Rubanenko, Oleksander Rubanenko, Inna Vishnak, Iryna Hunko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: In power system transmission, to improve the power factor series capacitors are installed at various points. However, due to this inclusion, there is a drawback of SSR oscillations that needs to be mitigated. In earlier research, various methods were used to effectively remission of these oscillations. This paper presents an analysis of how effectively the fuzzy logic controllers over the conventional PI controllers can reduce the SSR oscillations along with the inclusion of a Unified power flow controller.

title: Trajectory-Aware Deformable Transformer for Contextual Video Prediction
authors: Sidharth Bhanu Kamtam, Qian Lu, Olivier C. L. Hass, Stewart Birrell
abstract: Recent video transformers commonly adopt factorised spatial-temporal attention to reduce computational complexity. However, such factorisation limits temporal modelling by constraining attention to fixed spatial locations across frames, ignoring the motion-dependent spatial context essential for accurate prediction. To address this limitation, we propose Trajectory-Aware Deformable Attention for the video prediction task. This novel mechanism integrates motion-aware sampling into temporal attention by dynamically aligning keys and values along motion paths to restore spatial context. Patch trajectories are estimated in continuous pixel coordinates using a lightweight convolutional flow estimator with forward-backwards consistency checking for occlusion detection. For each query, a set of learnable deformable sample points are placed around the trajectory-predicted position at every source time step, and features are extracted via differentiable bilinear interpolation. The proposed method is trained and evaluated on the KITTI Caltech dataset. It achieves consistent improvements across all metrics, including a 7.6% reduction in MSE and a significant 20.1% reduction in LPIPS, and improvements in PSNR, MAE and SSIM, demonstrating the benefit of motion-aware sampling. These gains are achieved with only a 14.3% increase in FLOPs, demonstrating that it can be integrated into any video transformer at a modest additional cost.

title: A Distributed Cooperative Control for Islanded DC Microgrids Incorporating Time Delays
authors: Mohamed Zaery, Syed Muhammad Amrr, Tarek Younis, Ahmed S. Menesy, S.M. Suhail Hussain, Mohammad A. Abido
abstract: This manuscript proposes a novel distributed cooperative control to optimize generation costs in DC microgrids, with consideration of time delays. Artstein's reduction method is employed to transform the time-delay system into an equivalent delay-free model. Building on this, a finite-time distributed control algorithm is introduced to minimize generation cost by aligning the incremental costs of distributed generation units (DGUs) within an accelerated convergence timeframe, while respecting both the capacity limits of DGUs and the transmission line losses. Additionally, a distributed control approach is developed to ensure finite-time voltage stability, balancing generation and consumption across the microgrid. A key feature of the proposed control approach is incorporating a prescribed performance function, ensuring that the system's transient and steady-state responses consistently meet the required performance criteria. This ensures enhanced reliability and operational effectiveness of the system. The superiority and robustness of the proposed strategy are demonstrated through extensive simulation case studies.

title: SwiNNEt-Galaxy: Hierarchically Constrained and Rotation-Aware Hybrid CNN-Transformer for Galaxy Morphology Regression
authors: Sandeep Shiraskar, Hossein Beyzavi, Arohi Shiraskar, Georges Nehmetallah, Dominick Rizk, Frederic Rizk, Patrick Rizk, KC Santosh, Rodrigue Rizk
abstract: Galaxy morphology classification is a fundamental problem in modern astronomical surveys, providing critical insights into galaxy formation, evolution, and the large-scale structure of the Universe. Large-scale citizen science projects such as Galaxy Zoo have enabled morphology labeling at an unprecedented scale, but the resulting annotations follow a hierarchical decision-tree structure, in which each response is conditionally dependent on preceding answers. Existing deep learning approaches typically treat morphology labels as independent outputs, ignoring these logical dependencies and frequently producing physically inconsistent predictions. Moreover, galaxies exhibit rotational symmetry on the sky, yet many models do not explicitly encode rotation equivariance, limiting generalization. In this work, we propose SwiNNEt-Galaxy, a hybrid deep neural network that integrates EfficientNet-B4 and a Swin Transformer to perform probabilistic galaxy morphology regression with explicit hierarchical constraint enforcement and rotation-aware inference. We formulate the Galaxy Zoo decision tree as a directed acyclic graph and introduce a hierarchical consistency loss that penalizes violations of logical dependencies among 37 vote-fraction outputs across 11 sequential questions. Rotation equivariance is incorporated via a multi-view aggregation strategy that respects the absence of a preferred orientation in astronomical imaging. We evaluate our method on the Galaxy Zoo dataset containing 61,578 galaxy images. SwiNNEt-Galaxy achieves an RMSE of 0.0796, outperforming strong CNN and Transformer baselines while significantly reducing physically implausible predictions. Detailed per-question analysis and ablation studies demonstrate that hierarchical constraints improve inter-question consistency and stabilize predictions for low-signal classes. Our results indicate that combining hierarchical supervision with hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures provides a principled and scalable framework for domain-aware automated galaxy morphology classification in current and future surveys.

title: Quantum Möbius Transform via Block Encoding
authors: Cezary Pilaszewicz
abstract: Beyond-quadratic quantum speed-up is often based on an efficient transform between representations. We present a quantum algorithm to compute the Möbius spectrum of a pseudo-Boolean function. The algorithm produces a quantum state where the amplitude of each basis state is proportional to the Möbius coefficient of the corresponding index set. To achieve it, we implement the block encoding framework for the non-orthogonal Möbius transform. The algorithm runs in polynomial time with respect to the number of input bits and has many applications within function learning, image processing, and cryptography.

title: Development of the Methodology for Technical and Economic Justification of the Storage Batteries Selection for Electric Power Objects
authors: Anton Vorushylo, Serhii Stepenko, Anatolii Kvitsynskyi, Roman Buinyi, Dmytro Zakharchenko, Oleksandr Bondarenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The modern backup power supply systems are characterized by a shortening of the estimated service life because of rapid outdating. The storage battery is the most expensive component of the backup power supply system. In this work the methodological approaches proposed by the authors regarding the technical and economic substantiation of the choice of storage batteries for power substations of distribution system operators and transmission system operator in Ukraine. The technical and economic reasons for the choice of storage batteries have been discussed in detail. The example of technical specifications of the subject of procurement has been proposed.

title: Using Unified Power Flow Controller to improve the KSA network performance with RES integration
authors: Muath O. Al Omani
abstract: The integration of renewable energy sources, particularly photovoltaic (PV) systems, has become increasingly prevalent in industrial systems nowadays. However, the intermittent nature of solar power poses challenges for optimizing consumption, especially during nighttime hours when PV generation is unavailable. This paper proposes an approach leveraging Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC) technology to enhance the utilization of PV lines for industrial asset consumption optimization around the clock which will optimize the company assets consumption. The UPFC is employed as a key control device within the power network to regulate voltage and power flow dynamically, thereby enabling efficient transfer of power from PV sources during daylight hours and effective management of grid power during nighttime. The proposed approach offers several benefits to industrial companies, including reduced energy costs and enhanced energy efficiency. Moreover, by leveraging renewable energy sources more effectively, the system contributes to sustainability goals and supports the transition towards economic objectives. Also, some possible system contingencies will be simulated to measure its impact on the selected system. Overall, this paper simulate a real KSA subsystem between two operation areas to study the impact of the approach in the system.

title: FedALL: Minimizing Variance of Partial Participation in Federated Learning
authors: Zeyuan Zuo, Yi Zeng, Jason Pui Yin Cheung, Baochun Li, Teng Zhang
abstract: In federated learning, partial participation, where offline or delayed clients are absent, can significantly degrade model performance. A potential solution is to better utilize updates from these absent clients. Existing methods typically cache the most recent clients’ updates and reuse them in subsequent training rounds, but they often lack a clear theoretical justification for why they should use these updates and how to optimally incorporate them in model training. In this study, we show that leveraging the most recent updates can reduce variance in model aggregation, providing insights into the optimal strategy for using these updates. Specifically, we prove that selecting the appropriate decay coefficient for cached updates minimizes variance in aggregation. Building on this insight, we propose FedALL, a novel federated learning algorithm designed to minimize aggregation variance. Extensive experiments across different datasets, client participation ratios, and levels of data heterogeneity indicate that FedALL consistently outperforms FedAvg by an average increase of 2.51% in accuracy and an average decrease of 0.74% in standard deviation. Compared to the previous state-of-the-art method, FedALL demonstrates a 1.85% improvement in accuracy and a 0.6% improvement in standard deviation.

title: Inside front cover
authors: N/A

title: From Interpretable Learning to Actionable Decisions: A Bounded Preference Neural Network for Multi-Attribute Optimization
authors: Yi Liu, Haiming Liang, Yucheng Dong
abstract: Amidst the digital transformation, businesses face growing complexity in modeling consumer decision-making from multi-attribute data and translating predictions into actionable strategies. The existing machine learning techniques excel in predictive accuracy of customer decision-making, but criticized by lack of explanations. To bridge this gap, this paper designs the Bounded Preference-Constrained Utility Learning Model (BPCULM) and the Customer Segmentation and Profit Maximization (CSPM) model. Firstly, the BPCULM incorporates piecewise linear interpolation and bounded risk preference constraints into its loss function, enabling the learning of economically interpretable, structured utility parameters without sacrificing predictive power. Based on the obtained result by BPCULM, the CSPM model subsequently leverages these parameters to segment customers and formulate a unified profit optimization model that jointly determines attribute improvements, pricing, and budget allocation. The performance of BPCULM is analyzed by comparing with the existing machine learning techniques. Meanwhile, validated on real-world hotel rating data, the CSPM approach demonstrates superior performance in prediction, interpretability, and decision support.

title: CTS-PLL: A Robust and Anytime Framework for Collaborative Task Sequencing and Multi-Agent Path Finding
authors: Junkai Jiang, Yitao Xu, Ruochen Li, Shaobing Xu, Jianqiang Wang
abstract: The Collaborative Task Sequencing and Multi-Agent Path Finding (CTS-MAPF) problem requires agents to accomplish sequences of tasks while avoiding collisions, posing significant challenges due to its combinatorial complexity. This work introduces CTS-PLL, a hierarchical framework that extends the configuration-based CTS-MAPF planning paradigm with two complementary mechanisms for improved robustness and anytime solution quality. First, when the configuration-based planner encounters prolonged stagnation, a lock detection and release module constructs an auxiliary MAPF instance from the current CTS state and invokes complete MAPF method for recovery. Second, after a feasible plan is obtained for a candidate joint task sequence, an intra-sequence refinement procedure is applied to further improve path quality. Extensive evaluations across sparse and dense benchmarks demonstrate that CTS-PLL achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of success rate and solution quality, while maintaining competitive runtime efficiency. Real-world robot experiments further demonstrate the feasibility of the approach in practice.

title: Appearance-Based Cyberbullying Detection in Dialectal Arabic: A New Gulf Body-Shaming Benchmark and MARBERTv2-based Classification Framework
authors: Abeer Albluwi, Maram Albalawi, Sandeep Shiraskar, Arohi Shiraskar, Hossein Beyzavi, Georges Nehmetallah, Frederic Rizk, Patrick Rizk, Rodrigue Rizk, KC Santosh, Dominick Rizk
abstract: Cyberbullying on visual-based social platforms is a growing online safety risk, particularly for young women and adolescents. Appearance-targeted harassment, specifically body-shaming, induces severe psychological harm but remains understudied in the Arabic-speaking world. Existing Arabic cyberbullying research focuses primarily on general offensive language and largely employs Twitter corpora, overlooking culturally and linguistically nuanced body-shaming expressions in Gulf and wider dialectal Arabic. To address this gap, this paper introduces the first Arabic Gulf body-shaming dataset, comprising 14,716 comments collected from TikTok and Instagram and annotated into three categories: non-bullying (NBS), general bullying (B), and explicit body-shaming (BS). We present a transformer-based classification framework by fine-tuning MARBERTv2, a state-of-the-art Arabic language model, for robust detection of appearance-focused harms. Experimental results show that our method achieves 91.02% accuracy and 0.9093 macro F1, outperforming all baseline systems. The dataset and findings provide a foundational benchmark for future research and support emerging digital-safety initiatives across the Gulf region.

title: Performance-Based 3D Object Generation for Engineering under Limited Data
authors: Michael Hohmann, Abdou Diasso, Alexander Windmann, Wolfgang Weber, Oliver Niggemann
abstract: Designing 3D objects often requires meeting specific physical performance constraints. These objects are traditionally designed through iterative, resource-intensive processes. Generative models offer promising alternatives for generating new designs conditioned on physical conditions. However, generative design remains challenging due to the scarcity of 3D datasets labeled with continuous numerical conditions and the tendency of generative models to generate results that deviate from the target conditions, especially in data-limited scenarios. In this work, we propose a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) with a twin-head architecture to learn from small target datasets, we refer to it as the Twin-Head-CVAE. Our approach captures physical relationships in a shared latent space by using a source dataset with a smaller target dataset. We evaluate our approach on 3D point cloud representations of artificial concrete bridge structures using 1%, 10%, and 80% of the dataset as training set. Our results demonstrate a promising approach for generative design in scenarios with limited training data, which also enables the generation of 3D objects beyond the conditions present in the initial training data.

title: Generalizable Machine Learning for Corporate Bankruptcy Prediction: An XGBoost-Based Framework
authors: Graziano Duca, Andrea Bucci, Riccardo Rosati, Luca Romeo
abstract: Accurate prediction of corporate bankruptcy is a fundamental and challenging problem due to the rarity of default events and the heterogeneity of financial data across firms, sectors, and countries. This paper proposes a machine learning framework based on the eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) algorithm for early detection of financial distress in highly imbalanced settings. The inclusion of a scaling XGB parameter mitigates class imbalance, while maintaining interpretability through feature importance analysis. The approach is evaluated across three benchmark datasets (Taiwan, USA, and the Poland Bankruptcy datasets), which represent distinct economic contexts and temporal structures. The extensive experimental results demonstrate that XGB consistently outperforms traditional classifiers, such as Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, and Random Forests, achieving superior generalization across datasets and prediction horizons. Feature importance analysis further highlights profitability, leverage, and liquidity as the most stable and discriminative financial indicators of corporate distress. Overall, the proposed framework offers a robust and generalizable solution for bankruptcy prediction, supporting applications in risk assessment, credit scoring, and financial supervision.

title: Emotion-Aware Voice Monitoring: Mapping Continuous VAD to Operationally Interpretable Affect Levels
authors: Salma Ait Fares, James Birchall
abstract: Speech emotion recognition (SER) has advanced rapidly in commercial applications, but mission-critical domains such as 911 dispatch have seen limited benefit. Conventional SER systems output discrete emotion labels (e.g., happy, sad) that provide little operational support; call-takers need continuous, real-time indicators of caller distress and dispatcher regulation. In this paper, we present an emotion-aware monitoring framework that maps continuous valence-arousal-dominance (VAD) estimates to four interpretable levels, Calm, Alert, Escalate, and Distress, designed to align with how call-takers assess urgency and the need for intervention. We first validate a Wav2Vec2-based VAD model on the IEMOCAP corpus by correlating its outputs with acoustic stress markers (pitch, energy, speaking rate), showing that arousal and dominance are the VAD dimensions most consistently tied to vocal activation. When applied to dual-channel 911 recordings, the same model often places caller and dispatcher in similar high-activation regions, revealing a domain mismatch. To address this, we introduce a speaker-normalized, model-agnostic distress index that transforms VAD trajectories into the four levels using quantile thresholds and temporal smoothing. The resulting level timelines provide interpretable stress indicators suitable for real-time public-safety monitoring.

title: Test Stand for Tentative Determination of Criterion Levels of Damage to Radio-Electronic Components Under the Influence of Powerful Pulses of Electromagnetic Energy
authors: Nina Filimonenko, Kostiantyn Filimonenko, Nataliia Smyrynska, Viacheslav Levandovsky
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: This article examines how to improve experimental research for field conditions of resistance to a powerful electromagnetic pulse of radio-electronic equipment made on the basis of COTS technology, in particular in the nanosecond range of pulse duration. A test stand is proposed for tentatively determining the criterion levels of damage to radio-electronic components under the influence of powerful pulses of electromagnetic energy.

title: Innovative Approaches to the Formation of Electricity Tariffs: Challenges and Opportunities for the Ukrainian Market
authors: Inna Khomenko, Anastasiia Soroka
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article is devoted to the study of innovative approaches to the formation of electricity tariffs and their impact on the Ukrainian energy market. By analyzing current trends in tariff policy and considering examples from international practice, the article reveals the key challenges facing the energy sector. Summarizing the innovative approaches, recommendations are given for optimizing the tariff policy and stimulating the competitiveness of the Ukrainian electricity market.

title: Harmonization of Standards in the Field of Communication Networks and Systems for Automation of Electric Power Enterprises. Applied Modeling Approach
authors: Anatolii Voloshko, Danylo Filyanin
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The main purpose of this article is to present, in accordance with the IEC 61850 series of standards, an applied approach to creating an automated substation system and communications equipment based on information models and modeling methods. The development of an information model for a real substation device should incorporate details about its type as well as the necessary dynamic communication services. The dynamic operation mode of a real device within a substation should be determined by the configured attributes of the information model and the variations in their values. The article provides the necessary data that should form the basis of most information exchanges over the network, and also shows that the names of logical nodes determine the standardized meaning (semantics) of substation devices. The information model is also required to include numerous logical nodes and data attributes. This model should serve as a foundation for the substation configuration language. The substation configuration, in turn, must specify which optional information is utilized in a given device, the names of logical nodes, the available communication channels, the connections of IEDs to the single-line diagram, and any critical details needed for the system's continued development. A hierarchical data structure is used to organize information, as illustrated with a physical device example (BayUnit) that incorporates functions like Distance Protection, Time Overcurrent Protection, and Trip Signal Generation. The corresponding process data, core functionalities, and other critical elements related to the connection component are systematically represented as nodes within this tree structure. It is shown that IEC 61850 is a promising technology, the transition to which from more traditional versions should be carried out consistently. Particularly essential conditions for such a transition are: harmonization of the IEC 61850 series of standards; improvement of tools and equipment; the IEC 61850 standard itself (DSTU IEC); accumulation of experience in operation and technology.

title: Single-Layer Low-Profile Wideband Antenna Based on Non-Uniform Radiating Elements
authors: Thai Nguyen-Dinh, Anh Nguyen-Quang, Hoang Nguyen-Huy, Niamat Hussain
abstract: This paper presents a single-layer low-profile antenna with wideband operation based on non-uniform radiating elements. The antenna is first designed with four square patches with center excitation. Then, multiple patches with different dimensions are positioned on both sides to produce two adjacent resonances in the high frequency. Accordingly, wideband operation with single layer configuration can be obtained. The final antenna has overall dimensions of 1.04 0.61 0.02 at 5.2 GHz. The measured on the fabricated antenna demonstrate wideband operation of 15.4%, ranging from 4.8 to 5.6 GHz. Across this band, the realized gain remains above 5.0 dBi with a peak of 7.9 dBi. Furthermore, the antenna maintains stable radiation patterns, and the front-to-back ratio (FBR) consistently exceeds 16 dB. Compared with existing single-layer wideband designs, the proposed antenna offers the lowest profile while providing a well-balanced combination of bandwidth, gain, and FBR.

title: Predictive Beamforming and Resource Allocation for High-Mobility Cell-Free UAV Networks
authors: Chao Fang, Cheng Zhang, Wen Wang, Pengguang Du, Wei Zhang, Yongming Huang
abstract: Accurate acquisition of channel state information (CSI) is crucial for achieving high-rate communication, yet it introduces significant training overhead and latency, particularly in high-mobility cell-free massive multiple-input multipleoutput (CF-mMIMO) communication systems with unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). To address this challenge, we propose a predictive beamforming and resource allocation framework that significantly reduces training overhead while enhancing system throughput. Specifically, a novel frame structure is designed in which uplink training is performed only in the first time slot of each beam tracking frame, while distributed beam tracking and predictive beamforming are applied in all subsequent slots using the extended Kalman filter (EKF) at each access point (AP). Moreover, we develop a centralized information fusion algorithm that exploits cell-free multi-point cooperation to improve estimation accuracy with low fronthaul overhead. Then, we derive the theoretical posterior Cram´er-Rao bound (PCRB) for the fused estimation and show that, under the local linear-Gaussian approximation, the predicted PCRB coincides with the covariance of the fused estimate. We further establish an explicit analytical mapping between the predicted PCRB and the uplink pilot length. Leveraging this theoretical bridge, we formulate a prediction-aware joint optimization problem involving uplink pilot length, downlink AP-user association, and power allocation to actively adapt the training overhead and maximize the effective sum spectral efficiency (SE). A low-complexity iterative algorithm based on fractional programming is proposed to solve this problem. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves a favorable trade-off between signaling overhead and system throughput. Specifically, it reduces the training overhead by 95% with only a 3% decrease in positioning accuracy, incurs only modest additional fronthaul overhead, and improves the effective sum SE by 31% compared to traditional schemes. Furthermore, comprehensive evaluations show that the framework remains effective under multipath fading and higher UAV velocities, and continues to benefit from cooperative gains in expanded network deployments.

title: DiagConTA: Continual Test-Time Domain Adaptation for Industrial Fault Diagnosis Under Dynamic Working Conditions
authors: Bojian Chen, Jingde Li, Qi Li, Jinchuan Qian, Xinmin Zhang, Changqing Shen, Zhihuan Song
abstract: Dynamic working conditions in industrial systems cause continual distribution shifts in test data that degrade fault diagnosis performance, while vanilla domain adaptation for distribution shifts faces limitations like source data dependence and single-stage adaptation. Continual test-time domain adaptation, an emerging paradigm that integrates continual learning and test-time domain adaptation, has the potential to continually adapt a pretrained model to a series of domains during testing without using source data. In this article, we propose DiagConTA, a novel Continual Test-time Domain Adaptation framework for Industrial Fault Diagnosis under dynamic working conditions. DiagConTA consists of two stages: source domain pretraining and sequential target domain adaptation. In source domain pretraining, we adopt prototype contrastive learning to pretrain the model on the labeled source domain to learn the initial model parameters with better generalization ability. During sequential target domain adaptation, for effective adaptation, we introduce top-$k$ pseudolabeling to provide more reliable labels for the unlabeled instances and maximize mutual information to align the distribution shifts without source data. Meanwhile, considering the catastrophic forgetting and the stability-plasticity dilemma in continual learning, we propose stochastic recovery and prototype-guided contrastive alignment, which explicitly and implicitly constrain the update of the model parameters, respectively. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of DiagConTA in achieving robust fault diagnosis under dynamic working conditions.

title: NMPC-Based Dual-Channel Resilience Against False Data Injection in Pursuit–Evasion Games of Autonomous Surface Vehicles
authors: Ronghui Li, Zhouhua Peng, Nan Gu, Haoliang Wang, Anqing Wang
abstract: This article investigates a secure pursuit–evasion problem involving autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) under dual-channel false data injection (FDI) attacks. In this scenario, the pursuit process is interfered with by the evading ASV through independent tampering of either or both the guidance and control channels of the pursuing ASV. A nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC)-based dual-channel FDI resilience method is proposed for the pursuing ASV such that the capture is to be guaranteed regardless of attacks. In the guidance channel, a min–max pursuit–evasion guidance law is first developed based on NMPC. The pursuing ASV anticipates the worst-case strategy of the evading ASV, and its own optimal strategy is subsequently determined to ensure successful capture. Furthermore, within the NMPC framework, a min–max attack mitigation strategy is proposed to predict the most adversarial attack scenarios from the perspective of the evader and achieve proactive mitigation of the detrimental impacts induced by the attacks. In the control channel, a novel min–max resilience neural network-based control law is introduced. Sharing with the same proactive mitigation mechanism, the control law ensures accurate tracking of the guidance commands regardless of uncertainties, disturbances, and adversarial cyber attacks. Finally, simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed mitigation mechanism in countering dual-channel FDI attacks while ensuring pursuit–evasion performance.

title: Evaluating the Generalizability of Deep Learning Models for Acoustic Impedance across Diverse Process-based Sedimentary Stratigraphic Simulations
authors: Xiao Chen, Shangxu Wang, Genyang Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning has become a powerful tool for seismic inversion, with synthetic data commonly used to augment training datasets in both quantity and quality. However, synthetic elastic models available in geophysical community are sometimes constructed without incorporating geological principles, resulting in a lack of realistic stratigraphic detail. Consequently, the influence of such idealized models and synthetic data on the generalization capability of deep learning models in practical applications is largely unevaluated. To address this, we quantitatively evaluate how variations in stratigraphic rhythmic architecture affect the generalization performance of deep learning models. Our approach employs a process-based sedimentary simulation that explicitly models sediment transport, deposition, and erosion to generate stratigraphy under varied flow velocity and number of sandstone layers. Following sedimentary geological principles, rock-physics models, and the seismic convolutional model, these depth-domain stratigraphic volumes are transformed into time-domain 3D acoustic impedance and corresponding synthetic seismic data. Using these rhythmically varied synthetic datasets, we train a combined CNN-GRU network for acoustic impedance inversion. Results indicate that flow velocity exerts strong control on channel morphology and rhythmic character: channels formed at 2 m/s are approximately 2–3 times deeper and wider than those at 1 m/s. Within datasets sharing the same flow velocity but different sandstone-layer numbers, predictive performance remains robust, with minimal reduction in R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. In contrast, datasets with identical sandstone-layer numbers but different flow velocities lead to significantly higher R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> loss during testing. These findings suggest that deep learning networks encode rhythmic architectural features within each facies type during training. Moreover, the feature-space distance between training and test datasets governs the inversion model’s generalization capability. The proposed framework for generating synthetic training data can be extended to real exploration areas where hydrodynamic conditions are known. Crucially, the feature-space-based evaluation strategy introduced in this study offers an effective methodology for assessing and enhancing the quality of training datasets in applied seismic inversion.

title: Joint Optimized Supervised and Self-Supervised Embedding Learning for Legal Compliance in documents for Public Companies
authors: Gabriel Bertocco, Alexandre Pacelli, Vinicius Dias, Tiago Carvalho
abstract: Automatic legal compliance verification has attracted increasing attention in recent years due to its potential to assess whether legal documents conform to regulatory requirements with minimal human intervention. Typically, legal documents are segmented into text chunks that form a searchable database, from which relevant passages are retrieved to answer compliance queries or verify adherence to specific laws. In this context, we propose Joint Optimized Supervised and self-supervised Embedding learning (JOSE), a hybrid retrieval framework based on a two-phase training pipeline designed to identify document passages related to a given legal regulation. In the first phase, the retriever is trained in a supervised manner using labeled pairs composed of legal documents passages and their corresponding regulatory references. In the second phase, the supervised retriever is used to generate additional pseudo-labeled pairs from unlabeled documents, which are then merged with the original labeled dataset to form a unified training corpus. The retriever is subsequently retrained from its initial weights using both manually labeled and automatically generated data. The proposed self-supervised learning strategy is evaluated using a dataset provided by the Brazilian Securities and Exchange Commission (CVM), containing labeled data from 2023 and 2024 and unlabeled documents from prior years (2020–2022). Experimental results demonstrate that the retriever trained exclusively on pseudo-labeled data achieves performance comparable to a model trained solely on labeled data, while the combined dataset yields improved retrieval performance, particularly at top-ranked results (Rank-1).

title: Sustainable Horizons: Energy Efficiency in B5G Space-Air-Ground Integrated Networks
authors: Akanksha Sharma, Sharda Tripathi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: With the escalating demand for ubiquitous connectivity and the rapid proliferation of devices in Information and Communication Technology (ICT), energy optimization has become a fundamental challenge in the design of future wireless networks. Further, the integration of machine intelligence into daily life will cause the number of active wireless devices to multiply dramatically, intensifying the energy burden on existing infrastructures. To address this, Beyond Fifth Generation (B5G) networks are exploring the convergence of Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs), including satellites and aerial platforms, with Terrestrial Networks (TNs) to expand coverage and capacity. Operating at sub-THz and THz frequency bands, these networks require dense deployments of access points to overcome severe atmospheric and absorption losses, further complicating energy management. The resulting Space-Air-Ground Integrated Network (SAGIN) paradigm offers a multi-layered approach to connectivity but introduces complex energy trade-offs due to its heterogeneous and large-scale nature. This survey provides an in-depth review of Energy Efficiency (EE) challenges across the different tiers of SAGIN. We analyze relevant EE metrics and investigate emerging techniques and architectural modifications designed to optimize energy consumption. Next, we propose a novel energy-efficient SAGIN architecture that unifies terrestrial, aerial, and satellite segments under a dedicated EE layer for real-time, adaptive, and self-sustaining energy optimization, and demonstrate its effectiveness through a detailed case-study. Finally, we highlight the key future research directions to further enhance EE and sustainability in next-generation B5G wireless networks.

title: Get the MOST out of LEO Satellite Constellations Topology Design
authors: Robert Esswein, Quincy Bayer, Samuel Mergendahl, Mai Abdelhakim, Robert K. Cunningham
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: As Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite deployments scale to thousands of nodes, the design of satellite network topologies becomes increasingly complex yet critical for achieving the desired performance. Since each LEO satellite can only provide service to a small area on the ground, inter-satellite communication and sophisticated networking are required to achieve the best performance. Differing application-specific performance metrics and associated trade-offs can drive changes to topology design that optimize for specific use cases. However, satellite constellation topology design is not amenable to traditional optimization methods, such as linear programming, due to the complex interactions between different design parameters, especially as the number of satellites in the constellation grows. In this paper, we propose the Multifaceted Optimized Satellite Constellation Topology (MOST) framework, a framework that optimizes across multiple arbitrary performance metrics when creating a satellite constellation network topology, allowing a constellation designer to tune the network topology for specific applications. This framework is then solved using the proposed MOST algorithm, a cubic complexity algorithm that optimizes across the selected metrics. We present the LEO Approximate Minimum Propagation Delay, Up Time (LAMP-UP) topology, a topology produced with the MOST algorithm that optimizes for low latency and high link uptime. This topology is evaluated under different traffic models against existing constellation topologies in terms of network latency, capacity, and computational complexity. We demonstrate that the proposed LAMP-UP topology increases the network capacity by 3.2% over other recent topologies and 140.5% over the static topology used by the Iridium constellation with no hardware overhead.

title: Unified Optimization of STAR-RIS-Assisted Uplink Multiple Access in Near- and Far-Field
authors: Seunghyun Oh, Luiggi Cantos, Jinho Choi, Hyundong Shin, Yun Hee Kim
abstract: This paper investigates near-field channel effects in a simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS)–assisted uplink system with a multi-antenna base station. The considered system is based on generalized non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) with imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC), encompassing space division multiple access (SDMA) and NOMA with perfect SIC as special cases. For this general setting, we develop sum-rate maximization algorithms with and without quality-of-service (QoS) constraints using an alternating optimization (AO) framework and a penalty-based nonlinear optimization (NLO) method, providing flexible performance–complexity trade-offs. We also derive an upper bound on the achievable sum rate to characterize multiplexing and beamforming gains under both near- and far-field channel conditions. The results show that AO initialized with the proposed NLO solution achieves the best performance with moderate computational complexity, aligning well with the upper-bound behavior. Furthermore, analysis and simulations demonstrate that near-field channels increase the available degrees of freedom and provide enhanced robustness to imperfect SIC and QoS constraints.
