title: Dynamic Reconfigurations of Robot Categorization in Children Group Interaction
authors: Nicoleta Radu, Antonia Krummheuer, Matthias Rehm, Zhennan Yi, Selma Sabanovic, Randy Gomez
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: This study investigates how robot categorization is dynamically reconfigured in children's group interaction. Drawing on Membership Categorization Analysis and Conversation Analysis, we examine a video-recorded photo-sharing activity mediated by the social robot Haru. Rather than treating the robot's role as fixed by design, we analyze how children sequentially and multimodally negotiate what the robot is, what it knows or can do, and how it should be related to in the unfolding interaction. The findings demonstrate that children rapidly shift between treating the robot as person-like, socially accountable, knowledgeable, or mechanical, sometimes within only a few conversational turns. When oriented to as person-like, the robot becomes subject to moral evaluation and affiliative touch; when treated as knowledgeable, it is addressed as capable of understanding and procedural action; when framed as mechanical, its conduct is attributed to circuitry rather than comprehension. These categorial reconfigurations occur without corresponding changes in the robot's behavior, revealing an asymmetry between children's flexible sense-making and the robot's fixed system responses. Building on this analysis, we propose a three-domain model of dynamic robot categorization encompassing ontological, epistemic, and social-relational orientations. The study highlights that robots in group settings are not socially stable entities but are continuously re-specified in interaction. This interactionally grounded perspective contributes to research on socially impactful robotics by demonstrating how robot participation is collaboratively accomplished and renegotiated in practice.

title: A new early stopping proposal for efficient generative model inversion attacks
authors: F. Moreno, A. Rivera, M.J. del Jesus, M. D. Pérez-Godoy
abstract: Adversarial inversion methods have emerged as a significant threat to the privacy of deep learning models; however, their practical application is often hindered by high computational costs. Furthermore, prolonged training stages can increase the risks that a model may encounter during its learning process, which can result in a deterioration of the generalization and effectiveness of the attack. To address these limitations, we propose MM0_FID, a novel early stopping strategy designed for generative model inversion attacks. Our approach integrates a specialized loss function with a quality measure to dynamically identify the optimal termination point of the training process. By stopping the training process once optimal training quality is achieved, MM0_FID reduces the number of required iterations while mitigating overfitting. We evaluated our proposal through an extensive experimental framework involving diverse datasets and target architectures of varying complexity. The results demonstrate that MM0_FID substantially reduces computational overhead and execution time without compromising the fidelity of the reconstructed data, proving to be an efficient and robust solution for optimizing generative model inversion attacks.

title: PersoMoni: A Comprehensive Video-Based Benchmark Dataset for Fine-grained Personality Assessment with 15 Trait Dimensions
authors: Feng-Qi Cui, Jinyang Huang, Sirui Zhao, Kun Li, Zhi Liu, Meng Li, Ziyu Jia, Dan Guo, Meng Wang
abstract: Understanding personality from visual behavior remains challenging due to the limitations of existing personality trait identification (PTI) benchmarks, which are typically based on short, crowd-annotated clips and thus capture only coarse, impressionistic judgments. These datasets lack temporal continuity, clinical validity, and, crucially, the fine-grained sub trait structure emphasized in psychological assessment. To ad dress these gaps, we introduce PersoMoni, a clinically grounded benchmark designed for fine-grained, temporally rich personality computing. The dataset contains 168 full-length psychological interviews conducted by licensed counselors, producing over 23000 aligned facial video segments. Each participant is an notated using the full BFI-2 taxonomy, providing continuous labels for five major traits and, for the first time, fifteen validated sub-traits, which marks the first benchmark to extend personality granularity from 5 to 15 dimensions. This design enables long-horizon modeling, regression-based evaluation, and detailed analysis of subtle behavioral markers in ecologically valid settings. Leveraging this benchmark, we reveal two limitations of current PTI systems, i.e., limited sensitivity to sparse and weak but psychologically meaningful behavioral cues, and modeling difficulty to inter-individual variability across long interactions. To tackle these issues, we propose PSM-Net, a personality-aware sparse modeling framework that couples 3D CNN–based encoding with sparsity-driven temporal refinement to enhance trait-discriminative signals. Extensive experiments across compensatively diverse architectures, i.e., 2D CNNs, 3D CNNs, and Transformers, show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing approaches on both the Big Five traits and all fifteen BFI-2 sub-traits. The dataset and source code are released at https://github.com/QIcita/PersoMoni.

title: FocusKG: A Novel Multimodal Knowledge Graph Dataset With Temporal Information for Link Prediction
authors: Yifan Xue, Yingyao Ma, Wanqiang Cai, Rubing Duan, Jiasong Wu, Lotfi Senhadji, Huazhong Shu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Knowledge graphs (KGs) play a central role in enabling structured reasoning for AI applications. Recent research has advanced two prominent extensions of KGs: multimodal KGs, which integrate diverse data sources such as text, images, audio, and video; and temporal KGs, which capture the dynamic evolution of knowledge over time. However, these two paradigms remain largely disjoint—multimodal KGs typically ignore temporal dynamics, while temporal KGs overlook rich multimodal context. To bridge this gap, we introduce FocusKG, the first discrete-time multimodal knowledge graph that unifies four modalities (text, image, audio, and video) with temporal annotations. Built from the Focus news program, FocusKG captures real-world events as temporally grounded multimodal facts, offering new expressiveness for time-sensitive tasks. We further propose a Discrete-Time Multimodal Knowledge Graph Embedding (DTME) method, a novel framework tailored for learning representations over temporal multimodal KGs. DTME is composed of three key modules: (1) Multimodal Preprocessing, which encodes raw inputs from each modality into vector representations using modality-specific encoders; (2) Cross-Modal Graph Enhancer, which captures semantic interplay between entity and relation modalities through role-aware subgraph construction and a modality interaction network; and (3) Multi-Branch Time Aggregation, which injects temporal signals by modeling interactions between time and modality-aware features. Extensive experiments demonstrate FocusKG's value and DTME's efficacy. On link prediction, DTME outperforms state-of-the-art multimodal KG models and temporal KG models. We release FocusKG as a benchmark to foster research in multimodal temporal reasoning.

title: HIVE-6D: Hierarchical Visual-Tactile In-Hand Pose Estimation and Tracking
authors: Jin Liu, Weihao Wang, Hongzhan Yu, Can Zhao, Haonan Zhao, Daolin Ma, Weiming Wang
abstract: Robust in-hand 6D pose estimation is essential for dexterous robotic manipulation. Existing approaches typically rely on vision or tactile sensing, yet both modalities have inherent limitations: vision provides global pose cues but degrades under hand–object occlusion, while tactile remains available during contact but is local and often geometrically ambiguous. Moreover, their reliability varies across manipulation stages, making effective fusion challenging. In this work, we propose HIVE 6D, a hierarchical visual–tactile framework with two-level fusion for in-hand 6D pose estimation and tracking. At the feature level, an asymmetric anchor-and-refine design first establishes a global pose anchor from vision and then performs tactile residual refinement using a render-and-compare strategy with vision guided cross-attention. At the policy level, an uncertainty-aware routing mechanism dynamically selects the trusted modality and refinement anchor over time to stabilize tracking under contact and occlusion. Extensive experiments in simulation and on a real robot show that HIVE-6D improves single-frame pose accuracy, strengthens long-horizon tracking robustness, and increases success rates on a real-world peg insertion task, including under external disturbances.

title: Enhancing Self-Supervised Monocular Depth Estimation via Dual-Branch Local Distillation and Structural Priors
authors: Ting Yu, Shan Pan, Wei Chen, Wenping Bi, Zhijie Li, Zehua Wang, F. Richard Yu, Victor C. M. Leung
abstract: Perceiving scene depth and 3D structure is fundamental for environmental comprehension and interaction in Internet of Video Things (IoVT) devices. Self-supervised monocular depth estimation, which leverages photometric consistency across sequential video frames without requiring ground-truth labels from active sensors, has emerged as a compelling paradigm. Nevertheless, this paradigm suffers from inherent limitations in low-texture or occluded regions, where photometric supervision becomes ambiguous or invalid, typically leading to structural degradation and indistinct object boundaries when inferring depth. To mitigate this limitation, we propose a dual-branch local distillation framework that harnesses priors from depth foundation models (DFMs) to alleviate depth ambiguity and improve fine-grained estimation accuracy. Specifically, guided by a frozen DFM, the framework synergistically employs local-context and cross-context supervision to optimize a student network, facilitating robust perception of depth discontinuities and precise boundary modeling. Furthermore, to enhance the spatial representation within a lightweight architecture, we design DE-LiteMono as the student model, which recovers geometric details through a detail enhancement block and fusion modules. Moreover, a spatial distance consistency loss is introduced to explicitly model the relative geometric topology in the image plane, providing robust structural guidance. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks, including KITTI, Cityscapes, and Make3D, demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art self-supervised methods, achieving sharper depth boundaries, reduced ambiguity, and superior quantitative performance.

title: A Hierarchical Framework for the Management of Carpooling Using Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Gaetano Volpe, Antonio Salcuni, Ruotian Liu, Agostino Marcello Mangini, Maria Pia Fanti
abstract: The growing diffusion of Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) is reshaping urban mobility by enabling new forms of intelligent transportation, such as shared and on-demand services. Among these, carpooling with AVs represents a promising solution to reduce traffic congestion, emissions, and operational costs.This paper presents a Hierarchical framework for carpooling management based on two strategies: i) a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) model to optimize the sequence of pick-ups and drop-offs performed by AVs, and ii) a rerouting algorithm for selecting the best route considering dynamic traffic conditions. The carpooling problem is formalized using the Markov Decision Process framework. The proposed approach is implemented using the Proximal Policy Optimization algorithm and validated through simulations in the Simulation of Urban Mobility (SUMO) environment.As a case study, the cities of Bari (Italy) and Tampere (Finland) are analyzed to assess model performance in real urban scenarios. Results show that the proposed method improves route efficiency and supports the development of intelligent and sustainable mobility systems.

title: Dual UAV-NOMA based Covert Communications: A Perspective on Spatio-Temporal-Energy Domain Cooperative Differentiation
authors: Miao Liu, Bufan Guo, Haitao Zhao, Chun Zhu, Yue Yin
abstract: This paper investigates covert communications in an uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) assisted cellular system in the presence of a ground eavesdropper(Eav). To enhance the secrecy performance of a secure user (SU) while guaranteeing the quality-of-service (QoS) requirements of common users, a dual-UAV cooperative non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) framework with a two-slot transmission scheme, referred to as Spatio-Temporal-Energy Domain Cooperative NOMA (STEDC-NOMA), is proposed. In the considered architecture, an aerial base station (UAV-BS) and an aerial relay (UAV-Relay) collaboratively serve ground users, where the transmission is divided into two time slots and different common users are paired with the SU in each slot for NOMA superposition. By jointly exploiting temporal-domain two-slot cooperation, spatio-domain dual-UAV diversity, and energy-domain NOMA superposition, additional degrees of freedom are introduced for covert communication with fairer access. Based on this framework, a joint optimization problem of user pairing and resource allocation is formulated to maximize the overall secrecy capacity of the SU under the QoS constraints of common users. To support the proposed scheduling strategy, closed-form power allocation solutions are derived for the two slot respectively. Numerical results demonstrate that, compared with the previous benchmark schemes, the proposed STEDC-NOMA significantly improves the secrecy performance while satisfying the QoS demands and enhancing the access fairness of all users.

title: GPS-PPS Synchronized Ultrasonic Ranging for Low-Cost Compact Drone Swarms
authors: Raghavendra Pal, Suresh Dahiya, Dipti Rana, Anand Darji
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The paper presents design of drone swarm system with compact formation. It can be used for coordinated surveying missions. A low cost hardware module based on ultrasonic sensors and ESP32 TTGO module is used to measure distance from one drone to another drone in real time. One of the important feature is that we have used the Pulse Per Second (PPS) feature of the GPS to obtain a microsecond level time synchronization. It helped to measure accurate flight time distance measurements. The conventional GPS data is combined with ultrasonic ranging information to maintain tightly coordinated formation. It is not possible with using GPS alone. The hardware is implemented as a prototype on multiple drones using this system. Obtained results show that the hardware maintains precise inter drone spacing. Thus, formation control is enhanced for drone swarm systems. We have compared the proposed method with existing works in terms of several parameters as well. The most attractive feature is the low cost implementation of our hardware.

title: SAILS: Segment Anything with Incrementally Learned Semantics for Task-Invariant and Training-Free Continual Learning
authors: Shishir Muralidhara, Didier Stricker, René Schuster
abstract: Continual learning remains constrained by the need for repeated retraining, high computational costs, and the persistent challenge of forgetting. These factors significantly limit the applicability of continual learning in real-world settings, as iterative model updates require significant computational resources and inherently exacerbate forgetting. We present SAILS – Segment Anything with Incrementally Learned Semantics, a training-free framework for Class-Incremental Semantic Segmentation (CISS) that sidesteps these challenges entirely. SAILS leverages foundational models to decouple CISS into two stages: Zero-shot region extraction using Segment Anything Model (SAM), followed by semantic association through prototypes in a fixed feature space. SAILS incorporates selective intra-class clustering, resulting in multiple prototypes per class to better model intra-class variability. Our results demonstrate that, despite requiring no incremental training, SAILS typically surpasses the performance of existing training-based approaches on standard CISS datasets, particularly in long and challenging task sequences where forgetting tends to be most severe. By avoiding parameter updates, SAILS completely eliminates forgetting and maintains consistent, task-invariant performance. Furthermore, SAILS exhibits positive backward transfer, where the introduction of new classes can enhance performance on previous classes.

title: High-Accuracy Encoder-Based Position Control of DC Gearmotors Using a Low-Cost Embedded Platform
authors: Jaswanth Talluri, Siva Naga Purnima Madala, Charan Tej Pasupuleti, Mahesh Miriyala
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Accurate motor position control is a fundamental requirement in autonomous rovers and mobile robotic systems, where precise angular displacement directly determines navigation accuracy and trajectory stability. Unlike conventional speed-regulation approaches that focus on maintaining constant rotational velocity, position control requires the motor to reach and stop at predefined angular targets with minimal overshoot. This paper presents the design and experimental validation of a low-cost closed-loop PID position control system implemented on an ESP32 microcontroller for encoder-based N20 DC gearmotors. Independent discrete-time PID loops are executed for each motor, with gains tuned experimentally to balance rise time, overshoot, and steady-state accuracy. The proposed system achieves repeatable position tracking with a maximum deviation of ±6 encoder ticks per revolution, corresponding to approximately ±1.13% positional error. Experimental results demonstrate stable transient response, effective overshoot suppression through derivative action, and consistent performance across multiple trials. The findings confirm that high-accuracy motor position control can be achieved using cost-constrained embedded hardware, making the approach suitable for autonomous rover platforms, educational robotics, and multi-actuator embedded motion-control applications.

title:
Cs
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    AgBiBr
                    <sub>6</sub>
                    Perovskite Solar Cells: Insights from Absorber and Transport Layer Engineering
authors: Manas Ranjan Tripathy, Raghvendra Shukla, Saswat Kumar Ram, Sachchidanand, Ashutosh Kumar Dikshit, Sandeep Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Lead-free halide perovskites (LFPs) are quickly gaining popularity as alternatives to harmful lead-based perovskites. However, widespread use is hampered by low power conversion efficiency (PCE). Here, we report a comprehensive simulation study on an unconventional double perovskite absorber, Cs<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>AgBiBr<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf>, within a standard n–i–p device architecture. The simulations were carried out using CNTS as the common HTL, while different ETLs were explored: (1) AZnO, yielding the highest efficiency having V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">OC</inf> of 1.375 V, J<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SC</inf> of 21.37 mA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, FF of 80%, and PCE of 23.50%; (2) CdZnS, achieving V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">OC</inf> of 1.372 V, J<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SC</inf> of 21.41 mA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, FF of 78.82%, and PCE of 23.15%; and (3) LBSO, resulting in V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">OC</inf> of 1.373 V, J<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SC</inf> of 21.40 mA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, FF of 79.72%, and PCE = 23.42%. In all cases, Cs<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>AgBiBr<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf> served as the absorber layer. These findings provide a potential route for the rational design of efficient and stable LFPs solar cells.

title: AI-Enabled Smart Blind Stick Using Embedded Sensors: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Himanshi Pujari, Sneha Negi, Krishan Chandra Mishra, Khushi Verma, Bhumika Budhlakoti
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Embedded electronics, artificial intelligence and the latest sensing systems have accelerated the development of smart assistive technologies to support the blind. This review examines the recent advancement in smart blind sticks which are expected to enhance mobility, detect obstacles, improve situational awareness and make navigation safer to visually impaired persons. It assesses the sensing techniques, such as ultrasonic ranging, infrared measuring, and camera-based recognition with low-power board sensors, such as the ESP32-CAM. To determine the effect of these new computational methods on accuracy, responsiveness, and real-time performance, new convolutional neural networks, lightweight object-detection models, sensor-fusion strategies, and edge-AI processing are studied. New advancements in haptic feedback systems, voice-assist functions, GPS-based navigation, and IoT-enabled emergency communication are also discussed in the review. The existing trends are explainable AI to provide clarity in making decisions, energy-efficient hardware to make it portable, and privacy-safe mechanisms to process everything on the device. Overall, the review shows the potential that smart assistive devices have in enhancing the independence of movements and providing safer and more informed navigation to visually impaired users.

title: Design of Termination Resistance-Adjusted Passive Equalizer for Low-Power and High-Speed Signaling
authors: Seonghi Lee, Sanguk Lee, Seunghun Ryu, Hyunwoo Kim, Seongho Woo, Jinwook Lee, Jiseong Kim, Seungyoung Ahn
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: In this paper, a termination-resistance-adjusted passive equalization method is proposed to improve the signal-integrity performance while reducing power consumption. By increasing the receiver termination resistance, the proposed structure provides an inherent pre-emphasis effect at the receiver, while the resulting reflection components are structurally exploited to achieve de-emphasis and inter-symbol interference (ISI) mitigation. To analyze and design the proposed structure, a lattice-diagram-based reflection analysis and a state-transition function–based time-domain model are introduced. The proposed model enables separation of the channel response and the passive equalizer response, allowing efficient single-bit response computation and rapid design optimization by means of a PAM-4 peak distortion analysis (PDA). The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through simulations and measurements in a 32-Gbps single-ended PAM-4 signaling environment. Experimental results show that the proposed passive equalizer achieves a larger eye opening than conventional passive and active equalizers under the same supply voltage while consuming the lowest amount of power among all tested equalizers. Moreover, under identical eye-height conditions, the proposed structure enables up to 39.3% power reduction by operating at a lower supply voltage, demonstrating its suitability for power-efficient high-speed interfaces.

title: What Should Physics Foundation Model Benchmarks Measure?
authors: Shuai Jiang, Alejandro Francisco Queiruga
abstract: Physics foundation models are evaluated primarily on in-distribution accuracy at training resolutions. This measures in-domain approximation and not whether models have learned continuous physics. We propose validation protocols along two axes: out-of-distribution generalization (do models trained on easy problems generalize gracefully to hard ones?) and temporal consistency (do models produce coherent dynamics under resolution changes?). These benchmarks are straightforward to implement and would clarify whether current models are learning physics or memorizing discrete computational artifacts.

title: Influence of Thermocouple Position and Design Cavities on Temperature Measurement and Heat Distribution during Induction Hardening
authors: Vojtěch Vurm, David Rot
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Contact temperature measurements are widely used to validate numerical models of induction hardening; however, thermocouple installation can disturb the electromagnetic and thermal fields. This study evaluates the influence of Type K thermocouple position on temperature measurements during scanning induction hardening of cylindrical steel workpieces. A coupled electromagnetic–thermal finite-element model (ANSYS Maxwell 3D and Icepak) with constant material properties was used as a comparative sensitivity tool to identify critical sensor locations. The model predictions were compared with experiments on tool steel EN 40CrMnMoS8-6 using surface-mounted and embedded thermocouples. Embedded sensors require drilled holes, which were modelled as cylindrical air cavities. An axis of the hole located 1 mm below the heated surface produced a local hot spot; across seven repeated hardening cycles the temperature inside the cavity exceeded the nearby surface temperature by 76 ± 8 °C. The effect is expected to increase with decreasing workpiece diameter. Surface-mounted thermocouples may also influence local quenching conditions. Electromagnetic interference was observed only for thermocouples exposed directly to the coil field without a workpiece and was negligible when the thermocouple was welded to the grounded steel workpiece. The results provide practical guidelines for minimising measurement artefacts when validating induction hardening simulations.

title: From LLM Reasoning to Autonomous AI Agents: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Mohamed Amine Ferrag, Norbert Tihanyi, Merouane Debbah
abstract: Large language models and autonomous AI agents have evolved rapidly, resulting in a diverse array of evaluation benchmarks, frameworks, and collaboration protocols. Driven by the growing need for standardized evaluation and integration, we systematically consolidate these fragmented efforts into a unified framework. However, the landscape remains fragmented and lacks a unified taxonomy or comprehensive survey. Therefore, we present a side-by-side comparison of benchmarks developed between 2019 and 2025 that evaluate these models and agents across multiple domains. In addition, we propose a taxonomy of approximately 60 benchmarks that cover general and academic knowledge reasoning, mathematical problem-solving, code generation and software engineering, factual grounding and retrieval, domain-specific evaluations, multimodal and embodied tasks, task orchestration, and interactive assessments. Furthermore, we review AI-agent frameworks introduced between 2023 and 2025 that integrate large language models with modular toolkits to enable autonomous decision-making and multi-step reasoning. Moreover, we present real-world applications of autonomous AI agents in materials science, biomedical research, academic ideation, software engineering, synthetic data generation, chemical reasoning, mathematical problem-solving, geographic information systems, multimedia, healthcare, and finance. We then survey key agent-to-agent collaboration protocols, namely the Agent Communication Protocol (ACP), the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and the Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A). Finally, we discuss recommendations for future research, focusing on advanced reasoning strategies, failure modes in multi-agent LLM systems, automated scientific discovery, dynamic tool integration via reinforcement learning, integrated search capabilities, and security vulnerabilities in agent protocols.

title: An Open Simulation Platform for Team Training in Robotic Surgery
authors: Yihui Yao, Rayhan Papar, Haochen Wei, Peter Kazanzides
abstract: The predominant paradigm for robotic minimallyinvasive surgery places the main surgeon at a console, teleoperating robotic instruments inside the patient, with an assistant surgeon providing support at the bedside. We present an open-source simulation platform for training this robotic surgery team that integrates the console from a da Vinci Research Kit (dVRK) for the main surgeon, a haptic device (repurposed from an existing simulator) for the assistant surgeon, a simulation environment built on the Asynchronous Multi-Body Framework (AMBF), and an Augmented Reality (AR) interface on a head-mounted display (HMD) to provide a common training environment. Our system emulates a realistic surgical scenario in which the dVRK console enables the surgeon to control the patient-side manipulators while the haptic device provides both control and tactile feedback for the first assistant (FA) operating a virtual laparoscopic grasper, with the HMD providing visualization of the extra-corporeal parts of the robot. A user study with 6 teams (12 subjects total), each performing a retraction and suturing task with both a physical system and our proposed simulator, demonstrates that our simulation platform effectively replicates key aspects of team-based surgical training.

title: Identifying Smartphone-Using Pedestrians on Railway Platforms via Deep Learning–Driven Point Cloud Data
authors: H. Mieda, C. Premachandra
abstract: While the widespread use of smartphones has made life more convenient by facilitating communication and information access, it has also led to an increase in smartphone-related accidents. In particular, falling accidents on station platforms can impact not only the individuals who fall but also other passengers. Although the overall number of falling accidents from station platforms is on a declining trend, those caused by walking while using smartphones are increasing. To prevent such accidents, platform doors are being installed, but due to cost constraints, they are present at only 10% of stations nationwide in the case of country like Japan. Additionally, surveillance cameras have been installed, but challenges such as privacy concerns and reduced visibility at night remain unresolved. Previous studies have proposed methods to detect walking smartphone users by installing depth cameras on station platforms and analyzing the captured data. However, these methods primarily focus on individual pedestrians.In this study, we aimed to address situations involving multiple pedestrians by capturing data from depth cameras that include multiple individuals. After downsampling the data, we trained a deep learning model to detect walking smartphone users. Verification experiments of the proposed method demonstrated high-accuracy detection results.

title: Predictive Model of Solar Power Plant Generation after Missile Attack
authors: Andriy Pavlov, Oleksandr Moroz, Oleksandr Miroshnyk, Sergii Moroz, Andriy Milenin, Mohamed Qawaqzeh
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The results of experimental studies of the operation parameters of the solar power plant, which has damage to its electrical equipment as a result of rocket fire, and was put into operation after removing completely damaged elements of the plant, carrying out repairs of partially damaged equipment and creating new electrical circuits. The industrial solar power plant, with a design capacity of 2.4 MW of photovoltaic modules, is located near the city of Merefa, Kharkiv region. The results of experimental studies of the parameters of the station's operation made it possible to create a predictive model of generation with electrical equipment that has partial damage and whose characteristics differ from the passport data. The parameters of the average hourly specific power of the station in the hours of the maximum angle of the sun above the horizon on clear days, the average hourly specific power at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> cloud cover, the correction coefficients of the average hourly specific power, as well as the characteristics of the change in the temperature of the surface of photovoltaic modules depending on the generation power were determined. The forecast model also considers the average hourly angle of the sun above the horizon, the average hourly azimuth of the sun, the average hourly air temperature, the average hourly cloudiness, the state of the surface and the influence of precipitation and fog. The model created using Microsoft Excel provides forecasting results in graphical and tabular forms. The accuracy of the forecasting results of this program in 2023 exceeded the accuracy of the forecasting results of the state enterprise Guaranteed buyer by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$269 \text{MW}^{*} ~\mathrm{h}$</tex> or 11.26 %.

title: Increasing the Energy Efficiency of the Heating System of Substances in Tanks
authors: Mykhailo Spodoba, Oleksandr Spodoba
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article considers the possibility of reducing the consumption of electric energy for electric heating of matter in a vertical cylindrical reactor of small volume. The main hypothesis is to reduce the duration of heating the substance and increase the energy efficiency of the heating system due to the reduction of its operation time, using not monolithic stirrer paddles on which the heater is installed, but hollow ones with the use of wax as their filler. Research methods used in the article: construction of a mathematical model using differential equations and taking into account Reynolds, Nusselt and Prandl criteria. The work presents differential equations that complement the mathematical model of the dynamics of changes in energy parameters of objects located in the tank when using an electrothermomechanical system, taking into account the physical and chemical composition of the substance located in the cavities of the paddles. The given differential equations make it possible to determine the amount of electrical energy spent on heating the substance, based on the dependences of temperature changes of objects obtained by the numerical method. As a result of mathematical research, graphical dependences of changes in the temperature of the wax were obtained, which is a filler in the cavities of the blades on which the heating cable is placed, and it was established that the use of a metal blade, the cavity of which is filled with wax, and an electric heating cable is mounted on its surface, for heating a volume of substances 0.04 m 3 consumes is 10.8 % less energy compared to a monolithic metal paddle. Despite the positive results obtained from the use of filler in the cavity of the mixer paddle, it is advisable to conduct further research to consider the effect of other fillers on the energy performance of the heating system and their use in larger tanks.

title: Guest Editorial: Special Issue on Emotion AI and Sentiment Analysis in Social Systems
authors: Gwanggil Jeon, Xiaochun Cheng, Abdellah Chehri, David Camacho, Feng Xia, Joel Rodrigues
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems

title: From Robust Networks to AI-Ready Parcel Locker Systems: A Multilayer Perspective on Design, Demand and Resilience
authors: Francesco Cauteruccio, Enrico Corradini
abstract: The growth of e-commerce is intensifying pressure on last-mile delivery, making robust and equitable parcel locker networks a key urban challenge. Modeling city-scale locker systems as two-mode multilayer networks, and analysing them through Dual and Coverage centrality, reveals hidden vulnerabilities and "bridge" lockers that classical coverage-based siting overlooks, as shown for Milan, Rome, and Naples. In this discussion paper we argue that this network-centric view is a natural backbone for AI-ready parcel locker systems. We distill the main lessons from multilayer analysis, such as bipartite modelling, hub-bridge separation, and robustness curves, and outline how to couple them with modern AI methods: (i) learning-based demand models to replace potential-access weights with behaviourally grounded flows; (ii) graph representation learning and graph neural networks to approximate centrality and failure impact in real time; and (iii) reinforcement learning and bandit formulations for sequential locker siting and capacity decisions. Our contribution is conceptual: we sketch an integrated research agenda where multilayer centrality, AI-driven demand estimation, and learning-based policy search jointly inform adaptive, data-driven, and resilience-aware locker planning.

title: Tension-based Dynamic Control for Rope-Driven Robots under Edge Collisions
authors: Myeongjin Choi, Sahoon Ahn, Doyoung Park, Hwa Soo Kim, TaeWon Seo
abstract: This paper presents a tension-based dynamic control framework with torque-level tension tracking for a rope-driven ascending robot (RDAR), which differs from the position-based control schemes used in previous RDAR studies. The robot operates in unstructured environments where rope–edge collisions introduce significant disturbances such as anisotropic friction and tension loss. To address these effects, a hierarchical control architecture is developed that integrates a sliding-mode controller with adaptive gain (SMC-AG) and a disturbance observer (DOB). The high-level controller generates tension references using real-time tension feedback to reject collision-induced disturbances, while the low-level controller directly tracks these tensions in the motor-torque domain via PID control with model-based feedforward compensation derived from ascender dynamics. Lyapunov-based analysis guarantees closed-loop stability. A compact rope–edge experiment characterizes collision-induced friction and supports the disturbance modeling assumptions. Real-time tension regulation further mitigates rope hysteresis and improves responsiveness. Experimental results across various trajectories and speeds show that the proposed controller reduces the RMS tracking error by up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$80\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and the peak error by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$20\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> compared to conventional position-based PID with feedforward compensation, demonstrating strong robustness against edge interactions.

title: Modeling Mind and Mood with CLAIRE: Neuro-Symbolic Meta-Cognition for Interpretable Emotion Recognition
authors: Vishwa Bhatt, Divya Thakar, Dylan M. Diaz, Nainesh Rajani, Khalil Dajani, Jennifer Kim Jin
abstract: This study presents CLAIRE (Cognitive-Linguistic Architecture for Interpretable Reasoning and Emotion), a unified framework that integrates cognitive modeling, transformer-based language understanding, and neuro-symbolic reasoning to advance affective computing. CLAIRE incorporates cognitive priors derived from simulated attentional decay, entropy dynamics, and error accumulation to emulate human-like focus stability. These priors guide a transformer-based architecture that combines DeBERTa-v3 fine-tuning with entropy regularization and meta-cognitive control, enabling interpretable emotion recognition and psychologically grounded reasoning. Experiments are conducted on stratified subsets of GoEmotions (10k samples) and SemEval-2018 (5k samples) under controlled computational constraints to ensure consistent and reproducible evaluation across all model variants. The results demonstrated that CLAIRE 3.0 improved classification accuracy by up to 11%, reduced entropy variance by 35%, and increased affective correlation (r) by 0.10 compared to baseline BERT models. Furthermore, interpretability analyses using SHAP showed that CLAIRE 3.0 prioritized semantically meaningful emotion cues while down-weighting irrelevant linguistic noise, enhancing both transparency and cognitive alignment. The results confirmed that incorporating cognitive-regularization and neuro-symbolic reasoning produced emotion-aware models that were not only more accurate but also cognitively interpretable and stable.

title: Robust Training of Large Language Models under Non-IID Data in Wireless Ad Hoc Federated Learning
authors: Motoki Tanaka, Hiroshi Esaki, Hideya Ochiai
abstract: With the growing computational power of AI semiconductor devices, on-device learning is becoming increasingly practical. In this context, Wireless Ad Hoc Federated Learning (WAFL)—which enables collaborative model training in a fully distributed environment—has drawn attention for its potential to utilize device-to-device communication for fine-tuning models to specific communities. However, major challenges remain in addressing performance degradation caused by non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) data across devices. This study proposes WAFL-DIAL (wireless ad hoc federated learning with dynamic in-round adaptive learning-rate), a method that dynamically controls the learning rate of each device to enhance robustness under non-IID conditions. Specifically, the learning rate is adaptively adjusted based on each device’s loss value and parameter variation to prevent overfitting. We applied this algorithm to LLM fine-tuning and evaluated its effectiveness in a WAFL setting. Experiments on the GLUE benchmark (CoLA, SST-2, and QNLI) under IID and Dirichlet-based non-IID settings (four devices) demonstrate that the proposed adaptive learning rate control improves device-level metrics (MCC and Accuracy) compared to baseline methods such as naive LoRA, and FedProx. In particular, it proves effective for devices with limited or highly skewed data distributions.

title: Training Data Influence on GPT Models: A Human-AI-Hybrid Analysis
authors: Rithvik Krosuri, Vaishnavi Sen, Rashida Hasan
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in natural language processing; however, their behavior remains highly sensitive to the composition of training data. This study investigates how varying proportions of human-written, AI-generated, and hybrid text influence the stability and adaptability of GPT-based models. Experiments were conducted using GPT-2 and GPT-Neo across five controlled dataset configurations derived from two corpora. By systematically altering data composition, we observed how changes in content balance affect model behavior, representational consistency, and overall robust-ness. Results show that model sensitivity to data distribution is more substantial than commonly assumed, particularly when exposed to hybrid text blending human and AI characteristics. These findings emphasize that dataset composition, its balance, scale, and heterogeneity plays a decisive role in shaping model behavior and provide insights into designing more balanced and reliable training datasets for future large language models.

title: Cognitive Load as a Lens for Understanding AI-Augmented Workflows in Agile Teams
authors: Dominik Palla, Ondrej Krejcar, Ali Selamat, José Ramón Trillo
abstract: This research article introduces a novel perspective on the integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into Agile software development by examining its impact through the lens of Cognitive Load Theory. As Agile teams adopt AI-powered tools to enhance productivity, the unintended consequences on developers’ cognitive resources are often overlooked. This paper investigates the various ways AI tools can both increase and decrease cognitive load, leading to a complex interplay between automation benefits and new cognitive demands. Through an exploratory qualitative study of emerging Agile practitioners, we identify and categorize distinct cognitive load patterns emerging from human-AI interaction in Agile ceremonies. We propose a new conceptual framework that classifies AI-induced cognitive load into three types: intrinsic, extraneous, and germane. Based on this framework, we offer a set of design principles for developing AI tools that optimize cognitive efficiency and support sustainable team performance. Our findings suggest that a deliberate focus on managing cognitive load is crucial for unlocking the full potential of AI in Agile environments, moving beyond simple task automation to a more human-centric augmentation of team capabilities.

title: Trustworthy Neurosurveillance at the Edge: MLLM-Augmented Anomaly Detection and Adversarial-Robust EEG Monitoring for Consumer-Grade Epilepsy Wearables
authors: Chao Huang, Haochen Liu, Hongkai Yao, Wenxiu Ma, Ling Liu, Jialin Li, Yan Dong
abstract: Wearable electroencephalography (EEG) is becoming more common with edge-AI-enabled devices to facilitate ongoing monitoring of epilepsy, even outside the hospital environment. Current consumer-grade wearable EEG devices face three challenges: (i) adversarial evasion attacks reduce seizure detection accuracy from over 97% to less than 15%, (ii) motion artifacts create false alarm rates at unacceptable clinical levels, and (iii) no known edge framework provides semantically interpretable, multimodal reasoning for anomalies. NEUROGUARD is proposed as an integrated platform combining multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based anomaly detection and adversarially-robust seizure detection on consumer-grade EEG devices. NEUROGUARD contributes three capabilities: (1) a Spectral Language Aligner (SLA) module enabling seizure recognition from approximately 58% fewer labelled windows, (2) SLA-Guided Adversarial Training (SGAT), which ensures 87.2% seizure detection sensitivity under white-box projected gradient descent (PGD) attack while preserving 95.3% sensitivity under normal conditions, and (3) a Quantised Inference Kernel (QIK) with 11.6 ms inference time on a GAP9 processor at 0.38 mJ per inference. In benchmark testing on CHB-MIT and TUH EEG seizure datasets, NEUROGUARD provided 14.8 percentage points more adversarially robust detection over the next best method, Alignment-Based Adversarial Training (ABAT), reduced false positives to 0.21 per hour, and operated at more than threefold energy savings compared to currently available edge detectors.

title: BiIN-LPI: A Bi-directional Intention Network for Predicting lncRNA–Protein Interactions toward Personalized Biomedical Applications in Consumer Electronics
authors: Haoyuan Li, Tongmao Ma, Shudong Wang, Yiran Li, Kai Zheng
abstract: Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) interact with proteins to regulate diverse biological processes, and disruptions in these interactions are often linked to human diseases. However, experimental identification of such associations is timeconsuming and costly, making computational prediction a valuable complementary strategy. In this work, we propose BiIN-LPI, an end-to-end framework for predicting lncRNA–protein interactions. lncRNA features are extracted from nucleotide sequences using a convolutional neural network, while protein features are obtained from amino acid sequences through an ACmix-based encoder. These two feature spaces are fused via a bi-directional intention network that enhances cross-modal representation learning through mutual feature guidance and multi-head attention. A multi-layer perceptron then predicts potential interaction pairs. Although primarily designed for biological discovery, BiIN-LPI also provides a conceptual basis for future integration with intelligent healthcare systems and personalized health monitoring applications. Experiments on the NPInter dataset with five-fold cross-validation demonstrate that BiIN-LPI achieves an AUC of 0.9633 (0.0048) and an AUPR of 0.9473 (0.0072), outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of the proposed module. These results demonstrate that BiIN-LPI is an effective and robust framework for lncRNA–protein interaction prediction. The implementation of BiIN-LPI is publicly available at https://github.com/HYLPRC/BiIN-LPI.

title: A fitting algorithm for HTS cable measurement
authors: Lingfeng Lai, Jingyin Zhang, Li Li, Kai Zhao, Bo Li, Yang Tang, Yubin Yue, Chen Gu
abstract: Assembling HTS tapes into cables is an effective solution to enhance the operating current of superconducting devices. With the advancement of cable research and its practical engineering applications, the demand for quality control of cables is rapidly increasing. In this paper, a fitting algorithm for cable defect detection is being proposed. This algorithm can effectively extract defect information within the cable and identify and correct for movements including position displacement and rotation of the cable. The method has been validated using both simulation and experimental data from HTS tapes and cables.

title: AI for active learners: Applying insights from the learning sciences and human-centered AI to educational contexts

title: Lightweight Machine Learning Models for Pneumonia Detection in Chest Radiographs
authors: Ibtasam Ur Rehman, Jibran K. Yousafzai, Abdulraqeb Alhammadi, Muhammad Ikram, Zoha Farooq, Maheen Ishfaq
abstract: This paper presents a lightweight machine learning (ML) framework for pneumonia detection from chest X-ray images. Three classical classifiers are evaluated: Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Random Forest (RF). The models are trained using 5,856 pediatric chest X-rays. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is applied to reduce feature dimensionality. Among the evaluated models, the SVM achieves the best overall performance. It reaches 94.97% accuracy, an Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC–ROC) of 0.98, an Area Under the Precision–Recall Curve (AUC–PR) of 0.99, and a Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 0.87. The model shows high sensitivity (97.54%) while maintaining improved specificity (88.01%). Class imbalance is explicitly handled during training. Robustness is evaluated using cross-validation, bootstrapping, and statistical significance testing. The framework produces calibrated confidence scores and supports visual interpretation of predictions. Due to low computational cost and stable behavior, the proposed approach is more suitable for pneumonia screening in resource-limited clinical settings in comparison to deep learning approaches.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Machine Learning Framework for Reliable and Intelligent Solar Energy Systems
authors: Tanya, Vanshika Rana
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The use of renewable energy has grown steadily in many parts of the world, and among the available sources, solar energy has become especially important. This is primarily due to the urgency to mitigate the harmful impacts on the environment and decrease the amount of greenhouse gases produced from the burning of fossil fuels. However, it should be noted that solar energy has its own limitations and is highly dependent on meteorological and geographical parameters. Hence, it is important to accurately predict solar irradiance to make the best utilization of solar energy. This paper proposes a methodology to predict solar irradiance using machine learning (ML), which aids in operational planning to identify periods of high solar availability and times that require additional energy support. The results indicated that the stacked regression model achieved a coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of 0.974 and a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 0.064, performing better than individual models in both reliability and accuracy. Additionally, blockchain was used to safeguard the data and predicted results from tampering or unauthorized access, helping ensure transparency and trust when making important decisions in energy distribution.

title: Predictive Artificial Potential Fields for Energy Efficient 3D UAV Navigation
authors: Gurmeet Kaur, Kunwar Pal, Avinash Kumar Jha
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The insufficient onboard energy remains a persistent hindrance for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), particularly when operating in dense three dimensional areas and without guidance. The use of classical Artificial Potential Field (APF) methods for local planning is characterized by frequent oscillatory motion and abrupt heading changes, which consumes significant energy. The paper describes an energy aware local path planning framework for UAVs that utilizes a three dimensional extension of the Predictive Artificial Potential Field (PAPF). Rather than traditional APF, the proposed technique employs short horizon trajectory prediction, temporary goal guidance, and a three dimensional top quark mechanism to proactively bypass local minimals. Embedded in the planning process is an explicit energy consumption model that takes into account translational velocity and yaw rate dynamics, leading to smooth, stable, and dynamically feasible trajectories. Extensive simulations in environments with cuboid obstacles demonstrate that the proposed approach surpasses standard APF in terms of path smoothness, heading stability, and cumulative energy consumption. The outcomes demonstrate the efficiency of predictive potential field-based planning for energy limited UAV navigation in complex three dimensional domains.

title: Imaging Eyes in Motion: Dynamic Compensation with Robotic Optical Coherence Tomography
authors: Genggeng Zhou, Yi Wang, Yihan Ling, Guangshen Ma, Ryan P. McNabb, Anthony N. Kuo, Nita Valikodath, Mark Draelos
abstract: Motion is a longstanding challenge faced in ophthalmic imaging. Patients undergoing Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) eye exams rely on mechanical stabilization to suppress motion during high-quality imaging, which is a significant barrier for patients with impaired mobility or movement disorders. We previously developed a mobile robotic OCT system to overcome this barrier. The system still requires subjects to adopt a stable posture, however, which precludes imaging during large-scale motion, such as in Parkinson's disease or physical activity. Thus in this work, we exploit the periodic motions commonly seen during in-place activity or movement disorders and use an autoregressive filter to predict motions 100s of ms into the future based on 5 s of historical observations. This allows the robotic system to learn, anticipate, and compensate for motion to maintain high image quality. Using this approach, we conducted experiments with phantoms and with two healthy human subjects performing movement tasks. Results show that compensation reduced eye errors below the tolerance of our scanner for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq \mathbf{8 0}$</tex>% of subject imaging time. Our method demonstrates fast and robust motion prediction capabilities for subjects in various motion profiles, showing promising results for future research and clinical applications.

title: SeqRisk: Transformer-augmented latent variable model for robust survival prediction with longitudinal data
authors: Mine Öğretir, Miika Koskinen, Juha Sinisalo, Risto Renkonen, Harri Lähdesmäki
abstract: In healthcare, risk assessment of patient outcomes has been based on survival analysis for a long time, i.e. modeling time-to-event associations. However, conventional approaches rely on data from a single time-point, making them suboptimal for fully leveraging longitudinal patient history and capturing temporal regularities. Focusing on clinical real-world data and acknowledging its challenges, we utilize latent variable models to effectively handle irregular, noisy, and sparsely observed longitudinal data. We propose SeqRisk, a method that combines variational autoencoder (VAE) or longitudinal VAE (LVAE) with a transformer-based sequence aggregation and Cox proportional hazards module for risk prediction. SeqRisk captures long-range interactions, enhances predictive accuracy and generalizability, as well as provides partial explainability for sample population characteristics in attempts to identify high-risk patients. SeqRisk demonstrated robust performance under conditions of increasing sparsity, consistently surpassing existing approaches.

title: Facilitating Inter-Generational Communication through Virtual Reality: An Investigation of a Culturally Significant Topic (Xiqu)
authors: Xiaoying Wei, Fangtao Zhao, Yingna Wang, Zeyu Xiong, Mingming Fan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Inter-generational communication enhances mutual understanding and family connections. In China, traditional Chinese opera (Xiqu) serves as a culturally rich topic that provides common ground, fostering meaningful conversations across generations. However, traditional verbal discussion falls short in bridging the cultural and knowledge gaps, thereby failing to convey the full charm of Xiqu and hindering effective inter-generational Xiqu communication. To address this challenge, this study investigates the potential of Virtual Reality (VR) to bridge these generational divides. Using a mixed-methods approach, we developed and evaluated XiquVR to facilitate inter-generational Xiqu communication. Key findings show that XiquVR transforms one-sided storytelling into collaborative activities, visualizes abstract narratives to bridge generational gaps, and facilitates bidirectional exchanges through AI-assisted knowledge sharing. Furthermore, we propose design implications for developing VR applications centered on culturally significant topics to facilitate inter-generational communication.

title: Modern Environmental Problems and Ways to Overcome Them
authors: Volodymyr Zdanovsky, Hryhoriy Melkonov, Inna Melkonova
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article briefly describes global problems related to the state of ecology and its future positive and negative changes. Based on the analysis of the fuel and energy complex, atmospheric pollution with compounds that cause the greenhouse effect and dangerous climate change on the planet, problems with the accumulation and storage of huge volumes of production and household waste, as well as military actions and their consequences, it is shown what can lead to a negative development of events and how to prevent it. Energy and environmental problems are closely related, since it is nearly impossible to produce, transport, or consume energy without significant environmental impact. The environmental problems directly related to energy production and consumption include air pollution, water pollution, thermal pollution, and solid waste disposal. The emission of air pollutants from fossil fuel combustion is the major cause of urban air pollution. Coal mining can also pollute water and earth Changes in groundwater flow produced by mining operations often bring otherwise unpolluted waters into contact with certain mineral materials which are leached from the soil and produce an acid mine drainage. Solid waste is also a by-product of some forms of energy usage. Coal mining requires the removal of large quantities of earth as well as coal. In general, environmental problems increase with energy use and this combined with the limited energy resource base is the crux of the energy crisis. An energy impact assessment should compare these costs with the benefits to be derived from energy use.

title: Industry Corner Live With Adaptyx Co-Founder and CEO Vijit Sabnis

title: Early Warning System for Inflation Risks: A Hybrid Forecasting Framework Integrating Triple Exponential Smoothing with GARCH Models for Monetary Policy
authors: Rahmat Hidayat, Ansari Saleh Ahmar, Alde Alanda, Erianda Aldo, Hilda Amnur, Rasyidah
abstract: This research develops an early warning system for inflation risks by constructing a hybrid forecasting framework that integrates Triple Exponential Smoothing with GARCH models. Using monthly inflation data from Indonesia (January 2010 to September 2023), we address the critical need for proactive risk identification in monetary policy management. The hybrid approach leverages Triple Exponential Smoothing to capture trend and seasonal patterns while incorporating GARCH models to account for volatility clustering in inflation data. Results demonstrate that the hybrid model outperforms standalone models with a 27.8% reduction in forecast error and enhanced variance prediction. Most significantly, the framework enables policymakers to identify emerging inflation risks 2-3 months earlier than traditional methods, thereby expanding the window for calibrated policy responses. The Value-at-Risk estimates from the hybrid model achieve 94.5% coverage rate with narrower prediction intervals than conventional methods. Counterfactual policy analysis confirms that implementation of this framework would have enabled more timely intervention during key inflation episodes. This research contributes to risk sciences by establishing an integrated approach for inflation risk assessment that bridges statistical forecasting techniques with practical monetary policy applications.

title: Reliability of Inverter-Dominated Power Systems: Risks and Mitigation Strategies
authors: Aneta Bednarova, Radomir Gono
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Global decarbonization efforts and the massive integration of renewable energy sources are driving fundamental changes in the topology and physical behaviour of the entire grid. The gradual decommissioning of synchronous generators which are being replaced by inverter-based resources leads to a reduction in system inertia and altered fault dynamics, directly impacting grid reliability. This new environment reveals the limitations of traditional deterministic reliability assessment methods and necessitates a re-evaluation of established approaches. This paper analyses these technological challenges and discusses the tools essential for maintaining stability in a carbon-free energy sector.

title: TrustSearch: Towards Secure and Efficient Reverse Image Search via SGX
authors: Fang Zou, Jia Zhang, Jingwei Li, Dayan Wu, Xiong Li, Hongwei Li, Ting Chen, Xiaosong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Outsourcing image management to a cloud should not only protect the confidentiality of image data, but also maintain the capability of reverse image search, which requires identifying the existing stored images that are similar to an input image. Previous studies build on cryptographic approaches to realize reverse image search on encrypted images, yet failing to achieve either security or performance. This paper explores trusted image search, which uses Intel SGX to realize reverse image search in an enclave, in order to provide security guarantees via SGX while performing search on plain data (inside the enclave) for performance. However, due to the resource limits of SGX, directly realizing the search process in the enclave incurs high performance overhead. We present TRUSTSEARCH, which implements various design approaches to mitigate the resource overhead of SGX. We evaluate TRUSTSEARCH using real-world image datasets, and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art approaches for search performance while preserving space efficiency for the enclave.
