title: Channel-Selected Stratified Nested Cross-Validation for Clinically Relevant EEG-Based Parkinson’s Disease Detection
authors: Nicholas R. Rasmussen, Rodrigue Rizk, Longwei Wang, Arun Singh, KC Santosh
abstract: The early detection of Parkinson’s disease remains a critical challenge in clinical neuroscience, with electroencephalography (EEG) offering a non-invasive and scalable pathway toward population-level screening. While machine learning has shown promise in this domain, many reported results suffer from methodological flaws, most notably patient-level data leakage, inflating performance estimates and limiting clinical translation. To address these modeling pitfalls, we propose a unified evaluation framework grounded in nested cross-validation and incorporating three complementary safeguards: (i) patient-level stratification to eliminate subject overlap and ensure unbiased generalization, (ii) multi-layered windowing to harmonize heterogeneous EEG recordings while preserving temporal dynamics, and (iii) inner-loop channel selection to enable principled feature reduction without information leakage. Applied across three independent datasets with a heterogeneous number of channels, a convolutional neural network trained under this framework achieved 80.6% accuracy and demonstrated state-of-the-art performance under held-out population block testing, comparable to other methods in the literature. This performance underscores the necessity of nested cross-validation as a safeguard against bias and as a principled means of selecting the most relevant information for patient-level decisions, providing a reproducible foundation that can extend to other biomedical signal analysis domains.

title: Analysing renewable energy forecasts in smart grids: motivation, challenges, and the road ahead
authors: Zeynep Benay Uslu, Joris Depoortere, Fahad Mehmood, Hussain Kazmi
abstract: Renewable energy capacity in many countries, including Belgium, has grown significantly in recent years. In Belgium, solar and wind energy capacity rose from under 5 GW in 2016 to about 11.7 GW by 2023, with expectations for further expansion. Transmission system operators (TSOs) must manage generation data from renewable sources and provide accurate forecasts to balance electricity supply with demand. Poor data quality and large forecast errors can disrupt grid operations and lead to costly interventions. In this paper, we examine the energy generation data and forecasts shared by Belgium’s TSO, Elia. Our analysis finds some minor data quality issues and a notable rise in forecast errors for solar and wind energy from 2017 to 2023 across various time scales. These errors cannot be attributed solely to capacity growth, highlighting a decline in forecasting accuracy. Machine learning techniques, however, show promise for improving these forecasts, critical for future grid stability.

title: Optimal Design of Efficient Load Frequency Controller for Islanded Microgrid
authors: Ghali Ahmad, Md Shafiullah, Mohamed Zaery, Mujahed Al-Dhaifallah, M. A. Abido
abstract: This paper introduced novel load frequency control (LFC) strategies for microgrids (MGs) with Renewable Energy Integration. We investigated four scenarios with unique variations in loads, photovoltaic (PV) power, wind power, and white noise in Pv and wind power. The Proportional Integral Derivative (PID) controller is finely tuned and used as a secondary control mechanism to regulate frequency. This innovative approach enables the flexible load to harness surplus power during high-generation and reduce consumption during low-generation periods, thereby ensuring system stability. We utilized genetic Algorithm (GA) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) techniques to fine-tune and optimize the PID gains parameter by minimizing the Integral time absolute error (ITAE). The system is implemented, tuned, optimized, tested, and verified under various operating conditions, showcasing the robustness and reliability of our work. The parameter optimization of the PID controller through GA-PID and PSO-PID shows that PSO-PID consistently outperforms PID and GA-PID across all four cases, with reductions of ISE by 39.5%, 47.6%, 47.3%, 28.5%, IAE by 50.2%, 61.2%, 58.3%, 31.4%, and ITAE by 49.9%, 71.9%, 61.5%, 28.6% respectively. These results unequivocally establish the effectiveness of PSO-PID as an excellent optimization tool for MG response strategies.

title: Optimal Energy Management for a Car-Sharing Operator using Clustering Techniques
authors: Mario Galán, Carmen M. Valenzuela, Sebastián De La Torre, José Antonio Aguado
abstract: Large-scale energy management for electric vehicle (EV) car-sharing fleets requires optimization models with a high-dimensional spatio-temporal structure, often leading to prohibitive runtimes for operational use. This paper proposes a computationally efficient framework that couples Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering (AHC) with Linear Programming (LP) to reduce the spatial dimensionality of fleet scheduling while preserving mobility feasibility. Starting from a raw urban topology defined by administrative neighborhoods, the proposed method aggregates the operating area into a compact set of functional depots and derives mobility-consistent transition parameters from high-frequency fleet telemetry. The resulting reduced-order LP jointly optimizes charging and discharging decisions under state-of-charge constraints, charging power limits, travel energy consumption, and time-varying electricity considerations. The framework is validated using real-world data from a car-sharing fleet operating in Tel Aviv-Yafo, where 71 neighborhoods are aggregated into 10 operational depots, and a 260-vehicle scenario is optimized over 24 hourly intervals. Results fast solution times, enabling practical real-time deployment. Under the analyzed price profile, optimized Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) scheduling yields 795.74 € in discharging revenue against 197.76 € in charging costs; after including 357.67 € of battery degradation costs, the method delivers a net daily profit of 240.31 € while maintaining mobility energy requirements. These findings demonstrate that clustering-driven dimensionality reduction can enable scalable and economically viable energy management for urban car-sharing fleets, supporting the integration of EV fleets as flexible grid assets.

title: On the Resilience of Direction-Shift Keying Against Phase Noise and Short Channel Coherence Time at mmWave Frequencies
authors: Mohaned Chraiti, Ozgur Ercetin, Ali Ghrayeb, Ali Gorcin
abstract: Short channel coherence time and oscillator phase noise are two major impairments in millimeter-wave (mmWave) communication systems. Several studies indicate that a substantial fraction of the available bandwidth may be required as overhead to compensate for these impairments, potentially exceeding one third of the total capacity. In this paper, we study Direction-Shift Keying (DSK), a variant of Spatial Modulation (SM), which encodes information in the Direction-of-Arrival (DoA) rather than in the signal amplitude or phase. DSK is implemented over a Distributed Antenna System (DAS), enabling angular resolvability of the transmitted signals. We first derive the structure of the optimal detector for a mobile device equipped with M antennas. We then introduce and characterize the Direction Coherence Time (DCT), defined as the temporal interval over which the DoA remains approximately invariant. Our analysis shows that DCT scales with d/v (transmitter-receiver distance over velocity), whereas the conventional Channel Coherence Time (CCT) scales with λ/v, revealing a coherence-time gain proportional to d/λ, which can exceed several orders of magnitude in mmWave systems. Furthermore, we show that the proposed detector inherently cancels receiver phase noise, eliminating the need for explicit phase-noise tracking. Simulation results validate the analytical findings and demonstrate the robustness of DSK in mobile mmWave environments in the presence of phase noise.

title: QRS Complex Detection Using Machine Learning Approaches: A Survey on Methods, Datasets and Challenges
authors: Shivani S, Sharvari Abhay Walimbe, Naveen Kumar H N, Shreyas M N, Sanath G Ambiger
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The QRS complex representing ventricular depolarization, is the most critical segment that indicates right and left ventricular activation. The accurate detection of R-peaks/QRS complex in an electrocardiogram (ECG) plays a significant role in identifying arrhythmia analysis, and cardiovascular diseases. The computer-based techniques for R-peaks/QRS complex detection have been actively investigated for many years to achieve sufficient performance. The Pan–Tompkin’s algorithm is one of the most widely used and highly reliable real-time algorithms for detecting the QRS complex in ECG signals. Motivated by the limitations of fixed, rule-based processing under varying noise levels, morphologies, and sampling conditions, recent research has progressively integrated data-driven algorithms for QRS detection. This paper comprehensively reports the state-of-the-art of machine learning and deep learning algorithms that have been integrated with or developed upon the Pan-Tompkins framework to enhance detection accuracy. The study reports spectrum of methods, ranging from classical ML classifiers (Support Vector Machines and Decision Trees), which operate on handcrafted features from Pan-Tompkins, to advanced deep learning architectures like Convolutional Neural Networks for spatial feature extraction, Long Short-Term Memory networks for modelling temporal dynamics, and hybrid CNN-LSTM models that capture both spatial and temporal characteristics. The benchmark datasets on QRS complex detection and its prominent characteristics are reported in the paper. The results of the existing methods for the QRS complex detection are discussed in a coherent manner. The prominent challenges in implementing the QRS complex detection are summarized and the feasible solutions to address these issues are highlighted in the paper.

title: Parameter-agnostic Privacy-preserving Machine Unlearning for Large Language Models
authors: Lefeng Zhang, Tianqing Zhu, Zihan Xie, Shang Wang, Binxing Fang, Wanlei Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: In recent years, advancements in large language models have led to significant innovation and critical progress in AI. However, some of these innovations are raising privacy and security concerns. Machine unlearning has therefore emerged as a potential solution to mitigate such risks. Yet, while erasing data records from traditional models is relatively straightforward, making a large language model “forget” what it has learned is often very challenging. This is not just because they include so many parameters, it is also because the knowledge they possess is intricately entangled. Further, the privacy risk of unlearned data remains neglected in most unlearning solutions. To overcome these limitations, we took advantage of information retrieval and developed an efficient privacy-preserving unlearning mechanism. Our solution eliminates the impact of targeted information by removing high-risk semantic meanings from the model’s output. It also incorporates differentially-private randomization to make the unlearned information statistically indiscernible. Most importantly, the algorithm requires neither parametric fine-tuning nor in-context prompt calibration. A theoretical analysis demonstrates that this method satisfies rigorous privacy and unlearning guarantees. Additionally, experiments on real-world datasets prove that the method is both effective and has the capacity to handle practical unlearning tasks for large language model applications.

title: Memory-Enhanced Dynamic Self-Attention Communication Mechanism for Multi-UAV Base Stations Trajectory Planning
authors: Hanxiao Yuan, Yao Shi, Emad Alsusa, Qinyu Zhang, Yiping Duan, Xiaohu You
abstract: In large-scale temporary event scenarios, Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle base stations (UAV-BSs) provide flexible, on-demand wireless coverage to meet rapidly growing user demands. However, dynamic crowd movements and unpredictable user behaviors lead to highly uncertain environments. The absence of prior knowledge on user distribution, interference, and network load, coupled with UAVs’ limited sensing capabilities, makes real-time trajectory planning especially challenging. Although reinforcement learning has shown potential in these scenarios, many existing approaches remain limited. They often make decisions based on instantaneous local observations or rely on unavailable centralized training information in a practical environment, limiting their adaptability and scalability. To address these limitations, we propose Mem-DSACM (Memory-Enhanced Dynamic Self-Attention Communication Mechanism), a lightweight reinforcement learning framework that integrates memory-assisted temporal reasoning with distance-constrained decentralized communication. In Mem-DSACM, the exchanged data, including relative distances and communication loads, is combined with historical observations and processed by a memory-based temporal reasoning module using a Transformer decoder. This enables context-aware and predictive decision-making under decentralized conditions. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method improves the system rate by 12.8%, the cumulative satisfaction 8.0%and the algorithm reduces the training time by 6.3% compared to the the best baselines approaches, demonstrating superior performance in dynamic urban scenarios.

title: Congruent kinesthetic and tactile haptic rendering accelerates the learning of highly dynamic virtual sensorimotor tasks
authors: Alexandre L. Ratschat, Beatrice Luciani, David A. Abbink, Gerard M. Ribbers, Laura Marchal-Crespo
abstract: Advances in haptic technologies enable rich, multi-channel haptic rendering of interactions with virtual objects during virtual reality training. However, it remains an open question whether multi-channel haptic rendering (kinesthetic and tactile) provides superior motor learning and transfer when training dynamic tasks compared to simpler, single-channel sensory information.We investigated how40 participants learned to invert and balance a virtual pendulum after training under four haptic rendering conditions: congruent kinesthetic and tactile rendering, kinesthetic rendering alone, tactile rendering alone, and no haptic rendering. Kinesthetic information was delivered through a delta robot, and tactile information through a two-dimensional skin-stretch device at the fingerpads. Participant performance was measured in catch trials during training, in short- and long-term retention trials, and with a transfer task with a shorter pendulum. Participants from all four training conditions demonstrated the ability to improve and transfer their skills. However, we observed poorer performances during catch-trials when training with reduced or absent haptic rendering compared to training with congruent kinesthetic and tactile rendering. The advantage of congruent haptic rendering over conditions lacking kinesthetic rendering was maintained during short-term retention, whereas no significant performance differences were observed between conditions in long-term retention and the transfer task. These results suggest that congruent haptic rendering benefits the task’s early learning by supporting the generation of internal models of the task dynamics, with kinesthetic rendering playing a major role. Overall, our findings highlighting the potential benefits of multi-channel haptic rendering to accelerate virtual reality training.

title: Hazard-Gated Temporal Transformers for Real-Time Risk Control
authors: Vikrant Dey
abstract: Many operational domains require decisions whose value depends not only on future states, but on the timing of high-impact events. Classical sequence models predict future conditions, yet they struggle when events arrive irregularly, display clustering, or incur asymmetric execution costs. We propose HedgeFormer, a hazard-gated temporal transformer that jointly models (i) the time until the next high-volatility event, (ii) the cross-sectional location where risk concentrates, and (iii) the corresponding action intensity. The model integrates survivalstyle temporal point-process heads with a multi-horizon hedging policy, enabling actions to be activated only when forecasted risk exceeds execution costs. We evaluate the framework on short-dated cryptocurrency options, a domain characterized by bursty volatility, sparse liquidity, and extreme sensitivity to event timing. HedgeFormer significantly improves Sharpe, reduces CVaR0.99, and lowers turnover relative to delta-based methods, sequence-model hedgers, and Hawkes-or RL-driven timers. The architecture illustrates a general approach for decision-making under irregular event dynamics where timing, cross-sectional allocation, and action gating must be learned jointly.

title: Heuristic Approaches for Cache Node Placement in Content-Centric Networking under Maximum Link Traffic Minimization
authors: Praphan Pavarangkoon, Shohei Nakajima, Nattapong Kitsuwan
abstract: This paper proposes heuristic approaches for cache node placement in Content-Centric Networking (CCN) with the objective of minimizing maximum link traffic. The work builds on an exact Integer Linear Programming (ILP) formulation from our earlier study, which jointly models routing and caching decisions but becomes computationally expensive for large-scale networks. To address this limitation, we develop scalable heuristic algorithms that approximate the ILP solution while requiring much lower computation time. We introduce four heuristic approaches, comprising two Linear Programming (LP)-based local search algorithms, 2Swap and GreedySwap, which exploit the fractional solution of the LP relaxation, and two proxy-based heuristics, Population-Weighted Closeness (PWC) and Population-Weighted Betweenness (PWB), which estimate cache utility using population-weighted centrality measures without solving any optimization model. Experimental evaluations on four network topologies show that the LP-based heuristics remain within 0–6% of the ILP optimum while reducing computation time by factors ranging from about 40× to more than 160×. Compared with the proxy-based heuristics, the LP-based heuristics consistently yield lower maximum link traffic across all settings. These results indicate that the proposed heuristic approaches provide effective and scalable solutions for cache node placement in practical CCN deployments.

title: Advanced Load Modelling of Electric Vehicles: Static and Dynamic Behavior in Modern Power Grids
authors: Amer S. Alsaman, Abdullah S. Alnowaiser
abstract: As the penetration of Electric Vehicles (EVs) continues to rise, their impact on modern power grids becomes increasingly significant, especially due to their dual role as both loads and energy storage units. Accurate load modeling is essential to capture the complex behavior of EVs and to ensure grid stability. This paper presents a comprehensive approach to advanced EV load modeling using PSS/E (Power System Simulator for Engineering), focusing on both static and dynamic models. The static models address long-term, steady-state behavior, while dynamic models account for the time-varying characteristics of EV charging and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) capabilities. By simulating different EV penetration scenarios, the study evaluates the effects of EV integration on frequency stability, voltage profiles, and grid performance. The results underscore the importance of integrating EVs not just as flexible loads but also as active participants in grid operations through V2G, contributing to ancillary services such as frequency regulation. This research offers valuable insights for grid operators and planners as they prepare for the widespread adoption of EVs.

title: Deep Learning Approaches for Sustainable Road Safety and Hazard Management : A Comprehensive Review
authors: Shikhar Agrawal, Bina Kotiyal
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Road safety and vigilant road hazard management has always been a problem faced by the modern world. With constant expansion in road connectivity, there has been a rise in vulnerabilities like potholes, cracks and natural calamities as well like landslides and floods. The study aims to mitigate the risk of natural hazards and accidents caused due to human negligence by the help of deep learning approaches by including them in risk assessments. By assessing the human behaviour in real time by the help of automation services, there can be a constant evolution being brought up in the hazard management. There has always been a need of a good and flexible bridge between the technology and human civilization so that road safety can be achieved at a sustainable pace and with accuracy.

title: E-Commerce Transaction Fraud Detection Using GNN-Based Feature Embedding and Machine Learning Methods
authors: Sarwosri, Bintang Nuralamsyah, Nathaniel Ryo Kurniadi, Abdur Rach-Man Wahed, Siti Rochimah
abstract: E-commerce fraud poses a significant threat to digital economies, causing financial losses and weakening consumer trust in online platforms. Existing detection systems typically rely on supervised machine learning (ML) classifiers such as Artificial Neural Networks, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting applied to tabular transaction features. ML classifiers process transactions independently and cannot capture relationships across shared entities such as devices, IP addresses, and payment methods. Other approaches use Graph Neural Networks to capture relational dependencies between transactions. End-to-end GNN classifiers present deployment challenges including high computational cost and limited interpretability. To address this limitation, this study proposes a hybrid fraud detection pipeline to extract relational embeddings from a transaction graph and combine them with tabular features for conventional machine learning classifiers. Experiments are conducted on the Kaggle Fraudulent E-commerce Transac-tion dataset, which contains 1,472,952 training records and 23,634 test records. Three classifiers, namely Artificial Neural Networks, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting, are evaluated under three scenarios: tabular only, end-to-end GNN, and hybrid GNN embedding. Across all three classifiers, augmenting tab-ular features with relational embeddings yields consistent improvements over the tabular-only baseline. The Random Forest hybrid pipeline obtains the most balanced result (accuracy = 0.9688, F1 = 0.6091, PR-AUC = 0.6171), although the absolute gain over the best tabular baseline is modest, indicating that GNN-based feature embedding provides a complementary rather than transformative source of predictive signal.

title: Two-Phase Immersion Cooling for Short-Duration High-Power Electric Motors
authors: Yuqing Liu, Jiwei Cao, Yuchen Song, Bin Chen, Liyi Li
abstract: In response to the extreme heat dissipation challenges faced by short-term high-power motors, this paper proposes a solution based on two-phase immersion cooling. Unlike traditional cooling methods, this study analyzes motors subjected to high current density over a short period, utilizing the latent heat in the phase change process of the coolant to achieve efficient heat dissipation. During the experiment, the stator assembly was immersed in a dielectric liquid, and the short-term high-power operating conditions were simulated by controlled power input. The study focused on analyzing the relationship between superheat and heat flux and convective heat transfer coefficient (CHTC), and a boiling start time and power prediction model was established. Experimental results show that under short-term high-power conditions, this cooling method can achieve significantly higher heat transfer efficiency than traditional technologies, and the convective heat transfer coefficient can reach up to 2740 W/(m²·K). This study provides a new thermal management solution for short-duration high-power motor applications in aviation and other fields, and passive and efficient cooling can be achieved without an external pump system.

title: Towards Efficient Intrusion Detection: Feature Selection in Multiclass CNNs with Numerical Inputs
authors: Inaê Soares de Figueiredo, Leandro Aparecido Passos Junior, Douglas Rodrigues, Danilo Samuel Jodas, Fabrício Steinle Amoroso, Anderson Raymundo Avila, João Paulo Papa, Kelton Augusto Pontara de Costa
abstract: The last decade has seen exponential growth in data storage volume and network traffic. This phenomenon, associated with high-dimensional data representation, contributes to a complex scenario in which detecting malicious content becomes a harder challenge that intrusion detection systems struggle to address. This work proposes reducing traffic data dimensionality through dimensionality reduction techniques, namely Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Chi-Square (χ<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>). Further, it considers a Convolutional Neural Network to assess the impact of compressed data in the trade-off optimization between malicious content identification accuracy and computational burden. Experimental results demonstrate the classification effectiveness and computational efficiency of the proposed approach, even under CPU-based execution, obtaining virtually the same results as an MLP-based baseline, i.e., 99.63% and 98.02% accuracy for χ<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and PCA, respectively, against 99.70% obtained through the undiminished data at the cost of significantly higher training time.

authors: N/A

title: Spiking vs. Artificial Neural Networks for Lung Sliding Detection in E-FAST: A Capacity-Matched Low-Data Benchmark*
authors: Ege Özbilen, Sergio M. Navarro
abstract: We investigate whether spiking neural networks (SNNs) can achieve comparable accuracy to capacity-matched artificial neural networks (ANNs) in recognizing the lung sliding sign under conditions of limited labeling and embedded computing constraints characteristic of the extended focused assessment with sonography for trauma (E-FAST). We compile a 123-clip video corpus from public sources and a subset of COVIDx-US, transforming B-mode clips into compact (224 × 60) M-mode images that highlight pleural motion. We then compare two convolutional models with equivalent parameters: a ReLU-based artificial neural network and a Leaky Integrate-and-Fire spiking neural network trained with surrogate gradients over (T = 3) steps. Employing Repeated Stratified 5×5 cross-validation, we present the area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC), area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC), and F1 scores with 95% confidence intervals across probe types and policies on the inclusion of "partial" sliding examinations.In all configurations, the confidence intervals for ANN and SNN overlap, demonstrating that SNN performance is not inferior. The optimal configuration, linear probes with included partials, attains an AUROC of 0.71 for ANN compared to 0.69 for SNN. The SNN demonstrates consistently shorter fold-to-fold confidence intervals and elevated mean F1 across all eight configurations, indicating enhanced stability in low-data conditions. The properties of the data dictate the choice of architecture: limiting to linear probes enhances both models; using partials diminishes performance for mixed-probe datasets but benefits the linear-probe subset. These findings endorse SNNs as feasible, energy-efficient options for on-probe screening procedures in (E-)FAST, while emphasizing that probe geometry and labeling strategy have a more significant impact than activation dynamics. We provide a transparent preprocessing and assessment pathway to enhance repeatability and support future hardware-in-the-loop research.

title: Enhancing Explainable AI for Medical Imaging: Improved LIME Interpretation with Influence Mapping
authors: Abiha Tahsin Chowdhury, Dhanush Bavisetti, Daniel B. Hier, Rahul Dubey, Tayo Obafemi-Ajayi
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into medical imaging is progressing rapidly. It is essential for these AI tools to be transparent, interpretable, and explainable to gain the trust of clinicians and regulators. Current state-of-the-art explainable AI (XAI) techniques in imaging includes Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME), Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP), and Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM). Recent studies have shown that LIME often suffers from inconsistency and unreliability which limits their utility in sensitive fields like medical imaging. This paper proposes Influence Map based Explanation (IME), an enhanced variant of the original LIME framework, that aggregates multiple runs to reduce variability and improve explanation reliability. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, we apply it to image-based classification of Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) using deep neural networks trained to predict stage of AD from magnetic resonance images. We quantitatively evaluated IME using two complementary metrics (Confidence Impact Ratio (CIR) and Decision Impact Ratio (DIR)) and compared its performance to existing state-of-the-art XAI methods. Results show that the proposed approach performed better than state-of-the-art XAI methods. Furthermore, the results show statistically significant improvements, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed IME in producing meaningful explanations.

title: Optimized Scheduling of DAG Tasks on Heterogeneous Multi-Core Platforms with Reliability-Aware Multi-Objective Differential Evolution
authors: Yaodong Guo, Shuangshuang Chang, Dong Ji, Shiyue Qin, Zonghua Gu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Heterogeneous multi-core platforms have become the primary choice for embedded systems due to their ability to handle high-speed parallel computing applications. However, the heterogeneous nature of platforms often leads to high energy consumption. To address the energy challenges, Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) is widely used to reduce energy consumption. Unfortunately, it also increases the system’s susceptibility to transient faults, adversely impacting reliability, which means the applications may not be completed successfully. Therefore, the system designer needs to consider multiple conflicting design objectives, such as real-time, power/energy consumption, and reliability. In this paper, we address the multi-objective optimization problem of mapping and scheduling the application modeled as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) onto a heterogeneous multi-core platform, and aim to find the Pareto optimal solutions of minimizing both makespan and energy consumption while satisfying reliability constraints. To achieve this goal, we propose a Reliability-Aware Multi-Objective Differential Evolution (RA-MODE) algorithm. Specifically, RA-MODE incorporates reliability constraints to generate the initial task mapping and frequency configuration, while introducing segment-based mutation and crossover operations to enhance the search process. Experimental results show that RA-MODE significantly outperforms existing methods on both real and randomly generated DAGs, effectively balancing completion time and energy consumption in multi-objective optimization while also excelling in single-objective scenarios, achieving an average reduction of 13% in completion time and 18% in energy consumption.

title: Autonomous Control for LEO Multi-Satellite Systems via Orbital Edge Computing and Multi-Agent Learning
authors: Feng Tan, Xue Li, Yunlong Luo, Qifeng Sun, Jiangtao Luo
abstract: Low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite communication systems increasingly rely on large-scale constellations, which intensifies spectrum competition, interference management, and scheduling overhead. Existing multi-satellite beam-hopping schemes are mostly based on centralized control, where global demand collection, ground-based scheduling, and command delivery may introduce excessive control delay and limited scalability. To address these issues, this paper proposes an autonomous control framework for LEO multi-satellite systems based on orbital edge computing. In this framework, beam scheduling is offloaded from the ground control center to onboard satellites, enabling local perception and autonomous decision-making while explicitly reducing the propagation-related control delay caused by demand collection and command delivery. Based on this framework, a pheromone-enhanced graph neural network deep reinforcement learning algorithm is developed to optimize cooperative beam hopping under local observations. The proposed algorithm combines pheromone-based service memory, graph neural network-based state representation, and reward-sharing Double DQN to achieve implicit multi-satellite cooperation without explicit inter-satellite information exchange. Simulation results show that the proposed method outperforms representative baselines in terms of throughput, queueing delay, demand satisfaction, and interference suppression. Under high traffic load, PE-GDRL improves system throughput by at least 12%, reduces average queueing delay by about 9%–10%, and achieves nearly 100% demand satisfaction under different coverage conditions. Theoretical analysis further shows that the proposed framework provides better control timeliness and scalability than centralized control architectures.

title: Spectral Extension of Hyperspectral Images with Convolutional Sparse Representation
authors: Zhen Wang, Lianjie Li, Yuzhe Zhang, Sitian Liu, Chao Deng, Junling Fan, Liheng Bian
abstract: Hyperspectral images (HSIs) capture rich spatial-spectral information and are widely used in various fields. However, the spectral ranges of hyperspectral imaging cameras are generally limited to a narrow range, like visible (VIS) or near-infrared (NIR), due to the constraints of optical elements and imaging sensors. Existing methods primarily focus on enhancing the visual effect in the spatial or spectral domain, neglecting the correlation between different spectral ranges. Therefore, this work aims to accurately correlate HSIs across different spectral ranges, enabling efficient spectral extension using algorithms. To achieve spectral extension, we explore the sparsity of real-world HSIs and propose a sparse representation framework based on the spectral convolutional dictionary. This approach reveals latent features shared across different spectral ranges, which are effectively captured by learned convolutional dictionaries and their associated sparse coefficients. Building upon this representation, we introduce the Unfolding Convolutional Sparse Representation Transformer (UCSRT) model, jointly optimizing the convolutional dictionaries and sparse coefficients. The Spectral Coding Transformer in the UCSRT achieves accurate and efficient sparse encoding through a sparse self-attention mechanism guided by spectral sparsity. Additionally, Spectral Convolutional Dictionary Learning Module is proposed to learn the convolutional dictionary for the VIS-NIR spectral range. This framework enables the extension of HSIs from a narrow VIS range to a wider VIS-NIR range. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and accuracy of our spectral extension method. Comparative analyses with state-of-the-art spectral reconstruction models, as well as practical applications for small target detection in disguised environments and enhancement in snapshot hyperspectral imaging, further highlight the superiority of our UCSRT method.

title: Pushing physical limits and uncovering motion templates of spine-based quadruped locomotion via reinforcement learning_supp2-3697171.mp4
authors: Long Cheng
abstract: Flexible spines are critical to the remarkable agility and speed of animals. Translating this biological advantage to quadruped robots presents a significant control challenge, particularly in coordinating the spine and limbs for maximal velocity. In this work, we utilize reinforcement learning (RL) to develop high-speed locomotion for a bioinspired mouse robot with a lateral flexible spine. The resulting controller achieves motor performance that demonstrably surpasses non-spined and model-based methods. More importantly, our analysis reveals the principles behind this performance: the emergence of two distinct motion templates. For high-speed walking, the robot learns a “whip-like” spinal oscillation to increase leg swing frequency, while for agile turning, it adopts a dynamic “bend-and-straighten” pattern. These findings demonstrate the capability of RL to not only generate high-performance controllers but also to produce emergent strategies that, upon analysis, reveal underlying principles of high-speed, spine-driven locomotion.

title: Longitudinal Dynamics Modeling and Control of a Wheel-Legged Robot with Tilting Rotor Assistance
authors: Zhiheng Dai, Xinglu Xia, Xiang He, Yingxun Wang
abstract: Benefiting from high-efficiency mobility, wheel-inverted pendulum robots have been widely deployed in scenarios such as cargo transportation and search. However, their longitudinal motion exhibits non-minimum-phase behavior, which can induce inverse motion and thus degrades stability in transient maneuvers such as start-up or braking. Meanwhile, the system is underactuated, which makes it difficult to flexibly coordinate robot attitude and wheel position. To address these issues, this paper applies tilting dual rotors to a wheel-legged robot platform to help improve longitudinal motion. The longitudinal dynamic model of this hybrid-configuration robot is derived and analyzed, and an equilibrium-based scheduled LQR controller is developed and implemented. Experimental results demonstrate that the integration of the tilting dual-rotor assembly reduces the maximum inverse-speed excursion by 57.14% and the peak leg pitch angle excursion by 37.4%, while also reducing the attitude settling time after disturbance by about 18%, enhancing transient ground-locomotion performance in the tested longitudinal maneuvers.

title: Automatically Construct Hard Unit Commitment Datasets via Data-Driven Adjustment of Demand Curve
authors: Yisheng Cao, Qian Gao, Juan Yu, Zhifang Yang
abstract: Hard unit commitment (UC) problems require a long computing time to obtain the optimal solution, which is crucial for the development of UC algorithms. This article proposes a data-driven approach to automatically generate a set of hard UC instances that adapt to different industrial power systems. First, this article perturbs the demand curves of the original UC case to construct the initial dataset. For small-scale UC problems, by training a generative model with a loss function indicating computing time, a set of hard UC instances is generated. In addition, the generated hard instances are selected to train a new generative model. This iterative training strategy enables the generative model to focus more on latent features, progressively increasing the hardness of the generated UC instances. Second, to shorten the time required to generate hard UC instances for large-scale UC problems, we present a feature transfer method. The core idea is to use data-driven methods to extract key physical features of demand curves based on small-scale test systems that affect the UC hardness while remaining effective for large-scale systems. These key physical features are then used to constrain the construction of the initial dataset, avoiding the attempts at incorrect features during model training, which effectively reduces the time consumption of the hard case generation process. Experimental results on public test systems and industrial utility systems show that the proposed method can find the test cases with an average increase in solution time of 66.76 times without manual intervention

title: Multirotor UAVs Transporting Cable-Suspended Loads: A Literature Review
authors: Zongyang Lv, Gan Yu, Qing Zhao, Yuhu Wu, Wei Xie, Weidong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Load transportation using unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) presents both intriguing possibilities and significant challenges in research and practical applications. This study aims to present a comprehensive literature review of recent progress in the development of multirotor UAVs transporting cable-suspended loads. A secondary objective is to assist researchers and engineers in the design and development of flight control systems for UAV-slung-load applications. To this end, the survey begins by providing a historical overview of flight control hardware platforms and load swing measurement systems used in UAV-slung-load systems. Subsequently, representative modeling approaches for UAV-slung-load systems are introduced. The survey then reviews a range of existing flight control strategies, highlighting their key characteristics and advantages. Finally, general challenges and potential future research directions for UAV-slung-load systems are discussed.

title: Mid-infrared germanium-on-silicon waveguide sensor for therapeutic drug monitoring of phenytoin
authors: David J. Rowe, Siyu Chen, Mihai-Adrian Panainte, Callum J. Stirling, Pin Dong, Monika Bakalarz, Hanuushah Vizabaskaran, Glenn Churchill, Weilin Jin, Georgia Mourkioti, Martin Ebert, Graham T. Reed, Saul N. Faust, James S. Wilkinson, Milos Nedeljkovic, Daniel R. Owens, Goran Z. Mashanovich
abstract: We report the design, fabrication and characterization of evanescent mid-infrared germanium-on-silicon waveguide sensors for therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM). TDM requires rapid and accurate quantification of serum drug levels but existing clinical assays rely on laboratory‑based instrumentation that limits point‑of‑care deployment. In this work, tunable diode laser absorption spectroscopy was used to analyze dried samples of the antiseizure medication phenytoin in the spectral region of λ = 5.6 – 6.0 μm. A limit of detection of 2.20 mg/L was achieved for extracted samples, where phenytoin was first added to human serum and subsequently isolated using liquid-liquid extraction. This limit is significantly below the therapeutic window of 10-20 mg/L for phenytoin, enabling detection of sub‑therapeutic concentrations. At the same time, the sensor maintains a consistent dose‑dependent response up to 40 mg/L, demonstrating its capability to quantify concentrations across the therapeutic window and above the upper therapeutic limit. This validates the use of silicon photonics for biomedical infrared spectroscopy for patients undergoing drug therapy, whether the serum-drug concentration is either too high or too low. These results highlight the potential of mid‑IR integrated photonics to form the basis of compact, scalable platforms for point‑of‑care TDM.

title: Smart Self-Learning Reconfiguration for Partially Shaded PV Arrays
authors: Ashi Gautam, Monika Kashyap, Diwaker Pathak, Gourav Verma, Vidushi Sharma
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: Partial shading conditions (PSCs) can cause mismatch power loss (MPL) and large power fluctuations. Additionally, if the PSC persists for an extended period, it could damage the associated PV array in the absence of bypass diodes. In this context, the commonly used PV array reconfiguration methods can minimize the MPL. However, the scalability of the system might be compromised due to a lack of exact information about the ongoing shading pattern at the decision-making end of dynamic switching. Therefore, this research work proposes an Internet of Things (IoT)-enabled deep Q-network (I-DQN)-based reconfiguration algorithm to enhance the photovoltaic (PV) power generation capability during the PSCs. The designed IoT framework is implemented to monitor the PV array parameters recurrently, enabling reconfiguration actions to be executed in a closed-loop environment to adapt the changing PSC patterns. The current work is assessed in various case studies under the m × m and m × n PV array matrices to evaluate the efficacy of the proposed reconfiguration approach under various PSCs. Simulation results for a 6 × 6 and 4 × 6 PV array show that the mismatch power loss and fill factor are improved by about 14.42% and 22.65%, respectively, compared to previously reported promising metaheuristics-based PV array reconfiguration methods. Furthermore, the efficacy of the developed smart algorithm under partial shading conditions is validated in the real time environment of the hardware in loop (HIL) for a 6 × 6 array with different algorithms. The HIL analysis shows the satisfactory performance of the proposed I-DQN algorithm.
