title: Multi-Agent Systems: From Classical Paradigms to Large Foundation Model-Enabled Futures
authors: Zixiang Wang, Mengjia Gong, Qiyu Sun, Jing Xu, Shuai Mao, Xin Jin, Qing-Long Han, Yang Tang
abstract: With the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence, multi-agent systems (MASs) are evolving from classical paradigms toward architectures built upon large foundation models (LFMs). This survey provides a systematic review and comparative analysis of classical MASs (CMASs) and LFM-based MASs (LMASs). First, within a closed-loop coordination framework, CMASs are reviewed across four fundamental dimensions: perception, communication, decision-making, and control. Beyond this framework, LMASs integrate LFMs to lift collaboration from low-level state exchanges to semantic-level reasoning, enabling more flexible coordination and improved adaptability across diverse scenarios. Then, a comparative analysis is conducted to contrast CMASs and LMASs across architecture, operating mechanism, adaptability, and application. Finally, future perspectives on MASs are presented, summarizing open challenges and potential research opportunities.

title: An Orchestration System for Digital Twin Trustworthiness in Industrial Environments
authors: Nicola Bicocchi, Mattia Fogli, Carlo Giannelli, Enzo Mingozzi, Marco Picone, Antonio Virdis
abstract: Digital twins (DTs) are increasingly used in industrial environments to mediate interactions between applications and physical assets. However, existing orchestration approaches treat DTs as conventional software components, overlooking their cyber-physical nature and the resulting trustworthiness challenges. In such systems, violations of timing, resource, or data constraints can compromise the fidelity of the DT–physical twin (PT) relationship. In this article, we propose a trustworthiness-driven orchestration system, where trustworthiness is modeled as a multidimensional runtime invariant. The system implements a continuous control loop that monitors relevant metrics and enforces corrective actions, such as resource reallocation or migration across the cloud-to-edge continuum. We validate the approach through a Kubernetes-based prototype and large-scale simulations. Results show that the system effectively detects violations and restores DT–PT entanglement under dynamic conditions, demonstrating the feasibility of invariant-driven orchestration for industrial DTs.

title: Soft-Switching Bidirectional Converter for Electric Vehicle Power Interfaces
authors: Veera Venkata Subrahmanya Kumar Bhajana, Pravat Biswal, Vivek Kumar, Prajof Prabhakaran
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: This article proposes a bidirectional buck–boost converter for electric vehicle (EV) charging applications, featuring a single-auxiliary switch to achieve zero-voltage switching (ZVS) and enhanced efficiency across a wide operating range. The auxiliary network, composed of a resonant inductor, capacitor, and diodes, enables ZVS in both buck and boost modes regardless of load, duty cycle, or power direction. The converter operates in continuous conduction mode on the low-voltage side with constant-frequency pulsewidth modulation (PWM) control. All switches benefit from soft-switching under light and heavy load conditions. The topology preserves voltage and current stress on the main switch while reducing switching losses and component count. Theoretical analysis, dynamic modeling, and experimental validation using a 48-/160-V, 200-W prototype confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: AttFKAN-DEQ: Attention-Enhanced Fourier Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks Using Deep Equilibrium for Breast Cancer Histopathology Classification
authors: Hassan Ali, Muhammad Asghar Khan, Gordana Barb, Andrei-Marius Silaghi
abstract: Breast cancer histopathological classification remains a challenging task due to small patch resolution, staining inconsistencies, magnification variability, and class imbalance in datasets such as BreakHis and IDC, which hinder accurate malignancy detection. This paper proposes AttFKAN-DEQ, a novel framework that integrates Fourier Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (FKANs) with Deep Equilibrium Models (DEQs) for binary benign-versus-malignant classification. Unlike spline-based formulations, FKANs model univariate edge functions using finite trigonometric series with trainable sine and cosine coefficients, enabling robust multi-scale spectral representations of heterogeneous tissue morphology. To enhance feature discrimination, a Lightweight Convolutional Block Attention Module (LCBAM) is incorporated within the FKAN residual block to perform channel-wise and spatial feature recalibration during each DEQ iteration using depthwise separable convolutions. This mechanism emphasizes diagnostically relevant regions while suppressing irrelevant background variations. Furthermore, the DEQ mechanism implicitly models infinite network depth through fixed-point iterations at constant memory cost, allowing stable and progressive latent feature refinement. The proposed framework was evaluated using five-fold stratified cross-validation with standard data augmentation. AttFKAN-DEQ achieved 96.60% accuracy and 98.44% AUC on the BreakHis dataset and 94.52% accuracy with 97.31% AUC on the IDC dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms DEQ-KAN, ResNet variants, DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B0, Vision Transformer, Swin Transformer, ConvNeXt, and MobileViT across multiple evaluation metrics. Ablation studies further confirm the individual and combined contributions of Fourier parameterization, lightweight attention, and equilibrium-based feature refinement to the overall performance. Despite containing only 6.0M parameters and requiring 0.60 GFLOPs, AttFKAN-DEQ achieves high diagnostic accuracy with strong computational efficiency, highlighting its potential for deployment in real-world clinical histopathology applications.

title: DMOC: A Differentiable Model of Optical Camera for High-Accuracy Calibration Under a Fixed Reference Frame
authors: Dong Zhang, Haichao Sun, Xiaolin Chen, Hong Ren, Beichen Wu, Xin Zeng, Jincheng Jiang, Xinxin Chang, Yuanfu Yuan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Conventional camera calibration is predominantly formulated under the pinhole model, which approximates the imaging process with idealized linear geometry and often neglects the coupled effects of internal optical components. In the presence of lens-structure variations or assembly perturbations, such an equivalent approximation can introduce systematic residuals and degrade measurement accuracy. To address this issue, we propose a Differentiable Model of Optical Camera (DMOC) to mathematically describe the refractive imaging process. Based on Snell’s law, DMOC establishes a multi-surface refractive propagation model and formulates the mapping from 3-D scene points to sensor pixel observations as a fully differentiable imaging model. The model is differentiable with respect to camera intrinsics and extrinsics, sensor-plane parameters, and assembly-induced deviations, enabling joint optimization of these variables under a unified objective. We further introduce a metrology-assisted fixed reference frame to express multi-view poses in a consistent coordinate system, thereby simplifying the calibration formulation under externally measured target poses. Jacobians are efficiently computed via automatic differentiation, and the resulting problem is solved using the Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm. Simulations demonstrate stable convergence across different optical configurations with subpixel reprojection consistency. Real experiments show accuracy comparable to conventional models within the calibration range, while providing improved overall ranging accuracy over the compared baseline models, with the advantage becoming more evident at medium-to-long working distances.

title: Research on Energy Efficient Collaborative Control Method of Autonomous Consistency for Urban Rail Trains
authors: Ruxun Xu, Xiaoqiang Chen, Qiang Nie, Decang Li, Jianjun Meng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: To address the issue of autonomous consistency in energy-saving operation of multiple urban rail trains, a negotiation coefficient is introduced and an autonomous consistency protocol based on event triggering mechanism is designed. This paper performs consistency analysis based on Lyapunov stability theory, and proves the convergence of the system and eliminates the Zeno phenomenon. While ensuring that the train schedule remains unchanged, this paper adopts a parameter adaptive adjustment strategy to further improve the utilization rate of regenerative energy. First, The secondary traction time of trains participating in collaborative control is calculated based on the consistency protocol. Second, the maximum primary traction speed of trains is calculated based on the parameter adaptive adjustment strategy. Finally, based on the train dynamics equation, the operating condition transition points of the train consistency collaborative control are determined, and the train operation curve is ultimately generated. Through simulation analysis, compared with the leader-following consistency, the regenerative energy utilization of urban rail trains with the application of the autonomous consistency protocol no longer relies on the leader train. By negotiating coefficients and tuning parameter, the punctuality of the trains is ensured while further reducing their net energy consumption. Note to Practitioners—The motivation of this study is to improve the utilization rate of regenerative energy for urban rail trains, in order to achieve the goal of reducing the net energy consumption of trains. The popular methods adopted to reduce the net energy consumption of trains are optimizing train schedules and adjusting inter station operating curves. However, these methods exert an impact on the punctuality of urban rail trains, causing unnecessary waiting time for passengers and reducing the quality of service operation. To solve this problem, we take train punctuality as a constraint and train acceleration as a negotiation variable. We combine parameter adaptive adjustment strategy with autonomous consistency collaborative control algorithm to study the mechanism of mutual utilization of regenerative braking energy of urban rail trains. The aim is to maximize the utilization of regenerative energy and reduce the net energy consumption of urban rail trains while ensuring train punctuality. Obviously, this method can ensure that the original train schedule of urban rail transit remains unchanged to the maximum extent, and minimize the impact on urban rail transit operators, thus possessing high practical value.

title: Adaptive Extended Autocorrelation Method for Ultrasound Doppler Velocimetry
authors: André Luis Stakowian, Fábio Rizental Coutinho, Rigoberto E. M. Morales, Eduardo Nunes Dos Santos, Marco Jose Da Silva
abstract: In industrial fluid dynamics, providing reliable means for flow characterization still remains a challenge. Ultrasonic Doppler Velocimetry (UDV) offers a robust, non-intrusive solution for opaque fluids with high spatiotemporal resolution, overcoming limitations of invasive probes or optical techniques like Pitot tubes and Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). However, pulsed UDV is constrained by the Nyquist limit, which results in velocity aliasing. While transmission-based strategies such as the Staggered PRT mitigate this by using a non-uniform pulse interval between emissions, they often introduce complex hardware or data processing requirements. The Extended Autocorrelation Method (EAM) provides an algorithmic alternative for dealiasing but utilizes fixed processing windows, which compromises performance when flow velocities are unknown or exhibit wide distributions. This paper proposes an Adaptive EAM that dynamically adjusts the processing window size and the Nyquist folding number search range through an iterative state transition logic. Experimental validation was conducted in an 8.5-m flow loop (26-mm inner diameter acrylic pipe) using a 4-MHz transducer and polyethylene tracers (937 kg/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>) across superficial liquid velocities from 0.5 to 4 m/s. Results demonstrate that the adaptive algorithm automatically resolves velocities up to three times the Nyquist limit. Notably, computational efficiency improved by approximately 50% compared to the standard EAM for sub-Nyquist velocities, with performance converging toward the original method as the folding number increases.

title: Digital Twin enabled Autonomous Vehicles for Obstacle Avoidance in Disaster response
authors: Cara Rose, Tanveer Ahmad, Robert McMurray, Thippa Reddy Gadekallu, Muhammad Usman Hadi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) deployed in search and rescue missions must operate reliably across structurally diverse and unpredictable terrains. This paper proposes a novel digital twin based perception planning control framework that integrates the three dimensional Vector Field Histogram Plus (3D VFH) algorithm within the Field based Autonomous LiDAR Control for Obstacle Navigation (FALCON) architecture. The primary contribution is the establishment of a unified and consistent benchmarking baseline that isolates controller behaviour from environment specific tuning, enabling rigorous cross terrain performance evaluation without scenario dependent optimisation. The framework is validated in two geometrically distinct environments: a structured urban campus and an unstructured mountainous terrain. Performance is assessed using trajectory tracking accuracy, path length, mission time, and safety quantified through a 2 m Safety Envelope (SE). While all controllers exhibit comparable performance in the urban scenario, substantial differentiation emerges in rugged terrain. The manually tuned controller achieves the fastest completion with zero safety violations and a 6.21 m SE, whereas the PSO optimised controller also maintains zero violations with strong robustness characteristics. In contrast, GA and MA controllers experience violation rates of 14.3% and 28.6%, respectively, highlighting terrain induced sensitivity to gain optimisation strategies. The results demonstrate that controller robustness is strongly environment dependent, and that maintaining a fixed algorithmic baseline enables clear identification of perception control coupling effects. The proposed framework provides a scalable and reproducible testbed for evaluating environment aware UAV navigation strategies in safety critical applications.

title: Autonomous Seamless Transition between Grid-Connected and Islanded Operations for DC-Coupled EV Charging Infrastructures
authors: Anas Karaki, Ali Sharida, Sertac Bayhan, Haitham Abu-Rub
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: This paper proposes a novel seamless transition technique between grid-connected and islanded operations for DC-coupled electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructures. The main objective of the proposed approach is to ensure that the critical grid and EV requirements will be satisfied during the transition, including frequency, rate of change of frequency (RoCoF), current, rate of change of current (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$di/dt$</tex-math></inline-formula>), and voltage ride-through requirements. The proposed approach provides three main functionalities, the first one is to ensure the seamless transition between islanded to on-grid operation, the second one is to control the transition process from grid-connected to islanded operation (intentional islanding), while the third one is to perform a fast transition to grid-forming (GFM) operation when the utility grid is suddenly disconnected (unintentional outage). The algorithm is primarily based on trajectory-based frequency control to synchronize the charging infrastructure with the grid while adhering to RoCoF constraints. Moreover, the reference currents are adaptively generated to ensure a smooth transition, effectively controlling transient currents by minimizing overshoots and limiting <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$di/dt$</tex-math></inline-formula>. This approach mitigates surge power and prevents false tripping of the protection system while maintaining stable operation during dynamic conditions. The effectiveness of the proposed technique is validated experimentally.

title: Event-Based De-Snowing for Autonomous Driving_supp1-3697155.pdf
authors: Manasi Muglikar
abstract: Adverse weather conditions, particularly heavy snowfall, pose significant challenges to both human drivers and autonomous vehicles. Traditional image-based desnowing methods often introduce hallucination artifacts as they rely solely on spatial information, while video-based approaches require high frame rates and suffer from alignment artifacts at lower frame rates. Camera parameters, such as exposure time, also influence the appearance of snowflakes, making the problem difficult to solve and heavily dependent on network generalization. In this paper, we propose to address the challenge of desnowing by using event cameras, which offer compressed visual information with submillisecond latency, making them ideal for desnowing images, even in the presence of ego-motion. Our method leverages the fact that snowflake occlusions appear with a very distinctive streak signature in the spatiotemporal representation of event data. We design an attention-based module that focuses on events along these streaks to determine when a background point was occluded and use this information to recover its original intensity. We benchmark our method on DSEC-Snow, a new dataset created using a green-screen technique that overlays pre-recorded snowfall data onto the existing DSEC driving dataset, resulting in precise ground truth and synchronized image and event streams. Our approach outperforms state-of-the-art desnowing methods by 3 dB in PSNR for image reconstruction. Moreover, we show that off-the-shelf computer vision algorithms can be applied to our reconstructions for tasks such as depth estimation and optical flow, achieving a 20% performance improvement over other desnowing methods. Our work represents a crucial step towards enhancing the reliability and safety of vision systems in challenging winter conditions, paving the way for more robust, all-weather-capable applications.

title: An Effectiveness Study of Dithering for Improved Force Estimation on the dVRK-Si System
authors: Sara Martuscelli, Hao Yang, Elena De Momi, Jie Ying Wu, Peter Kazanzides
abstract: Accurate sensorless force estimation in teleoperated surgical robots remains challenging due to complex internal dynamics and high static friction, particularly in systems with harmonic drives such as the da Vinci Research Kit Si (dVRK-Si). This work investigates the application of dithering, a small, high-frequency oscillating signal at the robot joints, to reduce friction-related uncertainty and enhance force estimation accuracy. We first experimentally characterize the low-velocity friction behavior of the dVRK-Si Patient Side Manipulator (PSM-Si), identifying the Coulomb friction bands for its first three joints. A joint-space dithering scheme is then designed and implemented, with signal frequency and amplitude optimized through system identification and accelerometer-based feedback. Static and quasi-static experiments demonstrate that dithering improves the sensitivity and stability of Cartesian force estimation, reducing mean absolute errors by 50-80% depending on the operating condition. The results confirm that dithering effectively mitigates frictioninduced disturbances in surgical robots, enabling more precise force estimation without hardware modification and providing a pathway toward enhanced haptic feedback in teleoperated surgical systems.

title: Balanced Multi-Source Transfer Learning for Fruit Quality Prediction With Limited Data
authors: Yongjun Kim, Eun Jung Sun
abstract: Climate change is accelerating the introduction of new fruit varieties worldwide, yet machine learning models for agricultural quality prediction require years of historical data to be reliable. This bottleneck severely limits AI deployment in smart farming: a new variety planted today may lack sufficient quality records for a decade. Transfer learning from established crops offers a promising shortcut, but our investigation reveals a critical pitfall: naively combining data from multiple established crops is highly unreliable—improving results in some target domains (+23.7%) while causing catastrophic performance degradation in others (R2 drops from 0.553 to −0.494). We term this the source overwhelming problem, where abundant source data drowns the signal from scarce target observations. We present Balanced Multi-Source Transfer Learning (BMTL), a principled framework that controls source-target balance to enable effective cross-crop knowledge transfer, operating in a few-shot regime where a small target validation set (nvalT ≥ 10) is available. We establish a counterintuitive finding: source-target balance matters more than domain similarity for successful transfer. On fruit quality prediction spanning 4 crops (apple, citrus, grape, pear; 3,721 quality measurements with 20,896 integrated weather records from the Korea Meteorological Administration), BMTL improves R2 by +0.143 points (from 0.393 to 0.536, a +36.4% relative gain) over single-crop training in 7 of 8 tested scenarios, and up to +117.8% when target data is extremely scarce (nT =50). On UCI benchmarks, BMTL is the only transfer-based method avoiding catastrophic negative transfer on high-shift domains. The proposed method reduces the data collection burden for new crop varieties, enabling agricultural AI systems to become operational years earlier—with direct benefits for food security and supply chain management.

title: Application of FFT and DFT for Detection and Assessment of Current and Voltage Distortion
authors: Danylo Filyanin, Anatolii Voloshko, Vasyl Kalinchyk, Olexandr Meita
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Nonlinear loads and the growing prevalence of distributed generation systems contribute to the distortion of voltage and current waveforms within power supply systems (PSS). This results in the emergence of higher-order harmonics in both current and voltage. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is commonly employed as a method of harmonic analysis to identify the sources of distortion, which in the case of a sharply variable nonlinear load does not allow reliably assessing the distortion introduced by a sharply variable load. The relevance of the work is due to the need for a more accurate assessment of current and voltage distortions, identifying cause-and-effect relationships of the appearance of distortions in order to effectively implement measures to eliminate them. This approach will enhance the quality and reliability of the power supply provided to consumers. To analyze the frequency content of the signal within the measurement interval, it is suggested to compute the spectrum points derived from the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) by utilizing the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT).

title: L ux I A: A L ightweight U nitary matri X -based Framework Built on an I terative A lgorithm for Photonic Neural Network Training
authors: Tzamn Melendez Carmona, Federico Marchesin, Marco P. Abrate, Peter Bienstman, Stefano Di Carlo, Alessandro Savino
abstract: Photonic Neural Networks (PNNs) can accelerate machine learning workloads by implementing Matrix-Vector Multiplications (MVMs) in integrated photonic circuits, but existing simulation and training frameworks scale poorly to large Photonic Unitary Matrix (PUM) meshes because they explicitly construct and manipulate dense transfer matrices. This work introduces the Slicing method, which models PUM meshes as sequences of local 2×2 operations organized into computational windows and computes forward and backward propagation using only localized matrix℄vector updates with linear complexity in the number of active cells. The method is implemented in LuxIA, an open-source PyTorch-based framework for end-to-end PNN simulation and training that supports multiple mesh architectures and datasets. A formal analysis shows that Slicing reduces perpass work by one degree (from quartic to cubic) in the worstcase. Experiments on Clements, Fldzhyan, and Bell-optimized meshes trained on Iris, Digits, MNIST, and Olivetti Faces show that LuxIA matches the training dynamics and task accuracy of existing tools while substantially improving training efficiency: on large meshes and batches, LuxIA achieves up to 4.7× lower training time and more than an order-of-magnitude reduction in Graphics Processing Unit (GPU) memory compared with conventional transfer-matrix frameworks, and it remains within the memory budget where competing tools fail.

title: Cost-Effective Grid Congestion Relief Via Battery-based Virtual Power Lines
authors: Mohamed Elkadeem, Kotb M. Kotb, M. Hassan, M. Worku, Mohammad A. Abido, Yagoob Alsharief, Salem Bashraheel, Khalid Alharbi
abstract: Grid congestion occurs when network components reach their thermal limits due to excessive generation or demand. As an alternative to expensive upgrades to the transmission infrastructure, a non-wire alternative called virtual power line (VPL) is being deployed. VPLs typically involve utility-scale battery energy storage systems (BESS) installed at specific points within the power grid. For the first time, this paper proposes a techno-economic feasibility model for optimizing the size and management of BESS-based VPL. The objective function is formulated to minimize the total net present cost subject to various operational constraints of the system. The model was implemented and validated using real parameters from the Saudi transmission network. The results indicate that 12 Li-Ion batteries, each with a capacity of 1 MW/4.28 MWh, are optimal for alleviating the overloading scenario on the studied transmission network. Moreover, the findings confirm the viability of employing the proposed VPL solution over constructing new overhead transmission lines, showcasing lower costs and proved effective in managing the transmission congestion. The VPL solution successfully mimics transmission line flows, supports the existing network infrastructure, and enhances the cost performance and reliability of the power grid.

title: Trajectory Extraction for Urban Roundabouts from Aerial Imagery: A Proof-of-Concept for the Dataset for Observation of Navigation in Urban Traffic
authors: Jorge García-González, Lucia M. Doval, Javier Fernández-Andres, Nourdine Aliane, Javier Sánchez-Soriano
abstract: This work presents a trajectory extraction methodology and a proof-of-concept for a dataset of vehicle trajectories in roundabouts based on aerial images and preexisting annotations, constructed using two pipelines that integrate different systems to convert static detections into time series of positions in eight roundabouts. The work makes it possible to train and evaluate planning and arbitration algorithms at roundabouts and opens up future lines of research in classification automation, metric standardization, and generalization evaluation to unseen roundabouts.

title: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation Information for Authors
authors: N/A

title: Power Harmonics Distribution Study in System with a Complex Load and Solar Power Station
authors: Maxim Oliynichenko, Oleksii Bialobrzheskyi, Maksim Bezzub, Artur Postil
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Converting solar energy using the photovoltaic effect into electrical energy to increase efficiency of generation, maximum power point tracking is used, ensuring the power level at input of the inverter. Usually, the AC circuit inverter is connected to the external network. The spread of the use of nonlinear loads, and therefore the increase in level of current and voltage of higher harmonics forces us to investigate their influence on the microgrid. After analyzing previous studies, it was found that the authors do not study power distribution of higher harmonics in the microgrid. Using the IEEE Std 1459-2010 standard, expressions for calculating the power of higher harmonics are formulated. To study power distribution of higher harmonics in a system with a complex load and a solar power station, appropriate subsystems were implemented in the visual programming environment, and a microgrid model was built. A study was conducted, one of which was conducted under condition of simulating the shading of a solar panel. The values of the main and higher harmonics active and reactive powers are determined. It was established that active and reactive power of higher harmonics are transmitted to the network in a small amount. The proposed order of division of higher harmonics of active and reactive power is expedient to use in systems of technical electricity accounting.

title: I2I-Mamba: Multi-modal medical image synthesis via selective state space modeling_supp1-3698056.pdf
authors: Tolga Cukur

title: Analytical Design of High-Frequency Magnetic Coupling Inductive Transformer
authors: Ping Jin, Junshun Wang, Yujing Guo, Tianrui Zhou, Gang Lei, Jianguo Zhu
abstract: High-frequency magnetic coupling inductive transformer (MCIT) is a critical component of short-range wireless power transfer (WPT). However, existing designs focus on MCIT's electromagnetic rather than its frequency domain characteristics and lack fully analytical design methods. This paper proposes an analytical design for adjacent tank MCIT using an improved AP (Area Product) method. Considering the influence of the air-gap's fringing effect and the distribution parameters caused by the electric field on the design of MCIT, the structure of MCIT is determined based on the frequency-domain characteristics of the input admittance and voltage gain.

title: An AI-Powered Predictive Platform for Early Detection of Chronic Kidney Disease
authors: Shivani Mamunoori, Shounak Palnitkar Jitendra, Jinzhu Gao
abstract: This paper presents an AI-powered predictive platform for early detection and risk assessment of Chronic Kidney Disease. The system integrates hybrid machine learning models with clinical text data and medical images, which are standardized using Optical Character Recognition to form a unified dataset. Based on user-provided clinical attributes, the model generates a personalized risk score, assigns a risk category, and estimates five-year progression probability. The platform also visualizes individualized risk relative to population benchmarks and provides specialist referral suggestions. To enhance user engagement and health literacy, the system includes an integrated conversational agent and feedback interface. This work contributes a practical, user-centered CKD screening tool designed to support proactive health awareness while emphasizing the importance of professional medical diagnosis.

title: P -MAPPO: Feasibility-Projected Multi-Agent PPO for Discrete Macro-Actions with Application to Dynamic Airspace Sectorization
authors: Mercedes Premalatha Ramesh, Imen Dhief, Zhimin Li, Meng-Hsueh Hsieh, Mir Feroskhan
abstract: System performance in dynamic control environments, such as airspace management, often relies on rapidly reconfiguring spatial regions such as sectors or service areas through high-level macro-actions such as merging, splitting, maintaining the current layout, or adjusting handover timing. These decisions must be made within milliseconds while satisfying strict safety, capacity, and geometric constraints. Selecting appropriate macro-actions is a complex problem, and despite extensive prior work, notable limitations persist. Current approaches inevitably compromise among speed, robustness, and formal guarantees, often achieving only two of the three desired properties simultaneously. To address these challenges, this study introduces Projected Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (P-MAPPO), a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework designed to deliver safe, millisecond-scale decisions over discrete macro-actions. P-MAPPO represents neighboring regions as a graph with short temporal memory, applies centralized training with decentralized execution, and incorporates a feasibility projection immediately before action execution. This projection maps any proposed macro-action to the nearest admissible choice and enforces capacity limits, contiguity, closure avoidance, and a cool-down period to prevent rapid reconfigurations. A short-horizon Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP)/Constraint Programming-Satisfiability Solver (CP-SAT) optimizer provides feasible examples for initialization, and Proximal Policy Optimization refines the policy into a compact, low-latency controller. The approach is evaluated in dynamic airspace sectorization, a critical task in air traffic management (ATM) where the airspace is divided into controller-managed sectors to ensure safe aircraft separation and manageable controller workload. Because traffic flows and weather-induced restrictions evolve throughout the day, sector boundaries must be adaptively reconfigured through macro-actions. In this context, each sector behaves as an agent proposing region-level adjustments, and the feasibility projection ensures that all executed decisions remain safe, feasible, and stable. Experiments on historical ADS-B data show that P-MAPPO reduces delay by 16%, lowers coordination time by 46%, and operates 75! faster than state-of-the-art baselines, with zero execution-time violations.

title: A synergized combinational relaxation strategy assisted design method for the hydrogen network in refinery under price uncertainty: a distributionally robust optimization perspective
authors: Weimin Zhong, Shizhao Chen, Chao Shang, Kaixun He, Xin Peng
abstract: The design of the hydrogen network aiming at lowering the total annualized cost has been a compu-tational haro optimization problem due to the highly nonlinear constraints in the model. Besides, uncertainties evoked by the rapidly changing market of hydrogen and fuel may render the solution to be suboptimal. In this work, a two-stage distributionally robust optimization approach in which the uncertainty is described by a Wasserstein ambiguity set is proposed, aiming to find a solution that could perform more stably with better economic performance. A convex relaxation strategy is developed to tackle the computational effort caused by the bilinear terms and posynomial terms. The results show that compared to a traditional stochastic programming approach, the distributionally robust optimization model yields a design solution that is less sensitive to price change and an improved out-of-sample performance is achieved. Besides, a lower total annual cost could be obtained with a lower computational burden.

title:
A Conceptual Framework Based on AI Agents and Multimodal Prompt Engineering for Personalized and Adaptive Educational Experiences
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Adriana Reveiu, Diana-Elena Chivu
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence into education demands frameworks that are simultaneously theoretically grounded, technically flexible, and pedagogically coherent. This paper proposes a conceptual framework that combines multi-agent architectures with multimodal prompt engineering to deliver personalized, adaptive learning experiences. Specialized agents—including a Manager Agent, a Prompt Designer, and a Content Agent—collaborate through a structured Blackboard Architecture to orchestrate instructional workflows aligned with self-regulated learning, constructivism, and mastery learning principles. Multimodal prompts, incorporating text, images, audio, and video, extend agent capabilities beyond unimodal interaction, enabling richer knowledge acquisition and context-sensitive feedback. A lightweight proof-of-concept implemented in a high-school programming course illustrates the framework’s feasibility, and evaluation metrics including a Pedagogical Alignment Score and Prompt Grounding Accuracy are defined to support future empirical validation. The framework prioritizes architectural flexibility and human oversight, providing a reproducible foundation for transparent and trustworthy AI deployment in educational settings.

title: The First Needlescopic Wristed Grasper to Surpass da Vinci Grasping Performance
authors: Ethan R. Wilke, Aabhas Jain, Alejandro O. Chara, S. Duke Herrell, Irving J. Zamora, Harold N. Lovvorn, Robert J. Webster
abstract: Traditional surgical robotic grasping end effectors do not scale down to needlescopic (3 mm and smaller) sizes well, and performance challenges are compounded when wrist degrees of freedom are included. Current wristed needlescopic grasper designs typically produce low grasp forces, making it challenging to retract tissue or firmly hold suture needles. In this paper, we propose a new design that achieves grasp forces at least six times higher than the repurposed flexible graspers used in prior concentric tube robotics research. Our new design exceeds even the grasp force of the much larger da Vinci Surgical System. We achieve this by combining a nitinol push-pull actuation tube with distal metal cam joints that amplify grasp force. The result is a tool with concentric-tube-type wrist joints capable of not only strong grasps, but also active jaw opening (e.g., for blunt dissection), that exceed the requirements of even larger-scale laparoscopic surgery. We validate our new grasper experimentally, comparing it against three commercially available graspers of the types previously attached to concentric tube robots, as well as to a da Vinci Surgical tool. We find that even at 3 mm diameter, our prototype surpasses the maximum grasp forces of an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8. 5 ~ m m}$</tex> da Vinci tool, while providing more than 4 N of active opening force, and affording torsional stiffness many multiples higher than prior concentric tube end effectors. An additional useful feature of our design that is not present in prior grasper designs is the provision of an open central lumen useful for delivering tools such as laser fibers into the surgical field.

title: Sixth-Sense: Self-Supervised Learning of Spatial Awareness of Humans from a Planar Lidar
authors: Simone Arreghini, Nicholas Carlotti, Mirko Nava, Antonio Paolillo, Alessandro Giusti
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Reliable localization of people is fundamental for service and social robots that must operate in close interaction with humans. State-of-the-art human detectors often rely on RGB-D cameras or costly 3D LiDARs. However, most commercial robots are equipped with cameras with a narrow field of view, leaving them unaware of users approaching from other directions, or inexpensive 1D LiDARs whose readings are hard to interpret. To address these limitations, we propose a self-supervised approach to detect humans and estimate their 2D pose from 1D LiDAR data, using detections from an RGB-D camera as supervision. Trained on 70 minutes of autonomously collected data, our model detects humans omnidirectionally in unseen environments with 71 % precision, 80 % recall, and mean absolute errors of 13 cm in distance and 44° in orientation, measured against ground truth data. Beyond raw detection accuracy, this capability is relevant for robots operating in shared public spaces, where omnidirectional awareness of nearby people is crucial for safe navigation, appropriate approach behavior, and timely human-robot interaction initiation using low-cost, privacy-preserving sensing. Deployment in two additional public environments further suggests that the approach can serve as a practical wide-FOV awareness layer for socially aware service robotics.

title: Toward the Open World: Closed-Loop Psychophysiological Intervention Systems Driven by Biosignal Foundation Models
authors: Jingyu Liu, Xuan Zhang, Yang Li, Bin Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems

title: Possibilities to Reduce the Impact of Harmonics on Power Quality in Industry
authors: Alexander Shram, Yurij Kachan, Artem Sverhun
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: In this paper the effect of variable frequency drive on the total harmonic distortion (THD) coefficient of the voltage curve of intrashop power supply systems and a comparative assessment of the distortion coefficients of the sinusoidal voltage curve for different connection schemes of VFD on the basis of simulation modeling are carried out.

title: Assessment of Energy Efficiency of the European Union Countries and Their Grouping by Qualimetric Methods
authors: Roman Trishch, Pylyp Hovorov, Sergiy Fedin, Olena Cherniak, Eduard Khomiak, Viktoriia Burdeina
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The European Union Green Deal highlights the strategic necessity of enhancing productivity within the energy sectors of member states. The aim of this study is to assess the energy efficiency performance of European Union countries and classify them through the application of a nonlinear functional relationship. Alongside the conventional linear normalisation approach, a nonlinear normalisation method for grouping countries is proposed. This approach is based on establishing a functional dependence between the actual indicator values and their corresponding scores on a dimensionless scale. As a result, the countries were categorised into three groups according to their level of energy efficiency. The developed methodology for country classification is universal and may be applied to a wide range of international comparative analyses.

title: sxSNF: Similarity Network Fusion-Guided Deep Graph Learning for Single-Cell Multimodal Integration
authors: Hongyu Duan, Yang Wang, Qianwen Chen, Xiaoxia Zhen, Jianxiu Jin, Huiling Liu, Li C. Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Accurate identification of cell types from single-cell multimodal data requires integration methods that preserve biological neighborhood structure while reducing modality-specific sparsity and noise. We present sxSNF, a single-cell multimodal integration framework that first constructs modality-specific cell–cell similarity graphs, then performs iterative similarity network fusion (SNF) before self-supervised graph representation learning. This fusion-before-learning design uses the fused graph as a denoised structural prior and further refines it through a masked-edge reconstruction objective with negative sampling. On PBMC-10 k and SHARE-seq benchmarks, sxSNF achieved ARI values of 0.694 and 0.589, respectively, and showed higher NMI/AMI values than the evaluated baselines under the same evaluation protocol. On the Chen-2019 SNARE-seq dataset, sxSNF recovered major cortical cell identities, marker-gene programs, and refined oligodendrocyte-lineage subpopulations supported by coordinated RNA expression and ATAC-derived gene activity. These results suggest that combining SNF-based structural denoising with graph learning can improve multimodal single-cell clustering and downstream biological interpretation. The code for sxSNF is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/labxscut/sxSNF</uri>.

title: Creative Satisfaction in Human-AI Collaborative Writing Workflows: Insights from Large Language Model Use in Technical Communication Training
authors: Krista Speicher Sarraf
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used in communication-intensive technical fields; however, their impact on how humans experience creativity in AI-assisted writing remains underexplored. This qualitative study examines college students’ perceptions of creative satisfaction when using LLMs for professional writing tasks. Drawing on interviews and reflective writing, the analysis identifies four interrelated dynamics: (1) competition for creative ownership reduces creative satisfaction, (2) collaborative writing as a source of creative satisfaction, (3) creative satisfaction through response and reflection, and (4) tensions around risks and conventions in LLM-assisted communication. Collectively, these findings suggest that writers’ use of LLMs may impact—both positively and negatively—their creative satisfaction. The study offers implications for designing AI-integrated writing and communication training that supports creative satisfaction in technical communication.

title: Permanent Monitoring of Protection, Automation, Control and Communication Systems in Substations
authors: Burak Tahincioglu
abstract: It is obvious that in the near future substations will consist of networks which accomplishes various daily operational tasks not only protection, automation, and control but also equipment data analytics, remote access, remote testing, situational awareness, and many other additional non-operational missions. To run these complex substation networks without issues, they need to be proactively monitored like IT (information technology) networks.The functional requirements of monitoring substation (OT) networks are different than monitoring IT networks.The monitored non-operational variables are very much interest of operation teams to get insights of protection, automation, and control system.The paper addresses usage of IEC 61850 for an integrated asset management system. Monitoring is a complementary method to support offline test results. Hence, a comprehensive health analysis of the switchgear can be achieved. Condition based maintenance of the primary assets can then be planned based on the data monitored.

title: Robot-to-Human Object Handover Informed Using Human Object Transfer
authors: Xinchao Song, Ava Megyeri, Sean Banerjee, Natasha Kholgade Banerjee
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: As robots become increasingly integral to every-day environments, they must interact with humans safely and naturally, especially during object handovers. We propose an approach to generate robot-to-human (R2H) handovers by being informed by how human givers hand objects to receivers. Our handover approach addresses the pre-handover phase of robot movement by predicting and performing optimal robotic grasp aligned with human giver grasp, and using data on how humans pose objects at transfer to predict the optimal object transfer pose and move the robot end effector to position the object at the predicted transfer pose. We use the HOH-Grasps dataset, a large-scale dataset of human-to-human handover demonstrations augmented with robot grasps at the transfer pose to learn how to conduct grasp and transfer pose in a manner that human givers do. We contribute a novel attention-based neural architecture that uses point cloud input on the object and giver hand anchor prediction to generate a frame-independent transfer pose representation consisting of a 6D rotation output and translation vector. Evaluations of our handover approach using HOH-Grasps and through real-world robot experiments show improved outcomes in terms of quantitative metrics over object pose and robot grasp prediction baselines.

title: A New Hybrid CNN–FOPID Control for Maximum Power Point Tracking in Photovoltaic Systems
authors: Aditya Singh, Afzal Sikander, Sumit Kataria, Rahul Bisht
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The low conversion efficiency in photovoltaic (PV) systems can be attributed to the nonlinear and time-varying relationship between the produced power and environmental parameters like irradiance and temperature. The existing MPPT techniques in conventional systems tend to possess poor transient response characteristics along with steady-state oscillations. Against this background, a hybrid MPPT framework that integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a fractional-order PID (FOPID) controller is proposed in this work. The physics model dataset constructed using a single-diode simulation model of a PV system is utilized for training a one-dimensional convolutional neural network for the estimation of the reference voltage corresponding to the MPP. The reference value thus obtained is further tracked using a DC-DC boost converter controlled by a FOPID controller, which is properly optimized using the parametric optimization algorithm, Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO). Simulation results based on changing irradiance profiles demonstrate that the proposed approach based on a combination of CNN-FOPID offers superior transient response characteristics, steady-state accuracy, and higher power output compared with FOPI–FPA, FOPI–WCA, and the existing ANN–FOPID approaches, thus validating its robustness in fluctuating operational environments.

title: Convolutional Neural Network Pipeline with Feature Synthesis for Cardiovascular Risk Prediction
authors: Ibtasam Ur Rehman, Jibran K. Yousafzai, Abdulraqeb Alhammadi, Muhammad Ikram, Maheen Ishfaq
abstract: This work presents a convolutional neural network (CNN) pipeline for cardiovascular disease classification using structured clinical data. The pipeline integrates rigorous leakage-free preprocessing, clinically motivated feature engineering, and ANOVA-based feature selection. The model is evaluated on the Heart Disease dataset (1,025 patient records) using 5-fold cross-validation and comparison against four baseline models. The proposed approach achieves 98.54% test accuracy, a 98.56% F1-score, and an AUC-ROC of 98.60%, with a minimal training-testing accuracy gap of 1.09%. Five-fold cross-validation confirms robust generalization (mean accuracy: 98.17% ± 1.02%). Comparison with classical models shows the CNN outperforms Logistic Regression (83.90%), SVM (90.24%), and Gradient Boosting (97.07%). With only one false positive and two false negatives on the test set, the model demonstrates high specificity (99.00%) suitable for clinical screening applications.

title: Post-Quantum Secure Federated DeFi for Inclusive Banking
authors: S. Sachan, D. Fickett, R. Buchinger, T. Miller
abstract: Recent advances in error-corrected qubits have accelerated the timeline for practical quantum computing. It poses a threat to cryptographic primitives used to secure financial systems, government infrastructure, communication networks, and DeFi (Decentralized Finance) ecosystems. This paper introduces a post-quantum secure federated DeFi framework that enables inter-bank collaboration to improve the inclusivity of individuals underserved by local lenders due to limited financial histories. Multiple banks contribute encrypted information batches to a virtual server, where lattice-based Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) enables end-to-end homomorphic computation. The server fuses local data-driven probabilistic assessments, expert beliefs, and verifiable evidence generated by the NASA-IBM Prithvi Geospatial Foundation Model (GFM), in encrypted format. Decentralized technologies are employed to ensure tamper-proof evidence and auditable accountability for all encrypted data exchanges between institutions and the server. The framework is tested on agricultural lending decisions for rural borrowers in Virginia.

title: Generalized Barycentric Coordinates for Hierarchical Multiscale Finite Elements on Irregular Polygonal Meshes: A Comparative Analysis
authors: Furkan Şk, Fernando L. Teixeira, Balasubramaniam Shanker
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This work examines how different choices of generalized barycentric coordinates affect both accuracy and computational performance of the finite element method (FEM) incorporating convex, otherwise irregular polygonal elements. The use of polygonal elements arises naturally in scenarios such as adaptive FEM based on convex polygonal mesh hierarchies aimed at improving computational performance for multiscale problems. The results obtained here provide useful guidelines for selecting generalized barycentric coordinates for multiscale FEM simulations with convex polygonal elements.

title: Enhanced Source Detection in Incomplete Networks via Redundancy-Guided Sensing and Anchor-Bounded Inference
authors: Yan Kang, Le Cheng, Peican Zhu, Zun Liu, Jiang Long, Yangming Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Despite recent advances in source detection, accurate and efficient detection in incomplete networks remains a significant challenge. Current methods, which typically rely on the entire network structure, are limited when the acquired network is structurally incomplete due to the data acquisition limits in practice, and they also incur substantial computational overhead when applied to large-scale networks. Therefore, we propose an efficient approach for source detection in incomplete networks via Redundancy-Guided sensing and Anchor-Bounded inference (RGAB). To address the bias introduced by missing edges, RGAB develops a redundant coverage metric for sensor deployment and iteratively deploys sensors based on a likelihood score that integrates coverage capability and redundancy contribution of nodes. Furthermore, to mitigate the computational burden of detection, this approach introduces two anchor sensors and restricts the candidate region based on the radial circle of anchors. Comprehensive evaluations conducted on diverse benchmark datasets confirm RGAB's superior performance over baselines in source detection in incomplete networks.

title: How Breakable Is Privacy: Probing and Resisting Model Inversion Attacks in Collaborative Inference
authors: Rongke Liu, Youwen Zhu, Dong Wang, Gaoning Pan, Xingyu He, Weizhi Meng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Collaborative inference (CI) improves computational efficiency for edge devices by transmitting intermediate features to cloud models. However, this process inevitably exposes feature representations to model inversion attacks (MIAs), enabling unauthorized data reconstruction. Despite extensive research, there is no established criterion for assessing the difficulty of MIA implementation, leaving a fundamental question unanswered: What factors truly and verifiably determine the attack's success in CI? Moreover, existing defences lack the theoretical foundation described above, making it challenging to regulate feature information effectively while ensuring privacy and minimizing computational overhead. These shortcomings introduce three key challenges: theoretical gap, methodological limitation, and practical constraint. To overcome these challenges, we propose the first theoretical criterion to assess MIA difficulty in CI, identifying mutual information, entropy, and effective information volume as key influencing factors. We use the Mutual Information Neural Estimator to quantify mutual information, a key metric for assessing the privacy-preserving effect of defences, and to validate the proposed criterion. Building on this insight, we propose SiftFunnel, a privacy-preserving framework to resist MIA while maintaining usability. Specifically, we incorporate linear and nonlinear correlation constraints alongside label smoothing to suppress redundant information transmission, effectively balancing privacy and usability. To enhance deployability, the edge model adopts a funnel-shaped structure with attention mechanisms, strengthening privacy while reducing computational and storage burdens. Experiments show that, compared to state-of-the-art defence, SiftFunnel increases reconstruction error by $\sim 30\%$, lowers mutual and effective information metrics by $\geq 50\%$, and reduces edge burdens by almost 20×, while maintaining comparable usability.

title: Distribution-Level Local Imbalance Market for Multi-Timescale Settlement and Network-Constrained Balancing
authors: Yuanxing Xia, Linjun Shi, Ke Wang, Alexis P. Zhao, Feng Xue, Haoran Ji, Yu Huang, Feng Wu
abstract: Existing local energy markets (LEMs) and local flexibility markets (LFMs) in distribution systems are primarily designed to facilitate energy trading and alleviate congestion. However, they lack explicit mechanisms for modeling nodal power deviations, pricing marginal imbalance costs, and allocating fast frequency response. This paper proposes a local imbalance market (LIM) that introduces a node-level imbalance formulation and distribution locational imbalance prices (DLIPs), internalizing network constraints and the balancing costs induced by power imbalances. The LIM incorporates a multi-timescale clearing structure where the distribution system operator (DSO) computes minute-level balancing decisions and DLIPs. A frequency response sensitivity matrix (FRSM) allocates second-scale fast-response costs to the nodes that caused the deviations. A native ADMM-based LIM clearing algorithm is designed to achieve scalable, privacy-preserving coordination among prosumers, aggregators, and the DSO without revealing private information. The second-scale allocation algorithm ensures cost causality and budget consistency through real-time measurement and expost reconciliation. Case studies on three modified distribution networks demonstrate that the proposed LIM enhances balancing efficiency, preserves participant privacy, and achieves accurate cost attribution under renewable uncertainty. The LIM provides a practical and economically consistent pathway for integrating renewable-rich resources into distribution-level market operations.

title: Search-Space Efficient Optimization of Karatsuba Multipliers for ECDSA Hardware Accelerators
authors: Pruthvi Parate, Daksh Sharma, Alwin Shaju, Madhav Rao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: The elliptic-curve digital signature algorithm (ECDSA) has emerged as a secure, computation-efficient successor to the traditional digital signature algorithm (DSA), leveraging the hardness of elliptic-curve arithmetic to meet the demands of modern, resource-limited hardware platforms. This article presents a hardware-centric exploration of ECDSA architectures, in which GF(<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2{^{{163}}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) Karatsuba-based finite-field multipliers are systematically refined using fast-converging multiobjective optimization techniques. In contrast to conventional NSGA-II-driven methodologies, the adopted optimization strategies significantly reduce convergence time while yielding higher quality design solutions. The optimized multiplier hierarchies were embedded in ECDSA signature generation and verification engines and implemented on a Kintex Ultrascale FPGA, followed by an ASIC evaluation using OpenROAD with the Nangate 45-nm standard-cell library. Comparative analysis against state-of-the-art (SOTA) implementations reveals substantial efficiency gains with different multiplier sequences in terms of area, delay, and area-delay product (ADP). By coupling a fast-convergence multiobjective optimization algorithm and hierarchical Karatsuba multiplier optimization with accelerated design-space exploration, this work delivers ECDSA hardware architectures that significantly improve efficiency while preserving the correctness of ECDSA operations. To encourage reuse and further investigation, all hardware source files and experimental data are released publicly for the research community.

title: Delay-Focused Preamble-Free Blind Acquisition for Asynchronous Overloaded Grant-Free IoT Uplinks
authors: Mingdi Li, Yanbin Li, Wenzhe Fan
abstract: Grant-free uplink reduces signaling overhead for short-packet Internet-of-Things (IoT) traffic, but the receiver must acquire asynchronous payload streams without a dedicated preamble. This paper studies blind multiuser acquisition for overloaded grant-free IoT uplinks under waveform-level timing offsets and a fixed hypothesized stream order. After matched filtering, oversampled observations are lifted into a causal polyphase virtual multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) model. A time-grid polyphase focusing (TGPF) front end forms multiple delay-focused views instead of selecting one fractional-delay estimate. Each view is initialized by integer-delay-screened finite-alphabet alternating minimization and refined by robust convolutive updates. All retained candidates are then re-estimated on the same original polyphase block before ranking, avoiding comparisons across view-dependent residual scales. The final selector combines reconstruction error with zero-lag and lagged inter-stream correlations, while separate diagnostics trigger additional TGPF families or an original-domain fallback. Simulations under an asynchronous waveform model show consistent uncoded bit-error-rate (BER) gains over controlled ablation baselines for binary phase-shift keying (BPSK) and quadrature phase-shift keying (QPSK). In the overloaded QPSK setting, the receiver also outperforms implemented task-matched proxy baselines adapted from pilot-free, learned/unfolded, unsourced-random-access/compressed-sensing, and classical blind-source-separation receivers. Additional studies quantify multi-view runtime cost, sensitivity to residual carrier-frequency offset (CFO), CFO drift, multipath, stream-order mismatch, and higher-order modulation limits. The architecture is intended as an infrastructure-side blind-acquisition front end for signaling-light IoT access.

title: Exploring Representation Learning for Developmental Infant EEG
authors: Jaime Uclés, Kristina Stemikovskaya, Lina M. Cómbita, M. Ángeles Ballesteros-Duperón, M. Rosario Rueda, Alberto Fernández, Isaac Triguero
abstract: Studying the brain in early life is crucial for understanding how brain development unfolds across the lifespan. Representation learning has been widely applied to EEG, showing potential for learning general-to-specific representations, that is, representations learned by models that capture broadly useful abstract features that can later be specialized. However, infant EEG differs from adult EEG due to the large developmental changes that occur in the first years of life. This developmental variability introduces additional challenges to applying representation learning to developmental infant EEG.In this study, we provide a systematic assessment of representation learning for early-life EEG and highlight simple yet important methodological considerations for its application. In particular, we compare self-supervised and baseline representation learning approaches to analyze their respective strengths and limitations. Evaluation is performed on a real-world dataset of 45 infants, recorded longitudinally through their early growth, using complementary strategies such as downstream prediction of chronological age, unsupervised clustering analyses, and ablation studies across sessions and subjects.

title: A Universal Generalized Flip Decoder for Next-Generation URLLC Systems
authors: Bere Praveen Sai, Mohammed Zafar Ali Khan, Lajos Hanzo
abstract: Ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) is becoming a cornerstone of wireless networks, supporting applications that require stringent reliability and latency, such as industrial automation, remote surgery, immersive virtual reality, and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication. Hyper Reliable Low Latency Communication (HRLLC), which is envisioned as a Next-Generation (NG) technology, has even made stringent reliability and latency requirements. Achieving the required reliability in the face of low-latency constraints requires robust error correction schemes. This paper introduces the Universal Generalized Flip Decoder (UGFD) concept conceived for efficiently decoding short codes. UGFD leverages multiple sources of enhancing reliability, such as the Channel State Information (CSI) and the associated Log-Likelihood Ratios (LLRs), and allows the adjustment of control variables for flexibly balancing error-correction performance vs. complexity. Our simulation results demonstrate that UGFD is capable of outperforming the state-of-the-art decoders. Hence it is eminently suitable for NG applications, including mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communications) and V2X communication.

title: A Universal Action Space for General Behavior Analysis
authors: Hung-Shuo Chang, Yue-Cheng Yang, Yu-Hsi Chen, Chien-Yao Wang, Hong-Yuan Mark Liao
abstract: Analyzing animal/human behavior has long been a challenging task in computer vision. Early approaches from the 1970s to the 1990s relied on hand-crafted edge detection, segmentation, and low-level features such as color, shape, and texture to locate objects and infer their identities—an inherently ill-posed problem. Behavior analysis in this era typically proceeded by tracking identified objects over time and modeling their trajectories using sparse feature points, which further limited robustness and generalization. A major shift occurred with the introduction of ImageNet by Deng and Li in 2010, which enabled large-scale visual recognition through deep neural networks and effectively served as a comprehensive visual dictionary. This development allowed object recognition to move beyond complex low-level processing toward learned high-level representations. In this work, we follow this paradigm to build a large-scale Universal Action Space (UAS) using existing labeled human-action datasets. We then use this UAS as the foundation for analyzing and categorizing mammalian and chimpanzee behavior datasets. The source code is released on GitHub at https://github.com/franktpmvu/Universal-Action-Space.

title: An Anatomy-Specific Guidewire Shaping Robot for Improved Vascular Navigation
authors: Aabha Tamhankar, Jay Patil, Giovanni Pittiglio
abstract: Neuroendovascular access often relies on passive microwires that are hand-shaped at the back table and then used to track a microcatheter to the target. Neuroendovascular surgeons determine the shape of the wire by examining the patient's pre-operative images and using their experience to identify anatomy-specific shapes of the wire that would facilitate reaching the target. This procedure is particularly complex in convoluted anatomical structures and is heavily dependent on the surgeon's level of expertise. Towards enabling standardized autonomous shaping, we present a bench-top guidewire shaping robot capable of producing navigation-specific desired wire configurations. We present a model that can map the desired wire shape into robot actions, calibrated using experimental data. We show that the robot can produce clinically common tip geometries (C, S, Angled, Hook) and validate them with respect to the model-predicted shapes in 2D. Our model predicts the shape with a Root Mean Square (RMS) error of 0.56 mm across all shapes when compared to the experimental results. We also demonstrate 3D tip shaping capabilities and the ability to traverse complex endoluminal navigation from the petrous Internal Carotid Artery (ICA) to the Posterior Communicating Artery (PComm).

title: PAMS-PSO: A Predictive and Adaptive Multi-Swarm Particle Swarm Optimizer for Dynamic Optimization Problems
authors: Kang Yin, Yuji Sato
abstract: Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) struggles in Dynamic Optimization Problems (DOPs) due to outdated memories and loss of diversity after convergence. To address these issues, this paper proposes PAMS-PSO, a Predictive Adaptive Multi-Swarm PSO that integrates peak prediction, lightweight topology reconstruction, adaptive multi-swarm management, and elite-swarm protection. The algorithm proactively estimates peak movement, maintains diversity without disruptive reinitialization, and stabilizes sub-swarm allocation through overlap handling. Experiments on the DF1 benchmark under varying numbers of peaks, movement speeds, and shape-change rates show that PAMS-PSO achieves consistently low and stable Tracking Error compared with several representative dynamic PSO variants. Overall, PAMS-PSO provides an effective and robust framework for dynamic environments.

title: The First Approach to Modeling Direct Lightning Striking a Skyscraper Equipped with the Lightning Current Monitoring System
authors: Robert Ziemba, Grzegorz Karnas, Grzegorz Maslowski
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: The aim of the paper is to verify of direct lightning current impact flowing through a skyscraper construction on generation of nearby electric field signatures without the influence of the lightning channel. The skyscraper 220-m high located at "Olszynki Park" site in Rzeszów was modeled using HIFREQ module of CDEGS packet by application of the method of moments. Steel skeleton of the construction including about 60 m spire was imitated in a real scale in presence of lossy ground characterized by 100 Ωm soil resistivity. The lightning strike was modeled as a lumped current source generating the waveshape with the same peak current 30 kA for both mathematical approximations used in the simulation. A portion of electric field originated by the skyscraper structure presence was investigated. The results showed a significant influence of the used mathematical approximation representing the current flowing into the skyscraper structure. Vertical component of lightning electric field was calculated at a specific 2D distance of about 1.5 km from the tall structure. This place corresponds to the localization of the Lightning Observatory in Rzeszow equipped with a set of wideband electric field sensors. In the near future, modeling and computer simulations of lightning striking the skyscraper will help, among other things, to analyze lightning electromagnetic field signatures measured at the station operating at the Rzeszow University of Technology.

title: A Taxonomic Framework for Human-Robot Interaction in Healthcare - Research Gaps and Future Research Directions
authors: Muhammad Shahbaz Shah, Bethany Cole, Samuel Olatunji
abstract: Human-robot interaction (HRI) in healthcare has expanded rapidly, but it still lacks a systematic taxonomic framework. This study developed a four-dimensional taxonomy for HRI in healthcare by analyzing existing literature, including 49 articles from 2020 to 2025, using QDA Miner and WordStat. The taxonomy encompasses user types including patients, healthcare staff, caregivers, children, and older adults; robot types including surgical, socially assistive, physically assistive, and service robots with functional and appearance subcategories; interaction elements including task based, social, and therapeutic interactions; and context including acute care centers, community based, specialized units, research centers, institutional care units, and telehealth. In this analysis, child subcategories (child codes) along with parent categories revealed prominent and least studied research areas. Furthermore, multidimensional co-occurrence analysis showed clustering of various dimensions and both connected and isolated components of the taxonomy. Overall, this proposed taxonomic framework helps to identify the research gaps, potential research opportunities, and a roadmap for advancing HRI in healthcare.

title: Developing An AI Agent for Technical Support in the Machine Tools Industry
authors: Aitor Martinez-Seras, Unai Gomez-Ferreira, Javier Martin-Arauzo
abstract: In the machine tool industry, technical support services operate under strict efficiency constraints, yet technicians often struggle with extensive, highly granular documentation and inconsistent troubleshooting workflows. This paper presents the initial results of a pilot deployment of a conversational system based on AI Agents and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Unlike standard RAG approaches, our system employs a hierarchical summarization methodology and agentic reasoning to preserve narrative context and retrieve distributed technical assets. The pilot demonstrates the viability of these solutions in real world environments, while highlighting the need for more extensive validation.

title: Research of the Process of Combustion of RDF/SRF with Coal in Pulverise Coal Boilers
authors: Serhiy Kobzar, Liudmyla Haponych, Iryna Holenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: In order to determine the input data for modeling co-combustion processes, the morphological and chemical composition, volatile matter yield, and heating value of fuel derived from municipal solid waste in Ukraine were determined. The range of calorific value of RDF produced from Ukrainian MSW is 14.4-15.9 MJ/kg. Our calculations show that Ukraine has the potential to produce 1.6-2.7 mln tons of such RDF annually, which equates to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.8-1.5 \text{mln}$</tex> tons of fuel equivalent. Numerical modeling demonstrated the possibility of environmentally safe co-combustion of RDF with a mixture of G and DG coals in the TPP-312 boiler. Two RDF types were considered in the study: RDF1 corresponding to Solid Recovered Fuel (SRF) classes 1-2 according to EN 15359:2011, and RDF2 (class 3), whose composition and calorific value are typical for RDF produced from Ukrainian municipal solid waste. The results showed that replacing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10-15 \%$</tex> of coal with RDF leads to a reduction in nitrogen oxide formation compared to conventional coal combustion, while maintaining the required residence time of combustion products in the high-temperature zone necessary for neutralization of harmful substances. Cocombustion with RDF intensifies ignition in the near-burner zone and changes the temperature and CO concentration fields inside the furnace. Considering the technical and environmental performance of the TPP-312 boiler, the optimal RDF share in the heat balance was determined to be 10% for high-reactivity RDF1 and 15% for low-reactivity RDF2.

title: Evaluation of the accuracy of determining the moisture content of the cellulose component of transformer insulation using the Frequency Domain Spectroscopy method
authors: Konrad Kierczynski, Pawel Molenda, Andrzej Cichon, Pawel Zukowski
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: In this study, alternating current electrical parameters of six power transformers with rated voltages not lower than 110 kV were measured using a DIRANA PDC–FDS analyzer. For these transformers, the XY parameters according to CIGRE were known. Using the DIRANA software, the moisture content in the solid insulation component was determined by applying the actual X and Y values (Method I) as well as without providing these values (Method II). In the case of a transformer with an operating time of 29 years, the moisture content obtained using Method I was 0.3%. Such a low moisture content after a long period of service is rather unlikely. The application of Method II resulted in a moisture content of 2%. For the remaining transformers, the moisture content estimated using Method I was from 2 to 2.5 times higher than that obtained using Method II. A comparison was performed between the XS and YS values determined by the software (Method II) and the actual values. It was found that, in Method II, the DIRANA software significantly overestimates the pressboard volume for all investigated transformers, by approximately 1.4 up to even about 2.4 times compared to the actual volume. Consequently, it overestimates the influence of the alternating current parameters of the pressboard on the results of moisture content estimation in the cellulose component. At the same time, the influence of the insulating oil parameters is reduced. This indicates that the alternating current parameters of the insulating oil differ from those assumed in the software. The observed differences between the actual and software-assumed alternating current parameters of the insulating oil indicate that the estimation of moisture content, both with and without consideration of the XY parameters, yields results that deviate from the actual moisture levels in the solid insulation of power transformers.

title: AI-Powered Symptom Assessment and User Experience: A Case Study of Simtomi and Simtomi-Care
authors: Jinha Lee, Chan Hyung Lee, Hyunsung Lee, Seunghwan Kim, Ban Hyung Lee, Minjun Shin, Hojin Shin, Jungdo Park
abstract: Digital symptom checkers are widely used for quick guidance on health concerns, yet many systems still face challenges in collecting accurate information, supporting communication, or integrating with clinical workflows. To explore how these tools function in real use, we examine the case of the Simtomi system, which pairs a multilingual symptom assessment application with a provider-facing platform. Empirical studies were conducted in two countries. In South Korea, based on participants’ firsthand experience, we found that the system improved how patients communicated their symptoms and helped clinicians review cases more efficiently through structured summaries aligned with diagnostic reasoning. In the United States, responses from prospective users and healthcare professionals highlighted the value of multilingual support, structured questioning, and the system’s potential to assist clinical coordination. These findings offer a grounded account of how AI-based symptom assessment tools can operate across different healthcare contexts and provide broader insight into usability, trust, and usefulness in digital health.

title: Coordinated Restoration of Integrated Electricity-Gas Systems With Frequency Stability Constraint Learning: A Multi-stage Model with Exogenous-Endogenous Uncertainties
authors: Yuqi Zhang, Chuan He, Xuan Liu, Lei Wu, Xiaodong Huo, Lu Nan, Tianqi Liu
abstract: The ability of integrated gas-electricity distribution systems (IGEDSs) to survive under extreme disasters attaches great importance. This paper proposes a multi-stage coordinated restoration model of IGEDS considering exogenous-endogenous uncertainties with initial and conditional nonanticipativity conditions under disasters. The exogenous uncertainties consist of component failure uncertainty and energy generation/ consumption uncertainty, and endogenous uncertainty represents traffic uncertainty determined by repair crew routing decisions. Network reconfiguration and frequency reserve of distributed generators (DGs)/ wind turbines (WTs) are utilized for enhancing system resilience.
