The optimization with constraint learning (OCL) method is applied to build linearized frequency nadir constraints via dynamic sparse neural network training and pruning. Moreover, to solve the proposed model with exogenous-endogenous uncertainties, an enhanced outer approximation (EOA) algorithm is presented. Numerical analysis indicates that the proposed model could effectively improve the load restoration of IGEDS under extreme disasters.

title: OntoCRA-NS: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Explainable and Auditable Cybersecurity Compliance Aligned with EU Regulatory Frameworks
authors: Jasmin Cosic, Admir Jukan, Miroslav Baca
abstract: At the EU level, the NIS2 Directive, Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), and AI Act (AIA) form a strategic regulatory triangle designed to strengthen Europe’s digital sovereignty and cyber resilience. Together, they should ensure that we have common level of cyber-security (NIS2), that products with digital elements are built securely (CRA), and AI systems are trustworthy, robust, safe and aligned with fundamental rights and EU values (AIA). This integrated approach creates a unified cybersecurity framework that protects citizens, businesses, and critical infrastructure from emerging digital and AI-driven threats across the EU. This paper addresses the growing need for automated, explainable, and auditable cybersecurity compliance mechanisms under the CRA. It introduces OntoCRA-NS, a neuro-symbolic framework that integrates neural language understanding with formal ontological reasoning to support automated compliance assessment. The system combines a neural extractor that interprets unstructured documentation and vulnerability data with an OWL-based ontology (OntoCRA) containing formalized requirements encoded as SWRL rules. Extracted evidence is transformed into ontology instances and processed by the Pellet reasoner to infer Declaration of Conformity (DoC) or Banned from Market decisions, accompanied by human-readable justifications. A proof-of-concept implementation demonstrates end-to-end reasoning using representative products from the CURIUM EU project, achieving deterministic, traceable, and legally interpretable compliance outcomes. The results confirm that hybrid neuro-symbolic reasoning enables explainable, trustworthy, and reproducible CRA compliance evaluation, contributing to the emerging paradigm of regulatory AI under the EU cybersecurity and AI governance frameworks.

title: Increasing of the Rotary Machine Efficiency by the Numerical-Analytical Identification of Active Magnetic Bearing Stiffness and Damping Properties
authors: Volodymyr Martynenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The paper presents an approach for increasing of the efficiency of the rotor in active magnetic bearings by enabling the capabilities of the proper rotor dynamics simulation using a simple algorithm for active magnetic bearing stiffness and damping calculation and further optimization taking into account the electromagnetic part physical properties and tuning of the bearing electronics. The algorithm is based on the consideration of the collocated rotor arrangement and is applicable not only to rigid, but also to flexible rotors. The effectiveness of the approach is illustrated based on the example of the real rotary machine, and its value consists in the possibility to be used for rotor models simulated in the commercial finite-element software, independent finite-element codes, or even simple mass-inertia models of rigid rotors. The novelty of the approach consists in the unique set of component transfer functions used to implement the algorithm, as well as in the case study which shows the applicability of the algorithm to simulation of rotors in active magnetic bearings.

title: GlucoGuardian: An AI-Powered Platform for Diabetes Management
authors: Bhargavi Pathuri, Shounak Palnitkar Jitendra, Satya Hyma Likhitha Basina, Jinzhu Gao
abstract: We introduce a novel, closed-loop AI architecture for proactive diabetes management. At its core is a predictive-prescriptive engine powered by a novel multimodal data fusion mechanism, integrating a dynamic physiological model (digital twin) with continuous glucose monitoring data, AI-analyzed medical images, and geolocation-tracked activity. This enables forecasting of metabolic states and prescription of quantifiable, personalized interventions. The integrated platform includes a Diabetes Type Assessment Tool (92% T2D accuracy), an AI activity coach, a real-time alert system, and a Medical Imaging Analysis module (95% retinopathy screening accuracy). Methodologically, our integrated AI decision-support architecture demonstrates how fusing multi-modal data moves beyond passive monitoring to deliver an empirically validated, methodologically coherent holistic solution for personalized diabetes management and complication prevention.

title: A Hierarchical End-of-Turn Model with Primary Speaker Segmentation for Real-Time Conversational AI
authors: Karim Helwani, Hoang Do, James Luan, Sriram Srinivasan
abstract: We present a real-time front-end for voice-based conversational AI to enable natural turn-taking in two-speaker scenarios by combining primary speaker segmentation with hierarchical End-of-Turn (EOT) detection. To operate robustly in multi-speaker environments, the system continuously identifies and tracks the primary user, ensuring that downstream EOT decisions are not confounded by background conversations. The tracked activity segments are fed to a hierarchical, causal EOT model that predicts the immediate conversational state by independently analyzing per-speaker speech features from both the primary speaker and the bot. Simultaneously, the model anticipates near-future states (t+10/20/30 ms) through probabilistic predictions that are aware of the conversation partner’s speech. Task-specific knowledge distillation compresses wav2vec 2.0 representations (768 D) into a compact MFCC-based student (32 D) for efficient deployment. The system achieves 82% multi-class frame-level F1 and 70.6% F1 on Backchannel detection, with 69.3% F1 on a binary Final vs. Others task. On an end-to-end turn-detection benchmark, our model reaches 87.7% recall vs. 58.9% for Smart Turn v3 while keeping a median detection latency of 36 ms versus 800–1300 ms. Despite using only 1.14 M parameters, the proposed model matches or exceeds transformer-based baselines while substantially reducing latency and memory footprint, making it suitable for edge deployment.

title: A Novel Reconfigurable Grid Following/Grid Forming Control for Photovoltaic Inverters
authors: Supratik Bhowmick, Chandan Chakraborty
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a reconfigurable grid following (GFL)/grid forming (GFM) control for photovoltaic (PV) inverters in grid-connected system. The proposed control has the capability of combining GFL and GFM characteristics with any proportion depending on the system requirement. For demonstration and verification, a cascaded H-bridge (CHB) based multilevel inverter is considered which requires only one PV source per phase and DC links of the remaining H-bridge modules are supplied from floating capacitors. The proposed control not only minimizes the converter count in the system but also has the ability to transit flexibly from GFL to GFM control mode or vice versa. Based on this concept, an adaptive reconfigurable control is introduced which enables the inverter to operate with optimal performance and therefore enhances stability support to the grid under different types of contingency events. To accomplish this, a novel contingency detection technique is introduced which utilizes the sensed DC link voltages to accurately detect any contingency event. The system is tested on a laboratory based experimental prototype to verify the aforementioned claims and demonstrate effectiveness of the proposed control.

title: EPE 2026 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)

title: Integrated Neutronics Modeling for Inertial Fusion Energy Systems: Development and Application to LD-FIRST
authors: T. Hallam, B. El Dasher, J. Galbraith, N. Habtemariam, T. Ma, W. Meier, C. Fiorina
abstract: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (LLNL) is proposing a new Laser Driven Fusion Integration Research and Science Test Facility (LD-FIRST) with the goal of providing an experimental testbed for future Inertial Fusion Energy (IFE) systems. However, IFE systems require detailed and accurate multiphysics modeling to quantify material damage, thermal loading, and tritium breeding within complex chamber environments. This work presents the first step in an integrated multiphysics framework that couples meshed CAD-based geometry within Monte Carlo neutronic simulations to enable high-fidelity analysis of IFE chamber concepts, with future coupling to external codes. The neutronics workflow utilizes OpenMC and its third-party capability to use CAD-based geometries through DAGMC and tally on unstructured meshes with Libmesh to evaluate neutron transport behavior, geometric fidelity, and material performance under reactor-relevant conditions. The use of tailored tallies on unstructured meshes in this framework allows direct transfer without interpolating to CFD simulation tools. Two IFE chambers were evaluated, both conceived by LLNL: HYLIFE-II and Laser IFE (LIFE). This work produced high-fidelity conformal surface and volumetric meshes of the HYLIFE-II and LIFE chambers with mapped spatial insight into material damage, thermal loading, and tritium breeding. The HYLIFE-II model was built utilizing available resources and used as a test case to verify that the neutronics framework can handle complex geometries. The LIFE chamber CAD was provided by LLNL and was the main focus of this work. This work analyzes multiple ternary alloy breeding materials for the LIFE chamber, across different <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{6}\mathrm {Li}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> enrichments to produce data relevant to the LD-FIRST project. This work also investigates the level of model fidelity for the LIFE chamber, and results show that inclusion of detailed first wall and coolant structures increased the predicted tritium breeding ratio (TBR) by ~30%, highlighting the sensitivity of tritium breeding and the need for a high-fidelity simulation framework for IFE chambers. These developments provide a scalable toolset for the design and optimization of next-generation IFE chambers, forming a solid foundation for future coupled multiphysics analysis.

title: Quality-Guided Forgery Adapter for Generalizable AIGC Image Detection
authors: Jun Wang, Zitong Yu, Chaomeng Chen, Lingchen Gu, Wenbo Wan, Jiantao Zhou, Weiming Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The rapid advancement of AI-generated content (AIGC) presents significant challenges for digital forensics, necessitating robust and generalizable detection frameworks. Existing detection methods primarily rely on visual feature extraction, while vision-language model-based approaches are limited to class-label prompts, failing to capture quality-related artifacts introduced by different generative models. To address this limitation, we introduce QAFD, a novel Quality-Assisted Forgery Detection framework that incorporates image quality information into the detection process. Specifically, we design a quality queried attention block to effectively fuse class-based content prompts with quality-aware text prompts. This integration enhances the model’s ability to capture semantic artifacts related to degradation patterns commonly associated with AI-generated images. Furthermore, we introduce the Quality-Guided Forgery Adapter (QGFA) to incorporate quality-aware textual cues into the visual domain, improving feature extraction for both spatial and frequency-based forgery artifacts. This synergy allows frequency cues to enhance low-level artifact perception, while quality-aware guidance strengthens high-level discriminative representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that QAFD achieves superior generalization to unseen generative models over three datasets and significantly maintains its robustness against common image post-processing operations. The codes will be released at github.

title: Dual-band Terahertz Metamaterial Based Absorber for Biosensing Applications
authors: Shashikant Suman, Anil Kumar, Soni Singh
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Metamaterial terahertz (THz) absorbers have attracted many researchers for sensing and imaging analysis applications. This paper presents a comprehensive study of the design and simulation of dual-band metamaterial-based THz sensor. The proposed structure provides dual absorption peaks in the THz region, allowing multiple sensing layer channels. An analyte layer with varying refractive index and thickness is placed on the proposed absorber to study the shifts in the resonant frequency for sensing. Additionally, skin cancer tissue and normal tissue are modelled using their refractive index, and the absorber’s response is analyzed. The results demonstrate that the proposed design provides high absorption (>99%), polarization-insensitivity, good sensitivity, and well-defined frequency shifts that distinguish normal from cancerous samples. The design is simple, compact, and promising for biomedical detection.

title: Research on the Multi-Target Perception Capability of mmWave Radar in Complex Traffic Environments
authors: Bin Li, Tao Li, Yi Yang, Shuang Zhang, Jiashuai Chen, Xiaolei Dong, Geng Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: This paper addresses the need for multi-target detection using vehicle-mounted millimeter-wave radar in complex traffic scenarios such as dense traffic flow, multipath effects, and mutual interference. It constructs an edge-side real-time perception system based on AWR2944 and proposes an AE-CFAR detection framework. The system employs a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3\mathrm {Tx}\times 4\mathrm {Rx}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> array to form 12 virtual channels, generating 120 electronic beams within a ±30° field of view to construct a range–Doppler–angle three-dimensional data cube. A lightweight autoencoder is used to learn background reconstructable patterns, and CFAR threshold judgment is performed in the residual energy domain to improve detection stability under conditions of non-uniform clutter and dense targets. Verification through four types of experiments—anechoic chamber, urban vehicle-mounted, artificial smoke, and high-speed gantry—shows that: the detection rate remains above 92% under dense working conditions (number of targets > 40); the pedestrian detection rate is approximately 87% when visibility is 5 m (while cameras are below 40%); the detection rate for gantries at 0–200 m is above 93%, and remains above 90% beyond 200 m; the end-to-end latency P95 is approximately 68 ms, beamforming takes about 0.8 ms per frame, and AE inference time after INT8 quantization is less than 5 ms per frame, meeting the real-time budget for on-vehicle online deployment.

title: Optimizing Source–Drain Doping to Improve Drive Current and Transport Characteristics in FinFETs
authors: Ajay Kumar, Vaibhav Bhatt, Vanshika Sharma, Aditya Jain, Neha Gupta, Kaushal Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: With an emphasis on improving drain current performance through thorough source and drain doping approach optimization, this study offers a closer look at advanced FinFET design. The method shows how a minor change in doping engineering can result in a discernible enhancement of device behaviour. This study seems to explore the effects of different N-type doping concentrations in the source and drain regions on the electrical and transport properties of FinFET devices, in line with previous work on gate electrode work function manipulation. A number of doping profiles were applied and examined using Silvaco ATLAS TCAD 3D simulations in order to determine how they affected device potential and drain current (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf>). Even if some behaviors changed slightly depending on the situation, the analysis aids in understanding how various doping variations affect overall FinFET performance. The simulation findings indicate that while maintaining a steady V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> and regulated leakage behaviour, increasing the source-drain doping concentration greatly enhances carrier injection and drive current. Despite minor differences, an examination of the band structure, electric field, and carrier mobility reveals that optimised doping contributes to better charge transfer and lower series resistance.

title: A Multi-Model Ensemble YOLO Framework for Automated Detection of Dental Pathologies in Low-Quality Panoramic Radiographs
authors: Malik Haseeb Haider, Haider Raza, Aleš Filder, Rabiya Koya, Akhilanand Chaurasia
abstract: Accurate identification of dental pathologies in panoramic radiographs is vital for effective diagnosis and treatment planning; however, manual interpretation remains time-consuming, subjective, and prone to human error. This paper presents a deep learning-based object detection framework to automate the classification of dental diseases from low-quality panoramic X-rays. To address these issues, we propose a novel multi-model ensemble pipeline based on YOLOv11n architectures, where individual models are trained for each dental pathology and combined through a pseudo-labelling process to generate a refined, high-quality dataset. A consolidated YOLOv11m model trained on this pseudo-labelled dataset demonstrated a 6.4% relative performance improvement compared to transfer learning. Further evaluation using a new, high-quality 62-class dataset indicated that models trained from scratch outperform those fine-tuned via transfer learning. The findings highlight the critical influence of data quality on model performance and provide a robust comparative analysis of training strategies. Overall, this study introduces an effective multi-model ensemble methodology and establishes a foundation for developing accurate, automated diagnostic systems for low-quality panoramic dental radiographs in digital dentistry.

title: New IoT Architecture With Adaptive Predictive Controller for Resilience Enhancement of Smart Microgrids Toward Wind Power Penetration and Cybersecurity
authors: Mahmoud Elsisi, Chun-Lien Su, Seyed Hossein Rouhani, Irfan A. Khan, Charalambos Konstantinou, Mohammad Hassan Khooban
abstract: System parameter uncertainty, variable nature of renewable energy sources (RESs), and false data injection (FDI) due to cyberattacks represent the main challenges against the resilient operation of microgrids and cybersecurity. These factors can push microgrid frequencies beyond safe limits, leading to system instability. This article presents a novel architecture integrating an unknown input observer (UIO) with adaptive model predictive control (AMPC) and an Internet of Things (IoT) platform for effective microgrid frequency control. The UIO, employing linear matrix inequalities (LMI) and quasi-oppositional learning-based equilibrium optimizer (QO-EO), estimates disturbances caused by FDI and RES variations. The AMPC adjusts generator output and manages electric vehicle (EV) charging/discharging to mitigate these disturbances and maintain stable system frequencies. Real-time system status visualization and alerts via the IoT dashboard enhance operational oversight. The study evaluates the proposed UIO-based AMPC against various cyberattacks, load fluctuations, wind power variability, and parameter uncertainties. Comparative analysis with model predictive control (MPC), proportional-integral-derivative (PID), and fuzzy logic controllers demonstrates superior performance in estimating FDI and managing RES variability, thereby ensuring microgrid stability under challenging conditions. Quantitatively, the proposed AMPC reduces settling time to less than 0.3 s, whereas conventional methods require more than 5 s under parameter uncertainty. In addition, the maximum overshoot is limited to below 0.008, compared to 0.012–0.0246 for traditional controllers. Simulation and experimental results affirm the effectiveness of the approach in suppressing cyberattack impacts and maintaining frequency stability within operational thresholds.

title: Editorial: The Rise of Affective Computing: Challenges and Opportunities
authors: Jesse Hoey, Raya Ferdous, Tom Pan, Grace Yin

title: Stochastic Modelling of Low‑Voltage Distribution Feeder Operation
authors: Michal Bahernik, Marek Höger, Filip Petrik
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Evaluation of operating conditions and development planning in low‑voltage networks is increasingly challenging. Due to ongoing changes in customer behaviour and consumption patterns, these networks are often approaching their operational limits. As a result, the original design and sizing of many low‑voltage feeders are no longer aligned with current operating conditions. Distribution system operators must therefore analyse emerging trends and prepare appropriate measures to ensure the reliability and security of electricity supply.Estimating future operating conditions is particularly difficult because of the highly stochastic nature of consumer behaviour. Traditional deterministic methods, commonly applied in sub‑transmission and transmission systems, may produce misleading results in low‑voltage networks–either missing critical operational extremes or, when overly conservative, exaggerating potential issues. Such inaccuracies can lead to suboptimal development planning, compromising future operational security or causing unnecessary investments.This paper presents a histogram-based simple stochastic modelling framework for estimating operating conditions in low‑voltage networks, supporting decision‑making in network development and reinforcement planning.

title: Glaucoma Diagnosis and Progression Prediction Based on Deep Multimodal Fusion and Collaborative Attention Mechanism_supp1-3698681.pdf
authors: Wei Lan
abstract: Glaucoma is the leading cause of irreversible vision loss worldwide. Most approaches rely on unimodal data, failing to fully leverage the complementarity between multimodal information. Furthermore, clinically acquired fundus photography often suffer from poor quality issues such as noise interference and low contrast. In addition, effectively integrating data from different modalities remains a significant challenge. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a glaucoma diagnosis and progression prediction model (MUGLDEM) based on deep multimodal fusion and collaborative attention mechanisms. This model integrates multimodal data from the UK Biobank including clinical text, fundus photography and optical coherence tomography (OCT). For low-quality fundus photography, MUGLDEM integrates non-local denoising and contrast-constrained histogram equalization algorithms to improve the quality of image. Then, MUGLDEM independently designs feature extraction models for different modal data to preserve the feature integrity of different modal. Further, MUGLDEM effectively fuses multimodal features by designing dual-gate residual attention module and a bidirectional cross-attention module. Finally, the fused multimodal features are utilized to diagnose glaucoma and predict its progression. The experimental results on the UK Biobank demonstrate that MUGLDEM achieves state-of-the-art performance in both glaucoma diagnosis and progression prediction tasks. The source code for this work is available on GitHub (<uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lanbiolab/MUGLDEM</uri>).

title: Transformation calculation between the inner- and outer rotor permanent magnet motor
authors: Yunlu Du, Yunkai Huang, Baocheng Guo, Yanchen Wu, Ya Li, Runyu Wang, Yuxiang Wu, Shichuan Ding
abstract: In this paper, an equivalent conversion method for inner-rotor and outer-rotor permanent magnet motors that considers curvature effects is proposed. To consider the flux focusing and flux diffusion effects of different rotor structures, the transformation theory is derived in detail with the center radius of the air gap as the transformation reference. In addition, according to the harmonic modeling (HM) technology, a nonlinear semi-analytical model of a surface-mounted inner-rotor motor is established, and the proposed conversion method is verified. The finite element method (FEM) and experimental results are in good agreement with the results of the proposed method. Using the equivalent transformation theory, engineers can use an existing mature surface-mounted inner-rotor permanent magnet motor to design, simulate, and evaluate a surface-mounted outer-rotor permanent magnet motor, and vice versa.

title: Data-Efficient Design of Experiments for Lipid Nanoparticle Development through Prior-Informed Machine Learning
authors: Jacob Sanderson, Adrian Kucia, Wai Lok Woo, Gary Montague, Yvonne Perrie, Thomas McLean, David Vaughan Palmer, Bruce Richard Williams
abstract: Experimental design for nanomedicine product development is often limited by high experimental costs and small datasets. Traditional design of experiments (DOE) methods are typically applied to a single development programme with a fixed set of parameters, leading to redundant testing and inefficient exploration of the design space. This work presents a machine learning (ML) enabled DOE framework that leverages prior experimental knowledge to guide efficient sampling across conditions. The framework integrates ML models with adaptive point selection to identify informative regions of the design space while minimizing experimental runs. We propose two strategies for transferring knowledge between conditions: prior-informed learning, and residual learning, which we compare with the baseline approach of direct learning, demonstrating that prior-informed and residual approaches achieve comparable surface reconstruction accuracy with up to 3 times fewer samples than direct learning.

title: Bayesian Neural Network-Based Model Calibration and Uncertainty-Adapted Control for Robot Manipulators
authors: Naiyuan Liu, Xiaolong Yu, Jiaqi Yan, Wei Wang, Jinhu Lü, Xinyang Tian
abstract: Ensuring tracking precision and compliant interaction is essential for robotic manipulation. This requires an accurate robot dynamics model and an effective feedback gain adaptation strategy to improve control performance while preventing excessive stiffness under uncertainties. This article proposes a novel control framework that integrates uncertainty-aware Bayesian neural network (BNN) learning with uncertainty-adapted control design. A BNN infers the nominal model error as a probabilistic distribution, where the predictive mean calibrates the nominal model error and the variance quantifies the learning uncertainty. The control law then compensates for the BNN’s predictive mean, and adaptively adjusts the feedback gain based on the predictive variance. Our BNN is computationally efficient for real-time deployment in robotic control systems. The effectiveness and applicability of the proposed method are demonstrated through both simulation studies and real-world experiments on a 7-DoF robot manipulator.

title: Enhanced Half-Aperture OAM Mode Identification Based on Parity-Symmetry Decomposition
authors: Wenxi Zhang, Yan Sun, Jiuchuan Guo, Yusheng Fu, Maria Sabrina Greco, Fulvio Gini
abstract: Mode division multiplexing based on vortex electromagnetic waves holds great promise for enhancing wireless communication capacity. Accurate demodulation of vortex electromagnetic waves relies on precise identification of orbital angular momentum (OAM) modes. However, due to the hollow divergence of vortex electromagnetic waves, traditional full-aperture mode identification methods are difficult to implement in practice because of the large spatial resource requirements. Consequently, partial-aperture reception, particularly half-aperture reception, is often adopted. Nevertheless, half-aperture reception still faces many challenges that affect accurate mode identification. Motivated by the distinct symmetry properties of even and odd modes, this paper proposes a novel OAM mode identification method based on parity-symmetry decomposition. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method maintains high robustness under low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and sparse-sampling conditions where conventional methods suffer from pronounced performance degradation. The proposed method reduces hardware complexity and spatial requirements, offering a practical and scalable solution for next-generation OAM-based mode division multiplexing.

title: Efficiency of Energy Conversion in the Autonomous Electric Drive of the Metropolitan Rolling Stock
authors: Mykola Ostroverkhov, Liudmyla Spinul, Heorhii Veshchykov, Bogdan Pryymak
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The paper considers the topical issue of the efficiency of recuperation on the example of electric transport. The aim of the work is to study the efficiency of energy return when electric vehicles braking from different speeds. A battery of supercapacitors was used to power traction electric drive. The study was carried out by modeling on the example of an electric drive of a subway car. The paper presents a mathematical model of a supercapacitor and an asynchronous electric motor. The traction electric drive has a typical vector torque control system. A DC-DC converter is used to match the voltage level of the supercapacitor battery and the electric motor. Graphs of velocity, torque, discharge and energy use of supercapacitors, and the dependence of the recuperation efficiency coefficient on velocity, are obtained. The results of the simulation made it possible to determine the speed at which the recuperation efficiency will be the greatest.

title: Assessment of Accumulated Damage and Resource Exhaustion of the Low-Pressure Rotor of T-100/120-130 Turbine
authors: Olga Chernousenko, Vitalii Peshko, Dmitro Rindyuk, Oleksii Konovaliuk
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The study involves an assessment of accumulated damage to the low-pressure rotor turbine T-100/120-130 metal based on real operating conditions of Unit No. 2 of PJSC “Kharkiv CHP-5”. Three main startup modes of turbine operation, most characteristic in the practical usage of this CHP plant, have been considered. Both static and cyclic components of metal damage have been taken into account. An analytical model and calculation algorithm for metal damage under hightemperature conditions are provided. Conditions of contact interaction between the shaft and the mounted discs of the lowpressure rotor are considered. Simulation modeling of the rotor behavior during startup modes has been performed, and data on the thermal and stress-strain state of the low-pressure rotor have been obtained. Time moments with maximum temperature gradient values and stress amplitude in characteristic rotor areas for the main startup modes have been identified. Stress concentration zones have been established. Based on the obtained data, the cumulative damage to the main metal of the rotor is calculated at 66.9 %, along with an individual remaining resource assessment.

title: PACMNET V4, N2, June 2026 Editorial
authors: Marco Mellia, Gianni Antichi, Jiasi Chen
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Networking
abstract: None

title: Deep Learning-Based Melanoma Classification from Dermatoscopic Images
authors: Pratik Kumar, Seema Gulati
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Melanoma is an aggressive type of skin cancer, which requires early diagnosis and accurate identification to increase the chance of survival for patients. This study describes a strong, effective deep learning framework that will allow the automated classification of dermatoscopic images into two groups (benign & malignant). A dataset consisting of 9605 dermatoscopic images, and used a rigorous yet comprehensive preprocessing process that included stratified partitioning, geometric & color augmentation to improve the model's ability to generalize. This study optimized two state-of-the-art convolutional neural network models, EfficientNet-B0 & MobileNet-V3 Large, using transfer learning. To overcome the problem of class imbalance associated with medical imaging, a Weighted Random Sampler and Focal Loss (ℽ = 1.5) into the training protocol. The experimental results exhibit high levels of accuracy in diagnosing skin cancer using the EfficientNet-B0 model, which produced peak accuracies of nearly 95.4% and AUROC scores of 0.9899 (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic). Additionally, the ensemble method well performed through a soft voting technique with accuracy at 96% and AUROC at 0.986. These findings suggest that the proposed methodology yields a reliable, computationally efficient means of utilizing deep learning as a computer-assisted diagnostic tool across the continuum between high-performance deep learning and clinical application.

title: 1800 V/20 A E-mode GaN PSJ-HEMTs
authors: Ziwei Wang, Yuchuan Ma, Shuhui Zhang, Cheng Li, Jun Su, Yuki Satoshi, Tian Gao, Chao Liu
abstract: We report 1800 V/20 A enhancement-mode GaN-on-sapphire polarization super-junction (PSJ) HEMTs. The fabricated devices demonstrate a low on-resistance (RON) of 87 mΩ, a low specific on-resistance (RON,SP) of 4.6 mΩ·cm2, a high breakdown voltage exceeding 1900 V, and state-of-the-art Baliga’s figure of merit (BFOM) of over 785 MW/cm2 among ampere-level E-mode HEMTs. Furthermore, a high ON/OFF ratio of ~1010 and a low hysteresis voltage of 80 mV are characterized, together with a minimized threshold voltage (Vth) shift under positive gate bias stress at elevated temperatures. These results can pave the way for developing high-performance, cost-effective, high-voltage, and high-power GaN power devices.

title: A Nonlinear Chaos-Driven Cryptosystem for Biomedical Data Security
authors: Lalit Narayan Yadav, Manoj Joshi, Jayant Mishra, Rahul Kumar Gupta, Vandit Suryavanshi
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The growing use of remote healthcare technologies has raised the need for robust physiological data protection during transmission. When ECG signals are sent over wired or wireless channels, they can be intercepted and accessed by unauthorized parties. Conventional cryptography methods often have high computational complexity, memory requirements, and power consumption, making them unsuitable for real-time and low-power biomedical applications even though they can provide strong security. In this work chaotic masking and synchronization is used. This study suggests a chaos-based secure communication system for the transfer of ECG signals. A continuous-time chaotic signal generated by an analog implementation of Chua’s circuit is used as a masking carrier to produce a broadband, noise-like transmitted signal that effectively conceals identifiable ECG features. An identical chaotic system that functions in a master-slave configuration in the receiver synchronizes with the transmitter, allowing for precise reconstruction of the chaotic carrier and subsequent subtraction-based recovery of the initial ECG signal. The proposed system is modelled and analysed using MATLAB Simulink and Multisim to confirm chaotic behaviour, masking efficacy, and synchronization performance under ideal conditions. The method is further validated by using physical implementation of Chua’s circuits based on operational amplifiers. Simulation and experimental results that demonstrate effective signal concealment, reliable chaotic synchronization, and accurate ECG recovery validate the feasibility and suitability of chaos-based approaches for safe biomedical communication systems.

title: A Three-Stage On-Device Machine Learning Pipeline for Service-Aware Power Optimization in 5G User Equipment
authors: Jong-Phil Lee, Bo-Young Kim
abstract: Advancements in next-generation wireless communication technologies, such as 5G, have enabled the utilization of Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication (URLLC) and Enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB) resources. To enhance user-perceived performance, or Quality of Experience (QoE), it is essential to appropriately allocate network resources to applications based on their specific requirements. Consequently, there is a growing necessity for intelligent resource management at the User Equipment (UE) level. This study proposes a three-stage on-device ML pipeline for service-aware power optimization in 5G UE. The pipeline consists of: (1) a Traffic Classifier (XGBoost) that performs real-time binary classification of RT/NRT traffic; (2) a NO-DATA Predictor (Student-LogReg with Knowledge Distillation) that forecasts traffic-idle intervals in NRT sessions; and (3) a Finite State Machine (FSM) Controller that selectively suspends classifier inference during predicted NO-DATA periods. The FSM Controller suspends classifier operation at P(NO-DATA) ≥ 0.80 for two consecutive slots and resumes at P(NO-DATA) < 0.60, enabling recovery within one slot. Experimental evaluations on real-world KT 5G SA network traffic data demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves a 61.42% reduction in classifier inference calls while maintaining a QoE error rate of 0.26% and zero RECLASSIFY events, confirming that the Predictor's DATA Recall of 98.35% effectively serves as a safety net against classifier misclassification. This approach provides a practical solution for implementing application-aware, energy-efficient network management in resource-constrained on-device environments. To validate generalizability beyond simulation, the pipeline was installed on a custom-built Android measurement application and field-tested on seven Samsung Galaxy device models across six countries (Korea, Japan, Australia, Mexico, Brazil, and the United Kingdom) on commercial networks, yielding 28 measurement sessions with RECLASSIFY = 0 in all cases. Real-world field measurements show reduction rates ranging from 19.6% to 60.4%, with NRT applications (YouTube, Netflix) achieving a mean of 46.1% compared to 33.2% for RT applications (YouTube Live, Zoom), confirming a 12.9 pp service-type gap consistent with the simulation results.

title: CADEMAS – A Framework for Cooperative Automated Decision-Making Systems
authors: Pavel Novoa-Hernández, David A. Pelta, Mariia Godz, José L. Verdegay, Dionisio Buendia-Carrillo
abstract: The growing ubiquity of data and artificial intelligence has made it increasingly easy to construct decision models that automate or assist human decision-making. As organizations and infrastructures rely on multiple, heterogeneous Automated Decision-Making (ADM) systems, a new challenge emerges: how to integrate and reconcile their outputs into coherent, explainable, and context-aware collective decisions. This paper proposes a new conceptual and architectural framework, the Cooperative Automated Decision-Making System (CADEMAS), as a foundation for the study and design of cooperative decision architectures. CADEMAS formalizes the interaction among individual ADM systems through an Input Manager, an Output Manager, and a Context Model, allowing the transformation and aggregation of either homogeneous or heterogeneous decision outputs. CADEMAS generalizes cooperation across potentially heterogeneous decision agents, data types and goals, going beyond ensemble learning schemes, which combine homogeneous models over identical data. We argue that CADEMAS represents a necessary paradigm shift: from building isolated decision models to engineering cooperative ecosystems of decision-making entities. This paper outlines the theoretical basis, architectural elements, and implications for future research.

title: Non-Contact Infant Sleep Monitoring via Deep Learning-Based Facial Behavior Analysis
authors: Gabriela Garcia Gamarra, Fahad Wahab, Jörg Franke, Sebastian Reitelshöfer
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Sleep is essential for infant health and development, yet assessing infant sleep remains challenging because internal states must be inferred from observable behavior. This work presents a non-contact method for sleep-related behavioral inference based on facial analysis from video data. Unlike contact-based sensors or handcrafted feature approaches, the proposed system learns eye and mouth behavior directly from facial imagery and integrates these cues over time. Video frames are processed using facial region detection and landmark extraction, followed by MobileNet-based convolutional neural networks for binary classification of eye and mouth states. Frame-level predictions are aggregated through temporal decision logic to estimate sleep-related behavioral states, with blink dynamics and yawning serving as complementary indicators of transitional phases. Leveraging transfer learning enables efficient and robust inference suitable for real-world settings. Experimental results show reliable estimation of eye and mouth states and stable sleep-related behavioral inference. The proposed framework provides a practical, non-contact alternative to traditional infant sleep monitoring systems. Future work will address dataset expansion, robustness to occlusions and lighting variability, and the integration of multimodal inputs.

title: Privacy-Preserving Incentive Mechanism for Federated Learning in Regional Carbon Allowances Management and Trading
authors: Chenyang Guo, Shengnan Zhao, Yanyan Zhang, Shiyue Qin, Gongshu Wang
abstract: Integrating federated learning (FL) with differential privacy (DP) into regional carbon allowances management and trading enables collaborative model training that protects both local data privacy and model security. However, the noise injection in DP degrades model accuracy, necessitating additional local training and increasing the overall computational overhead for enterprises. This overhead reduces the willingness of rational and self-interested participants to contribute to model training. To address this challenge, this study models the interaction between the regulator and enterprises as a Stackelberg game and proposes a scalable, bandwidth-efficient FL framework for regional carbon allowances management and trading. The framework adaptively determines the number of local training iterations based on enterprises’ privacy budgets and employs a submodular enterprise selection strategy for aggregation, which decreases redundant uploads and enhances global model quality. Furthermore, a deep reinforcement learning–based multi-objective differential evolution with bi-level optimization (DRL-MODE2) algorithm is developed to efficiently solve large-scale, high-dimensional Stackelberg games. Comprehensive experiments were conducted on both a carbon emission time-series dataset and the MNIST benchmark dataset under different enterprise scales, incentive mechanisms, and optimization algorithms. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework incentivizes enterprise participation and improves model accuracy, without compromising privacy preservation and communication efficiency.

title: Autoencoder-Enabled Anomaly Reconstruction Framework for Intrusion-Tolerant Cloud Data Science Workflows
authors: Rahul Amte, Bhaskara Raju Rallabandi, Md Abul Kalam Azad, Keshav Kaushik, Renu Kumawat, Mukesh Soni
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: This research has developed an autoencoder based framework for the detection of anomalies within the cloud data science workflows. A key idea behind this development is learning how workflows should normally behave and subsequently measuring the amount of errors that occur when anomalies happen. Because obtaining real data from a cloud environment is typically difficult due to it's nature (messiness), the author created a simulated cloud environment using synthetic data. The results indicated that the proposed framework could be effective in identifying anomalies with varying amounts of noise, bursts in load, and timing shifts, as evidenced by the use of reconstruction error to identify early indicators of anomalies, as well as track how the issues propagate through various stages of the workflow. Compared to baseline frameworks, the results demonstrated that the framework produced fewer false alarms. The overall results indicate that the proposed framework provides a viable method for improving intrusion tolerance in cloud workflow systems.

title: Adaptive Fully-Actuated Control for Lower Limb Exoskeletons with High-Order Disturbance Compensation
authors: Jiange Kou, Yinghan Xia, Xiangkai Shen, Zhiguo Yang, Yixuan Wang, Yan Shi, Zongyu Zuo, Fei Xie
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter addresses disturbance-resilient, constraint-satisfying control for lower-limb rehabilitation exoskeletons operating under irregular patient-induced perturbations. We combine a fully actuated system (FAS) outer loop with a high-order disturbance observer (HODO) inner loop and a constrained gradient-based self-tuning scheme. The framework was validated in simulation and on a prototype device. In prototype tests, the hip and knee achieved peak tracking errors of 0.069 rad and 0.038 rad, with no violations of physical limits.

title: Weakly Paired Remote Sensing Change Captioning with Large Multimodal Model
authors: Qing Zhou, Junzheng Wu, Yuyu Jia, Shihao Zhou, Junyu Gao, Weiping Ni, Qi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Automatically generating textual descriptions of changes between bi-temporal remote sensing images remains challenging due to the labor-intensive and time-consuming process of obtaining fully aligned image-caption paired data. To address this limitation, we propose a novel weakly paired learning framework that harnesses the powerful visual and language capabilities of large multimodal models (LMMs) for remote sensing change captioning. Unlike conventional methods that rely on precisely annotated image-caption pairs, our approach effectively utilizes weakly paired tags that encapsulate changed objects and their corresponding actions for each image. The proposed framework is built upon an LMM architecture and operates synergistically across two distinct visual-language phases via parameter-efficient adaptation. In the visual change perception phase, we characterize spatiotemporal variations by identifying cross-temporal object changes and their associated actions through tag recognition. Subsequently, in the language interpretation phase, we leverage the pretrained LMM to convert these visual change tags into coherent and semantically rich textual descriptions, exploiting the linguistic knowledge embedded in the LMM. To further enhance the alignment of LMM-generated captions with human annotations, we introduce a train-time expansion strategy, which refines alignment by rewriting synonymously expressed captions back into their original phrasing. This strategy improves alignment without incurring additional inference costs. As a plug-and-play method, our framework is compatible with various LMMs and achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art fully supervised approaches, demonstrating its effectiveness in remote sensing change captioning. The code will be available at https://github.com/mrazhou/WeCap.

title: Design of Patient-Specific Robot for Left Coronary Artery Access: Algorithm and Demonstration
authors: Amber K. Rothe, Jaydev P. Desai
abstract: Continuum robots are a promising technology for minimally-invasive surgeries such as cardiac catheterization. However, due to anatomical differences between patients, a single robotic tool may not be appropriate for all patients. Therefore, designing custom, patient-specific robotic tools may improve the ease of certain procedures for clinicians. In this work, the design and fabrication of a tendon-driven notchedtube robotic tool for left coronary artery access based on a patient's chest computed tomography (CT) scan is demonstrated. The fabricated robot approximated the desired planar curve with an average error of 2.77 mm with an applied tendon tension within 3% of the expected tension. Additionally, the robot was successfully navigated through a two-dimensional phantom of the aortic arch from the descending aorta to the entrance of the left coronary artery.

title: Comparison of Selected Machine Learning Algorithms for Classifying On-Load Tap-Changer Defects Using Acoustic Emission Waveforms
authors: Michał Włodarz, Andrzej Cichoń
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This paper presents a machine learning (ML) approach for diagnosing defects in On-Load Tap-Changers (OLTCs) using acoustic emission (AE) signal analysis and wavelet-based feature extraction. A laboratory model of an oil-type OLTC was used to simulate eight operating conditions, including healthy operation and seven common fault scenarios. AE signals were recorded using a single transducer, and eighty-four datasets were generated by combining twelve mother wavelets with discrete wavelet transform (DWT) decomposition levels from two to eight. Statistical features were extracted from the detail coefficients and used to train three ML algorithms: Decision Tree (DT), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Support Vector Machine (SVM). Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC). The results show that both the choice of wavelet and the decomposition level significantly influence classification accuracy. The best performance was achieved by the SVM classifier trained on features extracted with the sym6 wavelet at seven decomposition levels, obtaining 0.83 in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, and 0.82 in MCC. The proposed method demonstrates strong potential for implementation in online OLTC condition monitoring systems.

title: Device for Metering He-Xe Gas Mixture Composition for Gas-Cooled Reactor Power Systems
authors: Yevhen Pistun, Halyna Matiko, Hanna Krykh
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Binary noble gas mixtures increase the efficiency of gas-cooled reactors and thermoacoustic devices. To ensure a certain ratio between the mixture components, it is necessary to provide continuous control of its composition during preparation. Therefore, the paper is devoted to the development of a device for metering the molar fraction of xenon in a He-Xe mixture in the range from 0 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$40 ~\text{mol} \%$</tex> using structural and parametric optimization according to the criterion of the average sensitivity with restrictions. The authors proposed to use a gasdynamic bridge diagram with two different oppositely connected glass capillaries and two jewel stones. A mathematical model of the device and algorithms for calculating the optimal constructive parameters of throttles have been developed, which provide an average sensitivity of 185 Pa/mol% Xe. The principal diagram of the gasdynamic analyzer of the He-Xe gas mixture is proposed and its static characteristic is calculated.

title: Challenges and Marketing Strategies of Renewable Energy Market Development
authors: Iryna Lysenko, Serhii Stepenko, Oleksandr Novoseltsev, Anna Verbytska, Hanna Dyvnych, Tetyana Yevtukhova
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Taking into account the growing demand for services in the field of renewable energy, increased attention to environmental issues, there is a need to improve marketing approaches to support this sector. The article is devoted to the analysis of the problems of marketing support of the renewable energy market and the development of proposals for their solution. The authors have identified the main challenges faced by the renewable energy industry and proposed marketing strategies that will help to develop this industry more effectively. The novelty of the research lies in the identification of the potential of market innovations and the development of practical recommendations for increasing the effectiveness of marketing support for renewable energy. The conducted research can be useful both for scientists and for practitioners in the field of marketing activity in the field of renewable energy.

title: Mathematical Modelling of Production and Economic Activities of an Enterprise of the Energy Sector
authors: Olena Parshyna, Marharyta Parshyna, Yurii Parshyn, Oleksandr Shapoval, Iurii Savchenko, Ihor Klymenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Analytical processing of production and economic activity is necessary for improving management efficiency of an enterprise of the energy sector. Mathematical modelling is of particular relevance for analytical processing. The article deals with mathematical modelling of the dynamics change in the complex of indicators of internal and external information. Information system for analytical processing of production and economic information of an enterprise of the energy sector of the national economy for selecting an adequate mathematical model of production and economic activities has been proposed. The application of a systems approach to the analysis of production and economic activities provides the opportunity to conduct comprehensive research and analyze the dynamics of a company's development, taking into account both internal indicators and external market information. Such a scientific approach allows for the integration of efficient production technologies with market management methods. However, the adoption of managerial decisions depends on how efficiently and thoroughly information is processed. An information system is proposed to enhance the efficiency of analytical processing of economic information. The mathematical methods in this information system enable the creation of adequate mathematical models for analyzing changes in the complex of performance indicators of an enterprise in the energy sector and its divisions. Using these mathematical models, it is possible to identify patterns in economic phenomena, study production development trends, and analyze the strategic development of an enterprise in the energy industry.

title: A Method for Analyzing the Damage Location of a Low-Noise Amplifier Based on High-Power Microwave Injection
authors: Jin Wang, Guihua Wang, Minjie Li, Yinxiang Jin, Jinjing Ren, Zhongyuan Zhou, Shiping Tang
abstract: This article proposes a cross-scale analysis method for locating the damage of low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) under wide pulsewidth high-power microwave (HPM) action. In response to the inherent defect of the traditional offline S-parameter cyclic testing, which has low efficiency, this article constructs a threshold rapid criterion based on real-time voltage response monitoring, significantly improving the timeliness and repeatability of identifying the damage initiation point. In view of the deficiency that existing simulations are mostly limited to using TCAD to simulate the lattice temperature of the device, which makes it difficult to reflect the real failure behavior of the packaging structure under the action of multiple physical fields, this article establishes a TCAD–COMSOL collaborative simulation. Finally, by characterizing the micro-area morphology of the damaged samples, the failure location is confirmed. The spatial distribution, damage mode, and evolution path predicted by the simulation are highly consistent with those confirmed by the actual measurement, verifying the comprehensive advantages of this method in terms of mechanism interpretability, positioning accuracy, and engineering reproducibility.

title: High-Quality Entity Segmentation and Grounding_supp1-3698232.pdf
authors: Xiangtai Li
abstract: Class-agnostic segmentation is of great importance for generalizing the localization of semantically-consistent regions from limited training data to unseen domains and categories. It has benefited numerous applications, such as human-interaction-assisted image editing. Despite their success, existing class-agnostic methods (e.g., SAM2 and entity segmentation) still struggle with boundary-accurate masks and semantic understanding. To address these issues, we propose ESG, a pipeline for high-quality entity segmentation and grounding supported by a new dataset EntitySeg. At first, the proposed dataset naming EntitySeg contains images spanning various image domains and entities, along with plentiful high-resolution images and high-quality mask annotations for training and testing. Then, the ESG mainly consists of two modules: CropFormer for high-quality entity segmentation whereas GELLA for accurate noun extraction from sentences and semantic matching between language and visual regions. Unlike existing grounding methods that jointly train a segmentation and a large language model, ESG adopts a two-stage decoupled design, preserving high-quality masks and grounding robustness without the trade-offs often introduced by joint training. CropFormer ensures high-quality entity segmentation results, which can then be encoded into the GELLA model for effective grounding. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed pipeline across five tasks, including entity segmentation, panoptic segmentation, open-vocabulary segmentation, referring segmentation, and panoptic localized narratives. Furthermore, GELLA module of ESG pipeline is highly flexible and capable of processing mask inputs from any segmentation framework, thanks to its lightweight colormap/vision encoder, language/mask decoder, and association module. The entity segmentation dataset and grounding code will be released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/qqlu/Entity</uri>.

title: The Trainable BCJR and Its Applications
authors: Martina Magnaldi, Guido Montorsi
abstract: We propose a trainable version of the Additive Bahl-Cocke-Jelinek-Raviv (A-BCJR) algorithm by interpreting it as a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). By leveraging the smoothness and differentiability of the max* operator, the core component of the A-BCJR forward and backward recursions, we derive a backpropagation algorithm that enables end-to-end training from the network’s output. The resulting model, referred to as T-BCJR, comprises a linear layer that computes edge metrics from state and input metrics, followed by a nonlinear max* layer that marginalizes these metrics back to the state and output domains. We further derive the corresponding delta backpropagation recursions, which exhibit a structural symmetry with the original forward-backward predictive steps. Unlike prior approaches that only replaced memoryless metric units in BCJR or Viterbi algorithms with Neural Networks (NNs), T-BCJR enables full trainability of the entire recursive structure. We derive a trainable detector, termed T-Detector, by connecting the T-BCJR to the channel output via an additional convolutional linear layer that emulates a channel shortening filter. The T-Detector can be trained to operate as a modulation- and channel-agnostic detector, capable of performing channel shortening, Maximum Likelihood (ML) sequence detection, and the computation of symbol-level or bit-level Log-Likelihoods (LLs) for soft-input decoding. Moreover, it is compatible with iterative receiver schemes involving outer channel decoders. The predictive step of the T-Detector maintains the same Digital Signal Processing (DSP) complexity as a conventional model-based detector, with the added benefit of being trainable from a cost function defined on the generated LLs. Experimental results demonstrate that the T-Detector can be trained to match the performance of the corresponding model-based detector for static and slow-time varying channels with appropriate pilot densities.

title: A 5-Bit Quantized and Programmable Racetrack Micro-Ring Modulator Integrated With Indium Tin Oxide for Photonic Neural Networks
authors: Li Yang, Lidan Lu, Yingjie Xu, Bofei Zhu, Guang Chen, Shuai Gao, Jie Liao, Mingli Dong, Jianzhen Ou, Lianqing Zhu
abstract: In this article, we propose a racetrack microring modulator integrated with indium tin oxide (ITO) for energy-efficient photonic neural networks. Our designed thermooptic modulator experimentally achieves 54 programmable states via voltage-controlled resonant tuning, demonstrating the modulation efficiency of 9.23 pm/mW and an extinction ratio of 26.67 dB. Leveraging the optical transparency and electrical conductivity of ITO, the design scheme eliminates metallic heater-induced losses while introducing submicron thermal transfer by directly integrating with silicon-on-insulator waveguide. Numerical simulations using the transfer matrix method and Joule heating models reveal the quadratic relationship between change of temperature and the bias voltage (ΔT-V<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>t) and obtain a theoretical tuning efficiency of 0.6 nm/V, it is experimentally verified that our ITO-based RMRM achieves a tuning efficiency of 0.24 nm/V. Employed in a wavelength-division multiplexing photonic convolutional neural network, the modulator array achieves 5-bit quantized optical convolutions with a classification accuracy of 98.80% using the Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology dataset. Overall, this design scheme can bridge the gaps between photonic device engineering and analog AI acceleration, providing a feasible solution to complementary metal oxide semiconductor-compatible framework for scalable photonic computing systems.

title: SplatXtRact: Tractable Gaussian Splatting via Open World Region-of-Interest Extraction and Refinement
authors: Hannah Schieber, Constantin Kleinbeck, Angela P. Schoellig, Stefan Leutenegger, Daniel Roth
abstract: We present a task-conditioned refinement for 3D Gaussian Splatting (GS) that enables robots or human operators to selectively extract task-relevant regions of a learned scene. Given a pre-trained GS map, our approach supports local region-of-interest (ROI) refinement, preserving a global map consistency while meeting close to real-time constraints required for interactive robotic perception. The framework decouples semantic ROI selection from initial GS optimization, allowing flexible integration with external and novel perception models. We evaluate our approach on indoor and outdoor data (TUM RGB-D, MipNeRF360), demonstrating a higher novel view syn-thesis quality compared to the state-of-the-art, reduced artifacts, and bounded latency suitable for human-in-the-loop operation.

title: Power Congestion Management of Subtransmission Areas Using Partial Renewable Power Curtailment: Modeling, Control, and Open-Source Software
authors: Guillaume Ganet-Lepage, Alessio Iovine, Sorin Olaru, Guillaume Giraud, Patrick Panciatici, Manuel Ruiz, Lucas Saludjian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Congestion challenges within power transmission networks are escalating due to the growing integration of renewable energy sources. Transmission system operators (TSOs) traditionally address these issues through network reconfiguration or by limiting the power generated from renewables in complex subtransmission areas. However, there is an increasing need for sophisticated methodological tools that empower operators to more efficiently manage optimal power flow. This entails exploring innovative solutions, including the integration of storage devices alongside curtailment strategies. In response to this demand, our article introduces mathematical models and numerical tools designed to facilitate the partial curtailment of renewable power. These models are presented as dynamic systems, offering a comprehensive depiction of the transmission network and accommodating the presence of storage devices. They are then employed within a model-based receding horizon control approach. Finally, these models are implemented in an open-source tool that facilitates the application of the proposed control strategies in realistic simulations based on MATPOWER.

title: A High-Frequency-Link Single-Phase Rectifier With a Simplified Active Clamping Circuit
authors: Wenjie Zhu, Zhiyu Wang, Yu Sun, Xintao Yang, Guangyu Zhang, Yang Wang, Keliang Zhou
abstract: In the high-frequency-link (HFL) single-phase ma-trix rectifier, severe voltage spikes and resonant issues occur on the AC side of the transformer, which are primarily caused by two factors:(1) the inability of the filter inductor current to achieve safe commutation, and (2) the resonant interactions be-tween the transformer leakage inductance and the parasitic ca-pacitance of the switching devices. To address these issues, a sin-gle-phase high-frequency-isolated rectifier with a simplified clamping circuit is proposed in this paper, and a novel modula-tion strategy is developed based on the active clamping circuit. Through the synergistic interaction of the simplified active clamping circuit and the novel modulation strategy, effective elimination of voltage spikes and safe commutation of the current are achieved, while soft switching of power devices is realized, resulting in a significant reduction in overall system losses. The proposed HFL rectifier topology and its modulation strategy were analyzed, and an experimental prototype rated at 1 kW has been established to validate its performance. The experimental results verify that the proposed HFL rectifier enables soft switch-ing for most of the power devices and exhibits excellent control performance with high efficiency. The proposed rectifier offers a high-performance, high-efficiency, and high-power-density solu-tion for isolated rectification applications.

title: Edge Intelligence-Driven Low-Altitude IoT via Digital Twin: Requirements, Challenges and Solutions
authors: Minghui Dai, Xin Li, Tianlei Song, Jin Jiang, Jiaming Pei, Ahmed Farouk
abstract: As the core infrastructure supporting applications such as air traffic management, logistics, and emergency rescue, the low-altitude Internet of Things (LAIoT) has experienced explosive growth in recent years. However, it faces bottlenecks including latency sensitivity, limited bandwidth, and security risks. Edge intelligence enables low-latency data processing, while digital twin (DT) constructs visual management and control capabilities via virtual-real mapping. The integration of edge intelligence and DT provides a promising approach for the development of LAIoT. This article proposes an edge intelligence-driven LAIoT via DT from the perspective of requirements, challenges, and technical solutions. We first introduce the core requirements of DT-enabled LAIoT, including sustainability, efficiency, and security. Secondly, the DT-enabled LAIoT framework integrating the cloud, edge, and terminal layers is presented. Thirdly, the state-of-the-art approaches for DT-enabled LAIoT are provided to meet the core requirements. Moreover, a case study of edge intelligence task scheduling in the proposed framework is conducted to validate the system performance. Finally, the future directions for facilitating high-quality development of the low-altitude economy are discussed.

title: Beyond Polarity in Bengali Texts: Can Multi-Task Modeling Capture Sarcasm Intensity and Classification?
authors: Meherunesa Hossain Ibnath, Khan Md Hasib, Korhan Cengiz, Nikola Ivković, Adnan Akhunzada
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Sarcasm detection in Bengali is challenging due to implicit sentiment, contextual dependence, and limited linguistic resources. This study proposes a novel multi-task learning framework that jointly predicts sarcasm polarity and estimates sarcasm intensity using a Cross-Task Attention mechanism to enable effective feature sharing and capture fine-grained sarcasm patterns. We preprocess a balanced Bengali dataset of 25,636 comments with a language-specific normalization pipeline and generate intensity scores using a transformer-based scoring model. The framework is evaluated on several pre-trained transformers, including BanglaBERT, Bangla-ELECTRA, mBERT, XLM-RoBERTa, and DistilBERT. BanglaBERT achieves the best performance with 77.38% accuracy and an F1-score of 0.7761, while ELECTRA and BERT-Base Uncased perform competitively. For interpretability, we apply LIME to Bengali predictions and SHAP to English-translated outputs. Overall, the proposed multi-task learning framework uses a shared transformer encoder with a Cross-Task Attention mechanism to capture sarcasm patterns more effectively.

title: Velocity Obstacle-Based Control Barrier Function for Safety-Critical Predictive Control
authors: Francesco Trotti, Daniele Meli
abstract: This paper introduces the Velocity Obstacle-based Control Barrier Function (VO-CBF), a novel safety framework bridging geometric collision prediction with dynamic control. While classical Velocity Obstacles ignore system dynamics, the VO-CBF encodes collision cones directly into the dynamic state space. By natively coupling position and velocity, this formulation mathematically reduces the constraint's relative degree from two to one for force-controlled systems, fundamentally eliminating the solver conservatism and input saturation conflicts associated with standard high-order predictive barriers. We propose a hybrid Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) architecture that enforces strict discrete-time invariance for the immediate step and affine approximations across the prediction horizon, balancing formal safety guarantees with computational tractability. Comparative simulations and hardware experiments demonstrate that the VO-CBF effectively throttles velocity to prevent collisions in high-inertia scenarios where standard kinematic baselines fail.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Membership Function Optimization and Non-Fragile Control for IT2 Polynomial Fuzzy Switched Positive Systems
authors: Xiaomiao Li, Zhendong Shao, Zhiyong Bao, Peng Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning-based membership function optimization and embedding (RL-MFOE) control scheme for interval type-2 (IT2) polynomial fuzzy switched positive systems. A non-fragile static output feedback controller is designed to handle parameter uncertainties and external disturbances, guaranteeing a prescribed L1-gain performance. A matrix factorization technique is introduced to resolve the non-convex coupling between unknown positive vectors and controller gains. In the proposed scheme, a reinforcement learning algorithm tunes the parameters of the IT2 fuzzy membership functions (MFs) in a data-driven manner, where the decay rate serves as the reward signal guiding the optimization. The optimized MFs are expressed in polynomial form and incorporated into sum-of-squares (SOS) stability conditions via relaxation matrices, which reduces the conservatism in stability analysis, facilitates higher decay rates, and in turn allows stricter mode-dependent average dwell time (MDADT) bounds. By iteratively updating the MF parameters, the scheme satisfies the prescribed L1-gain performance and supports higher switching frequencies while preserving system positivity and stability. Two simulation examples validate the effectiveness of the proposed methodology.

authors: Raula Gaikovina Kula, Christoph Treude, Xing Hu, Sebastian Baltes, Earl T. Barr, Kelly Blincoe, Fabio Calefato, Junjie Chen, Marc Cheong, Youmei Fan, Daniel M. German, Marco Gerosa, Jin L.C.
